questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must designate one or more designated Supervisors, who meet the proficiency requirements set out in Rule 2600, to be responsible for the discretionary accounts" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5352-5354

questions.stem: A compliance review at Selwyn Securities finds that a retail discretionary account holds common shares of Selwyn's publicly traded parent company, purchased under the account's discretionary authority. Which statement is correct?

options.A: The holding is permitted because the parent is not the Dealer Member itself
options.B: The holding is permitted if the client gave prior written consent
options.C: The holding violates the rule that a discretionary account must not hold any publicly traded securities of the Dealer Member or its affiliates
options.D: The holding is permitted up to 5% of the account's market value

questions.rationale: Subsection 3276(1) prohibits a discretionary account from holding any publicly traded securities of the Dealer Member or its affiliates — no consent or threshold exception exists for this prohibition. Option B is the trap: prior written client consent under 3276(3) relieves only the listed investment restrictions (officer/director-of-issuer situations and dealer-underwritten new issues), not the absolute bar on dealer/affiliate securities in 3276(1).
questions.source_quote: "A discretionary account must not hold any publicly traded securities of the Dealer Member or its affiliates." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5389-5390

questions.stem: Seven months into a discretionary account agreement with Ashby Securities, the client emails the firm written notice terminating the agreement; the firm receives the notice at 10:00 a.m. Wednesday. The Registered Representative had entered a discretionary order at 9:30 a.m. that morning, before the notice arrived. How does section 3274 treat this termination?

options.A: The termination by the client is effective when received by the Dealer Member on Wednesday, except for orders entered prior to receipt of the notice — so the 9:30 a.m. order stands
options.B: The termination is effective not less than 30 days from Wednesday, since 30 days' notice applies to either party
options.C: The termination is ineffective because a discretionary account agreement cannot be terminated before its 12-month term expires
options.D: The termination is effective Wednesday and requires the 9:30 a.m. order to be cancelled

questions.rationale: Subsection 3274(2)(i) provides that a discretionary account agreement may be terminated by written notice by the client, effective when received by the Dealer Member, except for orders entered prior to receipt of the notice — so the order entered at 9:30 a.m., before receipt, is unaffected. Option B misassigns the 30-day minimum, which under 3274(2)(ii) applies only to termination by the Dealer Member, not by the client.
questions.source_quote: "by the client, effective when received by the Dealer Member, except for orders entered prior to receipt of the notice" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5377-5378

questions.stem: Under the IDPC Rules definition, which condition is part of what makes an account a "discretionary account" rather than a managed account?

options.A: Investment decisions are made on a continuing basis by a Portfolio Manager
options.B: The Dealer Member has not solicited the discretionary authority, and the authority is accepted to accommodate a client who is frequently or temporarily unavailable to authorize trades
options.C: The account is exempt from the suitability determination
options.D: The Dealer Member charges a fee based on assets under management

questions.rationale: The definition of discretionary account requires that the Dealer Member has not solicited the discretionary authority and that the authority is accepted to accommodate a client who is frequently or temporarily unavailable to authorize trades, with no renewal and a term not exceeding 12 months. Option A describes the managed account definition (continuing investment decisions by a PM/APM or hired third party); option C is wrong because the discretionary account definition states the account remains subject to the suitability determination.
questions.source_quote: "(i) the Dealer Member has not solicited the discretionary authority, (ii) the discretionary authority is accepted to accommodate a client who is frequently or temporarily unavailable to authorize trades" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 348-350

questions.stem: Before the first option trade in a new retail advisory account at Kessler Securities, the designated Supervisor determines that the client's intended strategy of writing uncovered calls is not in keeping with her limited investment knowledge and modest financial circumstances, though covered writing would be. What should the designated Supervisor do?

options.A: Decline the derivatives account entirely, since one intended strategy is inappropriate
options.B: Approve the account without conditions and rely on trade-level suitability reviews to catch problems
options.C: Restrict the account from using the inappropriate strategy, note the trading restriction on the derivatives account approval, and communicate the restriction to the Registered Representative assigned to the account
options.D: Ask the client to sign a waiver accepting full responsibility for uncovered writing

questions.rationale: Subsection 3251(2) directs that where the risk characteristics of intended strategies are not appropriate for the client, the designated Supervisors should restrict the account from using inappropriate strategies, note any trading restrictions on the derivatives account approval, and communicate those restrictions to the RR, PM or APM assigned to the account. Option A overshoots — the rule contemplates targeted strategy restrictions rather than refusing an account whose other intended strategies are appropriate.
questions.source_quote: "the designated Supervisors should restrict the account from using inappropriate strategies and note on the derivatives account approval any trading restrictions imposed and communicate those restrictions" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5182-5184

questions.stem: A prospective retail client at Drummond Capital wants to transact in futures contracts through an advisory account. Which combination of steps must be completed before the initial derivatives transaction is executed in the account?

options.A: Obtain a completed derivatives account application and a completed, signed derivatives trading agreement, provide the most recent derivatives disclosure statement, and record the relevant designated Supervisors' approval in writing
options.B: Obtain only the derivatives account application; the trading agreement can follow within 25 business days
options.C: Provide the disclosure statement and obtain verbal Supervisor approval, with written approval to follow after the first trade settles
options.D: Obtain a letter of undertaking and a margin account agreement

questions.rationale: Subsection 3251(1) requires, before executing an initial derivatives transaction in any account type, a completed derivatives account application, a completed and signed derivatives trading agreement, provision of the most recent derivatives disclosure statement or similar document, and the relevant designated Supervisors' approval recorded in writing. Option D is wrong because the letter of undertaking is available only where the client is classified as an institutional client (3253(1)), and this client is retail.
questions.source_quote: "(i) obtain a completed derivatives account application... (ii) obtain a completed and signed derivatives trading agreement... (iv) record the relevant designated Supervisors' approval in writing" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5174-5178

questions.stem: Velma Okafor applies to add margin privileges to her advisory account at Trillview Securities. Beyond documentation, what does section 3246 require of the dealer in deciding whether to allow her to trade on margin?

options.A: Confirm that her annual income exceeds the firm's minimum margin-account threshold prescribed by CIRO
options.B: Ensure that she is aware of the risks and benefits associated with trading on margin
options.C: Obtain her spouse's consent to the margin facility
options.D: Obtain CIRO approval for the credit extension

questions.rationale: Section 3246(1) requires that, in deciding whether to allow a client to trade on margin, the Dealer Member ensure the client is aware of the risks and benefits associated with trading on margin. Option A invents a CIRO-prescribed income threshold — no such bright-line exists; the appropriateness of margin for the client is assessed under sections 3211 and 3402, not by a fixed income test.
questions.source_quote: "In deciding whether to allow a client to trade on margin, a Dealer Member must ensure that the client is aware of the risks and benefits associated with trading on margin." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5122-5123

questions.stem: During an OEO dealer's online account-opening process, an applicant telephones the help desk five times, repeatedly unable to complete basic steps of the online new-account application form despite guidance, and finally asks a representative to fill it in for him. Applying the account appropriateness assessment, the Supervisor should conclude:

options.A: The account may open since OEO dealers are exempt from all appropriateness obligations
options.B: The account may open if the applicant signs an extra risk acknowledgment
options.C: The clear and persistent difficulties with the online application indicate the person may be incapable of conducting online activities, so an OEO account may not be appropriate for this person
options.D: The account must open because OEO dealers cannot refuse applicants

questions.rationale: OEO appropriateness under syllabus parameter 4.21 expressly considers whether a person would be incapable of conducting online activities, citing clear and persistent difficulties in completing the OEO dealer's online new-account application form; under 3211(1)(i) the dealer must still determine that opening the account is appropriate for the person, putting the person's interest first. Option A overstates the OEO carve-out — 3211(2) exempts OEO accounts only from the scope-of-products limb (3211(1)(ii)), not from the basic appropriateness determination.
questions.source_quote: "If a person would be incapable of conducting online activities (e.g. clear and persistent difficulties in completing the OEO Investment Dealer's online new-account application form)" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 1046-1048; appropriateness limb: idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4573-4584

questions.stem: Before opening an order execution only account, what must the Dealer Member obtain from the client regarding the prescribed written disclosures that no recommendations or suitability determinations will be provided?

options.A: Nothing — posting the disclosures on the dealer's website is sufficient
options.B: A positive acknowledgement from the client, and each beneficial owner of the account, confirming receipt and understanding of the disclosures
options.C: A notarized declaration of investment experience
options.D: Confirmation that the client holds at least one advisory account elsewhere

questions.rationale: Clause 3241(3)(ii) requires the dealer to obtain a positive acknowledgement from the client, and each beneficial owner of the account, confirming that they have received and understood the disclosures described in clause 3241(3)(i) — which include statements that no recommendations will be provided, no suitability determination will be made, and no account/product appropriateness determination will be made. Option A fails because the rule requires a positive acknowledgement recorded in a prescribed form (signature/initials, electronic acknowledgement, or taped verbal acknowledgement), not passive availability.
questions.source_quote: "obtain a positive acknowledgement from the client, and each beneficial owner of the account, confirming that the client, and each beneficial owner, has received and understood the disclosures" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5014-5016

questions.stem: During the onboarding chat for a new account at Stonebriar Direct, an OEO Investment Dealer, an applicant writes: "I don't really follow markets — once the account opens, just tell me which funds I should buy." Applying the account appropriateness assessment for OEO accounts, what should the reviewing Supervisor conclude?

options.A: The investor is looking for advice, which an OEO dealer cannot provide; the dealer's written disclosure must confirm it will provide no recommendations and the client is solely responsible for all investment decisions, so an OEO account is not appropriate for this applicant
options.B: The account may open because the disclosure that no suitability determination will be made shifts all responsibility to the client
options.C: The account may open if the applicant is routed to the dealer's premium research tools
options.D: The dealer should open the account and have a Registered Representative call the client with fund recommendations after opening

questions.rationale: Syllabus parameter 4.21 directs the appropriateness assessment for OEO accounts to consider if the investor is looking for advice; clause 3241(3)(i)(a) requires the OEO dealer to disclose that it will not provide any recommendations and that the client is solely responsible for making all investment decisions — an applicant seeking to be told what to buy does not fit that account relationship. Option B is the trap: the disclosures describe what an OEO account is, but they do not convert an advice-seeking investor into an appropriate OEO client — the appropriateness determination under 3211(1)(i) still applies at opening.
questions.source_quote: "a statement confirming that the Dealer Member will not provide any recommendations to the client and that the client is solely responsible for making all investment decisions in the order execution only account" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5001-5003; "If investor is looking for advice" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1049

questions.scenario: Quill & Harbour Securities is an Investment Dealer with a busy suburban branch. On Monday morning, Renata Marchetti, a 41-year-old engineer, completes the firm's online application to open a retail advisory account. She tells the Registered Representative, Tomas Greer, that she plans to draw on a personal bank line of credit to fund some of her purchases. Quill & Harbour has never previously provided her with any disclosure documents. The branch's designated Supervisor, Priya Anand, is travelling Monday and Tuesday, so the branch uses the firm's alternative interim account-approval procedure and Renata's initial trade — a purchase of bank shares — is completed Monday afternoon. Tomas also begins assembling Renata's client account records, and the firm prepares its standardized Relationship Disclosure package for new advisory clients.
questions.stem: Quill & Harbour used its alternative procedure to approve Renata's account on an interim basis before her Monday trade. By when must Priya Anand, the designated Supervisor, provide final approval of the account?

options.A: Within 25 business days of account opening
options.B: Before any second trade is entered in the account
options.C: Within five business days of the interim approval
options.D: No later than one business day after the initial trade

questions.rationale: Subsection 3214(4) permits an alternative procedure to approve new accounts on an interim basis, provided the designated Supervisor provides final approval no later than one business day after the initial trade — the same outside deadline as the general rule in 3214(3). Option A confuses this with the 25-business-day window in 3213(2)(iii) for chasing outstanding supporting documents, which does not extend the Supervisor's approval deadline.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member may use an alternative procedure to approve new accounts on an interim basis, provided the designated Supervisor provides final approval no later than one business day after the initial trade." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4645-4647

questions.scenario: Quill & Harbour Securities is an Investment Dealer with a busy suburban branch. On Monday morning, Renata Marchetti, a 41-year-old engineer, completes the firm's online application to open a retail advisory account. She tells the Registered Representative, Tomas Greer, that she plans to draw on a personal bank line of credit to fund some of her purchases. Quill & Harbour has never previously provided her with any disclosure documents. The branch's designated Supervisor, Priya Anand, is travelling Monday and Tuesday, so the branch uses the firm's alternative interim account-approval procedure and Renata's initial trade — a purchase of bank shares — is completed Monday afternoon. Tomas also begins assembling Renata's client account records, and the firm prepares its standardized Relationship Disclosure package for new advisory clients.
questions.stem: Given Renata's stated plan to invest using a bank line of credit, what does section 3217 require Quill & Harbour to do?

options.A: Refuse to accept any orders funded with borrowed money in a non-margin account
options.B: Provide a margin account agreement instead, since borrowed funds are involved
options.C: Note her borrowing plan in the KYC record; no disclosure is required because the loan is from her bank, not the dealer
options.D: Provide her with a copy of the leverage risk disclosure statement and obtain her positive acknowledgement that she received it

questions.rationale: Subsection 3217(1) requires the dealer — when opening a new account for a retail client, before an initial leverage recommendation, or when first becoming aware of a retail client's intention to purchase securities using borrowed money — to provide a copy of the leverage risk disclosure statement and obtain the client's positive acknowledgement of receipt. Option C is the central misconception the rule targets: off-book borrowing from a bank is exactly the situation the leverage risk disclosure covers, since dealer-extended credit is instead governed by the margin account regime.
questions.source_quote: "when first becoming aware of a retail client's intention to purchase securities using borrowed money, a Dealer Member must: (i) provide each retail client with a copy of the leverage risk disclosure statement" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4849-4851

questions.scenario: Quill & Harbour Securities is an Investment Dealer with a busy suburban branch. On Monday morning, Renata Marchetti, a 41-year-old engineer, completes the firm's online application to open a retail advisory account. She tells the Registered Representative, Tomas Greer, that she plans to draw on a personal bank line of credit to fund some of her purchases. Quill & Harbour has never previously provided her with any disclosure documents. The branch's designated Supervisor, Priya Anand, is travelling Monday and Tuesday, so the branch uses the firm's alternative interim account-approval procedure and Renata's initial trade — a purchase of bank shares — is completed Monday afternoon. Tomas also begins assembling Renata's client account records, and the firm prepares its standardized Relationship Disclosure package for new advisory clients.
questions.stem: When assigning an account number to Renata's new account, which condition must Quill & Harbour satisfy under subsection 3214(1)?

options.A: The full and accurate name and address of the client must be known to the Dealer Member, with the complete account application received no later than the following business day
options.B: Priya Anand must have already provided final written approval of the account
options.C: Renata's identity verification documentation must have been independently audited
options.D: The Relationship Disclosure must have been signed and returned by Renata

questions.rationale: Subsection 3214(1) permits assigning an account number only if the full and accurate name and address of the client is known to the Dealer Member, with the complete account application received no later than the following business day. Option B confuses number assignment with account approval — final designated Supervisor approval is a separate step that may follow the initial trade by up to one business day under 3214(3)-(4).
questions.source_quote: "may only assign an account number to a new account if the full and accurate name and address of the client... is known... the complete account application must be received no later than the following business day" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4638-4640

questions.scenario: Quill & Harbour Securities is an Investment Dealer with a busy suburban branch. On Monday morning, Renata Marchetti, a 41-year-old engineer, completes the firm's online application to open a retail advisory account. She tells the Registered Representative, Tomas Greer, that she plans to draw on a personal bank line of credit to fund some of her purchases. Quill & Harbour has never previously provided her with any disclosure documents. The branch's designated Supervisor, Priya Anand, is travelling Monday and Tuesday, so the branch uses the firm's alternative interim account-approval procedure and Renata's initial trade — a purchase of bank shares — is completed Monday afternoon. Tomas also begins assembling Renata's client account records, and the firm prepares its standardized Relationship Disclosure package for new advisory clients.
questions.stem: Renata asks Tomas whether the firm will ever re-check that her account and holdings still fit her circumstances. Under the prescribed suitability-process content of the Relationship Disclosure, which statement must the document include?

options.A: A statement that the dealer's suitability obligation ends once the account is approved
options.B: A statement that suitability will be reassessed only if Renata requests a review in writing
options.C: A statement that the dealer will determine that any investment action it takes, recommends or decides on for her is suitable and puts her interest first, including on triggering events such as a change in the Registered Representative responsible for the account
options.D: A statement that suitability reviews are performed by CIRO rather than the dealer

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 3216(5)(ii)(d)(III) requires the Relationship Disclosure to state that the dealer will determine that any investment action it takes, recommends or decides on for the client is suitable and puts the client's interest first, including on listed triggering events such as securities received into or delivered out of the account, a change in the RR/PM/APM responsible for the account, and material KYC or security changes. Option B inverts the design — the triggers are dealer obligations that operate automatically, not client-initiated requests.
questions.source_quote: "a statement that the Dealer Member will determine that any investment action it takes, recommends or decides on, for the client is suitable for the client and puts the client's interest first, including when... there is a change in the Registered Representative" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4764-4770

questions.scenario: Aldergrove Private Counsel, an Investment Dealer, runs a discretionary and managed account platform. Marcus Liang is the designated Supervisor responsible for managed accounts; he also holds the Rule 2600 proficiencies to supervise discretionary accounts. Portfolio Manager Dana Whitfield manages forty managed accounts under the firm's centralized model portfolios. This month: (1) a new managed account for retiree Edith Okonkwo is ready to open; (2) Dana proposes that several of her managed accounts buy units of a closed-end fund whose manager is a company connected to Aldergrove; (3) long-time client Hugo Brandt, who sails offshore for months at a time, asks his Registered Representative to accept time-limited discretionary authority over his advisory account while he is unreachable; and (4) another client, Yusuf Demir, asks for an arrangement in which the firm makes all investment decisions for him indefinitely, year after year.
questions.stem: Before Edith Okonkwo's managed account can be operated, which approval and record-keeping steps does section 3277 require?

options.A: The Chief Compliance Officer must approve the account verbally, with the approval minuted at the next managed account committee meeting
options.B: Dana Whitfield, as the Portfolio Manager, must approve her own account in writing and file the record with CIRO
options.C: No individual approval is needed because the account follows a pre-approved model portfolio
options.D: Marcus Liang, the designated Supervisor, must approve the managed account in writing, and the Dealer Member must retain a record of that approval

questions.rationale: Clauses 3277(1)(iv) and (v) require the designated Supervisor to approve each managed account in writing and the Dealer Member to retain a record of the designated Supervisor's approval; the agreement must also be entered into before the account is opened. Option C is the model-portfolio misconception — centralized investment decisions do not remove the per-account written approval requirement; the rule says each managed account must be approved.
questions.source_quote: "(iv) the designated Supervisor must approve each managed account in writing, (v) the Dealer Member must retain a record of the designated Supervisor's approval" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5416-5417

questions.scenario: Aldergrove Private Counsel, an Investment Dealer, runs a discretionary and managed account platform. Marcus Liang is the designated Supervisor responsible for managed accounts; he also holds the Rule 2600 proficiencies to supervise discretionary accounts. Portfolio Manager Dana Whitfield manages forty managed accounts under the firm's centralized model portfolios. This month: (1) a new managed account for retiree Edith Okonkwo is ready to open; (2) Dana proposes that several of her managed accounts buy units of a closed-end fund whose manager is a company connected to Aldergrove; (3) long-time client Hugo Brandt, who sails offshore for months at a time, asks his Registered Representative to accept time-limited discretionary authority over his advisory account while he is unreachable; and (4) another client, Yusuf Demir, asks for an arrangement in which the firm makes all investment decisions for him indefinitely, year after year.
questions.stem: Reviewing Dana's proposal to buy units of the closed-end fund connected to Aldergrove for her managed accounts, Marcus should advise that the purchases may proceed only if:

options.A: Each affected client has given prior written consent before the managed account knowingly invests in a security of an issuer related or connected to a responsible person or to the Dealer Member
options.B: The purchases are disclosed in the clients' next quarterly statements
options.C: The fund units are bought at net asset value through an independent broker
options.D: The managed account committee ratifies the trades within 30 days

questions.rationale: Clause 3280(2)(i) prohibits a responsible person or the Dealer Member from knowingly allowing a managed account to invest in a security or derivative of a security of an issuer that is related or connected to a responsible person or to the Dealer Member without the prior written consent of the client — consent must precede the investment. Option B fails on timing and form: after-the-fact statement disclosure is not the prior written client consent the rule demands.
questions.source_quote: "must not, without the prior written consent of the client, knowingly allow a managed account to: (i) invest in a security or derivative of a security of an issuer that is related or connected to a responsible person or to the Dealer Member" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5455-5458

questions.scenario: Aldergrove Private Counsel, an Investment Dealer, runs a discretionary and managed account platform. Marcus Liang is the designated Supervisor responsible for managed accounts; he also holds the Rule 2600 proficiencies to supervise discretionary accounts. Portfolio Manager Dana Whitfield manages forty managed accounts under the firm's centralized model portfolios. This month: (1) a new managed account for retiree Edith Okonkwo is ready to open; (2) Dana proposes that several of her managed accounts buy units of a closed-end fund whose manager is a company connected to Aldergrove; (3) long-time client Hugo Brandt, who sails offshore for months at a time, asks his Registered Representative to accept time-limited discretionary authority over his advisory account while he is unreachable; and (4) another client, Yusuf Demir, asks for an arrangement in which the firm makes all investment decisions for him indefinitely, year after year.
questions.stem: Hugo Brandt signs a discretionary account agreement before departing on his voyage. Under clause 3273(1)(v), what must a designated Supervisor at Aldergrove do for the discretionary arrangement to be properly accepted?

options.A: Approve only the first discretionary trade; the account itself needs no separate approval
options.B: Approve the account as a discretionary account and approve the discretionary account agreement signed by the client
options.C: Forward the agreement to CIRO for registration before any discretionary trade
options.D: Obtain Hugo's verbal confirmation by satellite phone each time discretion is exercised

questions.rationale: Clause 3273(1)(v) requires the designated Supervisor to approve the account as a discretionary account and to approve the discretionary account agreement signed by the client; a record of that approval must be retained under 3273(1)(vi) in accordance with section 3803. Option D defeats the purpose of the arrangement — discretionary authority under the definition exists precisely to accommodate a client who is frequently or temporarily unavailable to authorize trades.
questions.source_quote: "the designated Supervisor must approve the account as a discretionary account and approve the discretionary account agreement signed by the client" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5361-5362

questions.scenario: Aldergrove Private Counsel, an Investment Dealer, runs a discretionary and managed account platform. Marcus Liang is the designated Supervisor responsible for managed accounts; he also holds the Rule 2600 proficiencies to supervise discretionary accounts. Portfolio Manager Dana Whitfield manages forty managed accounts under the firm's centralized model portfolios. This month: (1) a new managed account for retiree Edith Okonkwo is ready to open; (2) Dana proposes that several of her managed accounts buy units of a closed-end fund whose manager is a company connected to Aldergrove; (3) long-time client Hugo Brandt, who sails offshore for months at a time, asks his Registered Representative to accept time-limited discretionary authority over his advisory account while he is unreachable; and (4) another client, Yusuf Demir, asks for an arrangement in which the firm makes all investment decisions for him indefinitely, year after year.
questions.stem: Applying the account appropriateness assessment to Yusuf Demir's request that the firm make all of his investment decisions indefinitely, which account relationship is appropriate?

options.A: A discretionary account, renewed each year as long as Yusuf remains satisfied
options.B: An OEO account, since Yusuf does not want to place his own orders
options.C: An advisory account with a standing verbal instruction that the RR may trade without contacting him
options.D: A managed account, in which investment decisions are made on a continuing basis by a Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager, since a discretionary account agreement cannot exceed 12 months and is not renewable

questions.rationale: The managed account definition covers an account where investment decisions are made on a continuing basis by a PM or APM (or a hired third party) — the structure built for indefinite, ongoing discretion — whereas a discretionary account agreement must have a maximum term of no longer than 12 months and must not be renewable (3274(1)), and under the definition exists to accommodate temporary client unavailability. Option A fails because renewal of discretionary authority is prohibited; option C describes discretionary trading outside Part G, which section 3221 prohibits.
questions.source_quote: "“managed account” An account which is subject to a suitability determination where: (i) investment decisions are made on a continuing basis by a Portfolio Manager or an Associate Portfolio Manager or a third party hired by the Dealer Member" — idpc-rules.txt lines 528-531 (Rule 1201)

questions.stem: A new Supervisor at Lachine Securities asks the compliance department what the Canadian Securities Administrators (CSA) actually is. Which description is most accurate?

options.A: A federal Crown corporation that registers all Investment Dealers directly and supersedes provincial regulators
options.B: The umbrella organization through which provincial and territorial securities and derivatives regulators coordinate and harmonize regulation, including through instruments such as National Instruments
options.C: A self-regulatory organization that writes the IDPC Rules and UMIR for Investment Dealers
options.D: The federal agency that receives and analyzes suspicious transaction reports

questions.rationale: Securities regulation in Canada is provincial/territorial; the CSA is the coordinating umbrella of those regulators, and the syllabus tests its jurisdiction, mandate, and the forms of legislation it produces (NIs, MIs, National Policies, Staff Notices, Companion Policies). Option C describes CIRO, the SRO; option D describes FINTRAC; option A is wrong because there is no single federal securities regulator and the CSA does not supersede its member regulators.
questions.source_quote: "Understand the role and authority of the Canadian Securities Administrators (CSA) and provincial/territorial securities and derivatives regulators. Consider the: • Jurisdiction of the CSA • Mandate and objectives of the CSA" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 168

questions.stem: While updating branch procedures, a Supervisor encounters several CSA publications. Which of the following is among the forms of CSA legislation and policy instruments identified in the Supervisor Exam syllabus?

options.A: Guidance Notes under the IDPC Rules
options.B: Universal Market Integrity Rules
options.C: Royal Assent proclamations
options.D: Multilateral Instruments

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameter for 1.1 lists the forms of CSA legislation: National Instruments (NI), Multilateral Instruments (MI), National policies, Staff Notices and Companion Policies (CP). IDPC Guidance Notes (A) and UMIR (B) are CIRO instruments, tested under outcome 1.2, not CSA instruments; Royal Assent proclamations (C) belong to the legislative process for statutes, not CSA policy-making.
questions.source_quote: "- National Instruments (NI)" ... "- Multilateral Instruments (MI)" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 182, 186 (1.1 parameter list); contiguous anchor: "Multilateral Instruments (MI)" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 186

questions.stem: A newly designated Supervisor asks why CIRO, rather than a government body, can discipline her firm's Registered Representatives. Which concept best explains the source of CIRO's authority?

options.A: CIRO derives authority from recognition orders and delegated authorities granted by the securities regulators
options.B: CIRO is a division of the federal Department of Finance
options.C: CIRO's authority comes from its ownership of the exchanges on which dealers trade
options.D: CIRO has inherent constitutional authority over securities markets

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameter for 1.2 identifies the purpose of recognition orders and delegated authorities as the basis of CIRO's jurisdiction: provincial/territorial regulators recognize CIRO as a self-regulatory organization and delegate regulatory functions to it. CIRO is not a government department (B), does not own exchanges (C), and there is no inherent federal constitutional authority over securities (D) — securities regulation is provincial.
questions.source_quote: "Purpose of recognition orders and delegated authorities" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 210

questions.stem: Which pairing correctly matches a CIRO rule book or instrument with its role for an Investment Dealer's Supervisor?

options.A: National Instruments — CIRO's standards of conduct for Approved Persons
options.B: Companion Policies — CIRO's rules for sales practices at Investment Dealers
options.C: IDPC Rules — the rules governing Investment Dealer conduct, including standards of conduct, sales practices and external communications
options.D: Staff Notices — CIRO's market integrity rules for marketplace trading

questions.rationale: The 1.2 parameter lists the Investment Dealer and Partially Consolidated (IDPC) Rules and UMIR as CIRO's rule books and highlights the role of rules, guidance notes, forms and supporting schedules covering standards of conduct, sales practices and external communications. National Instruments, Companion Policies and Staff Notices (A, B, D) are CSA instruments under outcome 1.1, not CIRO rule books — the closest distractor, D, also misdescribes market integrity rules, which are UMIR.
questions.source_quote: "Investment Dealer and Partially Consolidated (IDPC) Rules • Universal Market Integrity Rules (UMIR) • Role and implications of rules, guidance notes, forms and supporting schedules" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 220

questions.stem: Verdun Capital Corp. wants to begin operating as an Investment Dealer, and its founder wants to act as a Registered Representative. Which statement best reflects how registration and approval work?

options.A: Only CIRO is involved; the provincial regulators have no role once a firm chooses the Investment Dealer category
options.B: Only the provincial regulator is involved; CIRO has no role in approving individuals
options.C: Both the CSA's provincial/territorial regulators and CIRO have roles in the firm's registration and the individual's approval process
options.D: Registration is optional if the firm trades only for institutional clients

questions.rationale: Outcome 1.3 requires understanding the requirement for Investment Dealer registration and individual approval and 'the role of CSA and CIRO in this process' — both layers participate: securities regulators register, and CIRO approves dealers as Members and approves individuals. Options A and B each erase one of the two required roles, and D is wrong because dealing in securities triggers registration requirements regardless of client type.
questions.source_quote: "Understand the requirement for Investment Dealer registration and individual approval requirements and the role of CSA and CIRO in this process." — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 175

questions.stem: A Supervisor reviewing order-routing disclosures sees client orders executed across several venue types. Which list contains only categories of marketplaces identified in the Supervisor Exam syllabus?

options.A: Exchanges, clearing agencies, and transfer agents
options.B: Alternative Trading Systems, custodians, and securities depositories
options.C: Exchanges, Alternative Trading Systems, Crypto-Asset Trading Platforms, and Foreign Organized Regulated Markets
options.D: Investment Dealers, introducing brokers, and carrying brokers

questions.rationale: Outcome 1.4 enumerates the marketplaces a Supervisor must understand: exchanges, Alternative Trading Systems (ATS), Crypto-Asset Trading Platforms (CTPs), and Foreign Organized Regulated Markets (FORM). Clearing agencies (A) belong to outcome 1.5 (CDS/CDCC), and custodians/depositories (B) and dealer categories (D) are not marketplaces — the closest distractor, A, mixes in clearing agencies, which settle trades rather than match orders.
questions.source_quote: "Understand the role and authority of exchanges and other marketplaces. Consider: • Exchanges" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 178; "Foreign Organized Regulated Markets (FORM)" — ~line 192

questions.stem: A junior Supervisor asks which clearing agency stands behind exchange-traded options and futures transactions in Canada, as opposed to clearing and depository services for securities generally. The correct distinction is:

options.A: The Bank of Canada clears derivatives; CDS clears securities
options.B: CDCC clears both securities and derivatives; CDS handles only government bonds
options.C: CDS is the clearing agency associated with derivatives; CDCC is the securities depository
options.D: CDCC is the Canadian Derivatives Clearing Corporation; CDS is the Canadian Depository for Securities Limited

questions.rationale: The 1.5 parameter names the two clearing agencies a Supervisor must understand: the Canadian Depository for Securities Limited (CDS) and the Canadian Derivatives Clearing Corporation (CDCC) — the names themselves carry the division of labour (securities depository/clearing vs. derivatives clearing). Option C reverses the two entities; A wrongly assigns clearing to the central bank; B misallocates both functions.
questions.source_quote: "Understand the role and authority of clearing agencies. Consider: • Canadian Depository for Securities Limited (CDS) • Canadian Derivatives Clearing Corporation (CDCC)" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 200

questions.stem: At a branch town hall, a Registered Representative claims that the Canadian Investor Protection Fund is financed by an annual levy on retail investors' accounts. How should the Supervisor correct this?

options.A: CIPF is funded through requirements imposed on Investment Dealers, not through charges levied on individual investors
options.B: CIPF is funded entirely by the federal government's consolidated revenue fund
options.C: CIPF is funded by fines collected from CIRO enforcement hearings
options.D: The RR is correct: investors pay a per-account CIPF premium

questions.rationale: The 1.6 parameter expressly includes 'Funding requirements by Investment Dealers' — CIPF's funding comes from the dealer side of the industry, which is why the syllabus frames it as a dealer obligation a Supervisor must understand. It is not taxpayer-funded (B), not financed by enforcement fines (C), and clients are not levied a CIPF premium (D).
questions.source_quote: "Funding requirements by Investment Dealers" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 208

questions.stem: A study group preparing for the Supervisor Exam sorts protection bodies by mandate. Which statement correctly isolates the Canadian Investor Protection Fund's purpose and objective relative to the other bodies named in Element 1?

options.A: CIPF investigates and prosecutes Approved Persons for standards-of-conduct breaches
options.B: CIPF analyzes suspicious transaction reports for money-laundering indicators
options.C: CIPF resolves client complaints through non-binding recommendations
options.D: CIPF exists to protect clients in connection with an Investment Dealer's bankruptcy or insolvency — distinct from CIRO's conduct enforcement, OBSI's dispute resolution, and FINTRAC's transaction analysis

questions.rationale: Parameter 1.6 frames CIPF around its purpose and objective and its role in an Investment Dealer's bankruptcy/insolvency, including pooling of client assets — protection against dealer failure, not conduct enforcement (A, a CIRO function under 1.2), not transaction analysis (B, FINTRAC under 1.7), and not complaint recommendations (C, the ombudsman under 1.14). Keeping the four mandates separate is the discrimination the exam tests across these outcomes.
questions.source_quote: "Role of CIPF in an Investment Dealer’s bankruptcy/insolvency, including pooling of client assets" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 216

questions.stem: A client of Outremont Securities relocates to Chicago and asks her Supervisor which American bodies parallel Canada's securities and derivatives oversight. According to the Supervisor Exam syllabus, the US regulatory authorities a Supervisor should be able to identify are:

options.A: The SEC, FINRA, CFTC and NFA
options.B: The Federal Reserve, FDIC, OCC and CFPB
options.C: The SEC, the Bank of England, IOSCO and the FSB
options.D: The IRS, FINRA, FBI and Treasury

questions.rationale: Parameter 1.7 names the US regulators a Supervisor must recognize: the Securities Exchange Commission (SEC), Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA), Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), and National Futures Association (NFA) — the securities/derivatives pairing of statutory regulator plus self-regulatory body. Option B lists US banking regulators, C mixes in non-US bodies, and D includes tax and law-enforcement agencies outside the securities/derivatives framework.
questions.source_quote: "US: Securities Exchange Commission (SEC), Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA), Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC), National Futures Association (NFA)" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 248

questions.stem: During a contingency-planning workshop, the CFO of Burgeo Sound Securities asks which federal statute specifically governs the bankruptcy of a securities firm. The correct answer is:

options.A: The Bank Act, consumer-protection provisions
options.B: The Proceeds of Crime (Money Laundering) and Terrorism Financing Act
options.C: The Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act, Part XII — Bankruptcy of a Securities Firm
options.D: The Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act

questions.rationale: Parameter 1.8 names the Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act, Part XII — Bankruptcy of a Securities Firm — as the federal statute governing a securities firm's bankruptcy, alongside the Bank Act's separate purposes (stability and integrity, consumer protection, competition). The Bank Act (A) governs banks, not securities-firm insolvency; PCMLTFA (B) is the anti-money laundering statute under 1.10; PIPEDA (D) is privacy legislation under 1.11.
questions.source_quote: "• Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act, Part XII – Bankruptcy of a Securities Firm" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 257

questions.stem: A finder circulating an offering document for a private venture knowingly overstates the company's signed contracts to induce investors to subscribe for its securities. Which Criminal Code categories from the syllabus list most directly capture (i) the dishonest inducement and (ii) the misleading offering document?

options.A: (i) Misappropriation of money under direction; (ii) uttering a forged document
options.B: (i) Theft; (ii) organized crime offences
options.C: (i) Illegal insider trading; (ii) forgery
options.D: (i) False statement or false pretenses (and obtaining execution of security by fraud); (ii) false prospectus

questions.rationale: Among the 1.9 parameters, 'false statement or false pretenses' and 'obtaining execution of security by fraud' capture dishonestly inducing subscription for securities, and 'false prospectus' captures the misleading offering document itself. Option A's misappropriation involves diverting money already entrusted under direction, not inducement; insider trading (C) involves trading on undisclosed material information, which is absent here; forgery and uttering (A, C) require a fabricated document purporting to be made by another, not merely a misleading one.
questions.source_quote: "False statement or false pretenses • Obtaining execution of security by fraud • Forgery • Uttering a forged document • False prospectus" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 265

questions.stem: Wexford Securities is rebuilding its anti-money laundering framework to align with the PCMLTFA topics tested on the Supervisor Exam. Which component belongs in that framework?

options.A: A guarantee fund that compensates clients of insolvent dealers
options.B: An enterprise risk assessment with indicators, supported by employee training
options.C: Pre-publication approval of research reports by a designated Supervisor
options.D: Quarterly best-execution disclosure on the dealer's website

questions.rationale: The 1.10 parameters enumerate the PCMLTFA topics: stages of money laundering, compliance program requirements, anti-money laundering policies and procedures, client information and due diligence requirements, enterprise risk assessment and indicators, employee training, and business relationship record-keeping. Option B combines two of those enumerated elements. Option A is CIPF (1.6); C is a communications control (Element 8); D is a best-execution disclosure obligation, not an AML element.
questions.source_quote: "Enterprise risk assessment and indicators" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 299; "Employee training" — ~line 303

questions.stem: During an AML program review at Gatineau Capital, the Chief AML Officer lists candidate workstreams. Which workstream is NOT among the PCMLTFA topics enumerated in the Supervisor Exam syllabus?

options.A: Client information and due diligence requirements
options.B: Business relationship record-keeping
options.C: Anti-money laundering policies and procedures
options.D: Setting client cumulative loss limits for leveraged derivatives accounts

questions.rationale: Outcome 1.10's enumerated PCMLTFA topics include compliance program requirements, AML policies and procedures, client information and due diligence requirements, enterprise risk assessment and indicators, employee training, and business relationship record-keeping. Cumulative loss limits (D) are a CIRO derivatives-account control under Element 4, not a PCMLTFA element — it is the closest distractor because it also involves client risk, but its source and purpose are entirely different.
questions.source_quote: "Client information and due diligence requirements" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 295; "Business relationship record-keeping" — ~line 307

questions.stem: A marketing manager at Brockville Investments wants to mass-email a purchased contact list about a new fund offering. The Supervisor flags Canadian Anti-Spam Legislation (CASL). According to the syllabus, the purposes of CASL include:

options.A: Reducing spam, enhancing privacy, promoting electronic commerce, deterring malicious activities, and encouraging compliance and accountability
options.B: Guaranteeing delivery of commercial electronic messages to consenting recipients
options.C: Setting record-retention periods for client complaint files
options.D: Regulating the disclosure of conflicts of interest in research reports

questions.rationale: The 1.11 parameter lists CASL's purposes verbatim: reducing spam, enhancing privacy, promoting electronic commerce, deterring malicious activities, and encouraging compliance and accountability. CASL restricts unsolicited commercial electronic messages — it does not guarantee delivery (B). Complaint record retention (C) is IDPC Rule 3700, and research conflict disclosure (D) is IDPC Rule 3600.
questions.source_quote: "- Reducing spam" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 317; "- Enhancing privacy" — ~line 321; "- Promoting electronic commerce" — ~line 325; "- Deterring malicious activities - Encouraging compliance and accountability" — ~line 329

questions.stem: Two requirements apply to the same activity at Wetaskiwin Securities: a securities-law provision sets one standard, and a Corporation requirement sets a stricter one. Under IDPC section 1406, the dealer must:

options.A: Comply with whichever requirement was enacted most recently
options.B: Comply with the most stringent of the Corporation requirements, securities laws or applicable laws
options.C: Comply with the less stringent requirement to reduce client friction
options.D: Ask CIRO to suspend the stricter requirement pending harmonization

questions.rationale: Subsection 1406(2) provides that where there is an inconsistency between Corporation requirements, securities laws and applicable laws, compliance with the most stringent of them is required — the conflict-of-laws principle underpinning the dealer's legislative obligations to clients and others under outcome 1.12. Recency (A) is not the test, choosing the laxer standard (C) is the inverse of the rule, and there is no suspension-pending-harmonization mechanism (D); exemptive relief is a separate, discretionary process.
questions.source_quote: "compliance with the most stringent of the Corporation requirements, securities laws or applicable laws is required" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 961

questions.stem: Hearst Junction Securities drafts the required contents of its complaint acknowledgement letter. Which item belongs in the letter under IDPC subsection 3725(2)?

options.A: The dealer's preliminary opinion that the complaint lacks merit
options.B: The 90 days time line to provide a substantive response to complainants
options.C: A binding release for the client to sign before the investigation begins
options.D: The personal home contact details of the Approved Person complained about

questions.rationale: Subsection 3725(2) prescribes the acknowledgement letter's contents: the name, job title and contact information of the individual handling the complaint; a statement inviting status inquiries and additional information; an explanation of the internal process including the designated complaints officer's role; reference to the CIRO-approved brochure and its statutes of limitations; the 90 days time line for a substantive response; and notice that more information may be requested. Pre-judging merit (A) contradicts the requirement that complaints not be dismissed without proper consideration; demanding a release upfront (C) collides with section 3711; and the subject AP's home details (D) are not a prescribed item.
questions.source_quote: "the 90 days time line to provide a substantive response to complainants" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6429

questions.stem: On Tuesday, a Registered Representative at Penticton Wealth receives a written complaint from her own client alleging misrepresentation. She intends to mention it to her Supervisor at the month-end branch meeting in three weeks. Under IDPC Rule 3700, the RR must report being the subject of a written client complaint to the dealer:

options.A: Within 30 calendar days
options.B: Only if she believes the complaint has merit
options.C: Within two business days
options.D: Within five business days

questions.rationale: Subsection 3702(1) requires an Approved Person to report to the Dealer Member within two business days if the Approved Person is the subject of a written client complaint; waiting for the monthly meeting breaches this. Option D confuses the AP's two-business-day reporting clock with the dealer's five-business-day acknowledgement letter to the client under 3725(1). Merit assessment (B) is the dealer's complaint-handling function, not a precondition to the AP's report.
questions.source_quote: "An Approved Person must report to the Dealer Member any of the following matters within two business days" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6149; "(iii) if the Approved Person is the subject of a written client complaint" — ~line 6154

questions.stem: A retail client of Chicoutimi Capital verbally tells a branch receptionist she is unhappy that her Registered Representative 'put her in funds she never agreed to.' A preliminary review of the account shows trades with no matching client instructions. Under Part E of IDPC Rule 3700, this verbal expression of dissatisfaction:

options.A: Is treated as a complaint subject to Part E, because it alleges misconduct and the preliminary investigation indicates the allegation may have merit
options.B: Cannot be a complaint because it was not in writing
options.C: Is a service complaint exempt from any handling requirements
options.D: Must first be converted to a civil action before the dealer can respond

questions.rationale: Section 3721(1) applies Part E both to a recorded expression of dissatisfaction alleging misconduct and to a verbal expression of dissatisfaction alleging misconduct 'where a preliminary investigation indicates that the allegation may have merit' — unauthorized trading is enumerated misconduct under 3721(2). Option B states the trap the rule expressly closes. The allegation is securities misconduct, not a service issue (C), and 3721(3) actually excludes matters that are the subject of a civil action or arbitration from the complaint definition — the opposite of D.
questions.source_quote: "a verbal expression of dissatisfaction with the Dealer Member or employee alleging misconduct where a preliminary investigation indicates that the allegation may have merit" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6356

questions.stem: A retail client rejects Trois-Rivières Securities' final complaint response and weighs her options. Which statement correctly contrasts the dealer-side effect of the approved arbitration program with the approved ombudsman service?

options.A: Both arbitration awards and ombudsman recommendations bind the dealer equally
options.B: Neither process can be initiated by the client without the dealer's consent
options.C: If the client requests arbitration the dealer must submit to binding arbitration, whereas the ombudsman's recommendations are non-binding on each participant
options.D: Arbitration is non-binding while ombudsman recommendations bind the dealer

questions.rationale: Under 9502(4), if a client requests arbitration the Dealer Member involved must submit to binding arbitration in any dispute with the client; under 9503(6), the ombudsman's recommendations are non-binding on each participant in the service. Option D reverses the two regimes — the closest trap. Option B is wrong because both processes are triggered on the client's request (9502(4), 9503(3)), and A wrongly equates a binding award with a non-binding recommendation.
questions.source_quote: "If a client requests arbitration, the Dealer Member involved must submit to binding arbitration in any dispute between the Dealer Member and the client." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 20211; "The ombudsman's recommendations are non-binding on each participant in the service." — ~line 20224

questions.stem: A retail client of Stephenville Securities serves the dealer with a statement of claim over alleged unsuitable trades and simultaneously emails the branch a letter restating the same allegations. For the purposes of the retail complaint-handling requirements in Part E of IDPC Rule 3700, how is the matter treated?

options.A: The letter must still receive the full Part E complaint-handling treatment in parallel with the litigation
options.B: Any matter which is the subject of a civil action or arbitration is not considered to be a complaint for the purpose of section 3721, so Part E complaint handling does not apply to it — although the dealer's separate reporting obligations to CIRO are engaged
options.C: The litigation is automatically stayed until the 90-day complaint response is delivered
options.D: The dealer must choose between defending the lawsuit and responding to the complaint

questions.rationale: Subsection 3721(3) provides that any matter which is the subject of a civil action or arbitration is not considered to be a complaint for the purpose of section 3721 — once the client litigates, the matter exits the Part E complaint-handling track, while reporting to the Corporation (civil claims involving securities matters are reportable under 3703(2)(iv)(e)) continues. Option A double-tracks what the rule expressly excludes; C invents a stay; D presents a false either/or — the dealer defends the claim and meets its reporting duties.
questions.source_quote: "Any matter which is the subject of a civil action or arbitration is not considered to be a complaint for the purpose of section 3721." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6365

questions.stem: A settlements clerk at Flin Flon Capital suspects that an off-book scheme involving several employees is defrauding investors who are not clients of the firm. She is unsure whether to use the dealer's client-complaint channel or CIRO's whistleblower service. Which framing best distinguishes the two channels per the syllabus?

options.A: The whistleblower service replaces the complaint process for all matters at the dealer
options.B: The whistleblower service is reserved for matters already proven in court
options.C: The client-complaint regime handles client account matters, while the whistleblower service addresses non-client account matters such as misconduct or illegal activity within an organization and potential securities frauds
options.D: The whistleblower service is only available to clients, not employees

questions.rationale: Outcome 1.15 frames CIRO's whistleblower service as addressing non-client account matters — misconduct or illegal activity within an organization, potential systemic wrongdoing, and potential securities frauds — which complements rather than replaces (A) the client-complaint machinery of Rule 3700. The service deals in potential wrongdoing, not adjudicated findings (B), and it exists precisely for insiders like employees who observe internal misconduct, not only clients (D).
questions.source_quote: "Understand situations that may require CIRO whistleblower service for non-client account matters, including:" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 314

questions.stem: A hearing panel finds that an Approved Person privately settled with a client without the dealer's written consent and concealed the related complaint, contravening Corporation requirements. Which sanction is within the panel's powers under IDPC section 8210?

options.A: Garnishment of the Approved Person's future wages at any employer in any industry
options.B: A maximum fine of $10,000 fixed by statute
options.C: A fine not exceeding the greater of $5,000,000 for each contravention and three times the profit made or loss avoided
options.D: A custodial sentence of up to two years

questions.rationale: Under 8210(1)(iii), a hearing panel may fine a Regulated Person other than a Dealer Member up to the greater of $5,000,000 for each contravention and an amount equal to three times the profit made or loss avoided as a result of the contravention; other available sanctions include suspension, revocation, permanent bars to approval and to employment by a Regulated Person. CIRO panels cannot imprison (D) or garnish wages economy-wide (A), and the $10,000 cap (B) is invented — the realistic exposure is far larger, which is the point of testing the penalty scale.
questions.source_quote: "an amount equal to three times the profit made or loss avoided by the person, directly or indirectly, as a result of the contravention" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 18295

questions.stem: A senior Registered Representative at Castlegar Securities runs a strategy that complies with every written rule, but he routinely allocates the most attractive new-issue fills to his highest-revenue clients and tells the branch, 'If it's not prohibited in writing, it's allowed.' Analyzing this under IDPC Rule 1400, the Supervisor should conclude:

options.A: The conduct is acceptable because standards of conduct only restate the written prohibitions elsewhere in the rules
options.B: The standards of conduct require acting openly and fairly and in accordance with just and equitable principles of trade, so conduct can contravene Rule 1400 even where no specific written prohibition is breached
options.C: Only the dealer, never the individual, can contravene the standards of conduct
options.D: Standards of conduct apply only to dealings with regulators, not with clients

questions.rationale: Subsection 1402(1) requires a Regulated Person, in the transaction of business, to observe high standards of ethics and conduct and to act openly and fairly and in accordance with just and equitable principles of trade — a principles-based obligation that reaches unfair conduct not captured by any specific prohibition, which is the ethics-compliance link tested in outcome 1.17. Option A is the precise misconception the principle exists to defeat. The standard applies to Regulated Persons including individuals (C) and governs the transaction of business generally, not only regulator dealings (D).
questions.source_quote: "in the transaction of business must observe high standards of ethics and conduct and must act openly and fairly and in accordance with just and equitable principles of trade" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 896

questions.stem: Four incidents land on a Supervisor's desk at Glace Bay Securities. Under subsection 1402(2), which incident is the clearest example of business conduct that 'may be conduct that contravenes' the standards of conduct because it displays an unreasonable departure from expected standards, even absent dishonesty?

options.A: An RR who, over months, repeatedly mails trade confirmations to the wrong clients despite corrective coaching, exposing confidential account details
options.B: An RR who declines to recommend a product she has not researched
options.C: An RR who reports his own potential rule contravention to the dealer within two business days
options.D: An RR who documents every client instruction before entering orders

questions.rationale: Subsection 1402(2) provides that business conduct that is negligent, fails to comply with an obligation, displays an unreasonable departure from standards expected of a Regulated Person, or is likely to diminish investor confidence may contravene the 1402(1) standards. Persistent careless disclosure of confidential client information after coaching is negligence and an unreasonable departure from expected standards — no dishonesty is needed. Options B, C and D describe diligent or required conduct (KYP restraint, 3702 self-reporting, documentation), which supports rather than contravenes the standards.
questions.source_quote: "displays an unreasonable departure from standards that are expected to be observed by a Regulated Person" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 904

questions.stem: The syllabus lists behaviours that define the CIRO standards of conduct. A branch debate at Carmacks Securities produces four candidate summaries. Which one accurately reflects the syllabus's description of those standards?

options.A: Maximizing shareholder returns, minimizing disclosure, and deferring to senior producers
options.B: Compliance with written rules only, with ethics left to personal preference
options.C: Strict liability for every client loss regardless of conduct
options.D: High standards of ethics and conduct; acting openly and fairly; not acting in a way that would be considered unbecoming or detrimental to public interest; and acting in accordance with the just and equitable principles of trade

questions.rationale: Parameter 1.17 describes the CIRO standards of conduct as: high standards of ethics and conduct; acting openly and fairly; not acting in a way that would be considered unbecoming or detrimental to public interest; acting in accordance with the just and equitable principles of trade; together with conduct that may contravene the standards. Option B is the written-rules-only fallacy the standards exist to close; C confuses conduct standards with loss insurance; A describes commercial pressures, not regulatory standards.
questions.source_quote: "• CIRO standards of conduct - High standards of ethics and conduct - Acting openly and fairly" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 362; "- Acting in accordance with the just and equitable principles of trade - Conduct that may contravene the standards of conduct" — ~line 373

questions.stem: A Supervisor at Souris Lighthouse Capital reviews a proposed cross between two unrelated client accounts that technically satisfies every execution rule but was timed and priced to quietly favour the larger, fee-heavier client at the smaller client's expense. Which standards-of-conduct analysis is most precise?

options.A: Since execution rules were satisfied, the standards of conduct are satisfied by definition
options.B: The arrangement offends the obligation to act openly and fairly and in accordance with just and equitable principles of trade: deliberately tilting terms between clients is unfair dealing even when each mechanical rule is met
options.C: The standards of conduct apply only to dealings with the public, not between two existing clients
options.D: The conduct is acceptable if the disadvantaged client never detects the difference

questions.rationale: Subsection 1402(1)(i) requires Regulated Persons in the transaction of business to observe high standards of ethics and conduct and act openly and fairly and in accordance with just and equitable principles of trade — a fairness standard that operates on top of mechanical execution rules, so technical compliance (A) does not immunize a deliberately tilted cross. The standards govern the transaction of business generally, including between clients (C), and non-detection (D) is irrelevant; concealment aggravates rather than cures unfair dealing.
questions.source_quote: "act openly and fairly and in accordance with just and equitable principles of trade" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 897

questions.stem: The board of Meridian Bluff Securities approves a bonus plan that pays branch managers a share of revenue generated by the Registered Representatives they supervise, with no offsetting metric. A director asks the Chief Compliance Officer to analyze the plan against the syllabus's corporate governance ethics framework. The strongest analysis is:

options.A: The plan creates a compensation conflict — supervisors are rewarded for the revenue of those they police — so governance requires dealing with that conflict through transparency, oversight and accountability rather than leaving it unmanaged
options.B: The plan is sound because revenue alignment always improves supervision quality
options.C: Compensation design is outside the scope of governance ethics
options.D: The plan is acceptable as long as it is kept confidential from CIRO

questions.rationale: Parameter 1.18 frames governance ethics around transparency, dealing with compensation conflicts, oversight and accountability, and training and awareness. A supervisor paid on supervisees' revenue has a direct incentive to under-supervise — the textbook compensation conflict governance must surface and resolve. Option B mistakes alignment of revenue interests for alignment of compliance interests; C is contradicted by the parameter's explicit inclusion of compensation conflicts; D inverts transparency, the first listed governance value.
questions.source_quote: "• Transparency" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 384; "• Dealing with compensation conflicts" — ~line 388; "• Oversight and accountability" — ~line 392; "• Training and awareness" — ~line 396

questions.stem: After a misconduct incident, the executives of Carleton Bay Capital quietly fix the underlying control gap but tell no one, provide no staff training on the lesson learned, and assign no executive ownership for monitoring recurrence. Analyzed against the syllabus's pillars of ethics and integrity in corporate governance, which pillars has the firm failed to apply?

options.A: Only training and awareness — secrecy and unassigned ownership are governance-neutral
options.B: Transparency, oversight and accountability, and training and awareness
options.C: None — fixing the control gap discharges all governance duties
options.D: Only transparency — accountability applies solely to compensation matters

questions.rationale: Parameter 1.18's pillars are transparency, dealing with compensation conflicts, oversight and accountability, and training and awareness. Concealing the incident fails transparency; assigning no owner for monitoring fails oversight and accountability; skipping the lesson-learned training fails training and awareness. Option C treats remediation as a substitute for governance, and options A and D each artificially narrow one pillar — accountability is a general governance pillar, not limited to compensation (D).
questions.source_quote: "• Oversight and accountability" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 392; "• Training and awareness" — ~line 396; "• Transparency" — ~line 384

questions.stem: Following a widely reported unethical sales episode at a competing dealer, a Supervisor briefs her branch on why ethics failures are costly even when no fine is levied. Which set of consequences matches the syllabus's list of risks of unethical behaviour?

options.A: Suspension of all marketplaces on which the dealer trades
options.B: Mandatory delisting of the dealer's clients' securities
options.C: Automatic CIPF intervention and pooling of client assets
options.D: Legal consequences, reputational damage, regulatory scrutiny, employee morale and turnover, and client confidence and turnover

questions.rationale: Parameter 1.19 enumerates the consequences and risks of unethical behaviour: legal consequences, reputational damage, regulatory scrutiny, employee morale and turnover, and client confidence and turnover. Options A and B describe marketplace events with no connection to a single dealer's ethics failure, and C confuses ethics consequences with CIPF's insolvency role under 1.6 — CIPF responds to dealer insolvency, not to misconduct as such.
questions.source_quote: "Understand the consequences and risks of unethical behaviour. Consider: • Legal consequences • Reputational damage • Regulatory scrutiny" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 400; "• Employee morale and turnover" — ~line 410; "• Client confidence and turnover" — ~line 414

questions.stem: A Registered Representative at Lunenburg Capital realizes that her brother-in-law has become the chief financial officer of an issuer she actively recommends to clients — a material conflict she has just identified. What does IDPC section 3110 require her to do first?

options.A: Resign from covering the issuer without telling anyone why
options.B: Disclose the relationship directly to affected clients and continue recommending the issuer
options.C: Nothing, provided she subjectively believes she can stay objective
options.D: Promptly report the conflict of interest to the Dealer Member

questions.rationale: Subsection 3110(3) requires that if an Approved Person identifies a material conflict of interest under 3110(2), the Approved Person must promptly report that conflict of interest to the Dealer Member. Going straight to client disclosure (B) skips the dealer's role — under 3111(3) she must not engage in trading or advising activity connected to the conflict until it is addressed in the client's best interest and the dealer consents. Subjective confidence (C) does not displace the reporting duty, and quiet withdrawal (A) leaves the dealer unable to supervise how the conflict is addressed.
questions.source_quote: "the Approved Person must promptly report that conflict of interest to the Dealer Member" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3922

questions.stem: Dieppe Securities identifies a material conflict between an Approved Person and his clients arising from a referral arrangement. The firm sends every affected client a prominent, plain-language written disclosure describing the conflict, then allows the activity to continue unchanged. Has the dealer met its obligations under IDPC Rule 3100?

options.A: Yes — written disclosure fully discharges the dealer's conflict obligations
options.B: Yes, provided the disclosure was delivered before account opening
options.C: No — disclosure alone does not satisfy the obligation to address material conflicts in the best interest of the client; the conflict must also be addressed, or avoided if it cannot be
options.D: No — conflicts may never be managed; they must always be avoided

questions.rationale: Subsection 3113(3) states expressly that a Dealer Member and an Approved Person do not satisfy 3111(1) or 3112(1) solely by providing disclosure to the client: the conflict must be addressed in the best interest of the client, and avoided under 3112(2) if it cannot be so addressed. Options A and B treat disclosure as the complete remedy — the precise misreading 3113(3) forecloses (timing under 3113(2)(iii) governs when disclosure happens, not whether it suffices). Option D overshoots: the framework is avoid/disclose/manage/resolve, with avoidance required only where the conflict cannot otherwise be addressed in the client's best interest.
questions.source_quote: "a Dealer Member and an Approved Person do not satisfy subsections 3111(1) or 3112(1) solely by providing disclosure to the client" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3963

questions.stem: Gander Capital appoints a branch manager who maintains his own substantial book of retail clients. When designing the supervision structure around this producing Supervisor, what does the syllabus's conflicts framework require the dealer to apply to him?

options.A: Nothing beyond the standard structure, because Supervisors are presumed objective about their own files
options.B: An exemption from account reviews for his personal book, since he is the branch's senior reviewer
options.C: The applicable standards of care, ethics and independent supervision requirements for producing Supervisors
options.D: A requirement to transfer his clients to CIRO for direct oversight

questions.rationale: Parameter 1.20 expressly includes 'applicable standards of care, ethics and independent supervision requirements for producing Supervisors' — a producing Supervisor's own client activity cannot escape supervision merely because he is the reviewer, since self-review is an unmanaged managerial/compensatory conflict. Option B grants the precise exemption the parameter forecloses; A relies on a presumption of objectivity the framework rejects; D invents a CIRO oversight transfer that does not exist — the dealer retains the supervision obligation and must structure it independently.
questions.source_quote: "Applicable standards of care, ethics and independent supervision requirements for producing Supervisors" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 427

questions.stem: An Approved Person at Tofino Strait Securities identifies a material conflict between herself and a client but believes she can keep advising while the firm 'sorts out the paperwork.' Under IDPC section 3111, before she engages in any trading or advising activity connected to that conflict, two things must be true:

options.A: She has notified the client verbally, and 30 days have elapsed
options.B: The client has signed an indemnity, and her Supervisor is copied on emails
options.C: CIRO has registered the conflict, and the client has not objected
options.D: The conflict has been addressed in the best interest of the client, and the Dealer Member has given her its consent to proceed with the activity

questions.rationale: Subsection 3111(3) prohibits an Approved Person from engaging in any trading or advising activity in connection with a material conflict identified under 3110(2) unless the conflict has been addressed in the best interest of the client and the Dealer Member has given the Approved Person its consent to proceed. Verbal notice plus the passage of time (A) satisfies neither condition; an indemnity and cc'd emails (B) substitute paperwork for the substantive best-interest standard; and CIRO does not maintain a conflict registry or operate on client non-objection (C).
questions.source_quote: "(i) the conflict has been addressed in the best interest of the client, and (ii) the Dealer Member has given the Approved Person its consent to proceed with the activity." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3936
