options.B: Guarantee that she will bear no responsibility for delegated tasks
options.C: Assign her a CIRO liaison officer
options.D: Inform her of the specific functions that cannot be delegated

questions.rationale: Subsection 3907(6) requires the Dealer Member to inform the Supervisor of specific functions that cannot be delegated — mirroring the syllabus parameter on prohibitions on delegation. Option A invents a budget obligation; C invents a liaison role; B contradicts subsection 3907(1), under which the Supervisor may delegate tasks and procedures but not the responsibility for their performance.
questions.source_quote: "The Dealer Member must inform the Supervisor of specific functions that cannot be delegated." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7963

questions.stem: Supervisor Devon Park delegated monthly account reviews two years ago and has not since looked at how the arrangement is functioning. According to the Element 2 syllabus parameters on documented controls for delegation, how often, at a minimum, must the delegation process itself be reviewed?

options.A: At minimum annually, with revisions to policies and procedures as necessary
options.B: Every five years, aligned with CIRO's examination cycle
options.C: Only when the delegate resigns or changes roles
options.D: Never, once the initial delegation has been documented

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameter for 2.10 calls for regular (at minimum annual) reviews of the delegation process, with revisions to policies and procedures as necessary. Park's two-year gap breaches that minimum. Option B invents a five-year cycle tied to examinations; C makes the review contingent on personnel events rather than a standing annual minimum; D treats documentation as a one-time event, contradicting the requirement for regular reviews.
questions.source_quote: "Regular (at minimum annual) reviews of the delegation process, with revisions to policies and procedures as necessary" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 523

questions.stem: Supervisor Maya Castillo examines a sample of account-opening reviews completed by her delegate. She checks whether the delegate is following the firm's policies and procedures. According to the syllabus parameter on examining a delegate's reviews, what second test should her examination also apply?

options.A: Whether the delegate completed the reviews faster than Castillo would have
options.B: Whether the delegate's reviews generated revenue for the branch
options.C: Whether the items the delegate recommended for approval are items that would have been approved by the delegator
options.D: Whether the delegate escalated every single item regardless of significance

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameter requires examination of the delegate's reviews to ensure they are following applicable policies and procedures AND to ensure that they are recommending items for approval that would have been approved by the delegator — a substantive quality test, not just process compliance. Option A substitutes speed for quality; B introduces an irrelevant commercial measure; D distorts the escalation control, which targets significant issues, into blanket escalation.
questions.source_quote: "recommending items for approval that would have been approved by the delegator" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 634

questions.stem: What position has CIRO (in the guidance originally issued by IIROC) taken on the use of automation in Dealer Member supervision?

options.A: The supervisory requirements are generally technology neutral, and CIRO supports Dealers using automation to assist them with supervisory tasks
options.B: Automation of any supervisory task is prohibited until specifically authorized by rule amendment
options.C: Automation may be used only by dealers above a prescribed revenue threshold
options.D: Automation entirely replaces the Supervisor's responsibilities once implemented

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 states that the supervisory requirements are generally technology neutral and that the regulator supports Dealers using automation to assist them with supervisory tasks. Option B inverts the technology-neutral position; C invents a size threshold; D misstates the role of automation — it assists with supervisory tasks, and per the syllabus the Supervisor retains a role and responsibilities, with some tasks that cannot be automated.
questions.source_quote: "Generally, our supervisory requirements are technology neutral and we support Dealers using automation to assist them with supervisory tasks." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 50

questions.stem: Why does an automated exception-flagging system at a retail dealer still leave a meaningful role for the Supervisor, according to the Account Supervision Guidance's framework for review selection?

options.A: Because the dealer must enquire into every flagged trade without judgment
options.B: Because automated alerts must all be deleted within 24 hours
options.C: Because a Dealer is not required to investigate every trade that meets review criteria, but should exercise reasonable judgment in selecting items for further investigation — a judgment function the automated flagging itself does not perform
options.D: Because the guidance bans automated alerting in retail supervision

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 states that a Dealer is not required to enquire into or investigate every trade that meets the criteria in Rule 3900 or the Guidance, but should exercise reasonable judgment in selecting items for further investigation; the automation surfaces candidates, while the judgment over which items warrant inquiry — and the resulting supervisory action — remains a human supervisory responsibility. Option A removes the judgment element and mandates universal inquiry; B invents a deletion timeline; D contradicts the guidance, which supports dealers using automation and technology to select activity for review.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer is not required to enquire into or investigate every trade that meets the criteria in Rule 3900 or this Guidance, but should exercise reasonable judgment in selecting items for further investigation." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 38

questions.stem: An automated new-account workflow at Trillium Direct receives an application from a numbered holding corporation. According to the syllabus parameters on automation controls, why should the dealer's process route this application out of the automated stream?

options.A: Because corporations may not open accounts at Investment Dealers
options.B: Because the applicant not being an individual is a factor that may trigger a manual review
options.C: Because corporate accounts must always be approved by CIRO staff
options.D: Because automation may only be used for accounts under $25,000

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameter for 2.12 lists 'Applicant not being an individual' among the factors that may trigger a manual review of an automated application. Option A is false — corporations can open accounts, they simply warrant manual handling; C invents CIRO-level account approval; D invents a dollar threshold restriction on automation that appears nowhere in the parameters.
questions.source_quote: "Applicant not being an individual" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 571

questions.stem: Riva Banerjee applies online at Quartz Direct to trade multi-leg option spreads, but her application discloses no prior derivatives knowledge and no trading experience. Under the syllabus parameters for automation controls, how should the dealer's automated approval process treat this application?

options.A: Approve it automatically because OEO dealers make no suitability determination
options.B: Decline it automatically with no human involvement permitted
options.C: Treat it as a factor triggering a manual review, because the applicant is applying to use derivatives trading strategies without an appropriate level of knowledge and trading experience
options.D: Approve it automatically provided she deposits additional margin

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameter for 2.12 lists, among manual-review triggers, an applicant applying to use derivatives trading strategies and not having an appropriate level of knowledge and trading experience. Option A confuses the suitability exemption for OEO order flow with the separate account-approval controls that still apply; B is wrong because the control routes the file to manual review rather than mandating automated rejection; D wrongly treats margin as a substitute for the knowledge-and-experience screen.
questions.source_quote: "Applicant applying to use derivatives trading strategies and not having an appropriate level of knowledge and trading experience" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 588

questions.scenario: Tania Brassard is the designated Supervisor for retail accounts at Lakefield Securities Inc., a mid-size Investment Dealer. Eight months ago she delegated the first-pass daily review of retail trade blotters to Omar Haddad, a compliance analyst qualified by training and experience. The delegation terms were documented in writing, Haddad sends Brassard a weekly summary, and the firm's policies list which functions can never be delegated. In March, Lakefield's compliance department, while testing the delegation arrangement, discovers that for six weeks Haddad systematically passed over trades in the accounts of one Registered Representative, Victor Onishenko, after Onishenko privately promised Haddad a share of his commissions. During those six weeks, Onishenko executed a series of discretionary trades in non-discretionary client accounts. Brassard had not examined any of Haddad's completed reviews since the delegation began, although the firm's procedures required her to sample them quarterly.
questions.stem: Considering Haddad's agreement to overlook Onishenko's trades in exchange for a share of commissions, into which common area of CIRO regulatory action does Haddad's own conduct most directly fall?

options.A: Failing to deliver client account statements
options.B: Aiding and abetting another in violations of securities and derivatives laws, regulations or CIRO requirements
options.C: Exceeding derivative position limits
options.D: Operating from an unapproved business location

questions.rationale: By knowingly suppressing the review of Onishenko's improper discretionary trading in return for compensation, Haddad assisted the violations — squarely the syllabus category of aiding and abetting another in such violations. Option A concerns client reporting, not in issue; C concerns derivatives limits, which the facts do not raise; D concerns location approval, irrelevant here. Onishenko's own conduct falls under the separate category of directly violating CIRO requirements, which is why the aiding-and-abetting category is the best fit for Haddad specifically.
questions.source_quote: "Aiding and abetting another in such violations" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 586

questions.scenario: Tania Brassard is the designated Supervisor for retail accounts at Lakefield Securities Inc., a mid-size Investment Dealer. Eight months ago she delegated the first-pass daily review of retail trade blotters to Omar Haddad, a compliance analyst qualified by training and experience. The delegation terms were documented in writing, Haddad sends Brassard a weekly summary, and the firm's policies list which functions can never be delegated. In March, Lakefield's compliance department, while testing the delegation arrangement, discovers that for six weeks Haddad systematically passed over trades in the accounts of one Registered Representative, Victor Onishenko, after Onishenko privately promised Haddad a share of his commissions. During those six weeks, Onishenko executed a series of discretionary trades in non-discretionary client accounts. Brassard had not examined any of Haddad's completed reviews since the delegation began, although the firm's procedures required her to sample them quarterly.
questions.stem: Applying the documented controls for delegation from the Element 2 syllabus parameters, which specific control did Brassard herself fail to perform during the eight months of the arrangement?

options.A: Documenting the terms of the delegation in writing
options.B: Confirming the delegate's qualification by training or experience
options.C: Receiving regular reports from the delegate
options.D: Examining the delegate's reviews to ensure they are following applicable policies and procedures

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameter for 2.10 requires examination of the delegate's reviews to ensure they follow applicable policies and procedures and recommend items the delegator would have approved; Brassard skipped the required quarterly sampling entirely, which is why Haddad's six-week suppression went undetected by her. Options A, B and C describe controls the scenario states were in place — written terms, a qualified delegate, and weekly summaries — so none of them is the failed control.
questions.source_quote: "Examination of the delegate’s reviews to ensure they are following applicable policies and procedures" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 632

questions.scenario: Tania Brassard is the designated Supervisor for retail accounts at Lakefield Securities Inc., a mid-size Investment Dealer. Eight months ago she delegated the first-pass daily review of retail trade blotters to Omar Haddad, a compliance analyst qualified by training and experience. The delegation terms were documented in writing, Haddad sends Brassard a weekly summary, and the firm's policies list which functions can never be delegated. In March, Lakefield's compliance department, while testing the delegation arrangement, discovers that for six weeks Haddad systematically passed over trades in the accounts of one Registered Representative, Victor Onishenko, after Onishenko privately promised Haddad a share of his commissions. During those six weeks, Onishenko executed a series of discretionary trades in non-discretionary client accounts. Brassard had not examined any of Haddad's completed reviews since the delegation began, although the firm's procedures required her to sample them quarterly.
questions.stem: Following the discovery, CIRO examiners ask Lakefield to produce its delegation records for the Brassard-Haddad arrangement. Applying subsection 3907(5), which required component of those records will the firm be unable to produce?

options.A: The record of the Supervisor's follow up and review of the delegated tasks, since Brassard performed no examination of Haddad's reviews during the eight months
options.B: The record of the terms of the delegation, since delegation terms are never reduced to writing
options.C: The record of Haddad's qualifications, since training and experience cannot be documented
options.D: The record of functions that cannot be delegated, since the firm maintained no such list

questions.rationale: Subsection 3907(5) requires the Dealer Member to maintain a record of the terms of the delegation, as well as the Supervisor's follow up and review of the delegated tasks. The scenario states Brassard examined none of Haddad's completed reviews for eight months, so no follow-up-and-review record can exist. Option B contradicts the scenario, which says the delegation terms were documented in writing; C is false — qualification by training or experience is routinely documented and the scenario establishes Haddad's qualification; D contradicts the scenario, which says the firm's policies list the functions that can never be delegated.
questions.source_quote: "The Dealer Member must maintain a record of the terms of the delegation, as well as the Supervisor’s follow up and review of the delegated tasks." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7961

questions.scenario: Tania Brassard is the designated Supervisor for retail accounts at Lakefield Securities Inc., a mid-size Investment Dealer. Eight months ago she delegated the first-pass daily review of retail trade blotters to Omar Haddad, a compliance analyst qualified by training and experience. The delegation terms were documented in writing, Haddad sends Brassard a weekly summary, and the firm's policies list which functions can never be delegated. In March, Lakefield's compliance department, while testing the delegation arrangement, discovers that for six weeks Haddad systematically passed over trades in the accounts of one Registered Representative, Victor Onishenko, after Onishenko privately promised Haddad a share of his commissions. During those six weeks, Onishenko executed a series of discretionary trades in non-discretionary client accounts. Brassard had not examined any of Haddad's completed reviews since the delegation began, although the firm's procedures required her to sample them quarterly.
questions.stem: Lakefield's Chief Compliance Officer concludes that the suppressed reviews created a reasonable risk of harm to the affected clients. Under section 3912, what must the CCO now do?

options.A: Wait to include the matter in the next annual written report to the board
options.B: Report to the Ultimate Designated Person as soon as possible
options.C: Quietly remediate the file and document nothing
options.D: Refer the matter to the firm's marketing department for client messaging

questions.rationale: Clause 3912(1)(iii) requires the CCO to report to the Ultimate Designated Person as soon as possible upon any indication of non-compliance where, among other things, the non-compliance creates a reasonable risk of harm to a client — exactly the conclusion the CCO has reached. Option A delays a prompt-escalation duty into the annual board report, which is a separate obligation under 3915; C defeats both the escalation and record-keeping framework; D is not a compliance escalation channel at all.
questions.source_quote: "(a) the non-compliance creates a reasonable risk of harm to a client," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8007

questions.scenario: Cobalt Direct Investing, the order-execution-only division of a CIRO Investment Dealer, uses an automated workflow to open new accounts and an automated surveillance system to screen daily account activity. The onboarding workflow auto-approves applications that pass programmed checks and routes flagged applications to a human queue reviewed by designated Supervisor Felix Arsenault. One week, the system processes: (1) an application from Maple Crest Holdings Ltd., a private corporation; (2) an application from Genevieve Ott, who discloses that she is a deputy minister of a provincial government; and (3) an application from Liam Doyle, a 34-year-old salaried engineer opening a basic cash account in his own name with no trading authorization and no derivatives features. Separately, the firm's internal audit team notes that the quarterly test scripts validating the onboarding system's screening logic have not been run for over a year.
questions.stem: Cobalt's executive team asks how CIRO's published position on supervision accommodates the firm's heavy reliance on automated tools. Which statement reflects the Account Supervision Guidance?

options.A: Supervisory requirements are generally technology neutral and CIRO supports Dealers using automation to assist with supervisory tasks
options.B: Automation is permitted only after the dealer obtains an exemption order
options.C: Automated systems may make all supervisory judgments without any Supervisor role
options.D: Automation is restricted to dealers that have no retail clients

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 states that supervisory requirements are generally technology neutral and that the regulator supports Dealers using automation to assist them with supervisory tasks. Option B invents an exemption-order precondition; C overstates automation's role — the syllabus parameter for 2.11 preserves the Supervisor's role and responsibilities and recognizes tasks that cannot be automated; D invents a no-retail restriction contradicted by the guidance's retail focus.
questions.source_quote: "Generally, our supervisory requirements are technology neutral and we support Dealers using automation to assist them with supervisory tasks." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 50

questions.scenario: Cobalt Direct Investing, the order-execution-only division of a CIRO Investment Dealer, uses an automated workflow to open new accounts and an automated surveillance system to screen daily account activity. The onboarding workflow auto-approves applications that pass programmed checks and routes flagged applications to a human queue reviewed by designated Supervisor Felix Arsenault. One week, the system processes: (1) an application from Maple Crest Holdings Ltd., a private corporation; (2) an application from Genevieve Ott, who discloses that she is a deputy minister of a provincial government; and (3) an application from Liam Doyle, a 34-year-old salaried engineer opening a basic cash account in his own name with no trading authorization and no derivatives features. Separately, the firm's internal audit team notes that the quarterly test scripts validating the onboarding system's screening logic have not been run for over a year.
questions.stem: Of the three applications received that week, which should the automated workflow route to Felix Arsenault's manual review queue, based on the syllabus parameters for automation controls?

options.A: Only Liam Doyle's application
options.B: All three applications
options.C: The applications of Maple Crest Holdings Ltd. and Genevieve Ott, but not Liam Doyle's
options.D: None; an order-execution-only dealer has no manual-review obligations

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameter for 2.12 lists, among factors that may trigger a manual review, the applicant not being an individual (Maple Crest, a corporation) and the applicant being a Politically Exposed Person (Ott, a senior provincial official). Doyle — an adult individual opening a basic cash account in his own name with no trading authorization and no derivatives strategies — fits the automated path. Option A flags the one routine file and clears the two trigger files; B over-routes Doyle, defeating the purpose of automation; D wrongly exempts OEO dealers from automation controls.
questions.source_quote: "Applicant being a Politically Exposed Person (PEP)" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 599

questions.scenario: Cobalt Direct Investing, the order-execution-only division of a CIRO Investment Dealer, uses an automated workflow to open new accounts and an automated surveillance system to screen daily account activity. The onboarding workflow auto-approves applications that pass programmed checks and routes flagged applications to a human queue reviewed by designated Supervisor Felix Arsenault. One week, the system processes: (1) an application from Maple Crest Holdings Ltd., a private corporation; (2) an application from Genevieve Ott, who discloses that she is a deputy minister of a provincial government; and (3) an application from Liam Doyle, a 34-year-old salaried engineer opening a basic cash account in his own name with no trading authorization and no derivatives features. Separately, the firm's internal audit team notes that the quarterly test scripts validating the onboarding system's screening logic have not been run for over a year.
questions.stem: Internal audit's finding that the onboarding system's quarterly validation scripts have not run for over a year is significant because it undermines which control identified in the syllabus parameters for automation processes?

options.A: The requirement to file a governance document with CIRO
options.B: The requirement for client signatures on margin agreements
options.C: The prohibition on delegating discretionary account reviews
options.D: Appropriate periodic testing and auditing to ensure the automated process is functioning as designed

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameter for 2.12 requires appropriate periodic testing and auditing to ensure the automated process is functioning as designed; a year-long lapse in validation means undetected screening defects could let trigger files (corporations, PEPs, minors, insiders) slip through auto-approval. Option A concerns the section 3916 governance filing, unrelated to automation testing; B concerns account documentation; C concerns a delegation prohibition for discretionary reviews, not automation validation.
questions.source_quote: "Appropriate periodic testing and auditing to ensure automated process is functioning as designed" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 620

questions.scenario: Cobalt Direct Investing, the order-execution-only division of a CIRO Investment Dealer, uses an automated workflow to open new accounts and an automated surveillance system to screen daily account activity. The onboarding workflow auto-approves applications that pass programmed checks and routes flagged applications to a human queue reviewed by designated Supervisor Felix Arsenault. One week, the system processes: (1) an application from Maple Crest Holdings Ltd., a private corporation; (2) an application from Genevieve Ott, who discloses that she is a deputy minister of a provincial government; and (3) an application from Liam Doyle, a 34-year-old salaried engineer opening a basic cash account in his own name with no trading authorization and no derivatives features. Separately, the firm's internal audit team notes that the quarterly test scripts validating the onboarding system's screening logic have not been run for over a year.
questions.stem: A consultant proposes that, because Cobalt's surveillance system runs continuously, the firm can remove Felix Arsenault's designation and operate with no Supervisor responsible for approving new accounts. Analyzing this proposal against Part B of Rule 3900, what is the correct conclusion?

options.A: The proposal is acceptable because OEO accounts are exempt from all of Rule 3900
options.B: The proposal is acceptable if the surveillance vendor agrees to indemnify the firm
options.C: The proposal fails: the dealer must designate one or more Supervisors to be responsible for approving the opening of new accounts and for establishing and maintaining procedures relating to account supervision
options.D: The proposal fails only because Arsenault has tenure rights

questions.rationale: Subsection 3925(2) requires the Dealer Member to designate one or more Supervisors responsible for approving the opening of new accounts and for establishing and maintaining procedures relating to account supervision and supervising account activity — a designated human role automation cannot eliminate. Option A is wrong: OEO accounts have their own supervision provisions within Rule 3900 (Part E), not a blanket exemption; B confuses commercial risk transfer with a regulatory designation requirement; D rests on employment concepts irrelevant to the rule.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must designate one or more Supervisors to be responsible for approving the opening of new accounts and for establishing and maintaining procedures relating to account supervision and supervising account activity," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8086

questions.stem: Marchand Securities operates twelve branch offices across Ontario and uses a two-tiered post-trade review system. According to CIRO guidance, what is the primary purpose of second-tier supervision compared to first-tier supervision?

options.A: To ensure no serious account issues were missed by first-tier supervision and that first-tier supervision is conducted adequately
options.B: To repeat the first-tier review at the same depth so every trade is reviewed twice
options.C: To replace the first-tier review for branch offices that lack a Resident Supervisor
options.D: To approve the opening of new accounts before the first-tier reviewer sees them

questions.rationale: Per GN-3900-20-001, second-tier supervision has a different purpose and is generally not at the same depth as first-tier supervision; it is designed to ensure no serious account issues were missed and that first-tier reviews are conducted adequately. Option B is wrong because the guidance expressly states the second tier is generally not at the same depth; C confuses the second tier with the assigned outside Supervisor used for locations without Resident Supervisors; D is account opening, a separate function.
questions.source_quote: "ensure no serious account issues were missed by ﬁrst-tier supervision, and ensure ﬁrst-tier supervision is conducted adequately." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 266

questions.stem: Tremblay Capital is deciding whether its small Sudbury branch, staffed by three experienced Registered Representatives, needs a Resident Supervisor. Under CIRO's account supervision guidance, which approach is acceptable for a business location that does not have a Resident Supervisor?

options.A: The location may operate unsupervised provided all Registered Representatives have more than five years of experience
options.B: An assigned outside Supervisor must oversee the location and should visit it periodically to review business conduct and compliance with applicable laws
options.C: The most senior Registered Representative at the location automatically assumes the Supervisor role
options.D: Head office must approve every order from the location before entry

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 states that business locations without Resident Supervisors should have an assigned outside Supervisor who should visit the location periodically to review business conduct and compliance, with the visits specifically addressed in policies and procedures. Option A is wrong: experience is only one factor in deciding whether a Resident Supervisor is necessary, not a basis for no supervision. C confers supervisory status without designation or qualification. D describes pre-approval of orders, an enhanced measure for Approved Persons with infraction histories, not the default control for remote locations.
questions.source_quote: "Business locations without Resident Supervisors should have an assigned outside Supervisor. The outside Supervisor should visit the location periodically to review business conduct and compliance with applicable laws." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 169

questions.stem: Lavoie Investments wants to conduct all first-tier reviews of branch trading activity centrally at head office rather than at each branch. Under CIRO guidance, this single centralized approach to first-tier reviews is acceptable provided that:

options.A: CIRO grants a written exemption from Rule 3900 before centralization begins
options.B: Each branch retains a Resident Supervisor who duplicates the central review
options.C: There are resources available to conduct the review at that location and the Dealer has adequate procedures for dealing with any issues identified
options.D: The central reviews are limited to accounts generating more than $1,500 in monthly commissions

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 permits first-tier reviews in a central location (head office or regional office) provided there are resources available to conduct the review at that location and the Dealer has adequate procedures for dealing with identified issues. Option A is wrong — no exemption is needed since the rules are structure-neutral; B defeats the point of centralization and is not required; D confuses the $1,500 figure, which is an example of a monthly review selection criterion, with a condition for centralizing reviews.
questions.source_quote: "Dealers can conduct these reviews in a central location (head oDce or regional oDce) provided: there are resources available to conduct the review at that location, and the Dealer has adequate procedures for dealing with any issues identiﬁed." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 229

questions.stem: The Chief Compliance Officer of Belliveau Securities is rewriting the firm's account supervision policies and procedures. In addition to AML/ATF compliance, which two categories of clients must the policies and procedures specifically address identifying?

options.A: Clients who trade frequently and clients who hold concentrated positions
options.B: Clients that present a high risk to the Dealer Member and clients that present a high risk of conducting improper activities in the securities markets
options.C: Clients with registered accounts and clients with margin accounts
options.D: Politically exposed persons and clients resident outside Canada

questions.rationale: Subsection 3926(2) requires policies and procedures that specifically address identifying clients that present a high risk to the Dealer Member, identifying clients that present a high risk of conducting improper activities in the securities markets, and complying with all anti-money laundering and terrorist financing requirements. Option D is the closest distractor — PEPs are an AML concept and a manual-review trigger in account-opening automation, but they are not the two client-identification categories named in 3926(2). A and C describe activity and account types, not the rule's risk categories.
questions.source_quote: "(i) identify clients that present a high risk to the Dealer Member, (ii) identify clients that present a high risk of conducting improper activities in the securities markets" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8099

questions.stem: A Supervisor at Quesnel Wealth is selecting retail accounts for post-trade review using a risk-based approach. According to CIRO guidance, which of the following client profiles may a Dealer determine presents a higher risk of improper market activity, warranting closer review?

options.A: Clients who hold only guaranteed investment certificates
options.B: Clients who place all orders through the firm's mobile application
options.C: Clients with access to material non-public information about issuers, holders of control blocks of public issuers, and market professionals
options.D: Clients whose accounts are charged flat annual fees rather than commissions

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 states a Dealer may determine some clients present a higher risk of improper market activity, such as those who have access to material non-public information about issuers, holders of control blocks of public issuers and market professionals. Option D is the closest distractor: fee-based accounts require review criteria other than commission levels, but the fee structure itself does not make the client a higher improper-market-activity risk. A and B describe low-risk products and an order channel, neither of which is a guidance-listed risk marker.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer may determine some clients present a higher risk of improper market activity, such as those who have access to material non-public information about issuers, holders of control blocks of public issuers and market professionals." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 200

questions.stem: An internal audit at Falaise Capital finds that any branch employee can edit a client's stated risk profile in the firm's systems without a record of who made the change. Which IDPC requirement does this most directly contravene?

options.A: The requirement that policies and procedures include controls for accessing and amending client records
options.B: The requirement to maintain a record of the names of Supervisors and their designation dates
options.C: The prohibition on discretionary trading outside approved discretionary accounts
options.D: The requirement to deliver a relationship disclosure document at account opening

questions.rationale: Subsection 3926(5) requires that a Dealer Member's policies and procedures include controls for accessing and amending client records — unrestricted, untracked editing of KYC data defeats that control. Option B concerns supervision records under 3908, not client records; C concerns trading conduct, not record integrity; D is an account-opening disclosure obligation. The closest distractor is B because both involve record-keeping, but 3908 records relate to the supervisory system itself rather than access to client files.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member’s policies and procedures must include controls for accessing and amending client records." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8111

questions.stem: The compliance department at Drummond Securities drafts substantive amendments to the firm's account supervision policies and procedures. Before implementation, who must approve these amendments under the IDPC Rules?

options.A: CIRO's Member Regulation Policy group
options.B: The Dealer Member's Chief Compliance Officer or another appropriate Executive
options.C: The Ultimate Designated Person and the board of directors jointly
options.D: The designated Supervisor responsible for each affected branch

questions.rationale: Subsection 3926(3) requires all policies and procedures relating to the supervision of accounts, and any substantive amendments, to be approved by the Dealer Member's Chief Compliance Officer or another appropriate Executive. Option C is the closest distractor — the board is responsible for ensuring an appropriate supervision system is in place under 3901(3), but the rule assigns approval of account-supervision P&Ps and amendments to the CCO or another appropriate Executive, not to a joint UDP/board sign-off. A is wrong because CIRO does not pre-approve dealer P&Ps; D understates the required approval level.
questions.source_quote: "any substantive amendments to such policies and procedures, must be approved by the Dealer Member’s Chief Compliance Officer or another appropriate Executive." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8105

questions.stem: Which statement best describes the Dealer Member's obligation under the IDPC Rules with respect to Approved Persons who have a history of regulatory infractions or questionable conduct?

options.A: The Dealer Member must terminate the Approved Person's registration within 90 days
options.B: The Dealer Member must transfer all of the Approved Person's accounts to another Registered Representative
options.C: The Dealer Member must establish and follow procedures for the implementation of additional supervisory measures applicable to those Approved Persons
options.D: The Dealer Member may rely on its standard sampling-based reviews because the regulator has already sanctioned the conduct

questions.rationale: Subsection 3927(3) requires the Dealer Member to establish and follow procedures for the implementation of additional supervisory measures applicable to Approved Persons with a history of regulatory infractions or questionable conduct. Option D is the misconception the rule exists to defeat: prior discipline increases, not discharges, the supervision obligation. A and B are not required by the rule — the prescribed response is enhanced supervision, not automatic termination or account reassignment.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must establish and follow procedures for the implementation of additional supervisory measures applicable to Approved Persons with a history of regulatory infractions or questionable conduct." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8131

questions.stem: Cloutier Direct, an order execution only division of an Investment Dealer, is updating its trade-review policies. Under section 3955, what specific risk must its policies and procedures relating to the review of client trading address?

options.A: The risk of unsuitable recommendations made by its Registered Representatives
options.B: The risks associated with the method of order entry and the absence of intermediation by employees of the Dealer Member
options.C: The risk that clients fail to read the relationship disclosure document
options.D: The risk of producing inaccurate suitability determinations for high-volume traders

questions.rationale: Subsection 3955(2) requires the OEO entity's policies and procedures relating to review of client trading to specifically address the risks associated with the method of order entry and the absence of intermediation by employees of the Dealer Member. Options A and D both invoke suitability — wrong for OEO accounts, which are exempt from suitability determination and where recommendations are prohibited; 3955(1)(iii) directs review for Rule 3900 concerns other than those relating to the suitability requirements. C is a disclosure issue, not the order-entry risk the rule targets.
questions.source_quote: "policies and procedures relating to review of client trading must specifically address the risks associated with the method of order entry and the absence of intermediation by employees of the Dealer Member." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8267

questions.stem: Gendron Securities placed a temporary hold on a vulnerable client's account 30 days ago based on a reasonable belief of financial exploitation, and the concern has not yet been resolved. Under section 3222, what must the Dealer Member now do, and continue to do for as long as the hold remains?

options.A: Apply to CIRO for an extension of the hold beyond 30 days
options.B: Within every 30-day period, either revoke the temporary hold or provide the client with notice of the decision to continue the hold and the reasons for that decision
options.C: Obtain the written consent of the client's trusted contact person to continue the hold
options.D: Convert the temporary hold into a permanent account restriction after 60 days

questions.rationale: Clause 3222(3)(iv) requires that within 30 days of placing the temporary hold, and within every subsequent 30-day period until the hold is revoked, the Dealer Member either revoke the hold or provide the client notice of the decision to continue it and the reasons. Option A is wrong — no CIRO application is involved; the obligation is internal review plus client notice. C is wrong because the trusted contact person may be a resource but their consent is not a condition for continuing a hold. D is wrong: the regime is temporary by design with recurring 30-day decision points, not conversion to a permanent restriction.
questions.source_quote: "within 30 days of placing the temporary hold and, until the hold is revoked, within every subsequent 30‐day period, do either of the following: (a) revoke the temporary hold, (b) provide the client with notice of the Dealer Member’s decision to continue the hold" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8947

questions.stem: An Approved Person at Pelchat Wealth wants to place a temporary hold on a withdrawal request, citing concern about the client's mental state. Under section 3222, a temporary hold on the basis of a client's lack of mental capacity is permitted only if:

options.A: The client is over the age of 65 and has named a trusted contact person
options.B: A physician has certified in writing that the client is incapable
options.C: The Dealer Member reasonably believes that the client does not have the mental capacity to make decisions involving financial matters
options.D: The client has previously been the subject of a financial-exploitation hold

questions.rationale: Subsection 3222(2) prohibits a temporary hold on the basis of lack of mental capacity unless the Dealer Member reasonably believes the client does not have the mental capacity to make decisions involving financial matters. Option B overstates the threshold — the standard is the Dealer Member's reasonable belief, not a medical certificate. A is wrong because age and the existence of a trusted contact person are not the test; vulnerability is not defined by an age cut-off. D conflates the two separate grounds (exploitation under 3222(1) and incapacity under 3222(2)); a prior hold is not a condition.
questions.source_quote: "must not place a temporary hold on the basis of a client’s lack of mental capacity unless the Dealer Member reasonably believes that the client does not have the mental capacity to make decisions involving financial matters." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8936

questions.stem: Hubert Capital offers both commission-based and fee-based retail accounts. Its monthly review selects accounts solely by gross commissions charged. A Supervisor objects that this design breaches Rule 3900. Why is the Supervisor correct?

options.A: Monthly reviews may only be selected on trade value, never on commissions
options.B: Policies for supervising accounts where a commission is not charged for trades, such as fee-based accounts, must use criteria other than commission levels
options.C: Fee-based accounts are exempt from monthly supervision entirely
options.D: Commission-based selection is permitted only for institutional accounts

questions.rationale: Subsection 3945(3) requires policies and procedures that specifically address supervising retail client accounts where a commission is not charged for trades placed by or for a client, such as fee-based accounts, and those policies must use criteria other than commission levels — a commissions-only filter would never select fee-based accounts for review. Option A is wrong: commission-based selection criteria remain a valid example for commission accounts (GN cites $1,500 gross commissions). C is the opposite of the rule — fee-based accounts still require supervision. D inverts the retail/institutional split; 3945 is the retail part of Rule 3900.
questions.source_quote: "such as fee based accounts. These policies and procedures must: (i) address account activity review Corporation requirements, and (ii) use criteria other than commission levels." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8176

questions.stem: When setting up its retail supervision system, which of the following sets of retail client accounts must Veilleux Securities specifically designate for supervision purposes under subsection 3945(4)?

options.A: Margin accounts, cash accounts, joint accounts, corporate accounts and estate accounts
options.B: Non-client accounts, discretionary accounts, managed accounts, registered accounts and restricted accounts
options.C: Fee-based accounts, DAP/RAP accounts, hedging accounts, OEO accounts and DEA accounts
options.D: Only accounts of clients identified as vulnerable or high-risk

questions.rationale: Subsection 3945(4) requires the Dealer Member to specifically designate non-client accounts, discretionary accounts, managed accounts, registered accounts, and restricted accounts for supervision purposes. Option A lists common account categories that are not the rule's designation list; C mixes account types governed by other Parts of Rule 3900 (OEO, derivatives) with fee-based accounts, which are addressed by 3945(3)'s review-criteria requirement rather than the designation list; D substitutes the client-risk identification obligation in 3926(2) for the account-designation obligation.
questions.source_quote: "(i) non-client accounts, (ii) discretionary accounts, (iii) managed accounts, (iv) registered accounts, and (v) restricted accounts." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8182

questions.stem: During a monthly review at Sicotte Investments, a Supervisor finds a long-tenured retail client's account where blue-chip equities have steadily been replaced over several months by thinly traded, speculative venture issues, although each individual trade appeared defensible when made. Which detection category from subsection 3945(2) does this pattern most directly engage, and what review design does CIRO guidance say is needed to catch it?

options.A: Undisclosed short sales; detected by reviewing settlement records
options.B: Late payment; detected through cash account violation reports
options.C: Improper crosses between clients; detected by comparing contra parties on each fill
options.D: Deterioration of the quality of client holdings in an account; detected by reviewing trades over a longer period rather than single trades

questions.rationale: Subsection 3945(2)(viii) lists deterioration of the quality of client holdings in an account as a required detection item, and GN-3900-20-001 explains that patterns of activity may not be apparent by reviewing single trades — reviewing trades over a longer period may detect issues with overall account activity even if each trade is suitable. Options A, B and C cite other genuine 3945(2) items, but none describes a gradual quality slide in holdings; C is the closest trap because it also involves multi-trade comparison, yet crosses involve transfers between clients, absent here.
questions.source_quote: "deterioration of the quality of client holdings in an account," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8156

questions.stem: Beyond transactional activity, subsection 3946(1) requires Aubin Securities' policies and procedures to address identifying, dealing with and informing the appropriate Supervisors of which other client related matters?

options.A: Client complaints, cash account violations, transfers of funds and positions between unrelated accounts or between non-client accounts and client accounts, and trading while the account is under margined
options.B: Marketing material approvals, outside activities, and gifts and entertainment
options.C: Dividend reinvestments, currency conversions, and account fee rebates
options.D: Only matters that result in a client complaint being filed with CIRO

questions.rationale: Subsection 3946(1) lists client complaints, cash account violations, transfers of funds and positions between unrelated accounts or between non-client accounts and client accounts or deposits from non-client accounts to client accounts, and trading while the account is under margined. Option B lists conduct-supervision topics governed elsewhere (communications and outside activities rules), not 3946 matters; C lists routine administrative events that are not red flags under the rule; D wrongly narrows the obligation to regulator-filed complaints when the rule covers the matters themselves.
questions.source_quote: "(i) client complaints, (ii) cash account violations, (iii) transfers of funds and positions between unrelated accounts or between non-client accounts and client accounts or deposits from non-client accounts to client accounts, and (iv) trading while the account is under margined." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8191

questions.stem: Designing its first-tier reviews, Normand Wealth wants to know the expected completion timing under CIRO guidance for (1) the daily review of the previous day's trading and (2) the monthly review. Which pairing is correct?

options.A: Daily review within one business day after the trading activity occurs; monthly review within 21 days of the review period, unless precluded by unusual circumstances
options.B: Daily review before market open the same day; monthly review within 10 days of month-end
options.C: Daily review within five business days; monthly review within 30 days of the review period
options.D: Daily review within one business day; monthly review by the end of the following calendar quarter

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 states first-tier daily reviews should be completed one business day after the trading activity occurs, and first-tier monthly reviews within 21 days of the review period, unless precluded by unusual circumstances; the same one-business-day and 21-day expectations apply at the second tier. Option B demands pre-open same-day completion, which is faster than the guidance and would make a post-trade review of the previous day impossible; C and D use plausible but wrong windows (5 days/30 days/quarter-end) — the tested figures are one business day and 21 days.
questions.source_quote: "Dealers should complete this review within 21 days of the review period, unless precluded by unusual circumstances." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 254

questions.stem: A first-tier monthly review is being scoped at Carbonneau Securities. According to CIRO guidance, which non-client account activity must be included in the first-tier monthly review?

options.A: Only non-client accounts that generated commissions above $3,000 in the month
options.B: All non-client accounts with any activity other than dividend and interest payments
options.C: Non-client accounts are reviewed only at the second tier
options.D: Only non-client accounts belonging to producing branch managers

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 states a first-tier monthly review should include all non-client accounts with any activity other than dividend and interest payments. Option A misapplies the $3,000 figure, which is a second-tier monthly selection example for client commissions; C is wrong because non-client trading also appears in second-tier daily criteria but the first-tier monthly inclusion is explicit; D confuses this with the separate second-tier criterion covering producing branch managers' client accounts.
questions.source_quote: "A ﬁrst-tier monthly review should include all non-client accounts with any activity other than dividend and interest payments." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 251

questions.stem: A retail client of Thivierge Securities buys a thinly traded corporate bond in the secondary market. The trade confirmation shows only the bond's price. The client later learns the firm embedded a mark-up. For a non-primary-market debt trade confirmed to a retail client, what does section 3816 require regarding the firm's remuneration?

options.A: Nothing further; mark-ups on debt securities never need to appear on confirmations
options.B: Disclosure of either the total amount of any mark-up or mark-down, commission or other service charges applied, or the total commission charged plus a prescribed notification that dealer firm remuneration was added to or deducted from the price
options.C: Disclosure of the mark-up only if it exceeds 5% of the trade value
options.D: A verbal disclosure by the Registered Representative before settlement

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 3816(2)(vi)(c) requires, where a debt security trade is not a primary market transaction and the confirmation is sent to a retail client, either the total amount of any mark-up or mark-down, commission or other service charges applied, or the total commission charged plus the prescribed notification that dealer firm remuneration was added to or deducted from the price. Option A is the common misconception that embedded debt remuneration is invisible; C invents a materiality threshold not in the rule; D substitutes verbal disclosure for the written confirmation content requirement. This is the transactional charge/commission information the syllabus requires Supervisors to know for retail instructions.
questions.source_quote: "the total amount of any mark-up or mark-down, commission or other service charges the Dealer Member applied to the transaction," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7609

questions.stem: The branch Supervisor at Routhier Capital is dually registered and also advises and trades for her own book of retail clients. Under CIRO's account supervision guidance, how should the Dealer address the supervision of her client activity?

options.A: She may review her own client trades provided she documents each review
options.B: Her client trades are exempt from review because she holds supervisory proficiency
options.C: The Dealer should ensure another Supervisor reviews her advice to and trades for clients
options.D: Her client trades need only be reviewed during the annual branch audit

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 states Dealers should ensure that where a Supervisor is dually-registered and also advises or trades for their own clients, another Supervisor reviews that Supervisor's advice to and trades for clients — the cross-supervision principle that no one supervises themselves. Option A is the precise conflict the guidance targets: self-review, even documented, is inadequate. B confuses proficiency with independence; D defers ongoing daily/monthly review obligations to an annual event, which does not satisfy Rule 3900 timing.
questions.source_quote: "Dealers should ensure that where a Supervisors is dually-registered and also advises or trades for their own clients, another Supervisor reviews that Supervisor’s advice to and trades for clients." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 148

questions.stem: Olivier, a producing branch manager at Magnan Securities, conducts the first-tier review for his branch. Under CIRO guidance, which second-tier daily review criterion specifically captures the gap his dual role creates?

options.A: Bond trades over $100,000 value per trade
options.B: Producing branch managers' client accounts
options.C: Stock trades with a value over $5,000 and a price under $5.00 per share
options.D: Suspense account trading

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001's example second-tier daily criteria include producing branch managers' client accounts — precisely because a producing manager cannot independently review their own client activity at the first tier, the second tier must catch it. Options A, C and D are also genuine items on the guidance's example list, but none addresses the self-review conflict in the scenario; they target large or low-priced trades and suspense activity generally.
questions.source_quote: "producing branch managers’ client accounts, all client accounts not reviewed by a branch manager," — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 283

questions.stem: A retail client of Sauriol Wealth, posted overseas for a ten-month contract, asks the firm to hold her mail for the full period. Under section 3219, how can the Dealer Member accommodate a hold-mail period longer than the standard limit?

options.A: It cannot; hold-mail orders are capped at six months in any 12-month period with no exceptions
options.B: Only if CIRO grants exemptive relief for the specific account
options.C: A longer period may be used if it is permitted by the Dealer Member's policies and procedures, the policies specifically address close supervision of such accounts, and the appropriate Supervisor pre-approves the extended period
options.D: Only if the client signs a waiver releasing the firm from supervision of the account

questions.rationale: Subsection 3219(2) permits a period longer than the clause 3219(1)(ii) six-month limit if it is permitted by the Dealer Member's policies and procedures, the firm has policies that specifically address close supervision of such accounts, and the appropriate Supervisor pre-approves the extended period. Option A states the default limit but ignores the express notwithstanding provision; B is wrong because the exception is internal, not CIRO relief; D is impossible — supervision obligations cannot be waived by the client, and held mail demands closer supervision, not less.
questions.source_quote: "a longer period may be used if: (i) it is permitted by the Dealer Member’s policies and procedures, (ii) the Dealer Member has policies and procedures that specifically address the close supervision of such accounts, and (iii) the appropriate Supervisor pre‐approves the extended period." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4890

questions.stem: Mail sent to a retail client of Bisson Securities is returned undeliverable. Under section 3219, the firm's returned mail procedures must, at a minimum, require:

options.A: Control and investigation by a person independent of the sales function, and that a record of all investigations and their results be maintained
options.B: That the Registered Representative for the account locate the client and update the address
options.C: Immediate closure of the account and remittance of assets to the provincial public trustee
options.D: Re-mailing the documents monthly until they are no longer returned

questions.rationale: Subsection 3219(3) requires returned mail procedures to include control and investigation by a person independent of the sales function (who may be located within a business location) and a rule requiring that a record of all investigations and their results be maintained. Option B is the exact conflict the rule prevents — the RR is part of the sales function and could be the person concealing a false address; C and D are not prescribed responses and ignore the independence and record-keeping requirements.
questions.source_quote: "a rule requiring the control and investigation by a person independent of the sales function, but may be located within a business location, and (ii) a rule requiring that a record of all investigations and their results be maintained." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4897

questions.stem: Falardeau Capital Markets serves only institutional clients. Its compliance team is drafting the supervision policies required by section 3950. Beyond outlining actions to deal with problems identified from supervisory reviews, the policies must specifically address detecting improper or suspicious account activity including:

options.A: Unsuitable trading, churning, and deterioration of the quality of client holdings
options.B: Manipulative and deceptive activities, trading in securities on the Dealer Member's restricted list, front running by employee or proprietary accounts, trading in securities that have restrictions on their transfer, and exceeding derivative position or exercise limits
options.C: Late payments, account number changes, and outstanding margin calls
options.D: Failure to deliver relationship disclosure documents to each institutional client

questions.rationale: Subsection 3950(2) requires institutional account supervision policies to specifically address manipulative and deceptive activities, trading in securities on the Dealer Member's restricted list, transacting in derivatives whose underlying interest is on the restricted list, front running by employee or proprietary accounts, trading in securities with transfer restrictions, derivatives whose underlying interest has transfer restrictions, and exceeding derivative position or exercise limits. Options A and C are retail detection items from 3945(2) — the classic trap is applying the retail list to institutional accounts, where suitability-driven items like churning are not in the 3950 list. D is an account-opening disclosure matter, not a 3950 detection item.
questions.source_quote: "(i) manipulative and deceptive activities, (ii) trading in securities on the Dealer Member’s restricted list," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8231

questions.stem: An institutional trader at Forand Securities, aware of a pension fund's unentered block order in a TSX-listed stock that will move the price, immediately buys call options on that stock for the firm's principal account before the client order is entered. Under UMIR 4.1, this conduct is:

options.A: Permitted, because the principal order was in a derivative rather than the security itself
options.B: Permitted, provided the principal position is unwound after the client order fills
options.C: Prohibited frontrunning, because a Participant with knowledge of such a client order must not enter a principal order for the security, derivative or any related security or related derivative before the client order is entered
options.D: Permitted, because frontrunning rules apply only to orders entered on foreign organized regulated markets

questions.rationale: UMIR 4.1(1)(a) prohibits a Participant with knowledge of a client order that on entry could reasonably be expected to affect the market price of a security or derivative from entering, prior to entry of the client order, a principal or non-client order for the purchase or sale of the security, derivative or any related security or related derivative — Policy 4.1 expressly gives the option-on-underlying example. Option A is the trap the related-derivative language closes; B is wrong because subsequent unwinding does not cure trading ahead; D inverts the rule's scope, which covers marketplaces, foreign organized regulated markets and other markets including OTC.
questions.source_quote: "enter a principal order or a non-client order on a marketplace, foreign organized regulated market or other market, including any over-the-counter market, for the purchase or sale of the security, derivative or any related security or related derivative;" — umir.txt ~line 4617

questions.stem: A second-tier reviewer at Forgues Capital identifies a pattern of suspicious closing-price trades in an institutional account but lacks the authority to restrict the account or contact the desk head. Under CIRO guidance, what should the Dealer's procedures provide for in this situation?

options.A: The reviewer must close the file because second-tier reviews are advisory only
options.B: Referring the issues identified to a Supervisor who has the appropriate authority
options.C: Reporting the pattern directly to the client's board of directors
options.D: Waiting until the next monthly review cycle to confirm the pattern persists

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 states that where second-tier reviews are conducted by personnel who do not have the authority to resolve the issues they identify, the Dealer should have procedures for referring those issues to a Supervisor with the appropriate authority — and 3950(1) requires institutional policies to outline the actions to deal with problems identified from supervisory reviews. Option A defeats the purpose of the review; C escalates outside the Dealer to the wrong party; D delays remediation, contrary to the guidance that supervisors take effective and timely remedial action.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer should have procedures for referring these issues to a Supervisor who has the appropriate authority." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 270

questions.stem: Compliance at Dufault Securities is testing its institutional supervision program against subsection 3950(2). Which of the following derivative-related events is on the list the policies and procedures must specifically address detecting?

options.A: Transacting in derivatives whose underlying interest is on the Dealer Member's restricted list
options.B: Speculative transactions in hedging accounts
options.C: Exposures arising out of uncovered option positions
options.D: Holding futures contracts into the delivery month

questions.rationale: Clause 3950(2)(iii) requires institutional supervision policies to address detecting transacting in derivatives whose underlying interest is on the Dealer Member's restricted list. Options B, C and D are all genuine detection items, but they appear in subsection 3963(1) for retail derivatives account trading activity, not in the institutional list — the question tests knowing which list an item belongs to. Exceeding derivative position or exercise limits is the other derivatives item shared with 3950(2).
questions.source_quote: "transacting in derivatives whose underlying interest is on the Dealer Member’s restricted list," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8233

questions.stem: Béland Capital, a Participant, provides direct electronic access to an institutional pension manager. Under UMIR 7.13, before any order may be transmitted using that access, each order must be subject to:

options.A: Manual review by a Registered Representative
options.B: The risk management and supervisory controls, policies and procedures established by the Participant, including the automated controls to examine each order before entry on a marketplace
options.C: Pre-clearance by the Market Regulator
options.D: A suitability determination for the underlying beneficial owner

questions.rationale: Clause 7.13(4)(b) prohibits the Participant from allowing any order to be transmitted using direct electronic access unless the order is subject to the risk management and supervisory controls, policies and procedures established by the Participant, including the automated controls to examine each order before entry on a marketplace. Option A is wrong — DEA is defined by the absence of human intermediation, and the required gate is automated; C invents regulator pre-clearance; D is wrong because a direct electronic access account is by definition not subject to suitability determination (other than the account-level determinations preserved by clauses 3402(3)(i) and 3403(4)(i)).
questions.source_quote: "the order is subject to the risk management and supervisory controls, policies and procedures established by the Participant including the automated controls to examine each order before entry on a marketplace." — umir.txt ~line 8927

questions.stem: An institutional client of Casgrain Securities deploys a new algorithmic trading strategy through its DEA arrangement. Under UMIR Policy 7.1 and the 7.13 standards, what testing obligation applies to every automated order system used by the Participant or any client of the Participant?

options.A: Testing only when the Market Regulator requests a demonstration
options.B: One-time certification by the marketplace on which the orders will trade
options.C: Testing in accordance with prudent business practices initially before use and at least annually thereafter, with a written record maintained
options.D: Continuous live testing in production using small client orders

questions.rationale: UMIR Policy 7.1 Part 8 requires that every automated order system used by the Participant, the Access Person or any client be tested in accordance with prudent business practices initially before use and at least annually thereafter, with a written record maintained demonstrating the testing; UMIR 7.13(2)(f) imposes the matching standard on DEA clients, including testing before introduction of a significant modification. Option A and B substitute external actors for the Participant's own obligation; D is not the standard — testing assumes various market conditions and is distinct from live production use.
questions.source_quote: "is tested in accordance with prudent business practices initially before use and at least annually thereafter. A written record must be maintained with sufficient details to demonstrate the testing of the automated order system" — umir.txt ~line 6986

questions.stem: A client algorithm at Périgny Capital begins flooding a marketplace with mispriced orders after a code defect — a 'runaway' algorithm. Under UMIR Policy 7.1, which capability must the Participant have to deal with this event?

options.A: The ability to immediately override or disable automatically any automated order system and thereby prevent its orders from being entered on any marketplace
options.B: The ability to shift responsibility to the client because the defect was in client-side code
options.C: The ability to request that the marketplace suspend trading in the affected securities
options.D: The ability to cancel resulting trades within 30 days of execution

questions.rationale: UMIR Policy 7.1 Part 8 requires each Participant or Access Person to have the ability to immediately override or disable automatically any automated order system and prevent its orders from being entered on any marketplace — the kill-switch obligation. Option B is expressly foreclosed: the Participant remains responsible for any order or trade resulting from improper operation or malfunction, even where the malfunctioning aspect was not accessible to the Participant for testing. C relies on the marketplace rather than the Participant's own control; D is not a Policy 7.1 control and trades cannot simply be cancelled by election.
questions.source_quote: "Each Participant or Access Person must have the ability to immediately override or disable automatically any automated order system and thereby prevent orders generated by the automated order system from being entered on any marketplace." — umir.txt ~line 7011

questions.stem: Vigneault Direct operates CIRO-approved order execution only account services as a separate business unit. A client with accounts at both the OEO unit and the full-service side asks the OEO unit which ETF to buy. Under section 3955, the OEO unit's policies and procedures must:

options.A: Permit recommendations to clients who also maintain advisory accounts with the Dealer Member
options.B: Ensure that clients are not provided with recommendations as a result of the client having an account with a separate legal entity of the Dealer Member, a separate business unit of the Dealer Member, or the Dealer Member itself
options.C: Route the question to the client's full-service Registered Representative for execution through the OEO account
options.D: Provide the recommendation but document that no suitability determination was made

questions.rationale: Clause 3955(1)(ii) requires OEO policies and procedures to ensure clients are not provided with recommendations as a result of having an account with a separate legal entity, a separate business unit, or the Dealer Member itself. Option A is the precise loophole the clause closes — having another account relationship does not entitle the client to recommendations in the OEO channel. C blends channels and would put advised order flow through the unsupervised-for-suitability OEO pipe; D is wrong because the suitability exemption is conditioned on no recommendations being made at all, not on disclaiming them.
questions.source_quote: "ensure that clients are not provided with recommendations as a result of the client having an account with: (a) a separate legal entity of the Dealer Member, (b) a separate business unit of the Dealer Member, or (c) the Dealer Member itself," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8252

questions.stem: Designing its supervision program, the OEO division of Hétu Securities asks what scope of trade review applies, given that no suitability determination is owed. Under clause 3955(1)(iii), the OEO entity must review customer trading and accounts for:

options.A: No concerns at all, because OEO accounts are exempt from Rule 3900
options.B: Only AML and terrorist financing concerns
options.C: Those concerns listed in Rule 3900, other than those relating to the suitability requirements
options.D: Only concerns referred to it by the Market Regulator

questions.rationale: Clause 3955(1)(iii) requires the OEO Dealer Member or business unit to review customer trading and accounts for those concerns listed in Rule 3900, other than those relating to the suitability requirements — manipulative activity, restricted-list trading, margin issues and the like still apply. Option A is the central misconception about OEO: the suitability exemption is not a supervision exemption. B narrows the obligation to one regime; AML compliance is required under 3926(2) but it is not the full review scope. D wrongly makes the review reactive to the regulator.
questions.source_quote: "to review customer trading and accounts for those concerns listed in Rule 3900, other than those relating to the suitability requirements." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8257

questions.stem: Damphousse Direct, an OEO dealer, offers over-the-counter derivatives such as CFDs to retail clients. In addition to providing the derivatives risk disclosure statement, what extra disclosure must accompany the statement for each order execution only account offering over-the-counter derivatives to retail clients?

options.A: A disclosure of the percentage of such accounts that were profitable for each of the four most recent quarters
options.B: A table of the dealer's average bid-ask spreads on CFDs over the past year
options.C: A list of the ten largest CFD losses incurred by clients in the prior quarter
options.D: A statement that CIRO guarantees CFD positions against counterparty default

questions.rationale: Clause 3254(1)(v) requires the Dealer Member to include with the risk disclosure statement, for each order execution only account offering over-the-counter derivatives to retail clients, a disclosure of the percentage of such accounts that were profitable for each of the four most recent quarters. Options B and C describe plausible-sounding cost and loss data the rule does not prescribe — the mandated metric is the quarterly account profitability percentage; D is false and would itself be misleading since no such guarantee exists.
questions.source_quote: "a disclosure of the percentage of such accounts that were profitable for each of the four most recent quarters." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5298

questions.stem: A sophisticated retail client of Grégoire Direct, an approved OEO dealer, wants to connect his own automated order system to generate and send orders to the dealer on a pre-determined basis. Under section 3241, the dealer must:

options.A: Allow it once the client signs an automated-trading risk acknowledgment
options.B: Not allow its order execution only account service clients to use their own automated order system to generate orders to be sent to the Dealer Member or send orders on a pre-determined basis
options.C: Allow it if the client's daily order count stays below 500 orders per trading day
options.D: Allow it only after assigning the client a Legal Entity Identifier

questions.rationale: Clause 3241(1)(ii)(a) prohibits an approved OEO dealer from allowing its OEO service clients to use their own automated order system, as defined in securities laws, to generate orders to be sent to the Dealer Member or to send orders on a pre-determined basis. Option C misuses the 500-orders-per-day daily average figure, which triggers the client identifier requirement under 3241(5), not an automation allowance; D likewise repurposes the LEI identifier mechanics as if they were a permission gate; A is wrong because no acknowledgment can override the prohibition.
questions.source_quote: "use their own automated order system, as defined in securities laws, to generate orders to be sent to the Dealer Member or send orders to the Dealer Member on a pre‐determined basis," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4986

questions.stem: Rondeau Futures Inc. transacts in both exchange-traded options and futures contracts. Under section 3960, how must the firm structure its designated supervision of derivatives activities?

options.A: Appoint a single Supervisor for all derivatives, with no possibility of splitting the role
options.B: Appoint a designated Supervisor for activities involving options contracts or similar derivatives, and a designated Supervisor for activities involving futures contracts, forward contracts, contracts for difference, futures contract options or similar derivatives
options.C: Assign derivatives supervision to the Chief Financial Officer
options.D: Rely on the clearing corporation's surveillance in place of a designated Supervisor

questions.rationale: Subsection 3960(1) requires a Dealer Member that transacts in or advises on derivatives to appoint, where applicable, a designated Supervisor for activities involving options contracts or similar derivatives, and a designated Supervisor for activities involving futures contracts, forward contracts, contracts for difference, futures contract options or similar derivatives — two product streams, each with qualified designated supervision. Option A is wrong because the rule splits the designations by product family (one person may hold both only if qualified for each); C assigns the role to an Executive whose mandate is financial requirements; D outsources a non-delegable dealer responsibility.
questions.source_quote: "appoint a designated Supervisor to supervise its activities that involve futures contracts, forward contracts, contracts for difference, futures contract options or similar derivatives." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8281

questions.stem: Under subsection 3960(4), in which two situations must an alternate Supervisor assume all or part of a designated derivatives Supervisor's responsibilities at Lessard Derivatives Corp.?

options.A: When the designated Supervisor is absent or unable to carry out their duties, or when the Dealer Member's trading activity requires additional qualified individuals to supervise its derivatives-related activities
options.B: When CIRO conducts an examination, or when a client complaint is received
