options.A: Destroyed after 30 days to protect client privacy
options.B: Controlled and investigated by a person independent of the sales function, with a record of all investigations and their results maintained
options.C: Re-mailed by the RR personally rather than the assistant
options.D: Forwarded to CIRO for address verification

questions.rationale: Subsection 3219(3) sets the minimum: returned mail procedures must include a rule requiring control and investigation by a person independent of the sales function — who may be located within a business location — and a rule requiring that a record of all investigations and their results be maintained. Letting the RR's team control returned mail destroys the independence that detects fictitious addresses, the same exposure syllabus 9.3 flags via poor control of client address changes. Option A destroys evidence, option C worsens the independence failure, and option D invents a CIRO role.
questions.source_quote: "a rule requiring the control and investigation by a person independent of the sales function," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4897

questions.stem: An anonymous tip alleges that an RR at Greywater Capital's Kelowna branch has been selling clients an illiquid mortgage-pool product while branch supervision files show no record of any review of his accounts for six months. Applying syllabus 9.3, which combination of retail distribution risks should drive the Supervisor's investigation plan?

options.A: Margin-management failure plus advertising deficiencies
options.B: Improper account reassignment plus client base risk
options.C: Sale of complex or opaque products, including illiquid products, plus the lack of documented evidence of overall supervision
options.D: Commission conflicts plus trade-error volume

questions.rationale: Two listed 9.3 risks intersect: the sale of complex or opaque products by individual registrants, including illiquid or alternative products, and failure to meet regulatory standards for supervision and control through the lack of documented evidence of overall supervision. The illiquid mortgage pool engages the first; the six-month documentation void engages the second and breaches 3927(2). Option A misreads the facts — no margin or advertising element is present. Option B requires a terminated registrant or client misuse, neither present. Option D requires compensation-cycle or error evidence, also absent.
questions.source_quote: "including illiquid or alternative products" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1644

questions.stem: An RR at Dunbarton Securities tells his Supervisor that a retiree client "basically gets" the leveraged ETF pair he has been day-trading in her account, though her notes say she wanted "savings that don't jump around." The product passed firm-level due diligence. Applying syllabus 9.3, the Supervisor's best characterization is:

options.A: No risk — firm-level product approval covers individual sales
options.B: Client base risk — the client is misusing the branch
options.C: A retail distribution risk: a complex product not understood by the retail investor, sold by an individual registrant, requiring suitability scrutiny of the activity
options.D: A trade-name violation

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.3 separately flags new or complex products that are not understood by retail investors and the sale of complex products by individual registrants — distribution risk lives at the point of sale even when the product itself passed firm due diligence, and the mismatch with the client's stated wishes demands suitability scrutiny. Option A repeats the error 9.3 is designed to prevent: approval at the shelf level does not validate each sale. Option B blames the client for the registrant's conduct. Option D is irrelevant to the facts.
questions.source_quote: "New or complex products that are not understood by retail investors" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1655

questions.stem: Velden Securities' compensation committee proposes paying branch RRs a 25% bonus on commissions from a new in-house alternative fund during its launch quarter. As the Supervisor consulted on distribution controls, which response best applies syllabus 9.3?

options.A: Approve without conditions, since bonuses are a management prerogative
options.B: Flag that the program layers a commission-based compensation conflict onto the sale of a complex or alternative product, and require enhanced supervision of the fund's sales before launch
options.C: Refuse because RRs may never be paid commissions
options.D: Require only that the bonus be disclosed to CIRO after the quarter ends

questions.rationale: Two 9.3 risks compound: commission-based compensation programs that may present conflicts of interest between the economic interests of the clients and an Approved Person, and the sale of complex or opaque products including alternative products. A launch bonus on an in-house alternative fund stacks the incentive precisely where product risk is highest, so supervision of those sales must be enhanced pre-launch. Option A ignores the Supervisor's control role. Option C overshoots — commissions are permitted; the issue is managing the conflict. Option D substitutes after-the-fact disclosure for supervision, and no such CIRO filing is prescribed.
questions.source_quote: "conflicts of interest between the economic interests of the" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1660

questions.stem: After RR Priya Shenoy resigns from Almer Ridge Securities, her accounts must be reassigned. To avoid the 9.3 risk of improper reassignment of terminated registrants' accounts, which approach should the branch Supervisor take?

options.A: Distribute accounts based solely on which remaining RR asks first
options.B: Assign the entire book to the branch's newest IR to build their experience
options.C: Freeze every account for one year
options.D: Reassign with regard to the receiving RRs' qualifications and capacity, review the book for issues before transfer, and ensure clients are appropriately informed of the change

questions.rationale: Improper reassignment terminated registrants' accounts appears on the syllabus 9.3 risk list; a proper reassignment matches accounts to qualified, available registrants, screens the departing book for problems, and keeps clients informed — which also serves the 3905(5) proficiency principle for those now handling the accounts. Option A ignores qualification and capacity entirely. Option C punishes clients with a needless freeze that no rule requires. Option B repeats the classic failure: loading an inexperienced registrant with a complex book.
questions.source_quote: "Improper reassignment terminated registrants’ accounts" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1658

questions.stem: A retiring couple at Glaston Securities' Thunder Bay branch holds a margin account their RR has been using to leverage dividend stocks. Their statements show the account drifting near margin limits monthly, and the RR resolves each shortfall by selling small positions without discussing the leverage with the clients. Which supervisory application best addresses the syllabus 9.3 margin-management risk here?

options.A: No action — shortfalls are being cured, so the requirement is met
options.B: Instruct the RR to stop sending margin calls to avoid alarming the clients
options.C: Convert the account to institutional status to remove margin rules
options.D: Review whether the ongoing leverage remains appropriate for the clients and whether the branch is identifying and escalating the recurring near-limit activity as its policies and procedures require

questions.rationale: Failure to meet requirements for managing the margin activities of clients is a listed 9.3 risk, and section 3946 requires policies and procedures for identifying, dealing with and informing the appropriate Supervisors of trading while the account is under margined; a chronic near-limit pattern in a retirement context demands review of the strategy's continued appropriateness, not just mechanical cures. Option A mistakes symptom management for supervision. Option C is impossible — client classification follows the definition, not convenience. Option B suppresses required client communication and worsens the control failure.
questions.source_quote: "• Failure to meet requirements for managing the margin activities of" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1650

questions.scenario: Aurora Crest Securities, an Investment Dealer headquartered in Winnipeg, operates 17 business locations across the Prairies, including the home office of RR Tomas Reyes in Brandon, from which he carries on regular and ongoing advising activity and keeps client files. Head office is building its annual business-location oversight cycle. The compliance team has drafted: (i) a statement of which locations and activities the program covers; (ii) an audit work program with training modules for the three head-office staff who will visit locations; (iii) a risk-scoring model that weights each location using registrant counts, product lines sold there, complaint and discipline history, staffing depth, agent-versus-employee structure, and client-base AML flags; and (iv) a reporting template that records findings, assigns owners and tracks corrective action to closure. The Brandon location has no resident Supervisor; an outside Supervisor in Winnipeg is assigned to it. Last cycle's audit of the Regina branch found stale trade-blotter reviews, and the new cycle must confirm what happened to that finding.
questions.stem: Reyes argues his Brandon home office is "just a home office" and should fall outside item (i)'s coverage statement. Under the IDPC Rules and the 9.1 scope component, is he correct?

options.A: Yes — residences can never be business locations
options.B: Yes — only locations with a resident Supervisor are business locations
options.C: No — a residence is a business location where regular and ongoing activity requiring registration or approval is carried out from it or related records are kept there, so it belongs within the program's scope
options.D: No — but only because Reyes is an RR rather than an IR

questions.rationale: The Rule 1201 definition includes a residence if regular and ongoing activity that requires registration or approval is carried out from the residence or if records relating to such activity are kept at the residence — Reyes does both, so the 9.1 scope component must capture his home office. Option A states the opposite of the definition. Option B confuses the branch-office-member concept in Rule 2200 Part D (Supervisor normally present) with the business-location definition. Option D invents a category distinction; the definition turns on activity and records, not approval category.
questions.source_quote: "relating to an activity that requires registration or approval are kept" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 236

questions.scenario: Aurora Crest Securities, an Investment Dealer headquartered in Winnipeg, operates 17 business locations across the Prairies, including the home office of RR Tomas Reyes in Brandon, from which he carries on regular and ongoing advising activity and keeps client files. Head office is building its annual business-location oversight cycle. The compliance team has drafted: (i) a statement of which locations and activities the program covers; (ii) an audit work program with training modules for the three head-office staff who will visit locations; (iii) a risk-scoring model that weights each location using registrant counts, product lines sold there, complaint and discipline history, staffing depth, agent-versus-employee structure, and client-base AML flags; and (iv) a reporting template that records findings, assigns owners and tracks corrective action to closure. The Brandon location has no resident Supervisor; an outside Supervisor in Winnipeg is assigned to it. Last cycle's audit of the Regina branch found stale trade-blotter reviews, and the new cycle must confirm what happened to that finding.
questions.stem: Mapping Aurora Crest's drafted items (i) through (iv) to the syllabus 9.1 components, which single guideline component is NOT yet represented by a drafted item, considering that the risk-scoring model in item (iii) covers risk identification?

options.A: Scope
options.B: Planning
options.C: Business location audit program and training
options.D: Audit report and follow up

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.1 lists five components: scope, planning, business location audit program and training, risk identification, and audit report and follow up. Item (i) is scope, item (ii) is the audit program and training, item (iii) is risk identification, and item (iv) is the audit report and follow up. No drafted item yet sets the schedule, sequencing and resource allocation for the cycle — that is planning. Options A, C and D each name a component already covered by a drafted item.
questions.source_quote: "• Audit report and follow up" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1617

questions.scenario: Aurora Crest Securities, an Investment Dealer headquartered in Winnipeg, operates 17 business locations across the Prairies, including the home office of RR Tomas Reyes in Brandon, from which he carries on regular and ongoing advising activity and keeps client files. Head office is building its annual business-location oversight cycle. The compliance team has drafted: (i) a statement of which locations and activities the program covers; (ii) an audit work program with training modules for the three head-office staff who will visit locations; (iii) a risk-scoring model that weights each location using registrant counts, product lines sold there, complaint and discipline history, staffing depth, agent-versus-employee structure, and client-base AML flags; and (iv) a reporting template that records findings, assigns owners and tracks corrective action to closure. The Brandon location has no resident Supervisor; an outside Supervisor in Winnipeg is assigned to it. Last cycle's audit of the Regina branch found stale trade-blotter reviews, and the new cycle must confirm what happened to that finding.
questions.stem: Within item (iii)'s risk-scoring model, how do the inputs "staffing depth" and "agent-versus-employee structure" map onto the syllabus 9.2 categories?

options.A: Both are internal risk factors — adequacy of resources and principal-agent respectively
options.B: Both are reputational risk factors
options.C: Staffing depth is client base risk; agent structure is size risk
options.D: Staffing depth is business activity risk; agent structure is reputational risk

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2's internal risk factors are adequacy of resources, key person reliance, and principal-agent. Staffing depth measures whether the location's resources are adequate; agent-versus-employee structure is the principal-agent factor. Option B is wrong — reputational risk covers community marketing, outside activities, discipline and complaints, none of which these inputs measure. Option C misplaces both: thin staffing is not about client misuse and structure is not a sizing metric. Option D misplaces both as well — neither input concerns products sold or conduct signals.
questions.source_quote: "- Adequacy of resources" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1622

questions.scenario: Aurora Crest Securities, an Investment Dealer headquartered in Winnipeg, operates 17 business locations across the Prairies, including the home office of RR Tomas Reyes in Brandon, from which he carries on regular and ongoing advising activity and keeps client files. Head office is building its annual business-location oversight cycle. The compliance team has drafted: (i) a statement of which locations and activities the program covers; (ii) an audit work program with training modules for the three head-office staff who will visit locations; (iii) a risk-scoring model that weights each location using registrant counts, product lines sold there, complaint and discipline history, staffing depth, agent-versus-employee structure, and client-base AML flags; and (iv) a reporting template that records findings, assigns owners and tracks corrective action to closure. The Brandon location has no resident Supervisor; an outside Supervisor in Winnipeg is assigned to it. Last cycle's audit of the Regina branch found stale trade-blotter reviews, and the new cycle must confirm what happened to that finding.
questions.stem: For the Brandon location with its assigned outside Supervisor in Winnipeg, what does CIRO's guidance expect of that supervisory arrangement?

options.A: The outside Supervisor may rely entirely on Reyes's self-attestations
options.B: The location must convert to employee status before any supervision applies
options.C: The outside Supervisor's only duty is approving Reyes's vacation requests
options.D: The outside Supervisor should visit the location periodically to review business conduct and compliance with applicable laws, and the dealer's policies and procedures should specifically address those visits

questions.rationale: The guidance states that business locations without Resident Supervisors should have an assigned outside Supervisor who should visit the location periodically to review business conduct and compliance with applicable laws, with the dealer's policies and procedures specifically addressing these outside Supervisor visits. Option A guts the requirement — self-attestation is not periodic on-site review of business conduct. Option C trivializes the supervisory function. Option B invents a conversion condition; supervision obligations apply regardless of the registrant's employment or agency arrangement.
questions.source_quote: "Supervisor. The outside Supervisor should visit the location periodically to review" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 170

questions.scenario: Saint-Laurent Capitale Inc., an Investment Dealer based in Montreal, runs a retail branch in Trois-Rivières with nine RRs and a producing branch manager, Chantal Aubin, who is the location's designated Supervisor. A head-office distribution-risk review of the branch surfaces four findings: (1) RR Marc Belisle has placed 35 retail households into a non-prospectus flow-through limited partnership with no secondary market, and his quarterly commissions doubled during the campaign; (2) the branch's client-file sample shows seven address changes processed on Belisle's emailed instructions alone, with no client verification, three of which redirected statements away from spouses on joint accounts; (3) the branch's supervision binder shows daily blotter reviews initialled only sporadically, with whole weeks blank, and last year's head-office audit letter directing fixes to this exact gap sits unanswered in the file; (4) Aubin approves her own client trades and no other Supervisor reviews her producing activity.
questions.stem: Finding (1) — concentrated placement of an illiquid non-prospectus partnership alongside doubled commissions — engages which pair of syllabus 9.3 retail distribution risks?

options.A: Improper reassignment of terminated registrants' accounts, and margin-management failure
options.B: The sale of complex or opaque products including illiquid or alternative products, and commission-based compensation conflicts between client and Approved Person economic interests
options.C: Poor control of client address changes, and lack of documented supervision
options.D: Client base risk and risk-weighting

questions.rationale: Two 9.3 risks combine in finding (1): the sale of complex or opaque products by individual registrants, including illiquid or alternative products — a non-prospectus flow-through LP with no secondary market is exactly that — and commission-based compensation programs that may present conflicts of interest between the economic interests of the clients and an Approved Person, evidenced by the doubled payout during the campaign. Option A names risks absent from these facts. Option C describes findings (2) and (3), not (1). Option D imports 9.2 location factors instead of 9.3 distribution risks.
questions.source_quote: "including illiquid or alternative products" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1644

questions.scenario: Saint-Laurent Capitale Inc., an Investment Dealer based in Montreal, runs a retail branch in Trois-Rivières with nine RRs and a producing branch manager, Chantal Aubin, who is the location's designated Supervisor. A head-office distribution-risk review of the branch surfaces four findings: (1) RR Marc Belisle has placed 35 retail households into a non-prospectus flow-through limited partnership with no secondary market, and his quarterly commissions doubled during the campaign; (2) the branch's client-file sample shows seven address changes processed on Belisle's emailed instructions alone, with no client verification, three of which redirected statements away from spouses on joint accounts; (3) the branch's supervision binder shows daily blotter reviews initialled only sporadically, with whole weeks blank, and last year's head-office audit letter directing fixes to this exact gap sits unanswered in the file; (4) Aubin approves her own client trades and no other Supervisor reviews her producing activity.
questions.stem: Regarding finding (2), what is the most defensible immediate supervisory response, applying syllabus 9.3 and the related IDPC control requirements?

options.A: Wait for the next annual audit to re-test the sample
options.B: Accept the practice if Belisle certifies the emails were genuine
options.C: Treat it as the flagged books-and-records risk: suspend RR-initiated address changes pending client verification, confirm each redirected address directly with the affected clients, and enforce controls for accessing and amending client records
options.D: Reclassify the three joint accounts as institutional to simplify review

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.3 directs particular attention to poor control of client address changes, and subsection 3926(5) requires policies and procedures to include controls for accessing and amending client records. Address changes on a registrant's sole say-so — especially ones diverting statements from joint holders — are a concealment red flag demanding immediate verification with the clients themselves and a control fix, not deferral. Option A leaves a live diversion channel open for a year. Option B lets the conflicted party validate his own changes. Option D is impossible — retail joint households cannot be reclassified institutional to dodge review.
questions.source_quote: "poor control of client address changes" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1646

questions.scenario: Saint-Laurent Capitale Inc., an Investment Dealer based in Montreal, runs a retail branch in Trois-Rivières with nine RRs and a producing branch manager, Chantal Aubin, who is the location's designated Supervisor. A head-office distribution-risk review of the branch surfaces four findings: (1) RR Marc Belisle has placed 35 retail households into a non-prospectus flow-through limited partnership with no secondary market, and his quarterly commissions doubled during the campaign; (2) the branch's client-file sample shows seven address changes processed on Belisle's emailed instructions alone, with no client verification, three of which redirected statements away from spouses on joint accounts; (3) the branch's supervision binder shows daily blotter reviews initialled only sporadically, with whole weeks blank, and last year's head-office audit letter directing fixes to this exact gap sits unanswered in the file; (4) Aubin approves her own client trades and no other Supervisor reviews her producing activity.
questions.stem: Finding (3) — sporadic review evidence plus last year's unanswered audit letter on the same gap — combines which two syllabus 9.3 retail distribution risks?

options.A: Margin-management failure and improper account reassignment
options.B: Complex-product sales and client base risk
options.C: Lack of documented evidence of overall supervision, and inadequate follow-up procedures to ensure compliance audit findings are properly tracked and corrective action is taken by a business location
options.D: Commission conflicts and community-specific marketing

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.3 lists failure to meet regulatory standards for supervision and control including the lack of documented evidence of overall supervision, and separately lists inadequate follow-up procedures to ensure that compliance audit findings are properly tracked and corrective action is taken by a business location. The blank weeks evidence the first; the unanswered audit letter on the identical gap evidences the second, compounding it — under 3927(2) completed reviews must be evidenced. Option A names risks not in the facts. Option B mixes a 9.3 risk with a 9.2 factor, neither matching finding (3). Option D names finding (1)'s conflict and a 9.2 reputational factor.
questions.source_quote: "findings are properly tracked and corrective action is taken by a" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1653

questions.scenario: Saint-Laurent Capitale Inc., an Investment Dealer based in Montreal, runs a retail branch in Trois-Rivières with nine RRs and a producing branch manager, Chantal Aubin, who is the location's designated Supervisor. A head-office distribution-risk review of the branch surfaces four findings: (1) RR Marc Belisle has placed 35 retail households into a non-prospectus flow-through limited partnership with no secondary market, and his quarterly commissions doubled during the campaign; (2) the branch's client-file sample shows seven address changes processed on Belisle's emailed instructions alone, with no client verification, three of which redirected statements away from spouses on joint accounts; (3) the branch's supervision binder shows daily blotter reviews initialled only sporadically, with whole weeks blank, and last year's head-office audit letter directing fixes to this exact gap sits unanswered in the file; (4) Aubin approves her own client trades and no other Supervisor reviews her producing activity.
questions.stem: To remediate finding (4) — Aubin approving her own client trades with no other review — what does CIRO's guidance indicate the dealer should ensure?

options.A: Nothing; producing Supervisors are exempt from review of their own client activity
options.B: That Aubin forfeit her clients to head office permanently
options.C: That Aubin's trades be reviewed only by her own sales assistant
options.D: That where a Supervisor is dually-registered and also advises or trades for their own clients, another Supervisor reviews that Supervisor's advice to and trades for clients

questions.rationale: The guidance states dealers should ensure that where a Supervisor is dually-registered and also advises or trades for their own clients, another Supervisor reviews that Supervisor's advice to and trades for clients — self-review is no review, and second-tier criteria in the guidance similarly capture producing branch managers' client accounts. Option A asserts an exemption that does not exist. Option C assigns review to a subordinate non-Supervisor, failing the independent-Supervisor expectation. Option B is an extreme remedy nothing in the guidance requires; the fix is independent review, not confiscation of the book.
questions.source_quote: "trades for their own clients, another Supervisor reviews that Supervisor’s advice to and" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 149

questions.stem: A designated Supervisor at Birchmount Capital is asked to approve a new retail account the same afternoon it is opened. The identity-verification documentation is on file, but the documentation supporting the account appropriateness assessment has not yet been completed. May the Supervisor approve the account?

options.A: Yes, provided the missing documentation is obtained within 25 business days of opening
options.B: Yes, because only the account application itself must be on file before approval
options.C: No, because the designated Supervisor must not approve a new account unless all client account records have been collected
options.D: No, unless the firm escalates the approval to its Chief Compliance Officer

questions.rationale: Subsection 3214(2) prohibits the designated Supervisor from approving a new account unless all client account records have been collected, and appropriateness-assessment documentation is a client account record under section 3210. Option A confuses the 25-business-day window in 3213(2)(iii), which governs follow-up action on outstanding documents, not the precondition for the Supervisor's approval itself.
questions.source_quote: "The designated Supervisor must not approve a new account unless all client account records have been collected." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4641-4642

questions.stem: Kelso Securities maintains an account in which one of its own Registered Representatives has an indirect financial interest beyond the commissions she earns. Under the IDPC Rule 1201 definitions, how is this account categorized?

options.A: As an institutional account, because an industry professional holds the interest
options.B: As a restricted account that must be closed within 30 days
options.C: As a discretionary account, because the Representative can influence its trading
options.D: As a non-client account, because an Approved Person has a direct or indirect interest other than the commission charged

questions.rationale: Rule 1201 defines non-client accounts as accounts in which the Dealer Member or an Approved Person has a direct or indirect interest other than the commission charged, which is exactly this situation. Option A is wrong because institutional-client status turns on the holder's qualification under the institutional client definition, not on industry employment of an interested Approved Person.
questions.source_quote: "Person has a direct or indirect interest other than the commission charged." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 666-667

questions.stem: Veridian Forwarding Ltd., a non-individual logistics company, wants its fuel-price derivatives account classified as institutional on the basis that it is a hedger. In the hedger determination, which condition relating to the market-value behaviour of the proposed derivatives positions must be satisfied?

options.A: There are reasonable grounds to believe market value changes in the positions will completely or materially offset market value changes in the position being hedged
options.B: The derivatives positions must be guaranteed to produce a net profit over the life of the hedge
options.C: The market value of the derivatives positions must always move in the same direction as the hedged exposure
options.D: The derivatives positions must be limited to exchange-listed contracts cleared in Canada

questions.rationale: The Rule 1201 hedger definition requires reasonable grounds to believe that market value changes in the positions resulting from the transactions will completely or materially offset market value changes in the underlying interest or position being hedged. Option C inverts the relationship: a hedge offsets the exposure, so the positions move opposite to the hedged interest, not with it.
questions.source_quote: "there are reasonable grounds to believe that the market value changes in the positions resulting from the transactions will completely or materially offset market value changes in the underlying interest or position being hedged" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 445-448

questions.stem: Galbraith Mining Supply Co., a hedger, requests and consents to being classified as an institutional client of Ostrander Investments. Under the Rule 1201 definition of institutional client, how far does this classification extend?

options.A: To every account Galbraith holds at the firm, including its speculative equity account
options.B: Only to accounts with qualifying hedging activities and hedge positions
options.C: To all accounts of Galbraith and its affiliates at any Dealer Member
options.D: To no accounts until Galbraith's assets under management exceed $10 million

questions.rationale: Clause (vii) of the institutional client definition covers a hedger who requests and consents to being classified as an institutional client for accounts with qualifying hedging activities and hedge positions — the classification is account-scoped, not entity-wide. Option D wrongly imports the $10 million threshold from clauses (v) and (vi); the hedger route in clause (vii) has no asset-size condition.
questions.source_quote: "a hedger who requests and consents to being classified as an institutional client for accounts with qualifying hedging activities and hedge positions" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 488-490

questions.stem: Mapleford Securities serves Tilbury Endowment Corp., a non-individual institutional client that is also a permitted client under NI 31-103 and is not a Dealer Member, regulated entity, exempt market dealer, portfolio manager, bank, trust company or insurance company. For Tilbury's transaction-level suitability protections under subsections 3403(1) and 3403(2) to cease applying, what must occur?

options.A: Tilbury must waive those protections in writing
options.B: Tilbury must verbally confirm its sophistication on a recorded line
options.C: Mapleford's board must pass a resolution exempting the account
options.D: Nothing — every institutional client is automatically exempt from section 3403

questions.rationale: Subsection 3404(3) exempts an account held by a non-individual institutional client that is also a permitted client, is not in the listed financial-entity category, and has waived, in writing, the protections under subsections 3403(1) and 3403(2). Option D is wrong because institutional status alone does not exempt the client; absent the written waiver or listed-entity status, the section 3403 determinations still apply.
questions.source_quote: "has waived, in writing, the protections offered to them under subsections 3403(1) and 3403(2)" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5656-5657

questions.stem: Northgale Clearing Corp. acts solely as a carrying broker for accounts introduced by another Dealer Member. With respect to those carried accounts, what is Northgale's obligation under the account appropriateness rule in section 3211?

options.A: It must perform the full appropriateness determination before each account is opened
options.B: It must perform only the scope-of-products portion of the determination
options.C: None — subsection 3211(1) does not apply to an account maintained at a Dealer Member who is a carrying broker for that account
options.D: It must repeat the introducing dealer's determination annually

questions.rationale: Subsection 3211(3) provides that the whole of subsection 3211(1) does not apply to an account maintained at a Dealer Member who is a carrying broker for that account or who only provides trade execution, clearing, settlement or custody services. Option B describes the narrower OEO/DEA relief in 3211(2), which removes only clause 3211(1)(ii) — a different exemption with a different scope.
questions.source_quote: "an account maintained at a Dealer Member who is a carrying broker for that account or who only provides trade execution, clearing, settlement or custody services" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4586-4587

questions.stem: Under subsection 3211(1), on what standard must a Dealer Member make the account appropriateness determination before opening an account for a person?

options.A: On a best-efforts basis, prioritizing the dealer's business interests
options.B: On the basis of written confirmation from the person that the account is appropriate
options.C: On a balance of probabilities, weighing the dealer's and person's interests equally
options.D: On a reasonable basis and putting the person's interest first

questions.rationale: Subsection 3211(1) requires the determination to be made on a reasonable basis and putting the person's interest first. Option B is wrong because the determination is the Dealer Member's own obligation; the rule does not let the dealer substitute the client's self-assessment for its determination.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must determine, on a reasonable basis and putting the person's interest first" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4573-4574

questions.stem: Calloway Capital opens an account for a provincial workers' compensation fund it intends to treat as an institutional client. Under section 3212, what specific step must Calloway take with respect to this classification?

options.A: Verify that the client qualifies as an institutional client
options.B: Obtain CIRO's written consent to the classification
options.C: Have the client sign a relationship disclosure acknowledging the classification
options.D: File the classification with the provincial securities regulator within 10 days

questions.rationale: Subsection 3212(2) requires that, for each institutional client, the Dealer Member verify that the client qualifies as an institutional client. Option C is wrong because section 3216 states Dealer Members are not required to provide relationship disclosure to institutional clients, so no such acknowledgment is part of the classification step.
questions.source_quote: "For each institutional client, the Dealer Member must verify that the client qualifies as an institutional client." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4596-4597

questions.stem: A retail client of Renfield Securities has her margin account guaranteed by her brother, and her account also carries a trading authorization in favour of her accountant. Which records must Renfield maintain for this account under section 3220?

options.A: Only the client account records; guarantee and authorization details are optional
options.B: A copy of the brother's most recent tax return and the accountant's professional licence
options.C: The name and address of the account guarantor, and a signed trading authorization from the account holder authorizing the accountant
options.D: A notarized guarantee and a power of attorney registered with the court

questions.rationale: Subsection 3220(1) requires account records to include the name and address of the account guarantor, if applicable, and a signed trading authorization from the account holder authorizing a person other than the account holder to give trading instructions. Option D overstates the formality: the rule requires a signed authorization, not notarization or court registration.
questions.source_quote: "the name and address of the account guarantor, if applicable, and (iii) a signed trading authorization from the account holder" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4903-4904

questions.stem: Silvermane Futures classifies Galway Agri-Processing as a hedger and then as an institutional client. Under the record-keeping rule in section 3804, what must Silvermane's records demonstrate about this process?

options.A: Only that the client signed the derivatives trading agreement
options.B: That the client's hedge positions were profitable in the prior year
options.C: That CIRO pre-approved the hedger classification
options.D: The determination undertaken to classify the client as a hedger and as an institutional client

questions.rationale: Clause 3804(2)(xx) requires records that demonstrate the determination undertaken to classify a client as a hedger and as an institutional client. Option C is wrong because the classification is the Dealer Member's own determination to make and document; CIRO pre-approval is not part of the process.
questions.source_quote: "demonstrate determination undertaken to classify a client as a hedger and as an institutional client" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 6771-6772

questions.stem: Which of the following client-protection items is required content of the relationship disclosure information under subsection 3216(5)?

options.A: A description of the circumstances under which the dealer might disclose information about the client or the client's account to a trusted contact person
options.B: A list of every security on the dealer's restricted list
options.C: The client's most recent credit score and how it was obtained
options.D: The names of the dealer's Executives responsible for wealth management

questions.rationale: Clause 3216(5)(ii)(n) requires a description of the circumstances under which a Dealer Member might disclose information about the client or the client's account to a trusted contact person, and clause (o) similarly requires a general explanation of temporary holds. Option B fails because restricted-list contents are internal trading controls, not prescribed relationship disclosure content.
questions.source_quote: "a description of the circumstances under which a Dealer Member might disclose information about the client or the client's account to a trusted contact person" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4823-4825

questions.stem: Oakhurst Direct, an order execution only dealer, asks whether its retail relationship disclosure must include the description of the suitability-determination process required by sub-clause 3216(5)(ii)(d). Which statement is correct?

options.A: Yes — every retail account requires the full suitability-process description
options.B: No — for order execution only accounts that content is not required, provided disclosure is made in compliance with the requirements in section 3241
options.C: No — OEO dealers are exempt from providing any relationship disclosure at all
options.D: Yes, but only if the client also holds an advisory account elsewhere

questions.rationale: Clause 3216(5)(iii) relieves OEO accounts of the sub-clause 3216(5)(ii)(d) suitability-process content provided disclosure is made in compliance with section 3241. Option C overshoots: relationship disclosure as a whole still applies to retail OEO clients; only the suitability-process description is carved out, conditional on the 3241 disclosures.
questions.source_quote: "For order execution only accounts, the Dealer Member does not have to provide the relationship disclosure information required under sub-clause 3216(5)(ii)(d), provided that disclosure is made in compliance with the requirements in section 3241." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4830-4832

questions.stem: Which idea must the prescribed leverage risk disclosure statement convey to a retail client, in substantially the prescribed words?

options.A: That margin interest rates may change without notice
options.B: That borrowing to invest is suitable only for clients with high risk tolerance
options.C: That the responsibility to repay the loan and pay interest remains the same even if the value of the securities purchased declines
options.D: That the dealer guarantees losses on leveraged purchases will not exceed the borrowed amount

questions.rationale: Subsection 3217(3) prescribes wording stating that if you borrow money to purchase securities, your responsibility to repay the loan and pay interest as required by its terms remains the same even if the value of the securities purchased declines. Option B states a suitability proposition the statement does not make; the prescribed text warns about the fixed repayment obligation, not who leverage suits.
questions.source_quote: "your responsibility to repay the loan and pay interest as required by its terms remains the same even if the value of the securities purchased declines" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4859-4861

questions.stem: Bramwell Securities wants the ability to take securities held in clients' margin accounts and use them to raise financing for the firm. For this to be permissible, what must the margin account agreement contain?

options.A: A description of the Dealer Member's right to raise money on and pledge assets held in the client's account
options.B: Nothing — this right exists by operation of law for all margin accounts
options.C: A separate pledge agreement renewed each calendar year
options.D: A clause transferring legal ownership of all account assets to the dealer

questions.rationale: Clause 3247(2)(iii) requires the margin account agreement to describe, at a minimum, the Dealer Member's right to raise money on and pledge assets held in the client's account. Option B is wrong because section 3247 makes this a required written term of the agreement rather than an automatic statutory right.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member's right to raise money on and pledge assets held in the client's account" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5133

questions.stem: A retail margin client of Lunden Capital notices the firm sweeps the free credit balance from her margin account into its general business. Which margin account agreement clause is the relevant control over this practice?

options.A: The clause describing the client's obligation to pay interest on debit balances
options.B: The clause describing the dealer's right to sell assets to cover short sales
options.C: The clause describing the dealer's obligation to transact in accordance with CIRO requirements
options.D: The clause describing the extent to which the dealer has the right to use free credit balances in the client's account for its own business or to cover debits in the same or other accounts

questions.rationale: Clause 3247(2)(iv) requires the agreement to describe the extent to which the Dealer Member has the right to use free credit balances in the client's account for its own business or to cover debits in the same or other accounts — the precise practice described. Option A concerns what the client owes on debit balances, the opposite side of the account from free credits.
questions.source_quote: "the extent to which the Dealer Member has the right to use free credit balances in the client's account for its own business or to cover debits in the same or other accounts" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5134-5135

questions.stem: A retail client of Pictou Brokers argues his margin account agreement should oblige him only to settle whatever debit remains when he eventually closes the account. Which client obligation must the agreement describe under clause 3247(2)(i)?

options.A: The client's obligation to settle debits only upon account closure
options.B: The client's obligation to pay their indebtedness to the Dealer Member and to maintain adequate margin
options.C: The client's obligation to insure the account against market losses
options.D: The client's obligation to trade a minimum monthly volume

questions.rationale: Clause 3247(2)(i) requires the margin account agreement to describe the client's obligation to pay their indebtedness to the Dealer Member and to maintain adequate margin — an ongoing maintenance obligation, not a closure-day settlement. Option A restates the client's own mistaken position: deferring all payment to closure is inconsistent with the continuous duty to maintain adequate margin.
questions.source_quote: "the client's obligation to pay their indebtedness to the Dealer Member and to maintain adequate margin" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5130-5131

questions.stem: Welland Derivatives Inc. wants the ability to seize funds sitting in a client's separate cash account to meet a margin call in the client's futures account. Under section 3252, how is this addressed in the derivatives trading agreement?

options.A: It cannot be addressed — cross-account application of client property is prohibited outright
options.B: It is permitted only for institutional clients who provide a letter of undertaking
options.C: It requires a fresh power of attorney signed at the time of each margin call
options.D: The agreement must set out the conditions under which the dealer may apply the client's funds, securities or other property in the account or any other accounts of the client to satisfy outstanding debts or margin calls

questions.rationale: Clause 3252(1)(iv) requires the agreement to include the conditions under which the Dealer Member may apply the client's funds, securities or other property in the account or any other accounts of the client to satisfy outstanding debts or margin calls. Option A is wrong because the rule contemplates and regulates the practice through disclosed conditions rather than prohibiting it.
questions.source_quote: "the conditions under which the Dealer Member may apply the client's funds, securities or other property in the account or any other accounts of the client to satisfy outstanding debts or margin calls" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5204-5206

questions.stem: Which pair of Dealer Member OBLIGATIONS must be included in a derivatives trading agreement under clause 3252(1)(v)?

options.A: To execute all client orders within one minute, and to rebate commissions on errors
options.B: To provide information to regulators regarding position limit and exercise limit requirements if required or requested, and to address situations when errors and omissions occur
options.C: To guarantee the client against assignment before expiry, and to waive margin calls during volatile markets
options.D: To match competitor commission rates, and to give the client first access to new derivative products

questions.rationale: Clause 3252(1)(v) lists the dealer's obligations: paragraph (a) covers providing information to regulators regarding position limit, exercise limit requirements and reporting derivative positions or transactions data, and paragraph (c) covers addressing situations when errors and omissions occur. Option C inverts the agreement's actual treatment of assignment, where the client bears the obligation to instruct close-outs before expiry.
questions.source_quote: "provide information to regulators regarding position limit, exercise limit requirements and reporting derivative positions or derivative transactions related data" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5209-5210

questions.stem: Under the client-obligation content of a derivatives trading agreement, with whose requirements must the client agree to comply, in addition to Corporation requirements?

options.A: The requirements of any entity through which the derivative is traded, cleared, or issued, including reporting, position limit and exercise limit requirements
options.B: The requirements of the federal Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions
options.C: Only the dealer's internal credit policies
options.D: The requirements of every foreign regulator in any market the dealer accesses

questions.rationale: Paragraph 3252(1)(viii)(a) requires the client's obligation to comply with Corporation requirements and the requirements of any entity through which the derivative is traded, cleared, or issued, including, without limitation, reporting, position limit and exercise limit requirements. Option D overreaches: the obligation runs to the entities through which the client's derivatives are actually traded, cleared or issued, not to every foreign regulator the dealer can access.
questions.source_quote: "comply with Corporation requirements and the requirements of any entity through which the derivative is traded, cleared, or issued, including, without limitation, reporting, position limit and exercise limit requirements" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5222-5224

questions.stem: Alongside the insider-status acknowledgment, what other acknowledgment must the client give in the derivatives trading agreement under clause 3252(1)(ix)?

options.A: Acknowledgment that derivatives losses are capped at the initial margin deposited
options.B: Acknowledgment that the client has read the Universal Market Integrity Rules
options.C: Acknowledgment that the dealer may trade against the client without consent
options.D: Acknowledgment of receiving the most recent derivatives risk disclosure statement

questions.rationale: Paragraph 3252(1)(ix)(a) requires the client's acknowledgment of receiving the most recent derivatives risk disclosure statement, paired with the paragraph (b) insider-status acknowledgment. Option C contradicts paragraph 3252(1)(v)(b), under which the dealer must obtain client consent before taking the other side of the client's transaction.
questions.source_quote: "the client's acknowledgement of: (a) receiving the most recent derivatives risk disclosure statement" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5229-5230

questions.stem: A retail client of Strathcona Options Corp. holds long index options approaching expiry and assumes the firm will automatically close them out for her. Which prescribed client obligation in the derivatives trading agreement contradicts her assumption?

options.A: The client's obligation to pay commission or other compensation
options.B: The client's obligation to maintain adequate margin collateral
options.C: The client's obligation to instruct the Dealer Member to close out positions before expiry
options.D: The client's obligation to report her insider status

questions.rationale: Paragraph 3252(1)(xi)(d) requires the agreement to set out the client's obligation to instruct the Dealer Member to close out positions before expiry, placing the close-out initiative on the client rather than the firm. Option B addresses collateral adequacy during the life of the position, not who must initiate pre-expiry close-outs.
questions.source_quote: "the client's obligation to instruct the Dealer Member to close out positions before expiry" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5249-5250

questions.stem: For a retail futures account, the derivatives trading agreement at Fenwick Commodities discloses the minimum margin standard the firm requires. The futures exchange prescribes $4,000 per contract, CIRO's requirement works out to $4,500, and Fenwick's house requirement is $5,200. Under clause 3252(1)(xii), what minimum margin must the disclosure reflect?

options.A: $4,000, because the marketplace amount always governs
options.B: $4,500, because CIRO requirements override house policy
options.C: $5,200, because the client must maintain minimum margin that is the greater of the marketplace or clearing house amount, the Corporation's requirements, or the Dealer Member's requirements
options.D: The average of the three amounts, recalculated daily

questions.rationale: Clause 3252(1)(xii) requires disclosure that the Dealer Member requires the client to maintain minimum margin that is the greater of the amount the derivatives marketplace or clearing house prescribes, the Corporation's requirements, or the Dealer Member's requirements — here the $5,200 house figure. Option B wrongly treats CIRO's figure as a ceiling when the rule expressly lets the dealer's own higher requirement govern.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member requires the client to maintain minimum margin that is the greater of: (a) the amount the derivatives marketplace or clearing house prescribes, (b) Corporation's requirements, or (c) the Dealer Member's requirements." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5252-5256

questions.stem: Which retail account at Danforth Investments requires a client cumulative loss limit under subsection 3252(2)?

options.A: A speculative advisory account transacting in contracts for difference
options.B: An advisory account holding only common shares bought without leverage
options.C: An account holding investment-grade bonds to maturity
options.D: A hedging account transacting in currency forwards to offset business exposure

questions.rationale: Subsection 3252(2) applies the cumulative loss limit to accounts whose transactions involve futures contracts, forward contracts, contracts for difference, futures contract options or similar derivatives, or highly-leveraged securities or derivatives, and applies to advisory, discretionary, managed and OEO accounts other than a hedging account. Option D fails because hedging accounts are expressly excluded, even though forwards are an in-scope product.
questions.source_quote: "applies to an account, where the transactions involve futures contracts, forward contracts, contracts for difference, futures contract options or similar derivatives, or highly-leveraged securities or derivatives" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5258-5260

questions.stem: Hartley Voss, a retail client with extensive futures experience, asks Eastvale Futures to accept a letter of undertaking from him instead of signing the firm's derivatives trading agreement. How should the designated Supervisor respond?

options.A: Accept it, since any experienced derivatives client may substitute a letter of undertaking
options.B: Accept it only if the letter is notarized
options.C: Decline, because a letter of undertaking may be obtained instead of a derivatives trading agreement only for accounts where the client is classified as an institutional client
options.D: Decline, because letters of undertaking are prohibited for all derivatives accounts

questions.rationale: Subsection 3253(1) permits a Dealer Member to obtain a letter of undertaking instead of a derivatives trading agreement only for accounts where the client is classified as an institutional client; a retail client cannot use this alternative regardless of experience. Option D overcorrects: the letter of undertaking is a valid alternative, just confined to institutional clients.
questions.source_quote: "Instead of a derivatives trading agreement, a Dealer Member may obtain a letter of undertaking for accounts where the client is classified as an institutional client." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5268-5269

questions.stem: Rosseau Private Counsel onboards 40 managed accounts in one month. Its designated Supervisor proposes signing a single written approval covering the entire monthly intake. Does this satisfy section 3277?

options.A: Yes, provided the batch approval lists every account number
options.B: No — the designated Supervisor must approve each managed account in writing
options.C: Yes, if the Chief Compliance Officer co-signs the batch approval
options.D: No — managed account approvals must come from CIRO, not the Supervisor

questions.rationale: Clause 3277(1)(iv) requires that the designated Supervisor approve each managed account in writing — an account-by-account approval, which a single program-level sign-off does not provide. Option A fails because listing account numbers in one omnibus document still substitutes a batch decision for the required per-account written approval.
questions.source_quote: "the designated Supervisor must approve each managed account in writing" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5416

questions.stem: Which set of client attributes must a managed account agreement describe or refer to under clause 3278(1)(i)?

options.A: The client's personal and financial circumstances, investment knowledge, investment time horizon, investment needs and objectives, and risk profile applicable to the managed account
options.B: The client's employment history, citizenship, and credit rating
options.C: The client's tax residency, banking relationships, and insurance coverage
options.D: Only the client's net worth and annual income

questions.rationale: Clause 3278(1)(i) requires the agreement to describe or refer to the client's personal and financial circumstances, investment knowledge, investment time horizon, investment needs and objectives and risk profile that are applicable to the managed account or accounts. Option D is incomplete: net worth and income are only a slice of personal and financial circumstances and omit knowledge, horizon, objectives and risk profile.
questions.source_quote: "describe or refer to the client's personal and financial circumstances, investment knowledge, investment time horizon, investment needs and objectives and risk profile that are applicable to the managed account or accounts" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5422-5424

questions.stem: A managed account client at Tannhauser Capital wants her agreement to prohibit the Portfolio Manager from buying tobacco issuers. How does section 3278 treat such client-imposed restrictions?

options.A: They are prohibited — managed account discretion cannot be limited by the client
options.B: They may exist only as a verbal side arrangement outside the agreement
options.C: They are mandatory — every managed account agreement must contain at least one client restriction
options.D: The agreement must describe any investment restrictions imposed by the client, where permitted by the Dealer Member

questions.rationale: Clause 3278(1)(ii) requires the managed account agreement to describe any investment restrictions imposed by the client, where permitted by the Dealer Member — restrictions are optional and dealer-permitted, but once imposed must be described in the agreement. Option B contradicts this written-description requirement by relegating the restriction to an undocumented side arrangement.
questions.source_quote: "describe any investment restrictions imposed by the client, where permitted by the Dealer Member" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5425-5426

questions.stem: A Portfolio Manager at Halloran Wealth proposes selling a corporate bond position out of several managed accounts directly to the Halloran Strategic Income Fund — an investment fund for which another responsible person at the firm acts as adviser — at the day's closing market price. Under section 3280, this transaction is:

options.A: Permitted, because the price is the market close
options.B: Permitted with the prior written consent of each managed account client
options.C: Prohibited — a responsible person or Dealer Member must not knowingly cause a managed account to purchase or sell a security from or to an investment fund for which a responsible person acts as an adviser
options.D: Permitted if the fund's independent review committee approves it

questions.rationale: Clause 3280(3)(ii) flatly prohibits knowingly causing a managed account to purchase or sell a security or derivative of a security from or to an investment fund for which a responsible person acts as an adviser; unlike the subsection 3280(2) restrictions, subsection 3280(3) offers no consent cure. Option B wrongly imports the prior-written-consent mechanism from 3280(2), which applies to related-issuer investments, not trades with responsible-person-advised funds.
questions.source_quote: "purchase or sell a security or derivative of a security of an issuer from or to an investment fund for which a responsible person acts as an adviser" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5467-5468

questions.stem: An Associate Portfolio Manager at Brightholm Securities, facing personal cash-flow problems, asks a long-standing managed account client whether the account could lend him $50,000, offering the client's lawyer full documentation and prior written client consent. As designated Supervisor, you should advise that the loan is:

options.A: Acceptable once the client's prior written consent is documented
options.B: Acceptable if the loan bears a market rate of interest
options.C: Prohibited — a managed account must not provide a guarantee or loan to a responsible person or an associate of a responsible person
options.D: Acceptable if disclosed in the firm's next conflicts-of-interest report

questions.rationale: Clause 3280(3)(iii) prohibits a responsible person or Dealer Member from knowingly causing a managed account to provide a guarantee or loan to a responsible person or an associate of a responsible person, and the subsection 3280(3) prohibitions are not curable by client consent. Option A is the closest trap: prior written consent legitimizes only the subsection 3280(2) investments, not subsection 3280(3) dealings.
questions.source_quote: "provide a guarantee or loan to a responsible person or an associate of a responsible person" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5469

questions.stem: A Portfolio Manager at Coverly & Nash receives an allocation of 5,000 shares in an oversubscribed secondary offering suitable for 30 of her managed accounts. She fills the ten accounts with the highest fee rates first and gives the rest nothing. Which managed account requirement does this breach?

options.A: Subsection 3280(1), the prohibition on trading ahead of managed accounts
options.B: Subsection 3281(2), the prohibition on volume-based compensation
options.C: Subsection 3280(4), the requirement to fairly allocate investment opportunities among its managed accounts
options.D: Clause 3278(1)(ii), the requirement to describe client-imposed restrictions

questions.rationale: Subsection 3280(4) requires the Dealer Member to fairly allocate investment opportunities among its managed accounts; rationing a scarce offering by fee rate favours the firm's revenue over fair treatment of all eligible accounts. Option A addresses a different abuse — a responsible person trading their own account on managed-account trade information — not skewed allocation among the managed accounts themselves.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must fairly allocate investment opportunities among its managed accounts." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5470

questions.stem: Avondell Securities is reviewing four fee and compensation arrangements connected to its managed accounts. Under section 3281, which arrangement is prohibited even if a client signs a written agreement permitting it?

options.A: Charging the client directly a fee based on the volume of transactions initiated for the account
options.B: Compensating the firm's Portfolio Manager on the basis of the value or volume of transactions in the account
options.C: Charging the client directly a fee contingent upon the performance of the account
options.D: Charging the client a flat annual fee calculated as a percentage of assets under management

questions.rationale: Subsection 3281(2) flatly states the Dealer Member must not compensate a person referred to in section 3279 — including a Portfolio Manager — on the basis of the value or volume of transactions in the account, and unlike subsection 3281(1) it contains no written-agreement exception. Options A and C describe the two subsection 3281(1) direct charges, which become permissible once the client provides a written agreement setting out the manner in which such fees may be charged.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must not compensate a person referred to in section 3279, on the basis of the value or volume of transactions in the account." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5479-5480

questions.stem: The non-delegation rule in subsection 3970(3) prevents the designated Supervisor for discretionary accounts from delegating which specific reviews?

options.A: The at-least-monthly review of each discretionary account's financial performance, and the review of whether the Registered Representative should continue to be authorized based on that performance
options.B: The daily review of every trade across the entire branch
options.C: The annual review of the firm's discretionary-account policies and procedures
options.D: The pre-approval of each client's account application

questions.rationale: Subsection 3970(3) prohibits delegating the performance of the reviews required in subsections 3970(1) and 3970(2): the at-least-monthly financial-performance review of each discretionary account, and the assessment of whether the Registered Representative authorized to affect trades should continue to do so based on that performance. Option D fails because account approval is a section 3273 function, not one of the two subsections 3970(1)-(2) reviews the non-delegation rule names.
questions.source_quote: "must not delegate the performance of the reviews required in subsections 3970(1) and 3970(2) to any other person" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 8384-8385

questions.stem: Why does clause 3273(1)(iii) require a Dealer Member to identify discretionary accounts in its books and records?

options.A: To allow supervision of the discretionary accounts in accordance with Rule 3900
options.B: To allow CIRO to bill higher membership fees on discretionary assets
options.C: To allow the accounts to be excluded from monthly statements
options.D: To allow automatic renewal of the discretionary agreements

questions.rationale: Clause 3273(1)(iii) requires the Dealer Member to identify discretionary accounts in its books and records to allow supervision of the discretionary accounts in accordance with Rule 3900. Option D contradicts clause 3274(1)(iv), under which a discretionary account agreement must not be renewable at all.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must identify discretionary accounts in its books and records to allow supervision of the discretionary accounts in accordance with Rule 3900" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5357-5358

questions.stem: After the designated Supervisor at Marwick Securities approves a discretionary account and the signed agreement, what does clause 3273(1)(vi) require the firm to do with that approval?

options.A: File it with the client's provincial securities commission
options.B: Destroy it once the 12-month discretionary term expires
options.C: Mail the original approval to the client within five business days
options.D: Maintain a record of the designated Supervisor's approval in accordance with the record retention requirements in section 3803

questions.rationale: Clause 3273(1)(vi) requires the Dealer Member to maintain a record of the designated Supervisor's approval in accordance with the record retention requirements in section 3803, which prescribes a minimum seven-year retention. Option B is wrong because the record must survive well beyond the agreement's 12-month maximum term under the section 3803 retention standard.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must maintain a record of the designated Supervisor's approval in accordance with the record retention requirements in section 3803" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5363-5364

questions.stem: A discretionary account agreement at Ledbury Capital is reaching the end of its term. The client, still travelling abroad, asks the firm simply to renew it for another year. Which response reflects section 3274?

options.A: The agreement may be renewed once, for a maximum of six further months
options.B: The agreement may be renewed indefinitely with fresh Supervisor approval each year
options.C: The agreement must have a maximum term of no longer than 12 months and must not be renewable; continuing the arrangement requires a new agreement
options.D: The agreement converts automatically into a managed account agreement at expiry

questions.rationale: Clauses 3274(1)(iii) and (iv) require a discretionary account agreement to have a maximum term of no longer than 12 months and not be renewable, so the existing agreement cannot simply be extended. Option D is wrong because a managed account is a distinct relationship requiring its own agreement and Portfolio Manager involvement; no automatic conversion exists.
questions.source_quote: "have a maximum term of no longer than 12 months, (iv) not be renewable" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5373-5374

questions.stem: A client with a discretionary account at Verlane Brokers offers, in writing, to have the account guarantee a personal bank loan taken out by the Registered Representative who exercises the account's discretion. Under section 3276, the guarantee is:

options.A: Permitted because the client volunteered it in writing
options.B: Permitted if the guarantee is capped at 10% of account equity
options.C: Prohibited — a responsible person or Dealer Member must not allow a discretionary account to provide a guarantee or loan to a responsible person or an associate of a responsible person
options.D: Permitted if the loan is repaid within the agreement's 12-month term

questions.rationale: Subsection 3276(4) prohibits allowing a discretionary account to provide a guarantee or loan to a responsible person or an associate of a responsible person, with no consent exception. Option A is the closest trap: written client consent cures only the subsection 3276(3) investment restrictions, not the subsection 3276(4) guarantee/loan prohibition.
questions.source_quote: "must not allow a discretionary account to provide a guarantee or loan to a responsible person or an associate of a responsible person" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5400-5401

questions.stem: Petra Lindqvist asks Cobalt Ridge Securities for an options account. Her Registered Representative obtains the completed derivatives account application and signed derivatives trading agreement, and the designated Supervisor records written approval, but no one sends Petra the current derivatives disclosure statement before her first trade is executed. Which section 3251 requirement has been missed?

options.A: Obtaining a completed derivatives account application from the client
options.B: Providing the client with the most recent derivatives disclosure statement or similar disclosure document
options.C: Recording the relevant designated Supervisors' approval in writing
options.D: Determining the client's cumulative loss limit

questions.rationale: Clause 3251(1)(iii) requires that, before executing an initial derivatives transaction, the Dealer Member provide the client with the most recent derivatives disclosure statement or similar disclosure document — the only listed step skipped here. Option D fails on scope: a cumulative loss limit under 3252(2) attaches to futures, forwards, CFDs, futures options or similar/highly-leveraged products, not to an ordinary options account requirement under 3251.
questions.source_quote: "provide the client with the most recent derivatives disclosure statement or similar disclosure document" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5176-5177

questions.stem: Holt Meridian Securities plans to launch derivatives accounts and proposes staffing the desk with Registered Representatives licensed only for equities, with derivatives questions routed to an external consultant. Applying subsection 3250(3), the designated Supervisor should object because:

options.A: Derivatives accounts can only be serviced by Executives
options.B: Derivatives accounts may only be offered through an OEO business unit
options.C: External consultants must always be CIRO arbitrators
options.D: The Dealer Member must ensure that persons trading on its behalf or advising clients in derivatives accounts meet minimum proficiency requirements

questions.rationale: Subsection 3250(3) requires the Dealer Member to ensure that persons trading on its behalf or advising clients in derivatives accounts meet minimum proficiency requirements, which equity-only Representatives advising derivatives clients would not satisfy. Option A invents an Executive-only restriction that does not exist; the rule sets a proficiency standard, not a category exclusivity.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must ensure that persons trading on its behalf or advising clients in derivatives accounts meet minimum proficiency requirements." — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5168-5169

questions.stem: A Registered Representative at Falconbrook Wealth, frustrated by a busy client's slow order confirmations, urges the client to sign a discretionary account agreement so the Representative can trade without calling. The client had never raised the idea. Applying the IDPC definition of a discretionary account, what is the appropriateness problem?

options.A: A discretionary account requires the client to hold institutional status
options.B: A discretionary account exists only where the Dealer Member has not solicited the discretionary authority, and here the Representative solicited it
options.C: Discretionary accounts may not be used by clients who are slow to confirm orders
options.D: The agreement is invalid unless it has a term of at least 24 months

questions.rationale: The Rule 1201 definition of a discretionary account requires that the Dealer Member has not solicited the discretionary authority and that the authority is accepted to accommodate a client who is frequently or temporarily unavailable to authorize trades; a Representative-initiated push for discretion fails the no-solicitation condition. Option D inverts the term limit: the authority's term must not exceed 12 months, with no minimum of 24 months.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member has not solicited the discretionary authority, (ii) the discretionary authority is accepted to accommodate a client who is frequently or temporarily unavailable to authorize trades" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 348-350

questions.stem: Before opening an account at Brightline OEO Trading, applicants see a statement that the firm will not make suitability determinations. Under clause 3241(3)(i), what must this written disclosure specifically confirm the firm will NOT consider?

options.A: The client's preferred trading platform and order types
options.B: The client's history of complaints at other dealers
options.C: The liquidity and volatility of each security the client trades
options.D: The client's personal and financial circumstances, investment needs and objectives, investment knowledge, risk profile, investment time horizon, nor other similar factors

questions.rationale: Paragraph 3241(3)(i)(b) requires a statement confirming the Dealer Member will not be responsible for making a suitability determination and, in particular, will not consider the client's personal and financial circumstances, investment needs and objectives, investment knowledge, risk profile, investment time horizon, nor other similar factors. Option C describes product-level analysis, whereas the prescribed disclosure enumerates the client-level KYC factors the firm disclaims considering.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member will not consider the client's personal and financial circumstances, investment needs and objectives, investment knowledge, risk profile, investment time horizon, nor other similar factors" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5007-5009

questions.stem: Vantor Direct, an OEO Investment Dealer, approves derivatives trading permissions through a fully automated workflow with no manual review. Per the restrictions on derivative account approvals for OEO Investment Dealers in the syllabus, which client request must NOT be cleared through the automated approval?

options.A: Permission to buy protective (long) puts against existing share positions
options.B: Permission to trade options strategies other than low-risk strategies based on long calls, long puts, covered calls or protective (long) puts
options.C: Permission to buy long calls on a broad index
options.D: Permission to write covered calls against fully paid shares

questions.rationale: The syllabus restricts OEO derivative account approvals for options trading strategies other than low-risk strategies based on long calls, long puts, covered calls or protective (long) puts — so anything outside that low-risk list cannot flow through automated approval, alongside OTC derivatives, CFDs and futures strategies. Option D fails because covered calls sit inside the enumerated low-risk strategies and therefore remain eligible for automated approval.
questions.source_quote: "Options trading strategies other than low-risk strategies based on long calls, long puts, covered calls or protective (long) puts" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 1071-1072

questions.stem: A long-time client of Arberry Securities is reassigned to a new Registered Representative after his previous one retires. Under subsection 3215(2), what must Arberry's procedures require of the new Representative and the designated Supervisor?

options.A: The new Representative must verify the client information in the account application with the client as soon as practicable, and both the Representative and the designated Supervisor must acknowledge in writing that the application was reviewed and, if necessary, updated
options.B: The client must complete an entirely new account application within 48 hours
options.C: Only the designated Supervisor must telephone the client to confirm the reassignment
options.D: The account must be frozen until the client visits a branch in person
