questions.rationale: Subsection 3215(2) requires that on a change of Registered Representative the new Representative verify the client information in the account application with the client as soon as practicable, and that the new Representative and the designated Supervisor acknowledge, in writing, that the account application was reviewed and, if necessary, updated. Option B overstates the obligation: the rule requires verification and written acknowledgment of the existing application, not a fresh application on a 48-hour clock.
questions.source_quote: "verify the client information in the account application with the client as soon as practicable to ensure the information is correct" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4668-4669

questions.scenario: Larkspur Derivatives Group, an Investment Dealer, is onboarding two new accounts on the same week. The first is for Camille Roy, a retail dentist who wants to trade equity options and stock-index futures speculatively through an advisory account; her completed derivatives account application is in hand and the firm's standard derivatives trading agreement is being prepared for her signature. The second is for Pemberley Grain Exporters Ltd., a non-individual firm with $14 million of securities under management, which Larkspur has verified qualifies as an institutional client; Pemberley trades currency forwards and proposes to provide a letter of undertaking instead of executing the trading agreement, and its account will carry a debit balance on which Larkspur charges interest. The designated Supervisor, Ines Okafor, reviews both files before any initial transactions are executed.
questions.stem: Camille notices the derivatives trading agreement lets Larkspur deploy the uninvested cash in her account elsewhere. Under clause 3252(1)(iii), what must the agreement state about this practice?

options.A: The extent of the dealer's right to use client free credit balances within its own business or to finance other client account debits
options.B: A guarantee that free credit balances earn the overnight rate
options.C: That free credit balances are held in trust and never used by the dealer
options.D: The dealer's deadlines for a client to submit an exercise notice

questions.rationale: Clause 3252(1)(iii) requires the agreement to disclose the extent of the Dealer Member's right to use client free credit balances within its own business or to finance other client account debits, along with using account assets as collateral and pledging assets. Option C contradicts the rule's premise: the agreement regulates the dealer's disclosed use of free credits rather than mandating trust segregation.
questions.source_quote: "use client free credit balances within its own business or to finance other client account debits" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5200-5201

questions.scenario: Larkspur Derivatives Group, an Investment Dealer, is onboarding two new accounts on the same week. The first is for Camille Roy, a retail dentist who wants to trade equity options and stock-index futures speculatively through an advisory account; her completed derivatives account application is in hand and the firm's standard derivatives trading agreement is being prepared for her signature. The second is for Pemberley Grain Exporters Ltd., a non-individual firm with $14 million of securities under management, which Larkspur has verified qualifies as an institutional client; Pemberley trades currency forwards and proposes to provide a letter of undertaking instead of executing the trading agreement, and its account will carry a debit balance on which Larkspur charges interest. The designated Supervisor, Ines Okafor, reviews both files before any initial transactions are executed.
questions.stem: Because Camille's advisory account will transact in stock-index futures for speculation, what must her derivatives trading agreement include under clause 3252(1)(vii)?

options.A: Nothing extra — loss limits apply only to OEO accounts
options.B: Her cumulative loss limit, subject to the conditions in subsection 3252(2)
options.C: A guarantee from a third party covering futures losses
options.D: A waiver of the firm's margin requirements during her first year

questions.rationale: Clause 3252(1)(vii) requires the agreement to include the client's cumulative loss limit subject to subsection 3252(2), which captures her speculative futures account: the limit applies to futures-trading accounts other than hedging accounts whether the account is an advisory, discretionary, managed or OEO account. Option A misreads 3252(2)(ii) — the provision lists advisory accounts alongside OEO accounts, so advisory status provides no exclusion.
questions.source_quote: "applies to an account, other than a hedging account, whether the account is an advisory account, a discretionary account, a managed account or an order execution only account" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5261-5262

questions.scenario: Larkspur Derivatives Group, an Investment Dealer, is onboarding two new accounts on the same week. The first is for Camille Roy, a retail dentist who wants to trade equity options and stock-index futures speculatively through an advisory account; her completed derivatives account application is in hand and the firm's standard derivatives trading agreement is being prepared for her signature. The second is for Pemberley Grain Exporters Ltd., a non-individual firm with $14 million of securities under management, which Larkspur has verified qualifies as an institutional client; Pemberley trades currency forwards and proposes to provide a letter of undertaking instead of executing the trading agreement, and its account will carry a debit balance on which Larkspur charges interest. The designated Supervisor, Ines Okafor, reviews both files before any initial transactions are executed.
questions.stem: Ines confirms Pemberley may use a letter of undertaking. Which compliance commitment must the letter itself state under clause 3253(2)(i)?

options.A: That Pemberley appoints Larkspur as agent for all exchange filings
options.B: That Pemberley will maintain account equity above $10 million
options.C: That Pemberley waives the protections of subsections 3403(1) and 3403(2)
options.D: That Pemberley agrees to comply with Corporation requirements, any applicable laws, and the requirements of any entity through which the derivative is traded, cleared, or issued

questions.rationale: Clause 3253(2)(i) requires the letter of undertaking to state that the client agrees to comply with Corporation requirements, any applicable laws, and the requirements of any entity through which the derivative is traded, cleared, or issued, including position limit, exercise limit and reporting requirements. Option C confuses the letter's prescribed content with the separate written waiver under clause 3404(3)(ii) by which certain permitted clients opt out of institutional suitability protections.
questions.source_quote: "that the client agrees to comply with Corporation requirements, any applicable laws, and the requirements of any entity through which the derivative is traded, cleared, or issued" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5271-5272

questions.scenario: Larkspur Derivatives Group, an Investment Dealer, is onboarding two new accounts on the same week. The first is for Camille Roy, a retail dentist who wants to trade equity options and stock-index futures speculatively through an advisory account; her completed derivatives account application is in hand and the firm's standard derivatives trading agreement is being prepared for her signature. The second is for Pemberley Grain Exporters Ltd., a non-individual firm with $14 million of securities under management, which Larkspur has verified qualifies as an institutional client; Pemberley trades currency forwards and proposes to provide a letter of undertaking instead of executing the trading agreement, and its account will carry a debit balance on which Larkspur charges interest. The designated Supervisor, Ines Okafor, reviews both files before any initial transactions are executed.
questions.stem: Larkspur accepts Pemberley's first forward order. Pemberley is not exempt from the institutional suitability determination. Under subsection 3403(1), when must a suitability determination be made for Pemberley?

options.A: Only at account opening, and never again
options.B: Before any order is accepted from the client, and before a recommendation is made to the client
options.C: Within 30 days after each quarter-end
options.D: Only when Pemberley's account loses more than 10% in a month

questions.rationale: Subsection 3403(1) requires a suitability determination for an institutional client before any order is accepted from the client and before a recommendation is made to the client to purchase, sell, exchange or hold a security or transact in any derivative, subject to the section 3404 exemptions. Option A wrongly converts a transaction-by-transaction trigger into a one-time account-opening event.
questions.source_quote: "a suitability determination must be made for an institutional client: (i) before any order is accepted from the client" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5594-5596

questions.scenario: Wrenfield & Doyle Securities runs a discretionary and managed account program. Sofia Petrakis, the designated Supervisor responsible for both account types, is handling three matters this week. First, Naveen Chandra, a retired surgeon, is opening a managed account; the firm's Portfolio Manager, Lucia Ferraro, has prepared the managed account agreement and the firm's fair-allocation policy document. Second, Lucia — who personally sits on the board of directors of Doyle Industrial Trust, an unrelated listed issuer — wants several existing managed accounts she handles to buy that issuer's preferred shares. Third, Bram Holloway, a client departing on an eight-month research posting in Antarctica, has asked his Registered Representative, Theo Marsh, to exercise discretion over his existing advisory account while he is unreachable; Theo has at least two years of active experience trading the products involved, and a draft discretionary account agreement has been prepared but not yet signed or approved.
questions.stem: Before Naveen's managed account is opened, which document must Wrenfield & Doyle both enter into with him, and which must it provide to him, under section 3277?

options.A: Enter into a managed account agreement with him prior to opening the account, and provide him with a copy of its policy ensuring fair allocation of investment opportunities
options.B: Enter into a letter of undertaking, and provide him with the firm's audited financial statements
options.C: Enter into a discretionary account agreement, and provide him with the firm's restricted list
options.D: Enter into a sub-advisor agreement, and provide him with the Portfolio Manager's compensation schedule

questions.rationale: Clause 3277(1)(iii) requires the Dealer Member to enter into a managed account agreement with the client prior to opening a managed account, and clause 3277(1)(vi) requires providing the client with a copy of its policy ensuring fair allocation of investment opportunities. Option C confuses account types: a discretionary account agreement under section 3274 governs the separate, time-limited discretionary relationship, not a managed account.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must enter into a managed account agreement with the client prior to opening a managed account" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5414-5415

questions.scenario: Wrenfield & Doyle Securities runs a discretionary and managed account program. Sofia Petrakis, the designated Supervisor responsible for both account types, is handling three matters this week. First, Naveen Chandra, a retired surgeon, is opening a managed account; the firm's Portfolio Manager, Lucia Ferraro, has prepared the managed account agreement and the firm's fair-allocation policy document. Second, Lucia — who personally sits on the board of directors of Doyle Industrial Trust, an unrelated listed issuer — wants several existing managed accounts she handles to buy that issuer's preferred shares. Third, Bram Holloway, a client departing on an eight-month research posting in Antarctica, has asked his Registered Representative, Theo Marsh, to exercise discretion over his existing advisory account while he is unreachable; Theo has at least two years of active experience trading the products involved, and a draft discretionary account agreement has been prepared but not yet signed or approved.
questions.stem: Applying subsection 3280(2) to Lucia's proposal that her managed accounts buy preferred shares of Doyle Industrial Trust, the issuer on whose board she sits, Sofia should advise that the accounts may not knowingly make the investment:

options.A: Under any circumstances, because directors can never overlap with managed accounts
options.B: Without the prior written consent of the client, because the individual authorized to deal with managed accounts is a director of the issuer, unless her position with the issuer is disclosed to the client
options.C: Unless Doyle Industrial Trust first lists on a designated exchange
options.D: Unless Lucia resigns from the firm's managed account program

questions.rationale: Clause 3280(2)(ii) prohibits a responsible person or Dealer Member, without the prior written consent of the client, from knowingly allowing a managed account to invest in a security of an issuer if the individual authorized under subsection 3279(1) to deal with managed accounts is an officer or director of the issuer, unless the position with the issuer is disclosed to the client. Option A overstates the rule into an absolute bar; the conflict is manageable through the consent and disclosure mechanism rather than prohibited outright.
questions.source_quote: "invest in a security or derivative of a security of an issuer if the individuals authorized under subsection 3279(1) to deal with managed accounts is an officer or director of the issuer, unless the position with the issuer is disclosed to the client" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5459-5461

questions.scenario: Wrenfield & Doyle Securities runs a discretionary and managed account program. Sofia Petrakis, the designated Supervisor responsible for both account types, is handling three matters this week. First, Naveen Chandra, a retired surgeon, is opening a managed account; the firm's Portfolio Manager, Lucia Ferraro, has prepared the managed account agreement and the firm's fair-allocation policy document. Second, Lucia — who personally sits on the board of directors of Doyle Industrial Trust, an unrelated listed issuer — wants several existing managed accounts she handles to buy that issuer's preferred shares. Third, Bram Holloway, a client departing on an eight-month research posting in Antarctica, has asked his Registered Representative, Theo Marsh, to exercise discretion over his existing advisory account while he is unreachable; Theo has at least two years of active experience trading the products involved, and a draft discretionary account agreement has been prepared but not yet signed or approved.
questions.stem: Eager to help while Bram is at sea, Theo proposes simply exercising discretion over Bram's existing advisory account right away, since the discretionary agreement is drafted anyway. Why must Sofia stop him?

options.A: Advisory accounts convert to discretionary status automatically after 30 days of client unavailability
options.B: Discretion may be exercised once the client leaves the country, regardless of documentation
options.C: Theo needs only verbal approval from a branch manager to begin
options.D: The Dealer Member must ensure individuals trading on its behalf do not engage in any discretionary trading unless discretion is exercised in a discretionary account or managed account in accordance with Part G of Rule 3200

questions.rationale: Subsection 3221(1) prohibits any discretionary trading, including time and price discretion, unless discretion is exercised in a discretionary account or managed account in accordance with the requirements set out in Part G of Rule 3200 — and Part G requires the agreement to be entered into and the account and agreement approved by the designated Supervisor before acceptance. Option A invents an automatic conversion; under clause 3273(1)(iv) and (v) the discretionary relationship arises only through the executed agreement and the designated Supervisor's approval.
questions.source_quote: "do not engage in any discretionary trading, including time and price discretion, unless discretion is exercised in a discretionary account or managed account in accordance with the requirements set out in Part G of Rule 3200" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 4918-4920

questions.scenario: Wrenfield & Doyle Securities runs a discretionary and managed account program. Sofia Petrakis, the designated Supervisor responsible for both account types, is handling three matters this week. First, Naveen Chandra, a retired surgeon, is opening a managed account; the firm's Portfolio Manager, Lucia Ferraro, has prepared the managed account agreement and the firm's fair-allocation policy document. Second, Lucia — who personally sits on the board of directors of Doyle Industrial Trust, an unrelated listed issuer — wants several existing managed accounts she handles to buy that issuer's preferred shares. Third, Bram Holloway, a client departing on an eight-month research posting in Antarctica, has asked his Registered Representative, Theo Marsh, to exercise discretion over his existing advisory account while he is unreachable; Theo has at least two years of active experience trading the products involved, and a draft discretionary account agreement has been prepared but not yet signed or approved.
questions.stem: Theo asks why his two years of experience matter for Bram's discretionary account. Under subsection 3275(1), a Registered Representative may only be authorized to affect trades for a discretionary account if:

options.A: The Representative is also approved as a Portfolio Manager
options.B: The Representative has at least two years of active experience in trading, advising or performing analysis with respect to all types of products to be traded on a discretionary basis, and the account is maintained at the Dealer Member on whose behalf the Representative conducts business
options.C: The Representative has passed the Supervisor Exam within the last 12 months
options.D: The Representative personally guarantees the account against losses

questions.rationale: Subsection 3275(1) imposes two conditions: at least two years of active experience in trading, advising or performing analysis with respect to all types of products to be traded on a discretionary basis, and that the discretionary account is maintained at the Dealer Member on whose behalf the Representative conducts business. Option A confuses discretionary accounts with managed accounts, where section 3279 requires a Portfolio Manager, Associate Portfolio Manager or contracted sub-advisor.
questions.source_quote: "the Registered Representative has at least two years of active experience in trading, advising or performing analysis with respect to all types of products that are to be traded on a discretionary basis" — idpc-rules.txt ~lines 5383-5385

questions.stem: At Chenard Securities, the branch Supervisor is qualified to supervise equity and fixed-income trading but not options trading by the Registered Representatives in her branch. Under CIRO's account supervision guidance, how may the Dealer structure supervision in this situation?

options.A: Divide the supervision between two or more Supervisors, provided there are appropriate mechanisms for them to communicate with one another, the system ensures the Dealer maintains an overall view of the client's situation and activity, and the assignment of responsibilities is clear and complete
options.B: Allow the branch Supervisor to supervise the options trading anyway, since branch-level supervision need not match product qualifications
options.C: Suspend all options trading in the branch until the Supervisor completes the options qualification
options.D: Have the Registered Representatives peer-review one another's options trades

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 s.1.1.2 states that if a Supervisor is unqualified to supervise trading in certain products, the Dealer may divide the supervision between two or more Supervisors provided there are appropriate mechanisms for them to communicate, the system ensures an overall view of the client's situation and activity, and the assignment of responsibilities is clear and complete; the Dealer may alternatively appoint a primary Supervisor advised by others. Option B contradicts the qualification requirement in 3905(5) that Supervisors understand the products those under their supervision trade; C is a business shutdown the guidance does not require because a structural fix exists; D substitutes unqualified peer review for designated supervision.
questions.source_quote: "a Dealer may divide the supervision between two or more Supervisors, provided: there are appropriate mechanisms for them to communicate with one another," — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 134

questions.stem: Jutras Wealth is building a real-time order screening system and wants to reduce its post-trade review workload. Under CIRO's account supervision guidance, on what basis may a Dealer conduct its account activity reviews, and what effect can a strong front-end process have?

options.A: Reviews must always be post-trade; pre-trade screening cannot count toward supervision
options.B: Reviews may be conducted on a pre-trade or post-trade basis, and a robust pre-trade review process may lessen or remove the need for post-trade reviews
options.C: Pre-trade reviews are permitted only for derivatives accounts
options.D: Pre-trade screening eliminates the requirement to document supervisory reviews

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 s.1.2 states Dealers may conduct their reviews on a pre-trade or post-trade basis and that a robust pre-trade review process may lessen or remove the need for post-trade reviews — the rules are structure- and technology-neutral. Option A is the opposite of the guidance; C invents a product limitation that does not exist; D is wrong because subsection 3927(2) still requires recording and keeping evidence of completed supervisory reviews regardless of when they occur.
questions.source_quote: "Dealers may conduct their reviews on a pre-trade or post-trade basis. A robust pre- trade review process may lessen or remove the need for post-trade reviews." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 182

questions.stem: A new Supervisor at Ostiguy Capital is handed the firm's trade-review queue on her first day but receives no documentation of what she is expected to do. Which subsection 3926(4) obligation has the Dealer Member failed?

options.A: The obligation to file its supervisory procedures with CIRO before assigning reviews
options.B: The obligation to obtain the Supervisor's signed indemnity before she begins reviews
options.C: The obligation to provide all supervisory staff with written procedures to be followed in reviewing account activity and written confirmation of the Dealer Member's expectations of supervisory staff with respect to their supervisory roles and responsibilities
options.D: The obligation to have the board of directors interview each new Supervisor

questions.rationale: Subsection 3926(4) requires the Dealer Member to provide all supervisory staff with written procedures to be followed in reviewing account activity and written confirmation of the Dealer Member's expectations of supervisory staff with respect to their supervisory roles and responsibilities. Option A is wrong because CIRO does not pre-approve or receive filings of dealer P&Ps; B invents an indemnity requirement; D overstates board involvement — the board's role is the supervision system generally, not interviewing individual Supervisors.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must provide all supervisory staff with written: (i) procedures to be followed in reviewing account activity, and (ii) confirmation of the Dealer Member's expectations of supervisory staff" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8107

questions.stem: Brodeur Securities wrote its head office and branch account supervision policies five years ago and has not revisited them since, although securities laws have changed twice. Which subsection 3926(6) obligation does this neglect?

options.A: The obligation to periodically review the policies and procedures used at its head office and its branch offices to provide reasonable assurance they continue to be effective and reflect current legislative and regulatory requirements, as well as industry practices
options.B: The obligation to replace all policies and procedures every three years regardless of changes
options.C: The obligation to submit policy updates to the managed account committee for ratification
options.D: The obligation to obtain client consent before amending supervision policies

questions.rationale: Subsection 3926(6) requires the Dealer Member to periodically review the policies and procedures used at its head office and its branch offices to provide reasonable assurance they continue to be effective and reflect current legislative and regulatory requirements, as well as industry practices — a five-year freeze through two law changes fails that. Option B invents a fixed replacement cycle the rule does not prescribe; C misuses the managed account committee, whose annual review covers managed-account supervision policies only; D is wrong because clients have no consent role over a dealer's internal supervision policies.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must periodically review the policies and procedures used at its head office and its branch offices to provide reasonable assurance the policies and procedures continue to be effective" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8113

questions.stem: Soulière Investments hires two Registered Representatives in the same month: Priya, newly approved with no prior registration history, and Daniel, an experienced transferee whom CIRO sanctioned last year for unauthorized trading. The Chief Compliance Officer asks which enhanced-supervision regime applies to each. Which pairing is correct?

options.A: Priya: close supervision for six months after approval under section 3947, as set out in the Registered Representative / Investment Representative Monthly Supervision Report; Daniel: additional supervisory measures under subsection 3927(3) for Approved Persons with a history of regulatory infractions or questionable conduct
options.B: Both fall only under section 3947's six-month close supervision, which covers all newly hired Registered Representatives
options.C: Priya: additional supervisory measures under subsection 3927(3); Daniel: no enhanced supervision, because CIRO already sanctioned him
options.D: Neither requires enhanced supervision provided the branch has a Resident Supervisor

questions.rationale: Subsection 3947(1) requires close supervision of Registered Representatives dealing with retail clients for six months after approval, documented in the Monthly Supervision Report — but subsection 3947(2) lifts that for transferees previously approved for six months or more at an SRO-member firm, so Daniel's trigger is not newness; it is subsection 3927(3), which requires procedures implementing additional supervisory measures for Approved Persons with a history of regulatory infractions or questionable conduct. Option B misses both the 3947(2) transfer exemption and Daniel's infraction-driven regime; C swaps the regimes; D is wrong because a Resident Supervisor is a structural feature, not a substitute for either mandated enhanced regime.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must closely supervise Registered Representatives and Investment Representatives dealing with retail clients for six months after approval, as set out in the Registered Representative / Investment Representative Monthly Supervision Report." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8197

questions.stem: A trader at Pagé Capital Markets, a Participant, received a caution letter from the Market Regulator last year for questionable order entry. Under UMIR Policy 7.1, what effect should this history have on the firm's supervision of marketplace trading?

options.A: None, because caution letters are informal and carry no supervisory consequence
options.B: The firm may reduce review frequency since the regulator is already monitoring the trader
options.C: It may warrant the implementation of more frequent supervision or compliance testing and more detailed supervision or compliance procedures
options.D: The trader's orders must be routed exclusively through another Participant

questions.rationale: UMIR Policy 7.1 Part 2 states that previous disciplinary proceedings, warning and caution letters from the Market Regulator, or identified supervision problems may warrant the implementation of more frequent supervision or compliance testing and more detailed supervision or compliance procedures — Participants must exceed the Policy's baseline elements where circumstances warrant. Option A understates caution letters, which the Policy expressly lists; B inverts the response — regulator attention increases, not decreases, the firm's own obligation; D is not a Policy 7.1 measure and would not satisfy the supervision obligation.
questions.source_quote: "the identification of problems with the supervision system or procedures by the Participant or the Market Regulator may warrant the implementation of more frequent supervision or compliance testing" — umir.txt ~line 6732

questions.stem: Therrien Direct routes client orders to marketplaces through an order execution service with no employee involvement. Under UMIR Policy 7.1, how should this absence of staff intermediation be reflected in the Participant's compliance testing of orders?

options.A: Each order must be manually approved by a Supervisor before entry, eliminating sampling
options.B: Orders through an order execution service are exempt from compliance testing because the client, not the Participant, entered them
options.C: Compliance testing may be reduced because the absence of employees removes the risk of employee misconduct
options.D: It may be appropriate to sample for compliance testing a higher percentage of orders entered by a client through an order execution service than the percentage of orders sampled in other circumstances

questions.rationale: UMIR Policy 7.1 Part 1 states the Participant retains responsibility for orders entered by direct electronic access, under a routing arrangement or through an order execution service, and that it may be appropriate to sample a higher percentage of such orders for compliance testing than orders entered in other circumstances — the limited staff involvement increases, not decreases, the post-entry risk. Option B is the central misconception: the Participant retains responsibility for those orders; C inverts the risk logic — the absent intermediary is itself the risk; A would convert the channel into an intermediated one, defeating the order execution service model, and is not what the Policy requires.
questions.source_quote: "it may be appropriate for the Participant to sample for compliance testing a higher percentage of orders that have been entered by a client under direct electronic access," — umir.txt ~line 6687

questions.stem: A Registered Representative at Sabourin Wealth wants to freeze a withdrawal from an elderly client's account because a new acquaintance is pressuring the client for money. Under subsection 3222(1), before placing a temporary hold on the basis of financial exploitation, what must the Dealer Member reasonably believe?

options.A: Only that the client is over 60 years of age
options.B: That a family member has complained in writing about the acquaintance
options.C: That the client is a vulnerable client, and that financial exploitation of the client has occurred, is occurring, has been attempted or will be attempted
options.D: That the police have opened an investigation into the acquaintance

questions.rationale: Subsection 3222(1) prohibits a temporary hold on the basis of financial exploitation unless the Dealer Member reasonably believes both that the client is a vulnerable client and that financial exploitation of the client has occurred, is occurring, has been attempted or will be attempted — a two-element reasonable-belief test. Option A reduces vulnerability to an age threshold the rule does not use; B and D make the hold contingent on third-party complaints or police action, neither of which is required — the standard is the Dealer Member's own reasonable belief.
questions.source_quote: "must not place a temporary hold on the basis of financial exploitation of a vulnerable client, unless the Dealer Member reasonably believes all of the following: (i) the client is a vulnerable client," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4925

questions.stem: Immediately after placing a temporary hold on a vulnerable client's withdrawal, the compliance team at Doiron Securities asks what the firm's first obligations are under subsection 3222(3). Which set is correct?

options.A: Notify CIRO, freeze all of the client's accounts at affiliated entities, and appoint a guardian
options.B: Document the facts and reasons for placing the hold, provide notice of the hold and its reasons to the client as soon as possible, and review the relevant facts as soon as possible and on a reasonably frequent basis to determine if continuing the hold is appropriate
options.C: Obtain a court order within five business days and suspend the Registered Representative pending review
options.D: Transfer the held funds to a trust account controlled by the client's trusted contact person

questions.rationale: Subsection 3222(3) requires the Dealer Member to document the facts and reasons that caused it to place (and if applicable continue) the hold, provide notice of the hold and the reasons to the client as soon as possible after placing it, and review the relevant facts as soon as possible and on a reasonably frequent basis to determine if continuing the hold is appropriate, plus the recurring 30-day revoke-or-notify cycle. Option A is wrong — there is no CIRO notification, affiliate freeze or guardianship in the rule; C invents a court order and an employment action; D is wrong because a trusted contact person is a resource for inquiries, not a recipient of the client's funds.
questions.source_quote: "(i) document the facts and reasons that caused the Dealer Member or Approved Person to place, and if applicable, to continue the temporary hold, (ii) provide notice of the temporary hold and the reasons for the temporary hold to the client" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4941

questions.stem: Designing second-tier daily review criteria, the compliance team at Filion Securities proposes capturing low-priced speculative stock activity. Which example criterion from CIRO's account supervision guidance targets exactly that activity?

options.A: Bond trades over $100,000 value per trade
options.B: Accounts of clients charged more than $3,000 in commission during the month
options.C: Stock trades with a value over $5,000 and a price under $5.00 per share
options.D: All non-client accounts with any activity other than dividend and interest payments

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 s.2.2.1 lists stock trades with a value over $5,000 and a price under $5.00 per share among example second-tier daily review criteria — the size-plus-low-price combination flags meaningful positions in speculative low-priced issues. Option A is also a genuine second-tier daily criterion but targets large bond trades, not low-priced stock; B is a second-tier monthly selection criterion, the wrong cycle; D is the first-tier monthly inclusion rule for non-client accounts, not a second-tier daily criterion.
questions.source_quote: "stock trades with a value over $5,000 and a price under $5.00 per share," — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 279

questions.stem: A monthly review at Vanasse Capital finds a retail client whose account, and two related accounts, now hold 85% of their combined value in a single junior mining issuer after a series of purchases. Which detection item in subsection 3945(2) does this most directly engage?

options.A: Account number changes
options.B: Excessive or improper crosses of securities between clients
options.C: Undisclosed short sales
options.D: Undue concentration of securities, derivatives or precious metals bullion in a single account or across accounts

questions.rationale: Clause 3945(2)(ii) requires retail supervision policies to address detecting undue concentration of securities, derivatives or precious metals bullion in a single account or across accounts — the rule expressly extends to concentration built across related accounts, which is the fact here. Option B involves transfers between different clients' accounts, not accumulation by one client; C concerns short positions, absent here; A is an administrative red flag for unauthorized trading, unrelated to position concentration.
questions.source_quote: "undue concentration of securities, derivatives or precious metals bullion in a single account or across accounts," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8146

questions.stem: Allaire Securities wants to use a risk-based approach to select retail account activity for post-trade review and to set the review period, rather than reviewing everything. Under CIRO's account supervision guidance, this approach is acceptable provided:

options.A: The board of directors signs off on each month's sample
options.B: The Dealer obtains written client consent to the reduced review frequency
options.C: Selected accounts are limited to those opened in the past twelve months
options.D: The reviewer has all relevant information they need to assess the risk

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 s.1.2 states Dealers may use a risk-based approach to select activity for post-trade review, provided the reviewer has all relevant information they need to assess the risk, and may use the same approach to determine the review period. Option A overstates governance — board sign-off on monthly samples is not required; C invents an account-age restriction; B is wrong because supervision design is the Dealer's obligation and is not subject to client consent.
questions.source_quote: "Dealers may use a risk-based approach to select the activity for post-trade review, provided the reviewer has all relevant information they need to assess the risk." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 197

questions.stem: Designing the first-tier monthly review at Crevier Wealth, the Supervisor needs a starting selection of retail client accounts. Which selection example does CIRO's account supervision guidance give as a design that would detect improper account activity?

options.A: Reviewing the activity of all clients charged gross commissions of $1,500 or more for that month
options.B: Reviewing every account alphabetically from A to F each month on a six-month rotation
options.C: Reviewing only accounts that filed a complaint during the month
options.D: Reviewing accounts of clients charged more than $3,000 in commission during the month

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 s.2.1.2 gives, as an example of a first-tier monthly selection designed to detect improper account activity, reviewing the activity of all clients charged gross commissions of $1,500 or more for that month. Option D is the closest distractor — the $3,000 commission criterion is the guidance's second-tier monthly example, not the first-tier one; B is rotation without risk design, which does not target improper activity; C is reactive only and would miss activity no one has complained about.
questions.source_quote: "a Dealer could review the activity of all clients charged gross commissions of $1,500 or more for that month." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 248

questions.stem: Goyette Capital Markets, a Participant, proposes that the traders' desk head also run the compliance monitoring program that tests the desk's own trading. Under UMIR Policy 7.1, why does this design fail?

options.A: Desk heads may not hold any compliance qualifications
options.B: The compliance function must be conducted by persons other than those who supervise the trading activity
options.C: Compliance monitoring may only be performed by an external audit firm
options.D: The Market Regulator must approve each individual appointed to compliance

questions.rationale: UMIR Policy 7.1 Part 2 requires every Participant to designate individuals responsible for supervision and compliance, and states the compliance function must be conducted by persons other than those who supervise the trading activity — the same independence principle that drives cross-supervision: no one checks their own work. Option A is wrong — there is no prohibition on a desk head being qualified, only on combining the roles; C outsources what may be done internally by independent persons; D invents regulator approval of individual compliance staff.
questions.source_quote: "The compliance function must be conducted by persons other than those who supervise the trading activity." — umir.txt ~line 6751

questions.stem: Painchaud Securities is writing its hold-mail account procedures for retail clients. Under subsection 3219(1), which three provisions must those procedures include at a minimum?

options.A: Verbal client authorization, a 90-day limit, and quarterly review by the branch receptionist
options.B: Written authorization from the client to hold mail, a limit on the length of time a hold-mail order may remain in force of no longer than six months in any 12-month period, and control and regular review of hold-mail accounts by a Supervisor
options.C: A client fee for the service, delivery of held mail to the Registered Representative, and annual renewal
options.D: CIRO notification of each hold, a 30-day limit, and review by the Chief Financial Officer

questions.rationale: Subsection 3219(1) requires, at a minimum, a requirement that the Dealer Member obtain written authorization from the client to hold mail, a limit on the length of time a hold-mail order may remain in force of no longer than six months in any 12-month period, and a rule requiring the control and regular review of hold-mail accounts by a Supervisor. Option A fails on all three elements — authorization must be written, the period limit is six months in 12, and review must be by a Supervisor; C routes held mail to the salesperson, the very concealment risk the rule controls; D invents CIRO notification and the wrong reviewer and period.
questions.source_quote: "a requirement that limits the length of time that a "hold mail" order may remain in force for no longer than six months, in any 12 month period, and (iii) a rule requiring the control and regular review of "hold mail" accounts by a Supervisor." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4883

questions.stem: The institutional surveillance team at Ducharme Capital, a Participant, is calibrating its review of orders for artificial-price concerns. According to UMIR Policy 7.1, at which times are orders intended to or that affect an artificial price more likely to appear?

options.A: At the open of each trading day, when liquidity is deepest
options.B: During the mid-day trading lull, when spreads are widest
options.C: On days when the issuer releases routine press releases
options.D: At the end of a month, quarter or year, or on the date of the expiry of options where the underlying interest is a listed security

questions.rationale: UMIR Policy 7.1 Part 5 states that orders which are intended to or which affect an artificial price are more likely to appear at the end of a month, quarter or year, or on the date of the expiry of options where the underlying interest is a listed security — period-end marks and option expiries create the incentive to paint prices, and supervision must concentrate there. The Policy also notes artificial-price activity is more likely to involve securities or derivatives with limited liquidity, which defeats A's deep-liquidity timing; B and C describe ordinary market conditions the Policy does not flag.
questions.source_quote: "orders which are intended to or which affect an artificial price are more likely to appear at the end of a month, quarter or year or on the date of the expiry of options" — umir.txt ~line 6890

questions.stem: How frequently, at a minimum, must Jodoin Securities, a Participant, review a sample of its trading for manipulative and deceptive activities under UMIR Policy 7.1?

options.A: At least monthly
options.B: At least annually
options.C: At least on a quarterly basis
options.D: Only when an alert is generated by its surveillance system

questions.rationale: UMIR Policy 7.1 Part 5 requires each Participant to review a sample of its trading for manipulative and deceptive activities at least on a quarterly basis. Option A overstates the minimum — monthly applies to other obligations such as trade-through testing under Part 6; B understates it — annual is the cadence for the supervision system review and board reporting under Part 2; D makes the review purely alert-driven, which is not a minimum periodic sample.
questions.source_quote: "Each Participant must review a sample of its trading for manipulative and deceptive activities at least on a quarterly basis." — umir.txt ~line 6912

questions.stem: A junior trader at Provencher Markets, a Participant, notices that a colleague's principal-account orders appear designed to set an artificial closing price in a thin stock. Under UMIR 10.16, what must the trader do?

options.A: Forthwith report to their supervisor or the compliance department of the Participant upon becoming aware of the activity they believe may be a violation of Rule 2.2 respecting manipulative and deceptive activities
options.B: Report directly to the Market Regulator within 15 days, bypassing the firm
options.C: Confront the colleague and document the conversation; no report is needed if the colleague stops
options.D: Wait for quarter-end compliance testing to catch the pattern

questions.rationale: Subsection 10.16(1) requires an officer, director, partner or employee of a Participant to forthwith report to their supervisor or the compliance department upon becoming aware of activity in a principal, non-client or client account that they believe may be a violation of enumerated provisions including Rule 2.2 on manipulative and deceptive activities. Option B confuses the employee's internal report with the firm's later obligation — it is the supervisor or compliance department that reports investigation findings of a violation to the Market Regulator by the 15th day of the following month under 10.16(4)(d); C substitutes self-help for the mandatory report; D defers a forthwith obligation to a periodic cycle.
questions.source_quote: "shall forthwith report to their supervisor or the compliance department of the Participant upon becoming aware of activity in a principal account, non-client account or client account" — umir.txt ~line 10552

questions.stem: Compliance at Lanthier Capital, a Participant, completes a gatekeeper investigation under UMIR 10.16 and concludes that a violation of UMIR did occur. By when must the findings be reported to the Market Regulator, and how long must the records of the report, review and findings be retained?

options.A: Report within 48 hours; retain records for two years
options.B: Report not later than the 15th day of the month following the month in which the findings are made; retain the records for a period of not less than seven years from the creation of the record
options.C: Report at the next annual filing; retain records for five years
options.D: No report is required if the client relationship is terminated; retain records for one year

questions.rationale: Clause 10.16(4)(d) requires the supervisor or compliance department to report the findings to the Market Regulator if the investigation finds a violation of an applicable UMIR provision occurred, not later than the 15th day of the month following the month in which the findings are made, and subsection 10.16(5) requires the records of the report, review and findings to be retained for not less than seven years from creation, available to the Market Regulator for inspection. Option C's five years is the Policy 7.1 Part 2 retention for general compliance review results, a different record set; A and D invent timelines and a termination exception that do not exist.
questions.source_quote: "such report shall be made not later than the 15th day of the month following the month in which the findings are made." — umir.txt ~line 10603

questions.stem: Magloire Capital, a Participant, wants to grant direct electronic access to a hedge fund. Under subsection 7.13(1) of UMIR, before granting the access the Participant must have done all of the following EXCEPT:

options.A: Established standards reasonably designed to manage, in accordance with prudent business practices, the Participant's risks associated with providing direct electronic access
options.B: Assessed and documented that the client meets the standards established by the Participant
options.C: Executed a written agreement with the client
options.D: Obtained the Market Regulator's prior written approval of the client

questions.rationale: Clause 7.13(1)(a) conditions granting direct electronic access on three things: established standards reasonably designed to manage the Participant's risks in accordance with prudent business practices, an assessment and documentation that the client meets those standards, and an executed written agreement with the client. There is no Market Regulator pre-approval of DEA clients — the only regulator notification in 7.13(6) concerns clients not eligible for a Legal Entity Identifier. Options A, B and C are each verbatim conditions, making D the exception.
questions.source_quote: "(ii) assessed and documented that the client, investment dealer or foreign dealer equivalent meets the standards established by the Participant, and (iii) executed a written agreement with the client," — umir.txt ~line 8798

questions.stem: A registered dealer asks Quintal Securities, a Participant, to provide it with direct electronic access to marketplaces. Under clause 7.13(1)(b) of UMIR, the Participant:

options.A: May grant the access if the dealer signs the standard DEA agreement
options.B: May grant the access only if the dealer posts additional collateral
options.C: Must not grant direct electronic access if the client is acting and registered as a dealer in accordance with applicable securities legislation
options.D: Must obtain a marketplace exemption before granting the access

questions.rationale: Clause 7.13(1)(b) prohibits a Participant from granting direct electronic access if the client is acting and registered as a dealer in accordance with applicable securities legislation — third-party electronic access for dealers is structured as a routing arrangement, a separate channel with its own conditions, not DEA. Options A and B suggest documentation or collateral can cure the prohibition, which is categorical; D invents a marketplace exemption process that does not exist in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "not grant direct electronic access if the client is acting and registered as a dealer in accordance with applicable securities legislation." — umir.txt ~line 8803

questions.stem: A retail investor with a large personal portfolio asks Verreault Capital, a Participant, for direct electronic access. Under UMIR Policy 7.1, what is the Market Regulator's expectation regarding non-institutional investors and DEA?

options.A: Non-institutional investors qualify automatically if they complete an online proficiency quiz
options.B: Non-institutional investors will be precluded from qualifying for direct electronic access except in exceptional circumstances generally limited to sophisticated former traders and floor brokers, or a person having assets under administration with a value approaching that of an institutional investor with access to and knowledge of the necessary technology
options.C: Non-institutional investors may receive DEA on the same standards as institutional investors
options.D: Non-institutional investors may receive DEA only through an order execution only dealer

questions.rationale: UMIR Policy 7.1 Part 9 states the Market Regulator expects that as part of the initial screening, non-institutional investors will be precluded from qualifying for direct electronic access except in exceptional circumstances generally limited to sophisticated former traders and floor brokers or a person or company having assets under administration approaching institutional value with access to and knowledge of the necessary technology — and that standards for non-institutional clients must be set higher than for institutional investors. Option C states the opposite of the higher-standard requirement; A trivializes the screening; D conflates DEA with the order execution only channel, which has its own rules and prohibits client automated order systems under IDPC 3241.
questions.source_quote: "non-institutional investors will be precluded from qualifying for direct electronic access except in exceptional circumstances generally limited to sophisticated former traders and floor brokers" — umir.txt ~line 7031

questions.stem: Before opening an order execution only account at Ravary Direct, an approved OEO dealer, which written disclosures must the client receive, and what must the dealer obtain in return, under subsection 3241(3)?

options.A: A risk-tolerance questionnaire, with the dealer retaining the completed scoring sheet
options.B: The derivatives risk disclosure statement, regardless of whether the account will trade derivatives
options.C: Only the relationship disclosure document, with a signature line for the client
options.D: Statements confirming the dealer will not provide any recommendations, will not be responsible for making a suitability determination, and will not be responsible for determining that the products and account types offered are appropriate for the client — plus a positive acknowledgement from the client and each beneficial owner that they received and understood the disclosures

questions.rationale: Subsection 3241(3) requires the dealer, before opening an OEO account, to provide written statements confirming it will not provide any recommendations and the client is solely responsible for investment decisions, will not be responsible for making a suitability determination (other than as required by clauses 3402(3)(i) and 3403(4)(i)), and will not be responsible for determining that the products and account types offered are appropriate for the client — and to obtain a positive acknowledgement from the client and each beneficial owner confirming receipt and understanding. Option A substitutes a KYC tool the OEO regime does not use for suitability purposes; C omits the three OEO-specific statements and the beneficial-owner acknowledgement; B misapplies the derivatives risk disclosure statement, which is required for derivatives clients under 3254, not every OEO account.
questions.source_quote: "obtain a positive acknowledgement from the client, and each beneficial owner of the account, confirming that the client, and each beneficial owner, has received and understood the disclosures described in clause 3241(3)(i)." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5014

questions.stem: An active retail client of Sansoucy Direct, an approved OEO dealer, has manually entered an average of 620 orders per trading day this calendar month on Marketplaces for which CIRO is the regulation services provider. Under subsection 3241(5), what must the dealer ensure?

options.A: The client's account is converted to a direct electronic access account
options.B: The client's order flow is throttled to 500 orders per day
options.C: A client identifier is assigned to the client, because their trading activity exceeds a daily average of 500 orders per trading day in a calendar month
options.D: The client signs an institutional-client waiver of suitability protections

questions.rationale: Subsection 3241(5) requires the Dealer Member to ensure a client identifier is assigned to each client whose trading activity on Marketplaces for which the Corporation is the regulation services provider exceeds a daily average of 500 orders per trading day in any calendar month — under 3241(8) the identifier is a Legal Entity Identifier for eligible clients or otherwise an account number. Option B misreads the threshold as a cap — the separate order-count restriction in clause 3241(1)(ii)(b) concerns thresholds set by the Corporation on manual order volume, and 500 is an identifier trigger, not a throttle level; A confuses OEO with DEA, distinct channels; D imports the institutional waiver mechanism from 3404, which has nothing to do with order counts.
questions.source_quote: "whose trading activity on Marketplaces for which the Corporation is the regulation services provider exceeds a daily average of 500 orders per trading day in any calendar month." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5029

questions.stem: Bellefeuille Direct operates its OEO services as a separate business unit of an Investment Dealer. Beyond review policies, what do subsections 3955(3) and 3955(4) require of the unit with respect to evidencing and resourcing its supervision?

options.A: Maintain an audit trail of all supervisory reviews as required in Rule 3900, and have sufficient supervisory resources allocated at head office and branch levels to effectively implement the required supervisory procedures
options.B: Submit monthly supervisory summaries to the Market Regulator and staff a 24-hour review desk
options.C: Retain only exception reports that triggered an inquiry, and rely on head office resources exclusively
options.D: Outsource trade review to the carrying broker and certify the arrangement annually

questions.rationale: Subsection 3955(3) requires the Dealer Member or separate business unit to maintain an audit trail of all supervisory reviews as required in Rule 3900, and subsection 3955(4) requires sufficient supervisory resources allocated at head office and branch levels to effectively implement the supervisory procedures required under section 3955. Option C fails both limbs — the audit trail covers all supervisory reviews, not just escalated ones, and resources must exist at both head office and branch levels; B invents regulator filings and a 24-hour desk; D substitutes outsourcing for the unit's own resource obligation.
questions.source_quote: "must have sufficient supervisory resources allocated at head office and branch levels to effectively implement the supervisory procedures required under section 3955." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8271

questions.stem: Under section 3961, what are the two responsibilities of the designated Supervisors for derivatives accounts at Archambault Futures?

options.A: Setting margin rates and negotiating clearing fees
options.B: Approving new derivatives accounts, and ensuring that the handling of clients' derivatives account trading complies with Corporation requirements
options.C: Executing client orders and reconciling exchange positions
options.D: Drafting the derivatives risk disclosure statement and filing it with CIRO

questions.rationale: Subsection 3961(1) makes the designated Supervisors responsible for approving new derivatives accounts and for ensuring that the handling of clients' derivatives account trading complies with Corporation requirements — the gateway function plus the ongoing-conduct function. Option A describes margin and cost functions belonging to financial and operations roles; C describes execution and operations tasks, not supervision; D is wrong because the derivatives risk disclosure statement is a Corporation-approved document the dealer provides under 3254, not one each designated Supervisor drafts and files.
questions.source_quote: "(i) approving new derivatives accounts, and (ii) ensuring that the handling of clients' derivatives account trading complies with Corporation requirements." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8293

questions.stem: Désormeaux Derivatives Corp. plans to name a single options-qualified Supervisor with no alternates, reasoning that its futures volume is low. Under subsections 3960(2) and 3960(3), which two requirements challenge this plan?

options.A: Alternates are required only for firms with more than 100 derivatives accounts, and only the Chief Compliance Officer may approve them
options.B: The Supervisor must be an Executive, and futures supervision must always be outsourced
options.C: The Supervisor must hold a seat on the derivatives marketplace, and alternates must be CIRO employees
options.D: The designated Supervisors must have the qualifications and experience required to supervise the Dealer Member's derivatives activities, and the Dealer Member must appoint one or more alternate Supervisors if necessary to ensure continuous supervision of its derivatives activities

questions.rationale: Subsection 3960(2) requires the designated Supervisors to have the qualifications and experience required to supervise the Dealer Member's derivatives activities — an options-qualified person does not cover futures-stream activities — and subsection 3960(3) requires the Dealer Member to appoint one or more alternate Supervisors if necessary to ensure continuous supervision of its derivatives activities. Option A invents an account-count threshold and an approval gate that do not exist; B and C attach Executive status, outsourcing, marketplace seats and CIRO employment to roles the rule defines by qualification and continuity alone.
questions.source_quote: "The Dealer Member must appoint one or more alternate Supervisors if necessary to ensure continuous supervision of its derivatives activities." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8285

questions.stem: Sirois Futures designates several retail derivatives accounts as discretionary accounts and managed accounts. Under subsection 3962(3), how must the designated Supervisor review these particular derivatives accounts?

options.A: On a quarterly sampling basis using risk-based selection criteria
options.B: Annually, as part of the managed account committee's policy review
options.C: Only when an exception report flags loss-limit or margin breaches
options.D: On a daily and monthly basis, the designated Supervisor must review all derivatives accounts that are designated as discretionary accounts and managed accounts

questions.rationale: Subsection 3962(3) requires the designated Supervisor, on a daily and monthly basis, to review all derivatives accounts designated as discretionary accounts and managed accounts — full coverage, not sampling, because discretion concentrates risk in the dealer's hands. Option A applies the risk-based selection permitted for other derivatives accounts under 3963(2), not these; C is reactive only and does not meet a daily-and-monthly full-review standard; B confuses the committee's annual policy review under 3972 with account-level review.
questions.source_quote: "On a daily and monthly basis, the designated Supervisor must review all derivatives accounts that are designated as discretionary accounts and managed accounts." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8314

questions.stem: A retail client of Labrosse Commodities writes uncovered calls on a volatile index, and separately holds long futures contracts that are about to enter their delivery month. Which pair of detection items under subsection 3963(1) do these two facts respectively engage?

options.A: Excessive intra-day transactions; transacting beyond credit limits
options.B: Speculative transactions in hedging accounts; transacting while under-margined
options.C: Exposures arising out of uncovered option positions; exposures to delivery obligations through the holding of futures contracts, forward contracts and similar derivatives into the delivery month
options.D: Transactions in derivatives whose underlying interest is on the restricted list; exceeding derivative position or exercise limits

questions.rationale: Clause 3963(1)(x) requires detection of exposures arising out of uncovered option positions — the naked calls — and clause 3963(1)(xi) requires detection of exposures to delivery obligations through the holding of futures contracts, forward contracts and similar derivatives into the delivery month — the long futures approaching delivery. Options A, B and D each pair other genuine 3963(1) items, but nothing in the facts shows trade frequency, a hedging-account classification, a margin deficiency, restricted-list involvement or limit breaches; the tested skill is mapping facts to the correct list items.
questions.source_quote: "(x) exposures arising out of uncovered option positions, and (xi) exposures to delivery obligations through the holding of futures contracts, forward contracts and similar derivatives into the delivery month." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8359

questions.stem: The retail derivatives review at Turgeon Capital flags two situations in different client accounts: (1) a client entered new futures positions while a margin deficiency was outstanding in the account; (2) another client's new position pushed total exposure past the credit line the firm assigned to the account. Under subsection 3963(1), how should the designated Supervisor classify these?

options.A: Both are the same detection item, transacting while the account is under-margined
options.B: (1) is transacting while the account is under-margined; (2) is transacting beyond margin or credit limits
options.C: (1) is exceeding derivative position or exercise limits; (2) is excessive intra-day and short-term transactions
options.D: Neither requires detection because both clients later met their margin calls

questions.rationale: Clause 3963(1)(ii) targets transacting while the account is under-margined — trading on top of an existing margin deficiency, fact (1) — while clause 3963(1)(iii) targets transacting beyond margin or credit limits — breaching the limits assigned to the account, fact (2). They are separate list items, which defeats A. Option C maps the facts to the wrong items: position/exercise limits are regulatory or marketplace product limits, and nothing shows trade frequency; D is wrong because the rule requires the review to detect the activity — later cure does not erase the detection obligation.
questions.source_quote: "(ii) transacting while the account is under-margined, (iii) transacting beyond margin or credit limits," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8342

questions.stem: A retail client of Gosselin Derivatives holds options whose underlying security was recently made subject to transfer restrictions following a private placement. Which clause of subsection 3963(1) requires the firm's review of derivative transactions to detect activity in this account?

options.A: The clause covering transactions in derivatives whose underlying interest has restrictions on their transfer
options.B: The clause covering transactions in derivatives whose underlying interest is on the Dealer Member's restricted list
options.C: The clause covering speculative transactions in hedging accounts
options.D: The clause covering excessive intra-day and short-term transactions

questions.rationale: Clause 3963(1)(viii) requires detection of transactions in derivatives whose underlying interest has restrictions on their transfer — transfer-restricted securities, such as those under a hold period from a private placement, are exactly that. Option B is the closest distractor: the restricted list in clause 3963(1)(vii) is the dealer's own internal conflicts list (for example during an underwriting), a different concept from legal transfer restrictions on the security itself; C and D concern account classification and trade frequency, neither in the facts.
questions.source_quote: "(viii) transactions in derivatives whose underlying interest has restrictions on their transfer," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8357

questions.stem: A Registered Representative at Sansregret Markets accepts a first options order from a retail client whose derivatives trading agreement is still sitting unsigned in the client's email inbox. Which requirement in subsection 3962(5) has the firm's supervision failed to enforce?

options.A: The requirement to notify the client of approaching expiry dates
options.B: The requirement to obtain the designated Supervisor's approval of derivatives solicitation campaigns
options.C: The requirement that policies prevent a client from transacting in derivatives without executing a derivatives trading agreement with the Dealer Member
options.D: The requirement that the client maintain minimum margin set by the clearing house

questions.rationale: Clause 3962(5)(ii) requires policies and procedures that specifically prevent a client from transacting in derivatives without executing a derivatives trading agreement with the Dealer Member — an unsigned agreement means no transaction may proceed. Option B is the closest distractor because it is also a 3962(5) item, but it governs soliciting clients to use derivatives, and nothing here says the order was solicited; A is a client-notification obligation under 3962(4) unrelated to account documentation; D is a trading-agreement content term under 3252, not the supervision control breached by accepting the order.
questions.source_quote: "prevent a client from transacting in derivatives without executing a derivatives trading agreement with the Dealer Member," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8325

questions.stem: Duquette Futures offers retail clients futures and CFD trading but staffs its derivatives-qualified Registered Representatives only from 6 p.m. to midnight to save costs. Which requirement does this staffing model violate?

options.A: Clause 3962(5)(v)'s 0.5%-of-float limit on retail CFD positions
options.B: Subsection 3960(4)'s requirement that alternate Supervisors assume duties during absences
options.C: Section 3964's requirement that the Dealer Member's policies and procedures specifically address that derivatives clients have access, during normal business hours, to an Approved Person qualified to deal in the applicable derivatives
options.D: Section 3253's letter of undertaking requirement for institutional accounts

questions.rationale: Subsection 3964(1) requires policies and procedures that specifically address that derivatives clients have access, during normal business hours, to a Registered Representative, Investment Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager qualified to deal in the applicable derivatives — evening-only staffing leaves normal business hours uncovered. Option B concerns Supervisor continuity, not client access to qualified Approved Persons; A is a position-size control irrelevant to staffing; D applies to institutional documentation, not retail access.
questions.source_quote: "derivatives clients have access, during normal business hours, to a Registered Representative, Investment Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager qualified to deal in," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8366

questions.stem: Each evening, retail clients of Patenaude Markets hold open contract-for-difference positions. Under subsection 3808(1), what account information obligation does this trigger for the Dealer Member?

options.A: It must make available daily account information, similar to the information included in a prescribed statement, to each retail client who at the end of the day has an open futures contract, forward contract, contract for difference or similar derivative position in their account
options.B: It must courier a paper statement to the client within 24 hours of each trade
options.C: It must send only the standard quarterly statement; open CFDs trigger no additional reporting
options.D: It must report each client's open positions to the issuer of the underlying shares daily

questions.rationale: Subsection 3808(1) requires the Dealer Member to make available daily account information, similar to the information in a statement prescribed under 3808(4), to each retail client who at the end of the day has an open futures contract, forward contract, contract for difference or similar derivative position, or an unexpired and unexercised option position — leveraged open derivatives demand daily visibility. Option C is the standard securities-account baseline that the open-derivatives trigger overrides; B converts an information-availability obligation into a paper-courier rule the provision does not impose; D invents issuer reporting that does not exist.
questions.source_quote: "must make available daily account information, similar to the information included in a statement as prescribed under subsection 3808(4), to each retail client who at the end of the day has in their account:" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6880

questions.stem: At Castonguay Private Counsel, the designated Supervisor responsible for managed accounts is planning her review calendar for individually managed accounts. Under section 3973, how often must each managed account be reviewed, and to provide reasonable assurance of what?

options.A: Monthly, to confirm that fees were charged correctly
options.B: Daily, to verify each discretionary order before entry
options.C: Annually, to confirm the client still meets the managed account minimum balance
options.D: Quarterly, to provide reasonable assurance that the client's investment objectives are being pursued and that the handling of each managed account complies with Corporation requirements

questions.rationale: Subsection 3973(1) requires the designated Supervisor to review each managed account quarterly to provide reasonable assurance that the client's investment objectives are being pursued and that the handling of each managed account complies with Corporation requirements. Option B is the closest trap: daily-and-monthly full review applies to derivatives accounts designated as discretionary or managed under 3962(3), and pre-entry order review belongs to the discretionary regime in 3970(4) — the managed account standard here is quarterly; A and C invent fee and minimum-balance review purposes the rule does not state.
questions.source_quote: "must review each managed account quarterly to provide reasonable assurance that: (i) the client's investment objectives are being pursued," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8439

questions.stem: Lespérance Asset Management runs a centrally managed model applied across four hundred managed accounts. Under subsection 3973(2), how may the designated Supervisor structure the quarterly managed account review?

options.A: The review may be skipped, because model portfolios are reviewed at construction
options.B: The review may be done at an aggregate level, subject to minor variations to allow for client directed investment restrictions and the timing of client cash flows into the managed account
options.C: Each account must still be reviewed individually, with no aggregation permitted
options.D: The review may be delegated to the model provider's own compliance team

questions.rationale: Subsection 3973(2) provides that if the investment decisions for a managed account are made centrally and apply to a number of managed accounts, the quarterly review may be done at an aggregate level, subject to minor variations to allow for client directed investment restrictions and the timing of client cash flows into the managed account. Option C states the default for individually managed accounts but ignores the express central-decision accommodation; A is wrong because model construction review does not replace the quarterly account review; D outsources the designated Supervisor's review to a third party, which the rule does not authorize.
questions.source_quote: "the quarterly review may be done at an aggregate level, subject to minor variations to allow for client directed investment restrictions and the timing of client cash flows into the managed account." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8450

questions.stem: During a managed account review at Rouillard Counsel, the designated Supervisor finds that a Portfolio Manager directed a managed account to sell a block of an illiquid bond directly to the Portfolio Manager's own personal account. Which conflict-of-interest prohibition in section 3280 does this transaction breach?

options.A: The prohibition on knowingly causing a managed account to purchase or sell a security or derivative of a security of an issuer from or to the account of a Portfolio Manager, an Associate Portfolio Manager or an associate of either
options.B: The prohibition on charging fees contingent on performance without a written agreement
options.C: The prohibition on a managed account investing in new issues underwritten by the Dealer Member without prior written consent
options.D: The requirement to validate the client's cumulative loss limit annually

questions.rationale: Clause 3280(3)(i) prohibits a responsible person or Dealer Member from knowingly causing a managed account to purchase or sell a security or derivative of a security of an issuer from or to the account of a Portfolio Manager, an Associate Portfolio Manager or an associate of either — and unlike the consent-based prohibitions in 3280(2), the 3280(3) prohibitions are absolute. Option C is the closest distractor because it is also a 3280 item, but it covers dealer-underwritten new issues and is curable by prior written consent — the cross-trade with the PM's own account is not; B is the fee rule in 3281; D belongs to the derivatives loss-limit regime in 3252.
questions.source_quote: "purchase or sell a security or derivative of a security of an issuer from or to the account of a Portfolio Manager, an Associate Portfolio Manager or an associate of a Portfolio Manager" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5464

questions.stem: A CIRO examiner asks Trottier Securities for evidence of supervisory activity at its branches over the past several years. Under section 3908, which records must the Dealer Member be able to produce?

options.A: Only the current organizational chart showing reporting lines
options.B: Adequate records of supervisory activity, including on-site branch reviews, compliance issues identified and the resolution of such issues, kept for the period set out in section 3803
options.C: Client account statements only, since these reflect what was supervised
options.D: Records of supervisory activity for the prior 12 months only

questions.rationale: Subsection 3908(3) requires the Dealer Member to maintain adequate records of supervisory activity, including on-site branch reviews, compliance issues identified and the resolution of such issues, and subsection 3908(5) requires the section 3908 records to be kept for the period set out in section 3803 — a minimum of seven years from creation by default. Option D is defeated by the 3803 retention period; A and C are not supervision records — an org chart shows structure, not activity, and client statements evidence client reporting, not the supervisor's reviews and resolutions.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must maintain adequate records of supervisory activity, including on-site branch reviews, compliance issues identified and the resolution of such issues." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7969
