questions.scenario: Montpetit Capital Markets, a Participant, provides direct electronic access to Halcyon Ridge Fund, an institutional asset manager that runs several algorithmic strategies. The written DEA agreement was signed two years ago. This quarter: (1) Halcyon Ridge deployed a significantly modified version of its momentum algorithm without telling Montpetit; (2) a Montpetit compliance analyst notices that orders from Halcyon Ridge's user ID arrive at hours when its named authorized personnel are not working, suggesting an affiliate may be using the access; (3) the head of electronic trading admits that the annual reconfirmation of Halcyon Ridge against the firm's DEA standards was never completed this year; and (4) on Tuesday the modified algorithm begins submitting a stream of orders that breach Montpetit's pre-set volume limits.
questions.stem: Consider fact (1): Halcyon Ridge put a significantly modified version of its momentum algorithm into production without retesting it or telling Montpetit. Which standard, required content of Montpetit's DEA standards under subsection 7.13(2) of UMIR, has the client failed to meet?

options.A: Testing is required only for automated order systems built by the Participant itself
options.B: Testing is required only after the system has caused a marketplace incident
options.C: Testing may be replaced by a marketplace conformance certificate obtained once at onboarding
options.D: The client must ensure each automated order system used by itself or any client is tested in accordance with prudent business practices, including initially before use or introduction of a significant modification and at least annually thereafter

questions.rationale: Clause 7.13(2)(f) requires the Participant's DEA standards to include a requirement that the client ensures each automated order system used by itself or any client is tested in accordance with prudent business practices, including initially before use or introduction of a significant modification and at least annually thereafter — deploying a significant modification without testing breaches the standard. Option A is wrong because the standard reaches client systems, the entire point of third-party access controls; C substitutes a one-time certificate for recurring and modification-triggered testing; B makes testing reactive when the rule requires it before use.
questions.source_quote: "ensures that each automated order system, used by itself or any client, is tested in accordance with prudent business practices, including initially before use or introduction of a significant modification and at least annually thereafter." — umir.txt ~line 8823

questions.scenario: Montpetit Capital Markets, a Participant, provides direct electronic access to Halcyon Ridge Fund, an institutional asset manager that runs several algorithmic strategies. The written DEA agreement was signed two years ago. This quarter: (1) Halcyon Ridge deployed a significantly modified version of its momentum algorithm without telling Montpetit; (2) a Montpetit compliance analyst notices that orders from Halcyon Ridge's user ID arrive at hours when its named authorized personnel are not working, suggesting an affiliate may be using the access; (3) the head of electronic trading admits that the annual reconfirmation of Halcyon Ridge against the firm's DEA standards was never completed this year; and (4) on Tuesday the modified algorithm begins submitting a stream of orders that breach Montpetit's pre-set volume limits.
questions.stem: Turning to the orders arriving outside the named personnel's working hours: under UMIR Policy 7.1, monitoring of a DEA client's orders must be designed to identify, among other things, whether the client may have:

options.A: Improperly granted or provided its access under direct electronic access to another person
options.B: Used a marketplace other than the one named in the agreement
options.C: Earned trading profits exceeding the Participant's projections
options.D: Submitted orders smaller than the standard board lot

questions.rationale: UMIR Policy 7.1 Part 9 requires a Participant that has granted DEA to monitor the client's orders to identify whether the client may have, among other things, improperly granted or provided its access to another person — orders arriving when no authorized personnel are working are the classic indicator, and clause 7.13(3)(b)(i) requires the agreement to name the client's authorized personnel. Options B, C and D are not Policy 7.1 Part 9 monitoring targets: marketplace selection, profitability and order size are routine matters, not access-integrity breaches.
questions.source_quote: "improperly granted or provided its access under direct electronic access or a routing arrangement to another person;" — umir.txt ~line 7061

questions.scenario: Montpetit Capital Markets, a Participant, provides direct electronic access to Halcyon Ridge Fund, an institutional asset manager that runs several algorithmic strategies. The written DEA agreement was signed two years ago. This quarter: (1) Halcyon Ridge deployed a significantly modified version of its momentum algorithm without telling Montpetit; (2) a Montpetit compliance analyst notices that orders from Halcyon Ridge's user ID arrive at hours when its named authorized personnel are not working, suggesting an affiliate may be using the access; (3) the head of electronic trading admits that the annual reconfirmation of Halcyon Ridge against the firm's DEA standards was never completed this year; and (4) on Tuesday the modified algorithm begins submitting a stream of orders that breach Montpetit's pre-set volume limits.
questions.stem: Regarding the missed annual reconfirmation: under subsection 7.13(5) of UMIR, what must Montpetit do at least annually with respect to Halcyon Ridge specifically?

options.A: Renegotiate the commission schedule in the DEA agreement
options.B: Obtain a fresh legal opinion that DEA remains lawful in the client's home jurisdiction
options.C: At least annually by the anniversary date of the written agreement, assess, confirm and document that the client is in compliance with the written agreement and has met the standards established by the Participant since the date of the agreement or the last annual review
options.D: Rotate the client to a different executing Participant to refresh independence

questions.rationale: Clause 7.13(5)(b) requires the Participant, at least annually by the anniversary date of the written agreement, to assess, confirm and document that the client is in compliance with the written agreement and has met the Participant's standards since the date of the agreement or the last annual review; clause 7.13(5)(a) separately requires an annual review of the adequacy and consistent application of the standards themselves. Option A concerns commercial terms, not the compliance reconfirmation; B and D invent a legal-opinion refresh and a rotation requirement found nowhere in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "at least annually by the anniversary date of the written agreement assess, confirm and document that the client, investment dealer or foreign dealer equivalent: (i) is in compliance with the written agreement with the Participant," — umir.txt ~line 8939

questions.scenario: Montpetit Capital Markets, a Participant, provides direct electronic access to Halcyon Ridge Fund, an institutional asset manager that runs several algorithmic strategies. The written DEA agreement was signed two years ago. This quarter: (1) Halcyon Ridge deployed a significantly modified version of its momentum algorithm without telling Montpetit; (2) a Montpetit compliance analyst notices that orders from Halcyon Ridge's user ID arrive at hours when its named authorized personnel are not working, suggesting an affiliate may be using the access; (3) the head of electronic trading admits that the annual reconfirmation of Halcyon Ridge against the firm's DEA standards was never completed this year; and (4) on Tuesday the modified algorithm begins submitting a stream of orders that breach Montpetit's pre-set volume limits.
questions.stem: On Tuesday, the modified algorithm starts breaching Montpetit's pre-set volume limits in live trading. Under the DEA agreement provisions required by subsection 7.13(3) of UMIR, what may Montpetit do immediately?

options.A: Nothing until it gives Halcyon Ridge 48 hours' written notice of default
options.B: Exercise its authorization, without prior notice, to reject any order, vary or correct any order entered on a marketplace to comply with Requirements, cancel any order entered on a marketplace, or discontinue accepting orders from the client
options.C: Petition the Market Regulator to halt trading in the affected securities
options.D: Only log the breaches for the next annual review, since the client controls its own algorithm

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 7.13(3)(a)(iv) requires the written agreement to authorize the Participant, without prior notice, to reject any order, vary or correct any order entered on a marketplace to comply with Requirements, cancel any order entered on a marketplace, or discontinue accepting orders from the client — the contractual teeth that let the Participant act the moment limits are breached, and 7.13(4)(b) bars transmission of any order not subject to the firm's automated pre-entry controls. Option A inserts a notice period the rule expressly excludes; C outsources the response to the regulator instead of using the Participant's own authority; D defers immediate risk to an annual cycle and wrongly treats client control of the algorithm as displacing the Participant's responsibility.
questions.source_quote: "the Participant is authorized, without prior notice, to: (A) reject any order, (B) vary or correct any order entered on a marketplace to comply with Requirements, (C) cancel any order entered on a marketplace, or (D) discontinue accepting orders," — umir.txt ~line 8857

questions.scenario: Beausoleil Futures Inc. is an Investment Dealer whose retail derivatives business is overseen by designated Supervisor Karim Osman. During his review of last week's derivative transactions, Karim notes three retail accounts: Account 1 belongs to a client whose derivatives trading agreement sets an annual cumulative loss limit of $40,000; realized losses on her CFD trades now total $47,500 for the year, yet new positions were opened yesterday. Account 2 belongs to a client who was appointed chief financial officer of Riverbend Metals Corp., a reporting issuer, last month and has since been buying short-dated call options on Riverbend Metals during the issuer's blackout window. Account 3 belongs to a client who holds long copper futures contracts that enter their delivery month next week, with no instructions on file.
questions.stem: Consider Account 1, where realized losses of $47,500 exceed the client's approved $40,000 annual loss limit and new positions were still opened. Which clause of subsection 3963(1) requires Beausoleil's review of derivative transactions to detect this situation?

options.A: The clause covering excessive intra-day and short-term transactions
options.B: The clause covering transacting beyond margin or credit limits
options.C: The clause covering cumulative losses exceeding approved client loss limits in accounts determined in accordance with clause 3252(1)(vii) and subsection 3252(2)
options.D: The clause covering exceeding derivative position or exercise limits

questions.rationale: Clause 3963(1)(iv) requires the review of derivative transactions to detect cumulative losses exceeding approved client loss limits in accounts determined in accordance with clause 3252(1)(vii) and subsection 3252(2) — the $47,500 of realized losses against a $40,000 annual limit is precisely that, and the account trades CFDs, an in-scope product under 3252(2)(i). Option B is the closest distractor but concerns margin and credit limits, which measure exposure financing, not accumulated losses; A concerns trade frequency, not stated; D concerns marketplace or regulatory position/exercise limits on the derivative product, not the client's loss limit.
questions.source_quote: "cumulative losses exceeding approved client loss limits in accounts determined in accordance with clause 3252(1)(vii) and subsection 3252(2)," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8344

questions.scenario: Beausoleil Futures Inc. is an Investment Dealer whose retail derivatives business is overseen by designated Supervisor Karim Osman. During his review of last week's derivative transactions, Karim notes three retail accounts: Account 1 belongs to a client whose derivatives trading agreement sets an annual cumulative loss limit of $40,000; realized losses on her CFD trades now total $47,500 for the year, yet new positions were opened yesterday. Account 2 belongs to a client who was appointed chief financial officer of Riverbend Metals Corp., a reporting issuer, last month and has since been buying short-dated call options on Riverbend Metals during the issuer's blackout window. Account 3 belongs to a client who holds long copper futures contracts that enter their delivery month next week, with no instructions on file.
questions.stem: For Account 1, Karim also wants to fix the loss-limit process going forward. Under subsection 3962(1), what is the designated Supervisor for retail derivatives accounts responsible for with respect to client loss limits?

options.A: Reporting each loss-limit breach to the clearing corporation within 24 hours
options.B: Setting an identical dollar loss limit for every retail derivatives client
options.C: Waiving the loss limit whenever a client signs a risk acknowledgment
options.D: Reviewing and approving client loss limits when they are set annually or updated, taking into consideration previous losses

questions.rationale: Clause 3962(1)(i) makes the designated Supervisors for retail derivatives accounts responsible for reviewing and approving client loss limits when they are set annually or updated, taking into consideration previous losses — under 3252(2)(iii) limits are determined on a lifetime basis validated annually or an annual basis updated annually. Option B is wrong because limits are client-specific, not uniform; C is wrong because the limit is a trading-agreement term under 3252(1)(vii) and supervision control, not waivable by acknowledgment; A invents clearing-corporation reporting the rules do not require for loss limits.
questions.source_quote: "reviewing and approving client loss limits when they are set annually or updated, taking into consideration previous losses," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8298

questions.scenario: Beausoleil Futures Inc. is an Investment Dealer whose retail derivatives business is overseen by designated Supervisor Karim Osman. During his review of last week's derivative transactions, Karim notes three retail accounts: Account 1 belongs to a client whose derivatives trading agreement sets an annual cumulative loss limit of $40,000; realized losses on her CFD trades now total $47,500 for the year, yet new positions were opened yesterday. Account 2 belongs to a client who was appointed chief financial officer of Riverbend Metals Corp., a reporting issuer, last month and has since been buying short-dated call options on Riverbend Metals during the issuer's blackout window. Account 3 belongs to a client who holds long copper futures contracts that enter their delivery month next week, with no instructions on file.
questions.stem: Account 2's client, now CFO of Riverbend Metals, has been buying call options on Riverbend Metals during the blackout window. In addition to the detection obligation, which acknowledgment in the derivatives trading agreement under section 3252 should have prompted the client to alert Beausoleil to his new status?

options.A: The acknowledgment of receiving the most recent derivatives risk disclosure statement
options.B: The acknowledgment of the client's obligation to inform and update the Dealer Member of any circumstances under which they would be considered to be an insider of a reporting issuer or any other issuer whose securities are publicly traded
options.C: The acknowledgment of the dealer's right to use free credit balances within its business
options.D: The acknowledgment of the dealer's deadlines for submitting an exercise notice

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 3252(1)(ix)(b) requires the derivatives trading agreement to include the client's acknowledgment of their obligation to inform and update the Dealer Member of any circumstances under which they would be considered an insider of a reporting issuer or any other issuer whose securities are publicly traded — the CFO appointment is exactly such a circumstance, and the update feeds the firm's detection of derivatives trading by an insider to avoid insider trading restrictions under 3962(5)(iv) and 3963(1)(ix). Option A is a different 3252(1)(ix) acknowledgment about risk disclosure; C and D are dealer-rights and options-mechanics terms with no connection to insider status.
questions.source_quote: "their obligation to inform and update the Dealer Member of any circumstances under which they would be considered to be an insider of a reporting issuer or any other issuer whose securities are publicly traded," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5231

questions.scenario: Beausoleil Futures Inc. is an Investment Dealer whose retail derivatives business is overseen by designated Supervisor Karim Osman. During his review of last week's derivative transactions, Karim notes three retail accounts: Account 1 belongs to a client whose derivatives trading agreement sets an annual cumulative loss limit of $40,000; realized losses on her CFD trades now total $47,500 for the year, yet new positions were opened yesterday. Account 2 belongs to a client who was appointed chief financial officer of Riverbend Metals Corp., a reporting issuer, last month and has since been buying short-dated call options on Riverbend Metals during the issuer's blackout window. Account 3 belongs to a client who holds long copper futures contracts that enter their delivery month next week, with no instructions on file.
questions.stem: Account 3's copper futures enter their delivery month next week and the client has given no instructions. Which policies-and-procedures requirement in subsection 3962(5) addresses how Beausoleil must be prepared for this situation?

options.A: The requirement to address the handling of futures contracts, forward contracts and similar derivatives with pending delivery months
options.B: The requirement to prevent retail CFD positions exceeding 0.5% of an issuer's float on an intra-day or short-term basis
options.C: The requirement that the designated Supervisor approve the solicitation of clients to use derivatives programs
options.D: The requirement to notify clients of changes in the Dealer Member's business policy

questions.rationale: Clause 3962(5)(iii) requires policies and procedures that specifically address the handling of futures contracts, forward contracts and similar derivatives with pending delivery months — without a documented process, an uninstructed long position can slide into a physical delivery obligation, the exposure clause 3963(1)(xi) requires the firm to detect. Option D is the closest distractor because client notification under 3962(4) includes approaching expiry dates, but expiry notification is a 3962(4) item and the question asks for the 3962(5) pending-delivery-month handling requirement; B and C concern CFD position size and solicitation approval, neither in the facts.
questions.source_quote: "address the handling of futures contracts, forward contracts and similar derivatives with pending delivery months," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8327

questions.scenario: Vaillancourt Securities Inc. is an Investment Dealer with eight retail branches. Supervisor Lise Arsenault conducts the first-tier reviews for the Rivière-Claire branch. This week her daily and monthly review queues surface the following from the book of Registered Representative Tomas Beck: (1) in one retiree's account, four trades were cancelled and rebooked into a different account number over two weeks, and the client has not responded to calls; (2) Beck personally bought 5,000 shares of a thinly traded issuer in his employee account minutes before entering a large buy order for a client in the same issuer; (3) Lise realizes the branch's review population, generated from client account codes, has never included Beck's own employee account, even though it trades actively; and (4) Lise keeps all of her review records and follow-up notes in paper files at the Rivière-Claire branch, which head office has never visited to inspect.
questions.stem: Item (1) shows repeated trade cancellations and rebookings into a different account number in the retiree's account. Under subsection 3945(2), which detection category do these facts most directly indicate?

options.A: Deterioration of the quality of client holdings in an account
options.B: Outstanding margin calls
options.C: Undisclosed short sales
options.D: Excessive transaction transfers and trade cancellations indicating possible unauthorized trading

questions.rationale: Clause 3945(2)(vi) requires retail supervision policies to address detecting excessive transaction transfers and trade cancellations indicating possible unauthorized trading — cancelling and rebooking trades across account numbers is how unauthorized activity gets concealed, and the unreachable client strengthens the inference. Account number changes are themselves a separate flag under 3945(2)(xii), reinforcing the same concern. Option A describes declining portfolio quality, not booking manipulation; B and C are unrelated margin and settlement flags with no support in the facts.
questions.source_quote: "excessive transaction transfers and trade cancellations indicating possible unauthorized trading," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8153

questions.scenario: Vaillancourt Securities Inc. is an Investment Dealer with eight retail branches. Supervisor Lise Arsenault conducts the first-tier reviews for the Rivière-Claire branch. This week her daily and monthly review queues surface the following from the book of Registered Representative Tomas Beck: (1) in one retiree's account, four trades were cancelled and rebooked into a different account number over two weeks, and the client has not responded to calls; (2) Beck personally bought 5,000 shares of a thinly traded issuer in his employee account minutes before entering a large buy order for a client in the same issuer; (3) Lise realizes the branch's review population, generated from client account codes, has never included Beck's own employee account, even though it trades actively; and (4) Lise keeps all of her review records and follow-up notes in paper files at the Rivière-Claire branch, which head office has never visited to inspect.
questions.stem: Item (2) — Beck buying shares in his employee account minutes before entering the client's large buy order in the same thinly traded issuer — engages which detection item that subsection 3945(2) requires retail supervision policies to address?

options.A: Late payment
options.B: Undue concentration of securities in a single account
options.C: Front running
options.D: Inappropriate or high risk trading strategies

questions.rationale: Clause 3945(2)(xi) lists front running among the matters retail supervision policies must detect — trading the employee account ahead of a known client order that can move a thin stock is the textbook pattern, and it simultaneously raises the improper or excessive employee trading flag in 3945(2)(x) and the conflict between Registered Representative and client trading activity in 3945(2)(v). Option D is the closest distractor because the conduct is certainly inappropriate, but that clause targets the strategy traded in client accounts, not trading ahead of a client; A and B have no factual basis here.
questions.source_quote: "(x) improper or excessive employee trading, (xi) front running, (xii) account number changes," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8159

questions.scenario: Vaillancourt Securities Inc. is an Investment Dealer with eight retail branches. Supervisor Lise Arsenault conducts the first-tier reviews for the Rivière-Claire branch. This week her daily and monthly review queues surface the following from the book of Registered Representative Tomas Beck: (1) in one retiree's account, four trades were cancelled and rebooked into a different account number over two weeks, and the client has not responded to calls; (2) Beck personally bought 5,000 shares of a thinly traded issuer in his employee account minutes before entering a large buy order for a client in the same issuer; (3) Lise realizes the branch's review population, generated from client account codes, has never included Beck's own employee account, even though it trades actively; and (4) Lise keeps all of her review records and follow-up notes in paper files at the Rivière-Claire branch, which head office has never visited to inspect.
questions.stem: Item (3) reveals that the branch review population has never included Beck's own actively trading employee account. Under CIRO's account supervision guidance, why is this a defect in Vaillancourt's review design?

options.A: It is not a defect — employee accounts are exempt from account activity review because employees are already supervised as registrants
options.B: Dealers should include employee and agent account activity in their review — employee accounts are exactly where conflicts such as trading ahead of clients surface, so excluding them blinds the review to that risk
options.C: Employee accounts need only be reviewed by CIRO during its periodic examinations
options.D: Employee accounts must be reviewed, but only if the employee consents in writing

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 s.1.2 states Dealers should include employee and agent account activity in their review — and the guidance's example second-tier criteria likewise capture non-client trading. Excluding Beck's employee account is what allowed his trading ahead of the client order in item (2) to go unflagged at the account level. Option A is the misconception the guidance forecloses: registrant status does not exempt the account from activity review; C shifts the dealer's own supervision duty onto the regulator; D invents a consent condition — supervision of non-client account activity is not optional for the employee.
questions.source_quote: "Dealers should include employee and agent account activity in their review." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 204

questions.scenario: Vaillancourt Securities Inc. is an Investment Dealer with eight retail branches. Supervisor Lise Arsenault conducts the first-tier reviews for the Rivière-Claire branch. This week her daily and monthly review queues surface the following from the book of Registered Representative Tomas Beck: (1) in one retiree's account, four trades were cancelled and rebooked into a different account number over two weeks, and the client has not responded to calls; (2) Beck personally bought 5,000 shares of a thinly traded issuer in his employee account minutes before entering a large buy order for a client in the same issuer; (3) Lise realizes the branch's review population, generated from client account codes, has never included Beck's own employee account, even though it trades actively; and (4) Lise keeps all of her review records and follow-up notes in paper files at the Rivière-Claire branch, which head office has never visited to inspect.
questions.stem: Item (4): Lise's review records and follow-up notes are kept in paper files at the Rivière-Claire branch, and head office has never inspected them. Under subsection 3908(4), what does keeping supervision records at a branch office oblige Vaillancourt to do?

options.A: Conduct periodic on-site reviews of branch office supervision and record keeping
options.B: Nothing — the location of supervision records is left entirely to the branch's discretion
options.C: Digitize and transmit every record to CIRO within 30 days of creation
options.D: Move all supervision records to head office, since branch storage is prohibited

questions.rationale: Subsection 3908(4) provides that where supervision records are kept at a branch office, the Dealer Member must conduct periodic on-site reviews of branch office supervision and record keeping — branch storage is permitted, but it triggers head-office verification that the records exist and the supervision is real. Option D is the closest distractor because it correctly senses a control is needed but imposes the wrong one — the rule permits branch-kept records and responds with on-site review, not relocation; B ignores the trigger entirely; C invents CIRO transmission, when the obligation under 3908(5) is retention for the section 3803 period, available for inspection.
questions.source_quote: "Where supervision records are kept at a branch office, the Dealer Member must conduct periodic on-site reviews of branch office supervision and record keeping." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7971

questions.stem: Marek Stolarz is an Investment Representative at Halverson Capital whom CIRO has approved to deal with institutional clients. A retail investor with a small RRSP phones the institutional desk by mistake and asks Marek to enter an unsolicited buy order for a blue-chip stock — no advice requested. May Marek take the order?

options.A: Yes — any Investment Representative may take unsolicited orders from any type of client
options.B: No, because Investment Representatives may never take orders directly from clients
options.C: Yes, provided he documents that the order was unsolicited
options.D: No — an Investment Representative dealing with institutional clients may take orders from institutional clients only

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 2553(3)(iii)(b) provides that an Investment Representative dealing with institutional clients may take orders from institutional clients only; only an IR dealing with retail clients may take orders from all types of clients under 2553(3)(iii)(a). Option A states the retail-side IR rule, the closest distractor; C wrongly treats unsolicited status as curing the client-type restriction; B denies the core order-taking function of the IR category.
questions.source_quote: "institutional clients, may take orders from institutional clients only." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3009

questions.stem: Orillia Securities hires Bao Tran, a newly approved Registered Representative, and wants to assign him immediately to its institutional fixed-income desk. The Supervisor asks compliance what must happen before Bao can begin dealing with institutional clients on the firm's behalf. Under subsection 2553(3), the firm must:

options.A: Notify the Corporation and seek the Corporation's prior approval on whether Bao will deal with either retail clients or institutional clients
options.B: Simply record the assignment in its internal books; no regulator interaction is needed
options.C: Wait until Bao completes six months of close supervision on the retail desk
options.D: Obtain written consent from each institutional client Bao will cover

questions.rationale: Clause 2553(3)(i) requires the Dealer Member to notify the Corporation, and seek the Corporation's prior approval on whether the Registered Representative will deal with either retail clients or institutional clients — an Approved Person may not conduct that type of dealing until the firm complies. Option C is the closest distractor — the six-month close supervision in 3947(1) applies to new RRs dealing with retail clients and is not a precondition tied to the institutional designation; B omits the required Corporation approval; D substitutes client consent for regulator approval.
questions.source_quote: "The Dealer Member must notify the Corporation, and seek the Corporation's prior approval on whether the Registered Representative, Investment Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager will deal with either retail clients or institutional clients." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3001

questions.stem: At Welland Wealth Inc., Registered Representative Sofia Andrade has 14 months of active trading experience, all in equities. The firm proposes authorizing her to affect trades for several discretionary accounts that hold equities and listed options. Which deficiency should the designated Supervisor identify?

options.A: None — any Registered Representative may be authorized for discretionary trades once a client signs the discretionary account agreement
options.B: She lacks the required minimum five years of portfolio management experience
options.C: A Registered Representative may only be authorized for a discretionary account with at least two years of active experience in trading, advising or performing analysis with respect to all types of products to be traded on a discretionary basis — Sofia falls short on both duration and product coverage
options.D: Only Portfolio Managers, never Registered Representatives, may be authorized to affect discretionary trades

questions.rationale: Clause 3275(1)(i) permits a Registered Representative to be authorized to affect trades for a discretionary account only if the RR has at least two years of active experience in trading, advising or performing analysis with respect to all types of products that are to be traded on a discretionary basis; Sofia has 14 months and no options experience. Option D is the closest distractor — 3275 expressly contemplates RRs affecting discretionary trades, so the prohibition is not categorical; A ignores the experience condition entirely; B invents a five-year portfolio-management standard.
questions.source_quote: "the Registered Representative has at least two years of active experience in trading, advising or performing analysis with respect to all types of products that are to be traded on a discretionary basis," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5383

questions.stem: Kingsbridge Asset Management, an Investment Dealer, has no Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager on staff but wants to launch a managed account program by outsourcing the investment decisions to Ridgemont Advisors, an external registered adviser. The Supervisor checks section 3279. May the dealer designate Ridgemont to deal with the managed accounts?

options.A: No — only individuals approved by CIRO as Portfolio Managers may ever make managed account decisions
options.B: No, unless every managed account client individually approves Ridgemont in writing each quarter
options.C: Yes, with no conditions, because outsourcing transfers all regulatory responsibility to the adviser
options.D: Yes — a Dealer Member may designate a sub-advisor with whom it has entered into a written sub-advisor agreement, provided the sub-advisor is appropriately registered or licensed and is subject to, or agrees to comply with, the section 3280 conflict of interest provisions

questions.rationale: Clause 3279(1)(iii) lets a Dealer Member designate, as the individual authorized to deal with managed accounts, a sub-advisor with whom the Dealer Member has entered into a written sub-advisor agreement; 3279(2) conditions this on the sub-advisor being registered or licensed (or exempt) to carry on managed account activities and being subject to conflict-of-interest provisions at least equivalent to section 3280 or agreeing by contract to comply with section 3280. Option A overlooks the APM and sub-advisor routes in 3279(1); C wrongly suggests outsourcing offloads the dealer's obligations; B invents a quarterly client re-approval mechanism.
questions.source_quote: "a sub‐advisor with whom the Dealer Member has entered into a written sub‐advisor agreement." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5437

questions.stem: Registered Representative Devraj Chauhan of Almonte Securities tells his Supervisor he has begun teaching a paid evening course on retirement planning at a community college. He informed the firm by email the day after his first class and sees no issue because the course 'doesn't compete with the dealer.' Measured against subsection 2554(2), what is wrong?

options.A: Nothing — outside activities need only be disclosed within 90 days
options.B: Outside activities are flatly prohibited for all Registered Representatives
options.C: The activity is permitted only if it is unpaid
options.D: He failed to obtain the Dealer Member's prior approval to engage in the outside activity — informing the firm after starting does not satisfy the rule

questions.rationale: Clause 2554(2)(ii) conditions an Approved Person's outside activity on the Approved Person obtaining the Dealer Member's prior approval to engage in the outside activity (alongside informing the firm, firm policies addressing continuous client service and conflicts, and NI 33-109 notification to the Corporation). Devraj started teaching before approval. Option A invents a 90-day disclosure window and drops the prior-approval condition; B contradicts 2554(1), which permits outside activities meeting the rule's conditions; C invents a compensation test.
questions.source_quote: "the Approved Person obtains the Dealer Member's prior approval to engage in the outside activity," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3042

questions.stem: Supervisor Helga Brandt at Coboconk Investments reviews the new business cards of Investment Representative Janik Roy, which read 'Janik Roy, Senior Wealth Advisor.' Janik is approved only to trade in, not advise on, securities. What dealer obligation does this engage?

options.A: A Dealer Member must ensure that, when dealing with the public, its Approved Persons use titles and designations that accurately indicate the type of business they are approved to conduct and the role they carry out — 'Wealth Advisor' misstates an order-taking role
options.B: None — business card titles are marketing puffery outside CIRO's jurisdiction
options.C: The title is acceptable so long as Janik passes the Retail Securities Exam within a year
options.D: The title is acceptable because clients can verify approval categories on CIRO's website themselves

questions.rationale: Subsection 2551(5) requires the Dealer Member to ensure that, when dealing with the public, its Approved Persons use titles and designations that accurately indicate the type of business they have been approved by the Corporation to conduct and the role they carry out or are approved to carry out; an 'Advisor' title on an Investment Representative — who under Rule 1201 trades in but does not advise on securities — misleads the public. Option C is the closest distractor — a future exam does not cure a presently inaccurate title; B wrongly places titles outside the rules; D shifts the firm's obligation onto clients.
questions.source_quote: "its Approved Persons use titles and designations that accurately indicate: (i) the type of business that they have been approved by the Corporation to conduct," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 2924

questions.stem: Tilbury Futures employs Annika Salo as an order execution only (OEO) Registered Representative within its OEO separate business unit. The advisory division is short-staffed during RRSP season, and the branch manager proposes that Annika 'help out' covering advisory clients two days a week. Which provision defeats this proposal?

options.A: Subsection 3221(1) — OEO staff may not exercise time and price discretion
options.B: Subsection 2551(6) — Annika ceased to be approved when she joined the OEO unit
options.C: Subsection 2207(4) — when operating as a separate business unit, an order execution only account services provider's Registered Representatives and Investment Representatives may not work for any other business unit within the Dealer Member
options.D: Subsection 3247(1) — advisory clients require a signed margin account agreement first

questions.rationale: Subsection 2207(4) requires that, if operating as a separate business unit within a Dealer Member, the order execution only account services provider have separate letterhead, accounts and account documentation, and that its Registered Representatives and Investment Representatives may not work for any other business unit within the Dealer Member — the cross-staffing proposal violates this segregation. Option A cites the discretionary-trading prohibition, which does not address unit segregation; B is false because joining the OEO unit does not end approval; D misapplies the margin agreement rule, which has nothing to do with staffing.
questions.source_quote: "its Registered Representatives and Investment Representatives may not work for any other business unit within the Dealer Member." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1357

questions.stem: Penhold Capital wants to start paying commissions to the employees who staff its order execution only division, calculated per transaction executed in OEO client accounts, to 'reward responsiveness.' The OEO Supervisor objects. Which rule supports the objection?

options.A: Commission compensation is permitted in OEO units if capped at 1% of trade value
options.B: Commissions are permitted because OEO clients receive no recommendations anyway
options.C: Commissions are permitted provided the employees hold the Retail Securities Exam
options.D: A Dealer Member must not compensate employees by giving them trade commissions for transactions executed in order execution only accounts

questions.rationale: Subsection 2207(5) prohibits a Dealer Member from compensating employees by giving them trade commissions for transactions executed in order execution only accounts — per-transaction commissions would create an incentive to stimulate trading in a channel built on the absence of recommendations. Option B is the closest distractor — the no-recommendation model is precisely why commission incentives are barred, not why they are tolerable; A invents a cap exception; C confuses proficiency with the compensation prohibition.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must not compensate employees by giving them trade commissions for transactions executed in order execution only accounts." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1363

questions.stem: At Glenavon Securities, a retail client transfers in a basket of securities from another dealer; no purchase or sale is contemplated. The Registered Representative says no suitability work is needed 'because nothing is being traded.' Under subsection 3402(2), the Supervisor should respond that the dealer must:

options.A: Do nothing until the client places the next order
options.B: Obtain a one-time waiver from the client covering transferred-in positions
options.C: Review the client's account and the securities in it to determine whether the suitability criteria are met, within a reasonable time after securities are received into the account by way of deposit or transfer-in
options.D: Liquidate any transferred position not already on the firm's product shelf

questions.rationale: Clause 3402(2)(i) requires the Dealer Member to review the retail client's account and the securities in it against the 3402(1) criteria, taking reasonable steps within a reasonable time, after securities are received or delivered into the client's account by way of deposit or transfer-in — transfer-in is an express non-trade trigger. Option A treats suitability as trade-only, the precise error the rule corrects; B invents a waiver mechanism unavailable to retail clients; D leaps to liquidation when the rule requires a review.
questions.source_quote: "securities, derivatives or precious metals bullion are received or delivered into the client's account by way of deposit or transfer-in," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5557

questions.stem: Brockhurst Capital serves Meridian Forge Pension Plan, a non-individual institutional client that also qualifies as a 'permitted client' under NI 31-103 and is not itself a dealer, bank or similar regulated entity. Meridian Forge wants to dispense with transaction-level suitability determinations altogether. Under subsection 3404(3), what makes that possible?

options.A: Nothing — institutional clients can never be relieved of section 3403 suitability determinations
options.B: The client may waive, in writing, the protections offered under subsections 3403(1) and 3403(2), in which case section 3403 (other than subsection 3403(4)) does not apply to the account
options.C: A verbal waiver given to the Registered Representative on a recorded line
options.D: The dealer's Chief Compliance Officer may unilaterally exempt the account by internal memo

questions.rationale: Clause 3404(3)(ii) provides that, other than subsection 3403(4), section 3403 does not apply to an account held by a non-individual institutional client that is also a permitted client as defined in NI 31-103, is not a client described in clause 3404(3)(i), and has waived, in writing, the protections offered under subsections 3403(1) and 3403(2). Option A ignores both 3404(3) exemptions; C fails the written-waiver requirement; D substitutes a dealer-side memo for the client's own written waiver.
questions.source_quote: "has waived, in writing, the protections offered to them under subsections 3403(1) and 3403(2)." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5656

questions.stem: A Supervisor at Caradoc Wealth reviews a proposed retail switch from one bond fund to a nearly identical fund with a materially higher management expense ratio and a new deferred sales charge. The client's KYC fits both funds. Which suitability factor in subsection 3402(1) most directly catches the problem with this switch?

options.A: The potential and actual impact of costs on the retail client's returns
options.B: The client's insider status under section 3202
options.C: The dealer's obligation to establish creditworthiness when financing a purchase
options.D: The maximum 12-month term of the account agreement

questions.rationale: Clause 3402(1)(i)(d) makes the potential and actual impact of costs on the retail client's returns an express suitability factor — a switch into a near-identical fund with higher ongoing costs and a new deferred charge fails on cost impact even where KYC otherwise fits, and 3402(1)(i)(e) reinforces this by requiring consideration of reasonable alternative actions such as staying put. Option B concerns identification, not transaction suitability; C applies only when the dealer finances the acquisition; D imports a discretionary account agreement term irrelevant to suitability.
questions.source_quote: "the potential and actual impact of costs on the retail client's returns, and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5548

questions.stem: Vermilion Bay Securities asks how Supervisor oversight of suitability is framed in Rule 3900 for its retail side. Under section 3948, the Dealer Member must:

options.A: Personally re-perform every suitability determination made by each Registered Representative
options.B: Supervise each Registered Representative, Investment Representative, Portfolio Manager and Associate Portfolio Manager to confirm that they are complying with their responsibilities relating to the suitability determination to retail clients under Rule 3400
options.C: Supervise only Portfolio Managers, since they carry the highest proficiency
options.D: Delegate suitability supervision to the client through annual KYC confirmations

questions.rationale: Subsection 3948(1) requires the Dealer Member to supervise each Registered Representative, Investment Representative, Portfolio Manager and Associate Portfolio Manager to confirm that they are complying with their responsibilities relating to the suitability determination to retail clients under Rule 3400 — supervision confirms compliance; it does not replace the assigned individual's primary responsibility under 3406. Option A converts oversight into re-performance, which the rule does not demand; C truncates the supervised population; D outsources supervision to the client.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must supervise each Registered Representative, Investment Representative, Portfolio Manager and Associate Portfolio Manager to confirm that they are complying with their responsibilities relating to the suitability determination to retail clients under Rule 3400." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8210

questions.stem: At Stronfield Securities, Registered Representative Mathieu Caron is authorized to affect trades for several discretionary accounts. Mathieu has NOT been approved as a Portfolio Manager and is NOT an Executive of the firm. He prepares a discretionary sell order for one of those accounts. The firm's procedures manual offers the designated Supervisor four review timings. Under subsection 3970(4), which one states the timing that applies to Mathieu's order?

options.A: No later than one business day after the trade was made — but only because that timing is reserved for a Registered Representative who has been approved as a Portfolio Manager or who is also an Executive, which Mathieu is not
options.B: Prior to the order being entered, because the later-review alternative is available only where the Registered Representative has been approved as a Portfolio Manager or is also an Executive
options.C: Within the designated Supervisor's monthly review of each discretionary account's financial performance
options.D: No review of the order is required, because the signed discretionary account agreement already authorizes Mathieu's discretion

questions.rationale: Subsection 3970(4) requires a designated Supervisor to review any discretionary order initiated in a discretionary account by a Registered Representative prior to the order being entered unless the RR has been approved as a Portfolio Manager or is also an Executive and the Supervisor reviews the order no later than one business day after the trade. Mathieu is expressly neither, so the pre-entry default governs. Option A is the closest distractor and is self-defeating: it correctly describes the one-business-day timing as the PM/Executive exception, then misapplies it to Mathieu, who does not qualify. Option C confuses order review with the monthly financial-performance review under 3970(1); D mistakes the client's grant of discretionary authority for a waiver of the Supervisor's order review.
questions.source_quote: "A designated Supervisor must review any discretionary order initiated in a discretionary account by a Registered Representative prior to the order being entered unless:" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8386
questions.qa_note: Clean redraft of killed SUP-E6-0043 (3970(4) prior-review default); passed Gate 1 blind solve 2026-06-04.

questions.stem: Drowes Capital's designated Supervisor for discretionary accounts, Imelda Reyes, is overloaded and asks a senior compliance analyst to take over the monthly financial-performance reviews of the discretionary book and the related assessments of whether each authorized Registered Representative should continue trading those accounts. Is this arrangement permissible?

options.A: Yes — Rule 3900 reviews may be delegated to any qualified compliance employee with documented terms
options.B: Yes, provided Imelda spot-checks a sample of the analyst's completed reviews quarterly
options.C: No — the designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts must not delegate the performance of the reviews required in subsections 3970(1) and 3970(2) to any other person
options.D: No, because monthly reviews of discretionary accounts may only be performed by the Chief Compliance Officer

questions.rationale: Subsection 3970(3) prohibits the designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts from delegating the performance of the reviews required in subsections 3970(1) (monthly financial performance) and 3970(2) (continued-authorization assessment) to any other person. Option A states the general delegation framework that 3970(3) expressly overrides for these reviews; B is the closest distractor — sampling oversight does not cure a prohibited delegation; D misassigns the duty to the CCO rather than the designated Supervisor.
questions.source_quote: "The designated Supervisor responsible for discretionary accounts must not delegate the performance of the reviews required in subsections 3970(1) and 3970(2) to any other person." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8384

questions.stem: Corina Vasquez is an Executive of Pickwick Securities and is also approved as a Portfolio Manager. She initiates a discretionary order for a discretionary account. By when must the designated Supervisor review Corina's order under subsection 3970(5)?

options.A: Prior to the order being entered, as with all discretionary orders
options.B: No later than the day after the trade was made
options.C: Within the quarterly managed account review cycle
options.D: Within 30 days, aligned with the temporary-hold review period

questions.rationale: Subsection 3970(5) requires a designated Supervisor to review any discretionary order initiated for a discretionary account by an Executive who is approved as a Portfolio Manager no later than the day after the trade was made. Option A states the 3970(4) default for an ordinary Registered Representative, the closest distractor — Corina's dual status as Executive and PM places her in the later-review provision; C borrows the managed account cycle from 3973; D borrows the 30-day cadence from temporary holds under 3222, neither of which governs discretionary order review.
questions.source_quote: "A designated Supervisor must review any discretionary order initiated for a discretionary account by an Executive who is approved as a Portfolio Manager, no later than the day after the trade was made." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8392

questions.scenario: Larkfield Wealth Inc., an Investment Dealer, runs a managed account program built around centralized model portfolios: investment decisions are made centrally and applied across hundreds of retail managed accounts, subject to minor client-directed restrictions. Solenne Mercier is the designated Supervisor responsible for managed accounts; the program's investment decisions are made by Portfolio Manager Aurelio Vanthier. A compliance sweep of the program surfaces four matters: (a) Solenne asks whether she must individually review every managed account each quarter, given the centralized model; (b) managed account client Dominique Aubry emailed written notice on Tuesday at 10:00 a.m. terminating her managed account agreement, yet Aurelio continued making new discretionary purchases in her account that week, citing 'the 30-day wind-down in the agreement'; (c) Aurelio caused several managed accounts to subscribe for newly issued units directly from an investment fund for which Aurelio himself acts as adviser, with no client consent on file; and (d) the firm has no standing governance body for the program — its managed account supervision policies were last reviewed three years ago by the Chief Compliance Officer acting alone.
questions.stem: Matter (a): Solenne asks whether she must individually review every managed account each quarter, given that decisions are made centrally across the program. Under section 3973:

options.A: Yes — every managed account must be reviewed individually each quarter with no exceptions
options.B: No — quarterly reviews of managed accounts are optional where a managed account committee exists
options.C: Reviews are required only annually for centrally managed programs
options.D: The quarterly review may be done at an aggregate level where investment decisions are made centrally and apply to a number of managed accounts, subject to minor variations for client-directed restrictions and the timing of cash flows

questions.rationale: Subsection 3973(1) requires the designated Supervisor to review each managed account quarterly for reasonable assurance the client's objectives are being pursued and handling complies with Corporation requirements, and 3973(2) permits that quarterly review to be done at an aggregate level where decisions are made centrally and apply to a number of managed accounts, subject to minor variations for client-directed investment restrictions and the timing of client cash flows. Option A denies the express aggregate-level accommodation; B wrongly treats the committee in 3972 as displacing the review; C halves the prescribed frequency.
questions.source_quote: "If the investment decisions for a managed account are made centrally and apply to a number of managed accounts, the quarterly review may be done at an aggregate level," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8449
questions.qa_note: 2026-06-05: scenario synced to the wave-4 clarified wording of matter (c) (set-denormalization fix; matter (c) tested only by SUP-E6-0099, sibling keys unaffected).

questions.stem: Quillan Securities approved Tomas Ferreira as a Registered Representative dealing with retail clients five weeks ago; it is his first approval anywhere. His Supervisor proposes treating his accounts under the firm's ordinary review program from day one. What does section 3947 require instead?

options.A: Nothing additional — new approvals carry no special supervision once proficiency exams are passed
options.B: Close supervision for two years, the same period as the experience requirement for discretionary authorization
options.C: Pre-approval by the Supervisor of every order Tomas enters, indefinitely
options.D: The Dealer Member must closely supervise Registered Representatives dealing with retail clients for six months after approval, as set out in the Registered Representative / Investment Representative Monthly Supervision Report, and keep a copy of every such report

questions.rationale: Subsection 3947(1) requires a Dealer Member to closely supervise Registered Representatives and Investment Representatives dealing with retail clients for six months after approval, as set out in the Registered Representative / Investment Representative Monthly Supervision Report, and 3947(3) requires the firm to complete and keep a copy of every such report for the Corporation's inspection; the 3947(2) prior-approval exemption does not apply because this is Tomas's first approval. Option C describes the strict measures for Approved Persons with infraction histories contemplated by 3927(3), not routine new-RR supervision; B borrows the two-year figure from 3275; A ignores the rule entirely.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must closely supervise Registered Representatives and Investment Representatives dealing with retail clients for six months after approval," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8197

questions.stem: Maberly Wealth converted most of its retail book to flat-fee accounts where no per-trade commission is charged. Its legacy monthly review still selects accounts for supervisory review purely by gross commissions generated. Under subsection 3945(3), what is deficient?

options.A: Nothing — commission-based selection automatically captures all account types
options.B: Fee-based accounts are exempt from monthly supervision because no commissions arise
options.C: The firm must develop policies and procedures that specifically address supervising retail accounts where a commission is not charged, using criteria other than commission levels
options.D: Fee-based accounts need only be reviewed annually, at fee-renewal time

questions.rationale: Subsection 3945(3) requires the Dealer Member to develop policies and procedures that specifically address supervising retail client accounts where a commission is not charged for trades, such as fee based accounts, and those policies must address account activity review requirements and use criteria other than commission levels — a commission-only filter leaves the fee-based book unreviewed. Option B converts a selection-criteria problem into an exemption that does not exist; A is empirically false because zero-commission accounts never trip a commission screen; D invents an annual cycle.
questions.source_quote: "These policies and procedures must: (i) address account activity review Corporation requirements, and (ii) use criteria other than commission levels." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8177

questions.stem: Compliance at Esterhazy Securities is building the account-flagging logic that feeds supervisory reviews of the retail book. Under subsection 3945(4), which retail client accounts must the Dealer Member specifically designate for supervision purposes?

options.A: Non-client accounts, discretionary accounts, managed accounts, registered accounts, and restricted accounts
options.B: Only accounts that have generated a client complaint in the prior year
options.C: Only margin accounts and short accounts
options.D: Accounts of clients older than 65 and accounts under powers of attorney

questions.rationale: Subsection 3945(4) requires the Dealer Member to specifically designate the following retail client accounts for supervision purposes: non-client accounts, discretionary accounts, managed accounts, registered accounts, and restricted accounts. Option B substitutes a complaint trigger for the standing designations; C lists credit-related account types the subsection does not name; D describes vulnerability-related considerations that, while supervisory concerns elsewhere, are not the prescribed 3945(4) designations.
questions.source_quote: "(i) non-client accounts, (ii) discretionary accounts, (iii) managed accounts, (iv) registered accounts, and (v) restricted accounts." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8182

questions.stem: An Approved Person at Roseisle Capital wants to begin a phone campaign encouraging suitable existing clients to start using the firm's covered-call writing program. Under subsection 3962(5), what must the firm's derivatives policies and procedures specifically require before such solicitation?

options.A: Notice to the local securities commission of the campaign script
options.B: The designated Supervisor's approval of the solicitation of clients to use derivatives programs, as well as clients' actual use of derivatives
options.C: Nothing — solicitation controls apply only to OEO accounts
options.D: A two-week cooling-off period between solicitation and the first derivatives trade

questions.rationale: Clause 3962(5)(i) requires policies and procedures that specifically require the designated Supervisor to approve the solicitation of clients to use derivatives programs, as well as clients' actual use of derivatives — campaigns to promote a derivatives program need Supervisor approval at both the solicitation and the usage stage. Option A substitutes a commission filing for the internal approval the rule prescribes; C inverts the rule — OEO units do not solicit at all; D invents a cooling-off period found nowhere in 3962.
questions.source_quote: "require the designated Supervisor to approve the solicitation of clients to use derivatives programs, as well as clients' actual use of derivatives," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8323

questions.stem: Two years ago, Aldergrove Securities approved a principal-protected note for its shelf after full due diligence. The issuer has since been downgraded twice and the note's secondary market has dried up, but the firm has not looked at the product since approval. Which limb of the firm's product due diligence obligation under subsection 3301(1) has been neglected?

options.A: The obligation to assess structure, features, risks and costs before approval
options.B: The obligation to obtain client acknowledgement of product risk
options.C: The obligation to take reasonable steps to monitor the securities for significant changes
options.D: The obligation to cap shelf size at a fixed number of products

questions.rationale: Clause 3301(1)(iii) requires the Dealer Member to take reasonable steps to monitor the securities or derivatives for significant changes — product due diligence does not end at approval, and a double downgrade plus liquidity collapse are significant changes the monitoring step exists to catch. Option A describes the pre-approval assessment limb in 3301(1)(i), which the firm did perform; B describes a client-facing acknowledgement that is not part of 3301; D invents a shelf cap.
questions.source_quote: "monitor the securities or derivatives for significant changes." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5497

questions.stem: Before recommending an approved split-share corporation to a retail client at Innisfree Capital, what does subsection 3302(1) require of the Approved Person personally?

options.A: Confirmation that the firm's research department has a buy rating on the product
options.B: Nothing — once the firm approves a product for the shelf, individual understanding is presumed
options.C: Completion of a product-specific exam administered by the issuer
options.D: Steps to understand the securities, including their structure, features, risks, initial and ongoing costs and the impact of those costs

questions.rationale: Subsection 3302(1) prohibits an Approved Person from purchasing or selling securities for, or recommending securities to, a client unless the Approved Person takes steps to understand the securities, including the securities' structure, features, risks, initial and ongoing costs and the impact of those costs — the personal know-your-product layer sits on top of firm shelf approval. Option B is the closest distractor — firm approval under 3301 does not discharge the individual obligation in 3302; A substitutes a research rating for personal understanding; C invents an issuer exam.
questions.source_quote: "the Approved Person takes steps to understand the securities or derivatives, including the securities' or derivatives' structure, features, risks, initial and ongoing costs and the impact of those costs." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5503

questions.stem: Ondine Clearing Corp., an Investment Dealer, acts solely as carrying broker for the client accounts introduced by Pinewood Securities; it provides trade execution, clearing, settlement and custody only. Pinewood's clients hold products Ondine has never reviewed. Does Ondine breach section 3301 by not running product due diligence on those holdings?

options.A: Yes — every Dealer Member must perform product due diligence on every security its systems touch
options.B: Yes, unless Pinewood indemnifies Ondine in the carrying agreement
options.C: No — section 3301 does not apply in respect to an account maintained at a Dealer Member who is a carrying broker for that account or who only provides trade execution, clearing, settlement or custody services
options.D: No, because product due diligence applies only to derivatives, not securities

questions.rationale: Subsection 3303(1) provides that section 3301 does not apply in respect to an account maintained at a Dealer Member who is a carrying broker for that account or who only provides trade execution, clearing, settlement or custody services (or a combination) to another Dealer Member, portfolio manager, exempt market dealer or their respective clients — the introducing dealer, not the carrying broker, owes product due diligence on its shelf. Option A ignores the express exemption; B treats a private indemnity as the source of the exemption, which flows from the rule itself; D misstates 3301's scope, which covers securities and derivatives alike.
questions.source_quote: "Section 3301 does not apply in respect to an account maintained at a Dealer Member who is a carrying broker for that account or who only provides trade execution, clearing, settlement or custody services" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5510

questions.stem: Beyond exam-based proficiency, what ongoing training does clause 2604(1)(iii) require Stratheden Securities to provide to its Approved Persons?

options.A: On-going training on compliance with Corporation requirements, securities laws and applicable laws, including the obligations relating to conflicts of interest, know-your-client, account appropriateness, product due diligence, know-your-product, and suitability determination
options.B: Annual re-examination on the full Canadian Investment Regulatory Exam syllabus
options.C: Training only when CIRO issues a new guidance note
options.D: Sales-skills coaching delivered by product manufacturers

questions.rationale: Clause 2604(1)(iii) requires the Dealer Member to provide on-going training to its Approved Persons on compliance with Corporation requirements, securities laws, and applicable laws including, without limitation, the obligations relating to conflicts of interest, know-your-client, account appropriateness, product due diligence, know-your-product, and suitability determination — KYC and KYP obligations are sustained through continuing dealer training, not exams alone. Option B converts the training duty into annual re-examination; C makes a standing obligation event-driven; D substitutes manufacturer sales coaching for compliance training.
questions.source_quote: "a Dealer Member must provide on-going training to its Approved Persons on compliance with Corporation requirements, securities laws, and applicable laws" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3201

questions.stem: While collecting KYC information from a new individual retail client, what must Galloway Securities concurrently take reasonable steps to obtain under subsection 3202(4)?

options.A: The client's most recent notice of assessment from the tax authority
options.B: The name and contact information of a trusted contact person, and the client's written consent for the dealer to contact that person about concerns such as possible financial exploitation or the client's mental capacity
options.C: A power of attorney in favour of the Registered Representative
options.D: The names of the client's beneficiaries under their will

questions.rationale: Subsection 3202(4) requires the Dealer Member, concurrently with the KYC steps, to take reasonable steps to obtain the name and contact information of a trusted contact person and the written consent of the client for the Dealer Member to contact that person to confirm or make inquiries about the dealer's concerns regarding possible financial exploitation, the client's mental capacity, the client's legal representative, or the client's contact information; under 3202(5) this does not apply to non-individual clients. Option C confuses a trusted contact — who holds no authority over the account — with a power of attorney; A and D request documents the rule does not seek.
questions.source_quote: "reasonable steps to obtain from the client the name and contact information of a trusted contact person, and the written consent of the client for the Dealer Member to contact the trusted contact person" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4441

questions.stem: After Kelfield Securities collects a new retail client's KYC information, the Registered Representative files it without further client contact, reasoning that the client supplied it personally. What follow-up step does subsection 3202(3) require?

options.A: Within a reasonable time after receiving the information, take reasonable steps to have the client confirm its accuracy
options.B: Have the branch receptionist witness the client's signature
options.C: Send the KYC file to CIRO for verification
options.D: No step — accuracy is solely the client's risk once information is supplied

questions.rationale: Subsection 3202(3) requires that, within a reasonable time after receiving the information collected under subsection 3202(1), the Dealer Member take reasonable steps to have the client confirm the accuracy of such information — a confirmation loop that also educates the client on what the firm has recorded about their circumstances. Option D shifts to the client a verification duty the rule places on the dealer; B substitutes witnessing for confirmation of content; C sends the file to the regulator, which the rule does not require.
questions.source_quote: "a Dealer Member must take reasonable steps to have a client confirm the accuracy of such information." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4438

questions.stem: Pendleton Wealth holds advisory, managed and discretionary retail accounts. Its procedures call for refreshing the KYC information collected under clause 3202(1)(iii) on a single five-year cycle for all account types. Against subsection 3209(4), the cycle is:

options.A: Compliant — five years is the prescribed maximum for every account type
options.B: Non-compliant only for the discretionary accounts, which require monthly KYC refreshes
options.C: Non-compliant across the board: the information must be reviewed no less frequently than once every 36 months, except for managed accounts and discretionary accounts, which must be reviewed no less frequently than once every 12 months
options.D: Compliant provided each client signs an annual statement waiving more frequent reviews

questions.rationale: Subsection 3209(4) requires the Dealer Member to review the information collected under clause 3202(1)(iii) no less frequently than once every 36 months, except for a managed account and a discretionary account which must be reviewed no less frequently than once every 12 months — a five-year cycle breaches both standards. Option B garbles the heightened standard, which is annual KYC review, not monthly (monthly is the 3970(1) financial-performance review); A invents a five-year maximum; D invents a client waiver of the review frequency.
questions.source_quote: "no less frequently than once every 36 months, except for a managed account and a discretionary account which must be reviewed no less frequently than once every 12 months." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4553

questions.stem: A retail client at Mosgrove Securities asks her Registered Representative how she will know whether her portfolio is 'doing well.' The Supervisor checks that the firm's Relationship Disclosure supports this education. Which content item does sub-clause 3216(5)(ii)(m) require on this point?

options.A: A guarantee that the account will outperform the S&P/TSX Composite
options.B: The Registered Representative's own historical performance record
options.C: A monthly ranking of the client's returns against other clients of the branch
options.D: A general explanation of how investment performance benchmarks might be used to assess the performance of a client's investments and any options for benchmark information the Dealer Member might make available

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 3216(5)(ii)(m) requires the relationship disclosure to contain a general explanation of how investment performance benchmarks might be used to assess the performance of a client's investments and any options for benchmark information that might be made available to the client by the Dealer Member — equipping the client to interpret performance. Option A converts an explanation into a performance guarantee; C compares against other clients, raising confidentiality issues and matching no rule; B substitutes the representative's record for benchmark education.
questions.source_quote: "a general explanation of how investment performance benchmarks might be used to assess the performance of a client's investments and any options for benchmark information that might be made available to the client by the Dealer Member," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4820

questions.stem: Harlowe Capital's Relationship Disclosure describes its suitability process. Per paragraph 3216(5)(ii)(d)(III), the document must state that the dealer will determine that any investment action it takes, recommends or decides on for the client is suitable and puts the client's interest first, including in which of the following situations?

options.A: When securities are received into or delivered out of the client's account by way of deposit, withdrawal or transfer
options.B: Only when the client explicitly requests a suitability check
options.C: Whenever the TSX falls more than 5% in a single session
options.D: Only at account opening and at account closing

questions.rationale: Paragraph 3216(5)(ii)(d)(III)(A) lists, among the situations the suitability statement must cover, when securities are received into or delivered out of the client's account by way of deposit, withdrawal or transfer — alongside a change of responsible Approved Person, KYC changes, security changes, and the 3209(4) periodic review. Option C is the closest distractor — paragraph (IV) requires only a statement of whether suitability will be reviewed on other triggering events such as significant market fluctuations, so market moves are not a mandatory determination trigger; B and D shrink the disclosed process below the rule's list.
questions.source_quote: "securities are received into or delivered out of the client's account by way of deposit, withdrawal or transfer," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4767

questions.stem: Renfrew Park Securities is appointing Dario Bellini as the designated Supervisor over Registered Representatives who advise retail clients on securities and derivatives. Dario will write the Supervisor Exam. Under sub-clause 2603(1)(i)(i), what additional exam burden attaches to his supervisory role?

options.A: None — the Supervisor Exam alone qualifies a Supervisor for any supervisory assignment
options.B: If supervising trading in, or accounts for those who trade in securities, derivatives, or managed accounts, the exam requirements applicable to the individuals being supervised also apply, except the Canadian Investment Regulatory Exam is not required if the Supervisor meets the prescribed experience requirement
options.C: The Chief Compliance Officer Exam, since supervision is a compliance function
options.D: The Derivatives Exam only, regardless of what those supervised actually trade

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 2603(1)(i)(i) requires a Supervisor to complete the Supervisor Exam and, if supervising trading in, or accounts for those who trade in securities, derivatives, or managed accounts, the exam requirements applicable to the individuals being supervised listed in sub-clauses 2603(1)(i)(a) to (f) and (h), except that the Canadian Investment Regulatory Exam is not required where the Supervisor satisfies the experience requirements in sub-clause 2603(1)(ii)(d). Option A stops at the Supervisor Exam and misses the mirrored requirements; C assigns the CCO's exam to a line Supervisor; D fixes on one exam without reference to the supervised activity.
questions.source_quote: "If supervising trading in, or accounts for those who trade in securities, derivatives, or managed accounts, the exam requirements applicable to the individuals being supervised" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3143

questions.stem: Wrexham Capital is sponsoring an application for approval from Lina Moroz, who passed the prescribed Retail Securities Exam four and a half years ago, has never been approved in any category, and has been out of the industry since. The Supervisor checks exam validity under subsection 2628(1). Is Lina's exam still valid?

options.A: Yes — prescribed exams never expire once passed
options.B: Yes, provided she completes a refresher webinar before approval
options.C: No — an exam is deemed successfully completed only if completed within three years prior to the application, or the individual was previously approved in a category requiring that exam within three years prior, or gained one year of relevant industry experience within three years prior; Lina meets none of these
options.D: No, because exams are valid for ten years and hers has two years left, but only for institutional approval
