An RR exploited this to retroactively justify unsuitable trades. Which specific account-supervision requirement does this design defeat?

options.A: The requirement in subsection 3926(5) that policies and procedures include controls for accessing and amending client records
options.B: The requirement that clients approve their own KYC updates by wet signature
options.C: The requirement that risk profiles be reviewed only by the board of directors
options.D: The requirement that all client records be stored exclusively at head office

questions.rationale: Subsection 3926(5) requires a dealer's policies and procedures to include controls for accessing and amending client records — precisely the control that uncontrolled, unlogged edits defeat, and the control whose absence allowed the record to be rewritten after the fact. No wet-signature rule exists in 3926 (B), the board does not review individual risk profiles (C), and records may be kept at branches subject to periodic on-site reviews under 3908(4), so exclusive head-office storage is not required (D).
questions.source_quote: A Dealer Member's policies and procedures must include controls for accessing and amending client records.

questions.stem: Meridian Bluff Capital designates Tessa Wren as Supervisor for new account approvals. Wren has never read the IDPC account rules and is unfamiliar with the firm's own account-opening procedures, relying entirely on what her assistant tells her. Against subsection 3925(3), what is the defect?

options.A: None, provided her assistant is well informed
options.B: She holds two supervisory designations simultaneously, which is prohibited
options.C: The designated Supervisor must be familiar with applicable Corporation requirements, securities laws and applicable laws and the dealer's policies and procedures
options.D: She has not been approved as an Executive, which all designated Supervisors require

questions.rationale: Subsection 3925(3) requires the designated Supervisor to be familiar with applicable Corporation requirements, securities laws and applicable laws and the dealer's policies and procedures; secondhand reliance on an assistant is not familiarity. Delegating knowledge does not cure the defect (A), nothing in 3925 prohibits multiple designations (B), and designated Supervisors are not required to be Executives (D).
questions.source_quote: The designated Supervisor must be familiar with applicable Corporation requirements, securities laws and applicable laws and the Dealer Member's policies and procedures.

questions.stem: Korrigan Wealth hires several new supervisory staff and tells them verbally to 'use good judgment' when reviewing account activity, providing no documentation of what is expected. Measured against subsection 3926(4), what must the dealer provide to supervisory staff?

options.A: An annual cash bonus tied to the number of reviews completed
options.B: A list of clients exempt from all supervisory review
options.C: Discretion to design their own review procedures without firm involvement
options.D: Written procedures to be followed in reviewing account activity and written confirmation of the dealer's expectations of supervisory staff with respect to their roles and responsibilities

questions.rationale: Subsection 3926(4) requires the dealer to provide all supervisory staff with written procedures to be followed in reviewing account activity and written confirmation of the dealer's expectations of supervisory staff with respect to their supervisory roles and responsibilities. Verbal instructions fail both written elements. Compensation design is not the requirement (A), individual discretion does not satisfy the firm's obligation to provide written procedures (C), and no review-exempt client list exists (B).
questions.source_quote: confirmation of the Dealer Member's expectations of supervisory staff, with respect to their supervisory roles and responsibilities

questions.stem: At Silver Birch Direct, branch Supervisor Marc Delisle is qualified to supervise equity trading but not the options activity his team has recently begun. The firm wants to keep Delisle in place. According to the Account Supervision Guidance, which arrangement is acceptable?

options.A: Delisle may continue to supervise the options activity as long as he reads the trade blotters carefully
options.B: The firm must terminate all options business at the branch
options.C: The options activity may simply go unsupervised because Delisle is unqualified
options.D: Divide the supervision between two or more Supervisors, with appropriate mechanisms for them to communicate, a system maintaining an overall view of the client's situation and activity, and a clear and complete assignment of responsibilities

questions.rationale: The Account Supervision Guidance states that where a Supervisor is unqualified to supervise trading in certain products, the dealer may divide supervision between two or more Supervisors provided there are appropriate communication mechanisms, the system ensures an overall view of the client's situation and activity, and the assignment of responsibilities is clear and complete; a primary Supervisor advised by others is also possible. An unqualified Supervisor continuing alone violates 3905(5) (A), terminating the business is not required (B), and leaving activity unsupervised is never acceptable (C).
questions.source_quote: the system ensures the Dealer maintains an overall view of the client's situation and activity, and the assignment of responsibilities is clear and complete.

questions.stem: Avoca Securities' branch Supervisor is dually-registered and personally advises a book of her own retail clients. She also conducts the first-tier review of all trading at her branch, including her own client trades. According to the Account Supervision Guidance, what should the dealer ensure?

options.A: Nothing further; first-tier review of one's own trades is standard practice
options.B: That another Supervisor reviews that Supervisor's advice to and trades for clients
options.C: That she resigns either her supervisory role or her client book within 30 days
options.D: That her own trades are reviewed only at year-end by the external auditor

questions.rationale: The Account Supervision Guidance states that where a Supervisor is dually-registered and also advises or trades for their own clients, the dealer should ensure another Supervisor reviews that Supervisor's advice to and trades for clients. Self-review of one's own trades is exactly the conflict the guidance addresses (A), the guidance permits the dual role with independent review rather than forcing resignation (C), and review by the external auditor at year-end is not a supervisory review (D).
questions.source_quote: another Supervisor reviews that Supervisor's advice to and trades for clients

questions.stem: Drumlin Capital shares its premises with an affiliated insurance agency. Its policies address client signage, but the firm has no system for communicating Corporation requirements to the employees and Approved Persons working at the shared location, and no process to confirm those requirements are understood. Which elements of subsection 3918(2) remain unmet?

options.A: A system for communicating Corporation requirements relating to employees and Approved Persons at the shared office premises, and a process providing reasonable assurance those requirements are understood and implemented
options.B: Only the requirement to obtain client consent to the shared premises
options.C: Only the requirement to register the premises with the local land registry
options.D: None; signage policies alone fully discharge section 3918

questions.rationale: Subsection 3918(2) requires adequate supervisory resources, a system for communicating Corporation requirements relating to employees and Approved Persons at the shared office premises, and a process providing reasonable assurance that those requirements are understood and implemented. Two of the three elements are missing here. Client consent (B) and land registration (C) are not section 3918 requirements, and signage addresses only the client-understanding objective in 3918(1), not the 3918(2) infrastructure (D).
questions.source_quote: a system for communicating Corporation requirements relating to employees and Approved Persons at the shared office premises, and

questions.stem: An online application at Vanterra Direct discloses that the applicant is a director of a TSX-listed reporting issuer. The automated workflow approves the account with no human involvement. Measured against the Element 2 syllabus parameters on automation controls, what is the deficiency?

options.A: None; insider status is relevant only after the first trade is placed
options.B: The application should have been rejected because insiders may not open accounts
options.C: The applicant being an insider is a factor that may trigger a manual review, so the application should not have completed on a purely automated path
options.D: The deficiency is that the application was taken online rather than in a branch

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameters for outcome 2.12 list 'applicant being an insider' among the factors that may trigger a manual review within an automated process. Insider status is an account-opening consideration, not merely post-trade (A); insiders may open accounts — the control is heightened review, not refusal, defeating B, the closest distractor; and the channel itself is permitted, so online intake is not the problem (D).
questions.source_quote: Applicant being an insider

questions.stem: Pemberley Wealth's Supervisors review and approve account openings as Corporation requirements demand, but the approvals are given orally in morning meetings and never captured anywhere. Applying subsection 3908(2), what must the dealer have?

options.A: A system in place to record the review and approval, conducted by any Supervisor, that is required under Corporation requirements
options.B: A notarized affidavit from each Supervisor at year-end attesting to the year's approvals
options.C: Video recordings of all morning meetings retained for ten years
options.D: Nothing further, because oral approval by a designated Supervisor is itself the record

questions.rationale: Subsection 3908(2) requires a dealer to have a system in place to record the review and approval conducted by any Supervisor that is required under Corporation requirements. Oral, uncaptured approvals leave no record (D). A year-end affidavit is not a contemporaneous recording system (B), and no video-retention requirement exists (C).
questions.source_quote: A Dealer Member must have a system in place to record the review and approval, conducted by any Supervisor that is required under Corporation requirements.

questions.stem: Tonbridge Securities documents the compliance issues its Supervisors identify but keeps no record of how those issues were resolved, and retains no documentation of the on-site branch reviews it conducts. Applying subsection 3908(3), what must the firm's records of supervisory activity include?

options.A: Only the names and designation dates of its Supervisors
options.B: Adequate records of supervisory activity, including on-site branch reviews, compliance issues identified and the resolution of such issues
options.C: Only records of issues that resulted in client compensation
options.D: Only records requested in writing by the Corporation in advance

questions.rationale: Subsection 3908(3) requires a dealer to maintain adequate records of supervisory activity, including on-site branch reviews, compliance issues identified and the resolution of such issues — both missing elements here. Names and designation dates are the separate 3908(1) register (A), the obligation is not limited to issues producing compensation (C), and records must be maintained proactively, not only on advance Corporation request (D).
questions.source_quote: A Dealer Member must maintain adequate records of supervisory activity, including on-site branch reviews, compliance issues identified and the resolution of such issues.

questions.stem: A CIRO examiner asks Caldera Direct to demonstrate, from its records, the supervision actions the firm has taken over the past several years. The firm's record-keeping policy never contemplated supervision actions as a record category. Applying subsection 3804(2), which prescribed record category does this omission breach?

options.A: Records that permit determination of the dealer's capital position
options.B: Records that provide securities pricing as required by securities laws
options.C: Records that document compliance, training, and supervision actions taken by the Dealer Member
options.D: Records that document the dealer's sales practices and compensation arrangements

questions.rationale: Clause 3804(2)(xv) requires records that document compliance, training, and supervision actions taken by the dealer — exactly the category never contemplated here. Capital-position records (A, clause (ii)), pricing records (B, clause (x)) and sales-practice/compensation records (D, clause (xvii)) are real categories in 3804(2) but are not the one engaged by the examiner's supervision-actions request.
questions.source_quote: document compliance, training, and supervision actions taken by the Dealer Member,

questions.stem: CIRO requests Wexford Capital's supervision records in a specified electronic format. The firm responds that it will provide paper printouts at its own convenience within six months. Applying subsection 3804(4), is this response adequate?

options.A: Yes; a dealer may choose any format and timeline for producing records
options.B: No, because records may only ever be produced through legal counsel
options.C: Yes, provided the printouts are certified by the firm's external auditor
options.D: No; a dealer must make its records available to the Corporation on request, in the manner requested by the Corporation

questions.rationale: Subsection 3804(4) requires a dealer to make its records available to the Corporation on request, in the manner requested by the Corporation — which includes the requested electronic format and responsiveness to the request, not the firm's own timeline. Format and timing are not the dealer's choice (A), auditor certification is not a substitute for compliant production (C), and no counsel-only production channel exists (B).
questions.source_quote: A Dealer Member must make its records available to the Corporation on request, in the manner requested by the Corporation.

questions.stem: Supervisor Elias Navarro at Larkspur Securities wants to delegate the pre-approval of a restricted Registered Representative's orders to that same RR's sales assistant, who reports to the RR. Setting aside the assistant's qualifications, what threshold restriction in section 3907 does this arrangement engage?

options.A: Any delegation of supervisory tasks must not be contrary to Corporation requirements, securities laws and applicable laws
options.B: Delegations are permitted only to employees of CIRO
options.C: Delegation requires the delegate to hold the Supervisor qualification themselves
options.D: Delegation is prohibited whenever the delegated task occurs daily

questions.rationale: Subsection 3907(2) provides that any delegation of supervisory tasks must not be contrary to Corporation requirements, securities laws and applicable laws — delegating supervision of a restricted RR's orders to a person subordinate to that RR conflicts with the supervisory purpose of the requirement. Delegates need not be CIRO employees (B), nor must they themselves be qualified Supervisors — qualification by registration, training or experience suffices under 3907(3) (C), and frequency of the task is not a prohibition criterion (D).
questions.source_quote: Any delegation of supervisory tasks must not be contrary to Corporation requirements, securities laws and applicable laws.

questions.scenario: Thornbury Pacific Securities, a mid-sized Investment Dealer, is preparing for the annual audit of its fiscal year Form 1 filing by its panel auditor. During audit planning, four issues surface: (1) the firm's Executives have identified several operations with a high risk of loss where inexpensive preventive fixes are available, yet the firm has implemented no preventive controls there, relying entirely on after-the-fact detection; (2) a back-office manager has quietly directed staff to set aside certain securities-custody exception reports 'until after the audit'; (3) the firm's record of which Executive manages each significant area of risk was never updated after a reorganization, so two risk areas have no documented Executive responsible for them; and (4) after the fiscal year-end audit date but before the Form 1 filing date, a large trading loss materially reduced the firm's risk adjusted capital level. The firm's CFO asks the compliance department to assess the issues against the IDPC rules.
questions.stem: Issue (1) — high-loss-risk operations left without preventive controls despite inexpensive available fixes — is measured against which internal-control requirement in Part B of Rule 4200?

options.A: When necessary, a dealer must implement preventive controls based on the Executives' view of the risk of loss and the cost-benefit relationship of controlling that risk
options.B: Preventive controls are optional in all circumstances because detective controls are always sufficient
options.C: Preventive controls must be implemented for every process regardless of cost
options.D: Preventive controls may be implemented only with CIRO's prior written approval

questions.rationale: Section 4223 requires a dealer, when necessary, to implement preventive controls based on the Executives' view of the risk of loss and the cost-benefit relationship of controlling that risk. Where loss risk is high and the fix is inexpensive, that cost-benefit analysis points to implementation, so relying solely on detection is deficient — defeating B, the closest distractor. The rule is risk- and cost-conditioned, not absolute (C), and no CIRO pre-approval is required (D).
questions.source_quote: a Dealer Member must implement preventive controls based on the Dealer Member's Executives' view of the risk of loss and the cost-benefit relationship of controlling that risk.

questions.scenario: Thornbury Pacific Securities, a mid-sized Investment Dealer, is preparing for the annual audit of its fiscal year Form 1 filing by its panel auditor. During audit planning, four issues surface: (1) the firm's Executives have identified several operations with a high risk of loss where inexpensive preventive fixes are available, yet the firm has implemented no preventive controls there, relying entirely on after-the-fact detection; (2) a back-office manager has quietly directed staff to set aside certain securities-custody exception reports 'until after the audit'; (3) the firm's record of which Executive manages each significant area of risk was never updated after a reorganization, so two risk areas have no documented Executive responsible for them; and (4) after the fiscal year-end audit date but before the Form 1 filing date, a large trading loss materially reduced the firm's risk adjusted capital level. The firm's CFO asks the compliance department to assess the issues against the IDPC rules.
questions.stem: Issue (4) — the large trading loss occurring after the fiscal year-end audit date but before the Form 1 filing, materially reducing risk adjusted capital — engages which obligation of the firm's auditor under section 4192?

options.A: None; the auditor's responsibility ends at the fiscal year-end audit date
options.B: The auditor must restate the prior year's Form 1 to absorb the loss
options.C: The auditor must personally arrange replacement capital for the firm
options.D: The auditor must report on any subsequent events, to the date of filing, which have had a material adverse effect on the dealer's risk adjusted capital level

questions.rationale: Subsection 4192(2) requires the dealer's auditor to report on any subsequent events, to date of filing, which have had material adverse effect on the dealer's risk adjusted capital level. The auditor's responsibility therefore extends past the fiscal year-end audit date through to filing — defeating A, the closest distractor. Restating the prior period is not the prescribed mechanism for a post-year-end event (B), and arranging capital is not an auditor function (C).
questions.source_quote: The Dealer Member's auditor must report on any subsequent events, to date of filing, which have had material adverse effect on the Dealer Member's risk adjusted capital level.

questions.scenario: Thornbury Pacific Securities, a mid-sized Investment Dealer, is preparing for the annual audit of its fiscal year Form 1 filing by its panel auditor. During audit planning, four issues surface: (1) the firm's Executives have identified several operations with a high risk of loss where inexpensive preventive fixes are available, yet the firm has implemented no preventive controls there, relying entirely on after-the-fact detection; (2) a back-office manager has quietly directed staff to set aside certain securities-custody exception reports 'until after the audit'; (3) the firm's record of which Executive manages each significant area of risk was never updated after a reorganization, so two risk areas have no documented Executive responsible for them; and (4) after the fiscal year-end audit date but before the Form 1 filing date, a large trading loss materially reduced the firm's risk adjusted capital level. The firm's CFO asks the compliance department to assess the issues against the IDPC rules.
questions.stem: Compliance recommends Thornbury Pacific document its internal controls more rigorously going forward. Applying section 4224, what written record must the firm maintain?

options.A: Only a one-page summary of controls, refreshed every decade
options.B: Verbal briefings to staff, documented only if CIRO requests it
options.C: A record kept solely by the external auditor in its working papers
options.D: A detailed written record of its internal controls, including at a minimum the policies and procedures the Executives have approved to provide reasonable assurance of compliance with Corporation requirements relating to internal controls

questions.rationale: Section 4224 requires a dealer to maintain a detailed written record of its internal controls, including at a minimum the Executive-approved policies and procedures providing reasonable assurance of compliance with all Corporation requirements relating to internal controls. A thin decennial summary fails the 'detailed' standard (A), the obligation is the dealer's, not the auditor's — auditor working papers under 4191 are separate (C), and verbal briefings are not a written record (B).
questions.source_quote: A Dealer Member must maintain a detailed written record of its internal controls, including, at a minimum, the policies and procedures the Dealer Member's Executives have approved

questions.scenario: Thornbury Pacific Securities, a mid-sized Investment Dealer, is preparing for the annual audit of its fiscal year Form 1 filing by its panel auditor. During audit planning, four issues surface: (1) the firm's Executives have identified several operations with a high risk of loss where inexpensive preventive fixes are available, yet the firm has implemented no preventive controls there, relying entirely on after-the-fact detection; (2) a back-office manager has quietly directed staff to set aside certain securities-custody exception reports 'until after the audit'; (3) the firm's record of which Executive manages each significant area of risk was never updated after a reorganization, so two risk areas have no documented Executive responsible for them; and (4) after the fiscal year-end audit date but before the Form 1 filing date, a large trading loss materially reduced the firm's risk adjusted capital level. The firm's CFO asks the compliance department to assess the issues against the IDPC rules.
questions.stem: While the two significant risk areas in issue (3) remain without a documented Executive responsible for them, which ongoing duty prescribed by section 3909 has no one performing it for those areas?

options.A: The duty to file each risk area's results with the Corporation weekly
options.B: The duty to obtain client consent before changing Executive assignments
options.C: The duty of each Executive to supervise and direct the dealer's activities, in accordance with the areas of its responsibility, to provide reasonable assurance of compliance
options.D: The duty to liquidate business lines that lack an assigned Executive

questions.rationale: Subsection 3909(1) requires each Executive to supervise and direct the activities of the dealer, and its employees and Approved Persons, in accordance with the areas of its responsibility, to provide reasonable assurance of compliance with Corporation requirements and securities laws. With no Executive documented as responsible for two risk areas, that supervision-and-direction duty has no owner for them. No weekly filing of risk-area results exists (A), client consent is irrelevant to internal assignments (B), and liquidation is not the prescribed remedy — assignment is (D).
questions.source_quote: Each Executive must supervise and direct the activities of the Dealer Member, and its employees and Approved Persons, in accordance with the areas of its responsibility, to provide reasonable assurance of compliance with Corporation requirements and securities laws.

questions.scenario: CIRO Enforcement Staff are investigating Renfrew Gate Securities over alleged discretionary trading by a former Registered Representative, Olivia Stamatis, who left the firm and the industry four years ago. Enforcement Staff send the firm a written request under Rule 8100 to produce supervisory review records for Stamatis's former client accounts and to make her former branch Supervisor, Victor Okoye, available to answer questions under oath. Okoye, who still works at the firm, tells colleagues he intends to 'say as little as possible'; a second Supervisor who is aware of the investigation quietly shreds his own handwritten review notes for the affected accounts. The next week, Enforcement Staff arrive unannounced at the branch during business hours and ask to image a workstation hard drive; the branch manager refuses entry, insisting on five business days' written notice. The firm's general counsel also questions whether CIRO can pursue Stamatis at all given her departure.
questions.stem: General counsel argues that because Stamatis left the industry four years ago, CIRO has no jurisdiction over her. Analyzed against Rule 8200, is this correct?

options.A: Yes; jurisdiction over an individual ends on their resignation date
options.B: No; a Regulated Person remains subject to Rule 8200 for six years after ceasing to be a representative of a Dealer Member, and a proceeding may be commenced up to six years after the last event on which it is based
options.C: Yes, unless Stamatis consents in writing to CIRO's jurisdiction
options.D: No, because CIRO's jurisdiction over former registrants is perpetual

questions.rationale: Subsection 8206(1) keeps a Regulated Person subject to Rule 8200 for six years after ceasing to be, among other things, an employee or representative of a Dealer Member, and subsection 8206(2) allows the Corporation to commence a proceeding up to six years after the last event on which it is based. Four years out is within both windows. Jurisdiction does not end at resignation (A), consent is irrelevant (C), and jurisdiction is time-limited, not perpetual — defeating D, the closest distractor.
questions.source_quote: The Corporation may commence a proceeding under Rule 8200 against a Regulated Person up to six years after the date of the occurrence of the last event on which the proceeding is based.

questions.scenario: CIRO Enforcement Staff are investigating Renfrew Gate Securities over alleged discretionary trading by a former Registered Representative, Olivia Stamatis, who left the firm and the industry four years ago. Enforcement Staff send the firm a written request under Rule 8100 to produce supervisory review records for Stamatis's former client accounts and to make her former branch Supervisor, Victor Okoye, available to answer questions under oath. Okoye, who still works at the firm, tells colleagues he intends to 'say as little as possible'; a second Supervisor who is aware of the investigation quietly shreds his own handwritten review notes for the affected accounts. The next week, Enforcement Staff arrive unannounced at the branch during business hours and ask to image a workstation hard drive; the branch manager refuses entry, insisting on five business days' written notice. The firm's general counsel also questions whether CIRO can pursue Stamatis at all given her departure.
questions.stem: What obligation does Rule 8100 place on Renfrew Gate Securities itself with respect to Okoye's intended approach to the investigation?

options.A: None; cooperation is a personal choice for each employee
options.B: The firm must instruct Okoye to answer only written questions
options.C: A Regulated Person must require its employees to cooperate with Enforcement Staff conducting an investigation and to comply with a request made under section 8103
options.D: The firm must suspend Okoye for the duration of the investigation

questions.rationale: Subsection 8104(3) requires a person to cooperate with Enforcement Staff and requires a Regulated Person to require its employees, partners, directors and officers to cooperate and to comply with section 8103 requests — so the firm must direct Okoye to cooperate, including attending and answering questions under clause 8103(1)(iv). Cooperation is not optional (A), the format of questioning is set by the request, not the firm (B), and suspension is not a prescribed obligation (D).
questions.source_quote: a Regulated Person must require its employees, partners, directors and officers to cooperate with Enforcement Staff conducting an investigation and to comply with a request made under section 8103

questions.scenario: CIRO Enforcement Staff are investigating Renfrew Gate Securities over alleged discretionary trading by a former Registered Representative, Olivia Stamatis, who left the firm and the industry four years ago. Enforcement Staff send the firm a written request under Rule 8100 to produce supervisory review records for Stamatis's former client accounts and to make her former branch Supervisor, Victor Okoye, available to answer questions under oath. Okoye, who still works at the firm, tells colleagues he intends to 'say as little as possible'; a second Supervisor who is aware of the investigation quietly shreds his own handwritten review notes for the affected accounts. The next week, Enforcement Staff arrive unannounced at the branch during business hours and ask to image a workstation hard drive; the branch manager refuses entry, insisting on five business days' written notice. The firm's general counsel also questions whether CIRO can pursue Stamatis at all given her departure.
questions.stem: How does Rule 8100 treat the second Supervisor's shredding of his handwritten review notes?

options.A: It is acceptable because handwritten notes are personal property, not firm records
options.B: It violates the prohibition on a person aware of an investigation concealing or destroying any record that may be relevant to the investigation
options.C: It is acceptable provided the firm's retention period for those notes has expired
options.D: It is a problem only if Enforcement Staff had already named those notes in a written production request

questions.rationale: Subsection 8104(4) prohibits a person who is aware that Enforcement Staff are conducting an investigation from concealing or destroying any record, document or thing that contains information that may be relevant to the investigation, or asking another person to do so. The prohibition turns on awareness of the investigation, not on whether the specific records were named in a production request — defeating D, the closest distractor. Supervisory notes are records of supervisory activity, not personal property (A), and retention-period expiry does not authorize destroying records relevant to a known investigation (C).
questions.source_quote: must not conceal or destroy any record, document or thing that contains information that may be relevant to the investigation

questions.scenario: CIRO Enforcement Staff are investigating Renfrew Gate Securities over alleged discretionary trading by a former Registered Representative, Olivia Stamatis, who left the firm and the industry four years ago. Enforcement Staff send the firm a written request under Rule 8100 to produce supervisory review records for Stamatis's former client accounts and to make her former branch Supervisor, Victor Okoye, available to answer questions under oath. Okoye, who still works at the firm, tells colleagues he intends to 'say as little as possible'; a second Supervisor who is aware of the investigation quietly shreds his own handwritten review notes for the affected accounts. The next week, Enforcement Staff arrive unannounced at the branch during business hours and ask to image a workstation hard drive; the branch manager refuses entry, insisting on five business days' written notice. The firm's general counsel also questions whether CIRO can pursue Stamatis at all given her departure.
questions.stem: Analyze the branch manager's refusal of entry pending five business days' written notice against section 8103.

options.A: It is wrong; in connection with an investigation, Enforcement Staff may enter the business location of any Regulated Person during business hours with or without prior notice, with free access to records including computer hard-drive images
options.B: It is correct; on-site entry always requires five business days' written notice
options.C: It is correct for hard-drive imaging, which requires a court order in all cases
options.D: It is wrong only if the investigation concerns the branch manager personally

questions.rationale: Subsection 8103(3) authorizes Enforcement Staff, in connection with an investigation, to enter the business location of any Regulated Person during business hours with or without prior notice, with free access to and the right to copy books and records of every description, including by taking an image of computer hard drives. No notice period is prescribed (B), the rule itself authorizes hard-drive imaging without requiring a court order (C), and the entry power does not depend on who the investigation targets (D).
questions.source_quote: may, with or without prior notice, enter the business location of any Regulated Person during business hours,

questions.scenario: Stellarton Online Investing, an order execution only division of an Investment Dealer, automates large parts of its supervision. Its account-opening workflow auto-approves routine applications, and software flags trading activity against risk criteria for human review. Supervisor Priya Raghunathan has delegated the first screening of the flagged-activity queue to a qualified senior analyst, Dario Mancuso. A consultant's review finds: Mancuso has no instruction on what to do when he encounters a serious red flag, so he has been quietly clearing items he is unsure about; Raghunathan's delegation policy says she will 're-examine the arrangement if problems come up' but identifies no specific circumstances that would prompt her to step back in; and last month the automated account-opening workflow approved, without any human involvement, an application in which the applicant granted trading authorization over the account to her brother.
questions.stem: Mancuso's practice of quietly clearing items he is unsure about exposes the absence of which documented control for delegation listed in the Element 2 syllabus parameters?

options.A: A requirement that the delegate be paid a supervisory differential
options.B: A prohibition on delegating any review touching client accounts
options.C: A requirement that the delegate hold an Executive designation
options.D: Processes for escalating any significant issues to the delegator

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameters for outcome 2.10 require, among the documented controls for delegation, processes for escalating any significant issues to the delegator — exactly what Mancuso lacked when serious red flags surfaced, leading him to clear them instead. No pay differential is prescribed (A), reviews touching client accounts are delegable subject to controls (B), and delegates need qualification by registration, training or experience, not an Executive designation (C).
questions.source_quote: Processes for escalating any significant issues to the delegator

questions.scenario: Stellarton Online Investing, an order execution only division of an Investment Dealer, automates large parts of its supervision. Its account-opening workflow auto-approves routine applications, and software flags trading activity against risk criteria for human review. Supervisor Priya Raghunathan has delegated the first screening of the flagged-activity queue to a qualified senior analyst, Dario Mancuso. A consultant's review finds: Mancuso has no instruction on what to do when he encounters a serious red flag, so he has been quietly clearing items he is unsure about; Raghunathan's delegation policy says she will 're-examine the arrangement if problems come up' but identifies no specific circumstances that would prompt her to step back in; and last month the automated account-opening workflow approved, without any human involvement, an application in which the applicant granted trading authorization over the account to her brother.
questions.stem: Raghunathan's 're-examine if problems come up' policy falls short of which documented control for delegation in the Element 2 syllabus parameters?

options.A: A requirement that all delegations expire after 90 days
options.B: A list of factors that trigger a review by the delegator
options.C: A requirement that CIRO pre-approve each individual delegation
options.D: A prohibition on delegating to anyone outside the compliance department

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameters for outcome 2.10 require a list of factors that trigger a review by the delegator; a vague 'if problems come up' standard identifies no such factors. No 90-day expiry exists (A), CIRO does not pre-approve individual delegations (C), and delegates may be qualified persons outside compliance (D).
questions.source_quote: A list of factors that trigger a review by the delegator

questions.scenario: Stellarton Online Investing, an order execution only division of an Investment Dealer, automates large parts of its supervision. Its account-opening workflow auto-approves routine applications, and software flags trading activity against risk criteria for human review. Supervisor Priya Raghunathan has delegated the first screening of the flagged-activity queue to a qualified senior analyst, Dario Mancuso. A consultant's review finds: Mancuso has no instruction on what to do when he encounters a serious red flag, so he has been quietly clearing items he is unsure about; Raghunathan's delegation policy says she will 're-examine the arrangement if problems come up' but identifies no specific circumstances that would prompt her to step back in; and last month the automated account-opening workflow approved, without any human involvement, an application in which the applicant granted trading authorization over the account to her brother.
questions.stem: The consultant asks whether Stellarton's use of software to flag specific trading activity for human review is itself a permissible supervision design. What does the Account Supervision Guidance say?

options.A: No; review items must be selected manually by a Supervisor in all cases
options.B: No; technology may be used only to archive completed reviews
options.C: Yes; the Guidance states dealers can use technology to select specific activity for review
options.D: Yes, but only if every flagged item is also re-selected manually

questions.rationale: The Account Supervision Guidance expressly states that dealers can use technology to select specific activity for review, within a risk-based approach where the reviewer has all relevant information needed to assess risk. Manual selection is not mandated (A), technology's role is not limited to archiving (B), and no parallel manual re-selection is required (D).
questions.source_quote: Dealers can use technology to select specific activity for review.

questions.scenario: Stellarton Online Investing, an order execution only division of an Investment Dealer, automates large parts of its supervision. Its account-opening workflow auto-approves routine applications, and software flags trading activity against risk criteria for human review. Supervisor Priya Raghunathan has delegated the first screening of the flagged-activity queue to a qualified senior analyst, Dario Mancuso. A consultant's review finds: Mancuso has no instruction on what to do when he encounters a serious red flag, so he has been quietly clearing items he is unsure about; Raghunathan's delegation policy says she will 're-examine the arrangement if problems come up' but identifies no specific circumstances that would prompt her to step back in; and last month the automated account-opening workflow approved, without any human involvement, an application in which the applicant granted trading authorization over the account to her brother.
questions.stem: According to the Element 2 syllabus parameters on automation controls, how should Stellarton's automated workflow have treated the application granting trading authorization to the applicant's brother?

options.A: Approved it automatically, because trading authorizations carry no supervisory significance
options.B: Routed it for manual review, because a trading authorization being used is a factor that may trigger a manual review
options.C: Rejected it outright, because trading authorizations are prohibited on automated platforms
options.D: Approved it automatically, provided the brother is over the age of majority

questions.rationale: The syllabus parameters for outcome 2.12 list 'trading authorization being used' among the factors that may trigger a manual review of an application processed through automation. The presence of an authorization is supervisory-relevant (A), but it does not make the account prohibited — it changes the review path, not the outcome (C). The authorized person's age does not remove the trigger (D).
questions.source_quote: Trading authorization being used

questions.stem: Glassbrook Capital Corp. has detailed written supervisory policies and procedures but staffs its compliance and supervision group so thinly that monthly account reviews routinely go unfinished. Which IDPC requirement does this staffing decision breach?

options.A: The requirement to assign sufficient personnel and commit adequate resources necessary to fully and properly apply and enforce its policies and procedures
options.B: The requirement to file its staffing plan with CIRO each quarter
options.C: The requirement that every Supervisor hold an Executive designation
options.D: The requirement to outsource supervision when internal staff is insufficient

questions.rationale: Section 3905(1) requires the dealer to assign sufficient personnel and commit adequate resources necessary to fully and properly apply and enforce its policies and procedures — written procedures that cannot be executed for lack of staff fail this standard. Option B invents a quarterly staffing filing; C confuses Supervisors with Executives, which are distinct designations under 3905; D treats outsourcing as a mandated cure, which no rule requires.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must assign sufficient personnel and commit adequate resources necessary to fully and properly apply and enforce its policies and procedures." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7921

questions.stem: The directors of Tarradale Investments Ltd. argue that responsibility for ensuring an appropriate supervision system rests entirely with the Chief Compliance Officer, not with them. How does IDPC Rule 3900 allocate this responsibility?

options.A: Each branch Supervisor is individually responsible for the firm-wide supervision system
options.B: CIRO designs the supervision system, so no internal party bears responsibility for it
options.C: The Dealer Member's board of directors is responsible for ensuring that an appropriate supervision system is in place
options.D: The external auditor certifies the supervision system annually, relieving the board

questions.rationale: Subsection 3901(3) places responsibility for ensuring an appropriate supervision system is in place on the dealer's board of directors. The CCO monitors and assesses compliance and reports upward, but that role does not displace the board's responsibility — the closest distractor is the directors' own argument. A inverts the hierarchy; B and D shift a non-delegable governance duty to the regulator and the auditor.
questions.source_quote: "The Dealer Member's board of directors is responsible for ensuring that an appropriate supervision system is in place." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7877

questions.stem: Mistaya Securities operates a six-person branch in a remote town with no on-site Supervisor. Under CIRO's Account Supervision Guidance, which factors may the dealer weigh in deciding whether a resident Supervisor is necessary at that location?

options.A: The branch's profitability and the local cost of office space
options.B: The number of Registered Individuals in the location, their experience, the business conducted there, Supervisor availability in nearby locations, and other systems and controls mitigating remote supervision risk
options.C: Only the total assets under administration at the branch
options.D: Whether the branch staff have requested a resident Supervisor

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 s.1.1.3 lists exactly these factors for determining the necessity of a resident Supervisor: number of Registered Individuals, their experience in the location, the business conducted, Supervisor availability nearby, and other mitigating systems and controls; where there is no resident Supervisor, an assigned outside Supervisor must visit periodically. Options A and C substitute commercial metrics the guidance does not use; D makes the decision turn on staff preference rather than supervision-risk factors.
questions.source_quote: "the number of Registered Individuals in the location, Registered Individuals' experience in the location, the business conducted in the location, Supervisor availability in nearby locations, and other systems and controls mitigating remote supervision risk." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 163

questions.stem: The Executives of Copperline Financial are deciding whether to implement a costly preventive control over wire transfers. Under IDPC section 4223, on what basis must the decision to implement preventive controls be made?

options.A: Preventive controls must be implemented for every identified risk regardless of cost
options.B: Preventive controls are optional and may be omitted whenever detective controls exist
options.C: Preventive controls may only be implemented with prior CIRO approval of the control design
options.D: When necessary, based on the Executives' view of the risk of loss and the cost-benefit relationship of controlling that risk

questions.rationale: Section 4223(1) requires preventive controls to be implemented, when necessary, based on the Executives' view of the risk of loss and the cost-benefit relationship of controlling that risk — a judgment-based standard, not an absolute mandate. Option A removes the cost-benefit judgment the rule builds in; B wrongly treats detective controls as a complete substitute; C invents a regulator pre-approval step for internal control design.
questions.source_quote: "When necessary, a Dealer Member must implement preventive controls based on the Dealer Member's Executives' view of the risk of loss and the cost-benefit relationship of controlling that risk." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 9248

questions.stem: During a CIRO examination, Yellowrock Securities can describe its internal controls verbally but produces no documentation of them. Which IDPC requirement has the dealer failed?

options.A: The requirement to publish its internal controls on its public website
options.B: The requirement to translate internal controls into both official languages
options.C: The requirement to file internal controls with each provincial securities commission
options.D: The requirement to maintain a detailed written record of its internal controls, including at a minimum the policies and procedures the Executives have approved

questions.rationale: Section 4224(1) requires the dealer to maintain a detailed written record of its internal controls, including at a minimum the policies and procedures the Executives have approved to provide reasonable assurance of compliance with internal control requirements — verbal descriptions cannot satisfy a written-record rule. Options A, B and C invent publication, translation and multi-regulator filing duties that section 4224 does not impose; the record is maintained by the dealer, not broadcast.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must maintain a detailed written record of its internal controls, including, at a minimum, the policies and procedures the Dealer Member's Executives have approved" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 9252

questions.stem: Two Executives at Foxhaven Capital disagree about whether the firm's reconciliation controls are adequate, and they ask whether the IDPC Rules prescribe a quantitative adequacy test. What standard do the rules set for determining whether internal controls are adequate?

options.A: Internal controls are adequate if the firm has had no losses in the past three years
options.B: Adequacy is determined exclusively by the external auditor's annual opinion
options.C: The Executives must use best judgment in determining whether internal controls are adequate
options.D: Controls are adequate if they cost at least 2% of the dealer's annual revenue

questions.rationale: Subsection 4222(3) requires the Executives to use best judgment in determining whether internal controls are adequate — the rule sets a judgment standard rather than a numeric test. Option A substitutes a backward-looking loss record for forward-looking judgment; B assigns the determination to the auditor when 4222(2) makes it an Executive responsibility; D invents a spending ratio that appears nowhere in Rule 4200.
questions.source_quote: "The Dealer Member's Executives must use best judgment in determining whether internal controls are adequate." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 9245

questions.stem: A client of Larkfield Wealth will be at sea for four months and asks her Registered Representative to exercise trading authority over her account while she is unreachable; the RR did not propose the arrangement. Under the IDPC Rule 1200 definition, which conditions characterize a discretionary account?

options.A: The account is exempt from suitability, the authority is dealer-solicited, and the term is unlimited
options.B: The client retains sole responsibility for all investment decisions
options.C: Investment decisions are made on a continuing basis by a Portfolio Manager
options.D: The account is subject to suitability, the dealer has not solicited the discretionary authority, the authority is accepted to accommodate a client frequently or temporarily unavailable to authorize trades, it has not been renewed, and its term does not exceed 12 months

questions.rationale: The Rule 1200 definition of discretionary account requires that the account is subject to the suitability determination, the dealer has not solicited the authority, the authority is accepted to accommodate a client who is frequently or temporarily unavailable, it has not been renewed, and the term does not exceed 12 months — the sea-voyage facts fit this model. Option C states the managed-account definition, the closest distractor since both involve discretion; B describes the OEO model; A reverses the suitability, solicitation and term elements.
questions.source_quote: "(i) the Dealer Member has not solicited the discretionary authority, (ii) the discretionary authority is accepted to accommodate a client who is frequently or temporarily unavailable to authorize trades," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 348

questions.stem: Brindlemere Securities describes one of its account programs as follows: the account is subject to suitability, and investment decisions are made on a continuing basis by a Portfolio Manager, an Associate Portfolio Manager, or a third party hired by the dealer. Under IDPC Rule 1200, what type of account is this?

options.A: An order execution only account
options.B: A direct electronic access account
options.C: A discretionary account
options.D: A managed account

questions.rationale: The Rule 1200 definition of managed account is an account subject to a suitability determination where investment decisions are made on a continuing basis by a Portfolio Manager, Associate Portfolio Manager or a third party hired by the dealer, with the dealer or third party and the PM/APM responsible for all investment decisions. Option C is the closest distractor: a discretionary account also involves discretion but is an unsolicited, non-renewed accommodation with a maximum 12-month term, not continuing professional management; A and B are channels where the dealer gives no recommendations at all.
questions.source_quote: "(i) investment decisions are made on a continuing basis by a Portfolio Manager or an Associate Portfolio Manager or a third party hired by the Dealer Member," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 529

questions.stem: Agent Renate Olufsen of Dunbar Hill Investments wants to begin teaching paid evening finance courses at a local college. Under the minimum terms of the dealer-agent agreement, what must occur before she may take on this outside activity?

options.A: She must disclose the activity to the dealer and obtain its written consent
options.B: She need only notify CIRO directly, bypassing the dealer
options.C: Nothing — agents, unlike employees, face no outside activity conditions
options.D: She must resign her agency agreement first

questions.rationale: Clause 2304(7)(viii)(a) requires the agent to agree not to conduct any outside activity without disclosing to and obtaining the written consent of the dealer; the dealer then monitors and enforces compliance directly rather than through another employer or principal. Option C states the misconception that agent status loosens conduct requirements — Rule 2300 imposes them through the agreement; B routes disclosure to the regulator instead of the dealer that must consent; D is unnecessary because the regime exists precisely to manage outside activities within the agency relationship.
questions.source_quote: "The agent agrees not to conduct any outside activity without disclosing to and obtaining the written consent of the Dealer Member." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1850

questions.stem: When agent Theo Marchetti's agency agreement with Windgate Securities ends, he insists the client files and trade blotters he kept at his storefront office belong to him because he created and stored them. Under the minimum terms of the dealer-agent agreement, who owns those books and records?

options.A: Marchetti, because the records were created on his premises
options.B: Ownership is split equally between the agent and the dealer
options.C: CIRO takes custody of all agent records on termination
options.D: The books and records kept by the agent for the dealer's business are the dealer's property and must be delivered to the dealer on termination of the agreement

questions.rationale: Clause 2304(7)(x) requires the agreement to provide that the books and records kept by the agent for the dealer's business conform to Corporation requirements, are the dealer's property, are available at all times for review by and delivery to the dealer, and shall be delivered to the dealer on termination. Option A reflects the common physical-possession fallacy the clause is designed to defeat; B invents an apportionment; C wrongly inserts the regulator into a dealer-property obligation.
questions.source_quote: "(b) are the Dealer Member's property, (c) are available at all times for review by and delivery to the Dealer Member, and (d) shall be delivered to the Dealer Member on termination of the agreement." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1871

questions.stem: Cobble Hill Securities uses agents and must make the prescribed liability disclosure in its account application. According to the form of agreement in IDPC section 2303, what does the prescribed disclosure language tell clients about the dealer's liability for an agent's acts and omissions?

options.A: The dealer is liable only up to the amount of commissions the client paid
options.B: The dealer's liability is conditional on the client first suing the agent
options.C: The dealer is irrevocably liable to the client for any acts and omissions of the investment advisor with regard to the dealer's business as if the investment advisor were an employee
options.D: The dealer disclaims all liability for agents' conduct

questions.rationale: Item 5 of the section 2303 form prescribes account-application language stating that the dealer is irrevocably liable to the client for any acts and omissions of the investment advisor with regard to the dealer's business as if the investment advisor were an employee, and that by continuing to deal with the firm the client accepts the offer of indemnity. Option D is the opposite of the prescribed disclosure; A and B add caps and litigation preconditions the language does not contain — the liability is irrevocable and employee-equivalent.
questions.source_quote: "is irrevocably liable to you for any acts and omissions of your investment advisor with regard to [Dealer Member name] business as if the investment advisor were an employee of [Dealer Member name]." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1747

questions.stem: Ironquill Markets offers retail clients an account in which the dealer provides no recommendations, the account is not subject to suitability determination (other than limited exceptions), and clients have been provided with direct electronic access within the meaning of National Instrument 23-103. Under IDPC Rule 1200, which additional condition forms part of the definition of this direct electronic access account?

options.A: The client must be an Approved Person of another dealer
options.B: The dealer complies with the Universal Market Integrity Rule requirements applicable to the direct electronic access service offering and the requirements of NI 23-103
options.C: The account must hold only fixed income products
options.D: All orders must be reviewed by a Supervisor before entry

questions.rationale: The Rule 1200 definition of direct electronic access account has three limbs: the client has been provided with direct electronic access within the meaning of NI 23-103, the dealer provides no recommendations, and the dealer complies with the UMIR requirements applicable to the DEA service offering and the requirements of NI 23-103. Option D contradicts the un-intermediated nature of DEA, which is exactly why the definitional compliance conditions exist; A and C invent client-status and product restrictions found nowhere in the definition.
rule_refs: IDPC 1201 (definition of direct electronic access account)
questions.source_quote: "(iii) the Dealer Member complies with the Universal Market Integrity Rule requirements applicable to the direct electronic access service offering and the requirements of NI 23-103." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 340

questions.stem: Aurelia Stone, an individual with $13 million in securities under management, wants Pickford Securities' institutional desk to serve her rather than its retail division. What is required, under the IDPC Rule 1200 institutional client definition, for an individual in her position to receive institutional classification?

options.A: No path exists — individuals can never be institutional clients regardless of assets
options.B: Nothing beyond the asset level — exceeding $10 million classifies an individual as institutional automatically
options.C: She must request and consent to being classified as an institutional client, in addition to having total securities and precious metals bullion under administration or management exceeding $10 million
options.D: Only if she first registers as a portfolio manager

questions.rationale: Branch (vi) of the institutional client definition covers an individual with total securities and precious metals bullion under administration or management exceeding $10 million who requests and consents to being classified as an institutional client — for individuals, unlike non-individuals under branch (v), the request-and-consent step is part of the definition. Option B is the closest distractor: it drops the request-and-consent element that distinguishes the individual branch; A ignores branch (vi) entirely; D imports a registration status from branch (iv), which applies to non-individual registrants.
questions.source_quote: "(vi) an individual with total securities and precious metals bullion under administration or management exceeding $10 million who requests and consents to being classified as an institutional client," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 483

questions.stem: Cormorant Grain Ltd., a food processor, wants Westerlee Securities to classify it as an institutional client for its derivatives accounts on the basis that it is a hedger. Under the IDPC Rule 1200 definition of hedger, which characteristic must Cormorant exhibit?

options.A: It must hold more than $10 million in securities under management
options.B: It must be a non-individual that is exposed to one or more risks as a necessary part of its business activities and seeks to hedge such risk by engaging in derivatives transactions
options.C: It must be registered as a derivatives dealer in its home province
options.D: It must trade derivatives at least monthly for speculative purposes

questions.rationale: The Rule 1200 definition of hedger is a non-individual that is exposed to one or more risks as a necessary part of its business activities and seeks to hedge such risk by engaging in derivatives transactions meeting the underlying-interest, intended-effect and offset conditions; under branch (vii) of the institutional client definition, a hedger may then request and consent to institutional classification for accounts with qualifying hedging activities. Option A imports the asset-threshold branch, which is an alternative route, not the hedger test; D is the opposite — speculation is what the hedging conditions exclude; C invents a registration requirement.
questions.source_quote: "A non-individual that: (i) is exposed to one or more risks as a necessary part of its business activities, and (ii) seeks to hedge such risk by engaging in derivatives transactions" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 430

questions.stem: A Registered Representative at Quartzline Capital began exercising discretion over a client's account two weeks ago; the discretionary account agreement is still in draft and unsigned. Which sequencing requirement of IDPC section 3273 has the dealer breached?

options.A: The dealer must enter into a discretionary account agreement with the client prior to accepting the account as a discretionary account
options.B: Discretionary agreements must be signed in the presence of a notary
options.C: Agreements may be completed within 90 days of the first discretionary trade
options.D: Only the client, not the dealer, needs to sign the agreement, so timing is irrelevant

questions.rationale: Clause 3273(1)(iv) requires the dealer to enter into a discretionary account agreement with the client prior to accepting the account as a discretionary account, and under clause (v) the designated Supervisor must approve the account and the signed agreement — discretion exercised before the agreement exists violates the sequencing. Option C invents a grace period that does not exist; B adds a formality the rule does not require; D misses that the gating issue is the agreement's existence before acceptance, not who signs.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must enter into a discretionary account agreement with the client prior to accepting the account as a discretionary account," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5359

questions.stem: A client of Strathnairn Wealth signed a discretionary account agreement 11 months ago and now asks to extend the same agreement for another year because the arrangement works well. How do the IDPC requirements treat this request?

options.A: The agreement may be renewed annually without limit if the client consents in writing
options.B: The agreement may be extended once for a further 6 months
options.C: The agreement automatically converts to a managed account agreement at 12 months
options.D: It cannot be accommodated by renewal — a discretionary account agreement must have a maximum term of no longer than 12 months and must not be renewable

questions.rationale: Clauses 3274(1)(iii) and (iv) require the discretionary account agreement to have a maximum term of no longer than 12 months and to not be renewable — the discretionary regime is a temporary accommodation, so continuing discretion requires a different arrangement (such as a managed account with qualified personnel), not renewal. Option A is the closest distractor because written client consent cures many things, but not a flat non-renewability rule; B invents a partial extension; C describes a conversion mechanism that does not exist automatically.
questions.source_quote: "(iii) have a maximum term of no longer than 12 months, (iv) not be renewable, and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5373

questions.stem: Pellbrook Securities proposes to authorize Registered Representative Anouk Yazdani to effect trades for discretionary accounts. She has 14 months of experience trading the equities and options the accounts will hold. Why does the proposal fail under IDPC section 3275?

options.A: Only Executives may be authorized to effect discretionary trades
options.B: Registered Representatives may never trade options on a discretionary basis
options.C: Discretionary authority requires ten years of industry experience
options.D: A Registered Representative may only be authorized if they have at least two years of active experience in trading, advising or performing analysis with respect to all types of products to be traded on a discretionary basis

questions.rationale: Subsection 3275(1) permits a Registered Representative to be authorized to affect discretionary trades only if they have at least two years of active experience in trading, advising or performing analysis with respect to all types of products to be traded on a discretionary basis, and the account is maintained at the dealer for which the RR conducts business — 14 months falls short. Option C inflates the threshold; A confuses the experience requirement with the order-review exceptions in 3970(4) for PMs and Executives; B invents a product ban where the rule instead requires experience across all products traded.
questions.source_quote: "the Registered Representative has at least two years of active experience in trading, advising or performing analysis with respect to all types of products that are to be traded on a discretionary basis," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5383

questions.stem: Tannerby Investments manages client portfolios in its in-house managed account program and waives trade confirmations under written client consents. A new operations manager asks which fee condition applies when the dealer itself manages the account and relies on the confirmation waiver in IDPC section 3816. Which condition is correct?

options.A: The account must pay a performance fee of at least 10% to qualify
options.B: The account is not charged any commissions or fees based on the volume or value of transactions in the account, and the dealer sends a compliant monthly statement containing the prescribed confirmation information
options.C: All fees must be paid annually in advance
options.D: The waiver removes any obligation to report the trades to the client in any document

questions.rationale: Sub-paragraph 3816(2)(x)(a)(IV)(B) conditions the managed-account confirmation waiver, where the dealer manages the account, on the account not being charged any commissions or fees based on the volume or value of transactions, and on the dealer sending a monthly statement compliant with section 3808 containing the confirmation information (with limited exceptions available on request) — the waiver presumes the asset-based fee model. Option D overstates the relief: trade information still reaches the client through the monthly statement; A inverts the fee logic; C invents a payment-timing condition.
questions.source_quote: "the account is not charged any commissions or fees based on the volume or value of transactions in the account," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7684

questions.stem: Marrowstone Securities sells a retail client a corporate bond that the issuer may redeem before maturity and whose coupon rate floats with a reference rate. Which notations must appear on the trade confirmation under IDPC section 3816 for this debt purchase?

options.A: No special notations — debt confirmations show only price and quantity
options.B: A notation of the bond's credit rating from two agencies
options.C: The notation "callable" because the security is subject to call prior to maturity, and the notation "The coupon rate may vary." because it has a variable coupon rate
options.D: A notation that the bond is insured by CIPF

questions.rationale: Sub-clauses 3816(2)(vi)(b)(II) and (III) require, for a debt purchase, the notation "callable" where the security is subject to call prior to maturity through any means, and the notation "The coupon rate may vary." where the coupon rate is variable — the disclosure points address exactly the call and coupon features that make fixed income supervisory risks hard for retail clients to see. Option A ignores the yield and notation content the rule prescribes; B substitutes ratings, which 3816 does not require; D misstates CIPF, which protects client property on insolvency, not bond performance.
questions.source_quote: "where the debt security is subject to call prior to maturity through any means, the notation of "callable" must be included, and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7603

questions.stem: Harrowsmith Securities Inc. is controlled by Harrowsmith Bank. A retail client buys units of a mutual fund sponsored by the bank, branded under an unrelated name. What must the trade confirmation disclose under IDPC section 3816?

options.A: Nothing, because mutual fund trades never require relationship disclosure
options.B: The relationship between the dealer and the financial institution, because the trade is in securities of a mutual fund sponsored by the financial institution and the names are not sufficiently similar to indicate the affiliation
options.C: The fund manager's compensation grid
options.D: A guarantee that the fund's value will not fall below the purchase price

questions.rationale: Clause 3816(2)(ix) requires a dealer controlled by or affiliated with a financial institution to disclose the relationship on each trade confirmation for a trade in securities of a mutual fund sponsored by the financial institution or its controlled/affiliated corporation, except where the names of the dealer and the fund are sufficiently similar to indicate common control or affiliation — here the unrelated branding removes the exception. Option A overlooks this fund-specific disclosure; C and D invent compensation and guarantee content that confirmation rules do not require.
questions.source_quote: "the relationship between the Dealer Member and the financial institution shall be disclosed on each trade confirmation issued in connection with a trade in securities of a mutual fund sponsored by the financial institution" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7654

questions.stem: A Registered Representative at Veldstone Capital wants to recommend a newly listed equity to several clients before the firm's product committee has assessed it, arguing that ordinary common shares need no approval. What does IDPC section 3301 say about this?

options.A: Common shares are exempt from product due diligence, so the recommendation may proceed
options.B: The RR may recommend the security if she personally understands it well
options.C: An Approved Person must not purchase securities for, or recommend securities to, a client unless the securities have been approved by the dealer to be made available to clients
options.D: Approval is required only for derivatives, not securities

questions.rationale: Subsection 3301(2) prohibits an Approved Person from purchasing securities or derivatives for, or recommending them to, a client unless they have been approved by the dealer under the product due diligence process in 3301(1) — even a plain equity must pass through the dealer's assess-approve-monitor process before being made available. Option B is the closest distractor: personal understanding satisfies the separate KYP obligation in 3302 but does not replace dealer-level approval; A and D invent product-class exemptions that section 3301 does not contain (the exemptions in 3303 are account-based, for carrying brokers and execution/clearing/settlement/custody-only service).
questions.source_quote: "An Approved Person must not purchase securities or derivatives for, or recommend securities or derivatives to, a client unless the securities or derivatives have been approved by the Dealer Member to be made available to clients under subsection 3301(1)." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5498

questions.stem: Lockridge Direct acts solely as a carrying broker for the accounts of Pintail Advisors, an introducing firm whose clients hold structured products and crypto assets. Lockridge asks whether it must run its own product due diligence on those holdings. How does IDPC section 3303 answer?

options.A: Section 3301 does not apply in respect of an account maintained at a dealer who is a carrying broker for that account or who only provides trade execution, clearing, settlement or custody services for it
options.B: Carrying brokers must duplicate the introducing firm's full product due diligence
options.C: Carrying brokers are exempt only for products trading above $100 per unit
options.D: The exemption applies only to mutual funds

questions.rationale: Subsection 3303(1) provides that section 3301 does not apply in respect to an account maintained at a dealer who is a carrying broker for that account or who only provides trade execution, clearing, settlement or custody services, or a combination, to another dealer, portfolio manager, exempt market dealer or their clients — the due diligence burden sits with the firm making the products available, not the service provider. Option B would impose redundant diligence the exemption removes; C and D invent price and product-class limits that do not exist in 3303(1).
questions.source_quote: "Section 3301 does not apply in respect to an account maintained at a Dealer Member who is a carrying broker for that account or who only provides trade execution, clearing, settlement or custody services" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5510

questions.stem: Before recommending a fee-heavy principal-protected note, a Registered Representative at Carradine Wealth wants to know how far her duty to understand the product extends. Under IDPC section 3302, what determines the steps she must take to understand the security?

options.A: Reading the issuer's press release is always sufficient
options.B: She must complete a CIRO product certification for each structured note
options.C: She must hold the product personally for six months before recommending it
options.D: The steps required are those that are reasonable to enable the Approved Person to meet their obligations under Rule 3400

questions.rationale: Subsection 3302(2) calibrates the know-your-product duty: the steps required to understand the securities or derivatives are those that are reasonable to enable the Approved Person to meet their suitability obligations under Rule 3400 — for an opaque structured note with embedded costs, the reasonable steps are correspondingly deeper. Option A understates the duty for a complex product; C and B invent personal-holding and certification requirements; the rule sets a purpose-driven reasonableness standard, not fixed formalities.
questions.source_quote: "For purposes of subsection 3302(1), the steps required to understand the securities or derivatives are those that are reasonable to enable the Approved Person to meet their obligations under Rule 3400." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5506

questions.stem: Crossfell Capital's account supervision policies treat all retail clients identically when screening for improper market activity. According to CIRO's Account Supervision Guidance, which clients may a dealer determine present a higher risk of improper market activity, warranting risk-based review attention?

options.A: Clients who hold index funds and GICs exclusively
options.B: Clients who have access to material non-public information about issuers, holders of control blocks of public issuers, and market professionals
options.C: Clients over the age of 65
options.D: Clients who trade less than once per year

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 s.1.2 states a dealer may determine some clients present a higher risk of improper market activity, such as those who have access to material non-public information about issuers, holders of control blocks of public issuers and market professionals — and a risk-based approach may direct review resources toward them. Options A and D describe low-activity, plain-vanilla profiles that the guidance does not flag; C concerns vulnerability and financial-exploitation supervision, a different risk axis from improper market activity.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer may determine some clients present a higher risk of improper market activity, such as those who have access to material non-public information about issuers, holders of control blocks of public issuers and market professionals." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 200

questions.stem: Bramblewood Securities is allocating its product shelf to the syllabus risk categories for training purposes. Its analysts ask where asset-backed securities sit in the Element 3 framework. Which classification is correct?

options.A: Asset-backed securities are one of the basic types of securities under outcome 3.6, alongside equities
options.B: Asset-backed securities are a derivative type under outcome 3.8, alongside swaps
options.C: Asset-backed securities are a complex type of securities/products under outcome 3.7, alongside leveraged and inverse ETFs and structured products
options.D: Asset-backed securities are excluded from the Supervisor Exam syllabus entirely
