questions.rationale: The 3.7 parameter enumerates the complex types of securities/products as leveraged and inverse ETFs, structured products, asset-backed securities, and specialized (e.g. cryptocurrency) — ABS belong to the complex-product analysis, where securitization structure and opacity drive the supervisory risk. Option A would place ABS with the basic types (equities, mutual funds and ETFs, fixed income), the closest distractor because ABS are debt-like; B confuses securitized instruments with derivatives; D is contradicted by the parameter itself.
questions.source_quote: "• Structured products - Direct Electronic Access (DEA) • Asset-backed securities" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 690

questions.stem: A trainer at Oakhampton Capital tells new Supervisors that cryptocurrency risk analysis belongs to the syllabus outcome on basic security types. Why is the trainer wrong?

options.A: Cryptocurrency appears in outcome 3.8 as a derivative type
options.B: The syllabus lists specialized products, with cryptocurrency as the example, under outcome 3.7's complex types of securities/products — not among the basic types in 3.6
options.C: Cryptocurrency is covered only in the syllabus element on trading and market rules
options.D: The syllabus prohibits dealers from offering cryptocurrency, so no outcome covers it

questions.rationale: The 3.7 parameter includes "Specialized (e.g. cryptocurrency)" among the complex types of securities/products whose supervisory risks must be analyzed; the basic types in 3.6 are equities, mutual funds and ETFs, and fixed income products. Option A misplaces crypto among the 3.8 derivatives list (options, futures contract options, futures, forwards, swaps, CFDs); C moves it to Element 7, which addresses market rules, not product risk categories; D converts a risk-analysis topic into a prohibition the syllabus does not state.
questions.source_quote: "• Specialized (e.g. cryptocurrency)" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 698

questions.stem: Foxden Markets accepts a first options order from a new retail client before sending the client any derivatives risk disclosure statement, planning to mail it with the trade confirmation. Which IDPC requirement does this sequence violate?

options.A: The dealer must provide each derivatives retail client with the most recent derivatives risk disclosure statement, approved by the Corporation, before accepting an initial derivatives order from the client
options.B: Risk disclosure statements are required only for futures, not options
options.C: The statement may be provided within 30 days after the first trade
options.D: Risk disclosure is required only for institutional derivatives clients

questions.rationale: Clause 3254(1)(i) requires the dealer to provide each derivatives retail client with the most recent derivatives risk disclosure statement or similar Corporation-approved document before accepting an initial derivatives order, and clause (ii) requires the client's acknowledgement of receipt — delivery with the confirmation is too late. Option C invents a post-trade grace period; B narrows the requirement to one derivative class when it applies to derivatives retail clients generally; D inverts the retail focus of the protection.
questions.source_quote: "provide each derivatives retail client with the most recent derivatives risk disclosure statement or other similar document, approved by Corporation before accepting an initial derivatives order from the client," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5286

questions.stem: Compliance staff at Wrenfield Derivatives can confirm that risk disclosure statements were mailed to options clients but cannot say to whom, where or when. Which record-keeping element of IDPC section 3254 is missing?

options.A: A notarized affidavit from each client confirming they read the statement
options.B: A record of the serial number of each printed statement
options.C: A record of the names and addresses of all clients to whom the statement and any amendments were provided, and the dates on which they were provided
options.D: A video recording of each disclosure conversation

questions.rationale: Clause 3254(1)(iv) requires the dealer to maintain a record of the names and addresses of all clients to whom it has provided the risk disclosure statement (and any amendments under clause (iii)) and the dates on which they were provided — the record proves delivery client by client. Options A and D substitute heavier formalities the rule does not require (the acknowledgement under clause (ii) suffices for receipt); B tracks the document rather than the clients and dates the rule specifies.
questions.source_quote: "maintain a record of the names and addresses of all clients to whom it has provided the statement, or similar document under clause 3254(1)(i) and any amendments under clause 3254(1)(iii) and the dates on which they were provided," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5293

questions.stem: At Calloway Futures, branch managers have been approving new derivatives accounts to speed up onboarding, leaving the designated derivatives Supervisors to handle only trade reviews. Which allocation of responsibility does IDPC section 3961 prescribe?

options.A: Any Approved Person may approve new derivatives accounts if the client is experienced
options.B: Derivatives accounts open automatically once the trading agreement is signed
options.C: New derivatives accounts require approval by the board of directors
options.D: The designated Supervisors are responsible for approving new derivatives accounts and for ensuring that the handling of clients' derivatives account trading complies with Corporation requirements

questions.rationale: Subsection 3961(1) makes the designated Supervisors responsible for approving new derivatives accounts and for ensuring the handling of clients' derivatives account trading complies with Corporation requirements — account approval is part of the designated role, not a task for any branch manager who lacks the designation. Option A loosens approval to client experience, which is not the test; C escalates a Supervisor function to the board; B removes approval entirely, contrary to the rule.
questions.source_quote: "The designated Supervisors are responsible for: (i) approving new derivatives accounts, and (ii) ensuring that the handling of clients' derivatives account trading complies with Corporation requirements." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8292

questions.stem: Stonemoor Capital's derivatives desk plans a calling campaign inviting retail clients to enrol in a covered-call writing program. Under IDPC section 3962, what must the dealer's policies and procedures require before this campaign and the clients' resulting derivatives use can proceed?

options.A: The designated Supervisor must approve the solicitation of clients to use derivatives programs, as well as clients' actual use of derivatives
options.B: Each client must pass a written derivatives examination administered by CIRO
options.C: The campaign needs only marketing department sign-off because covered calls are low risk
options.D: Solicitation of derivatives business is prohibited outright for retail clients

questions.rationale: Clause 3962(5)(i) requires policies and procedures that require the designated Supervisor to approve the solicitation of clients to use derivatives programs, as well as clients' actual use of derivatives — a two-part control covering both the outreach and the resulting activity. Option C bypasses the designated Supervisor whom the rule names regardless of strategy risk level; D converts an approval requirement into a ban; B invents a CIRO-administered client exam.
questions.source_quote: "require the designated Supervisor to approve the solicitation of clients to use derivatives programs, as well as clients' actual use of derivatives," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8323

questions.stem: A monthly derivatives review at Pencarrow Futures shows repeated speculative long crude-oil positions in an account the dealer approved strictly for hedging a client's fuel-cost exposure. Which detection item in IDPC section 3963 do these facts engage?

options.A: Exposures arising out of uncovered option positions
options.B: Transacting while the account is under-margined
options.C: Speculative transactions in hedging accounts
options.D: Transactions in derivatives whose underlying interest is on the dealer's restricted list

questions.rationale: Clause 3963(1)(vi) requires the review of derivative transactions to detect speculative transactions in hedging accounts — positions unconnected to the hedged exposure in an account approved for hedging are precisely this item, and they also undermine the basis on which hedging accounts receive distinct treatment (for example, exclusion from cumulative loss limits). Options A, B and D are genuine 3963(1) detection items, but nothing in the facts shows options exposure, a margin deficiency, or a restricted-list underlying.
questions.source_quote: "(v) exceeding derivative position or exercise limits, (vi) speculative transactions in hedging accounts," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8346

questions.stem: Quenton Markets offers retail clients options and CFDs. A client phones at 11 a.m. on a trading day needing to discuss closing a complex position, but the only staff available are unlicensed customer-service agents. Which IDPC requirement is engaged?

options.A: Policies and procedures must ensure derivatives clients have access, during normal business hours, to a Registered Representative, Investment Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager qualified to deal in the applicable derivatives
options.B: Derivatives clients may only be served by the Chief Compliance Officer
options.C: Clients must wait until the designated Supervisor's weekly office hours
options.D: Unlicensed staff may advise on closing trades if a Supervisor later ratifies the call

questions.rationale: Subsection 3964(1) requires policies and procedures specifically addressing that derivatives clients have access, during normal business hours, to an RR, IR, PM or APM qualified to deal in, as applicable, options contracts and similar derivatives, the futures class, or all derivatives — staffing a derivatives line solely with unlicensed agents during market hours fails this. Option D would have unqualified persons dealing in derivatives, which qualification requirements prohibit; B and C funnel routine client access to the CCO or to weekly windows, neither of which the rule contemplates.
questions.source_quote: "The Dealer Member's policies and procedures must specifically address that derivatives clients have access, during normal business hours, to a Registered Representative, Investment Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager qualified to deal in, where applicable:" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8365

questions.stem: Hollowtree Securities designs its first-tier monthly review for commission accounts. According to the example in CIRO's Account Supervision Guidance, which selection criterion would satisfy the requirement to design the monthly selection to detect improper account activity?

options.A: Reviewing the activity of all clients charged gross commissions of $1,500 or more for that month
options.B: Reviewing only accounts that filed a complaint during the month
options.C: Reviewing a random 1% of accounts regardless of activity
options.D: Reviewing only accounts opened in the current month

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 s.2.1.2 gives reviewing the activity of all clients charged gross commissions of $1,500 or more for the month as an example of a first-tier monthly selection designed to detect improper account activity; the same section requires including all non-client accounts with any activity other than dividend and interest payments. Option B waits for harm signals instead of screening; C is not designed to detect anything in particular; D reviews account age rather than activity that commission incentives can distort.
questions.source_quote: "For example, a Dealer could review the activity of all clients charged gross commissions of $1,500 or more for that month." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 248

questions.stem: Each trade a commission-paid Registered Representative at Aldergate Capital places looks individually suitable, yet the head office suspects the RR's overall trading level is driven by compensation. According to CIRO's Account Supervision Guidance, how should the dealer's review approach address this?

options.A: Review trades over a longer period, because patterns of activity may not be apparent by reviewing single trades, and issues can exist with overall account activity even if each trade is suitable
options.B: Restrict reviews to single-trade screens, since suitability is determined trade by trade
options.C: Stop reviewing the RR's accounts once each trade passes the daily screen
options.D: Rely solely on the client to complain about excessive trading

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 s.1.2 states that patterns of activity may not be apparent by reviewing single trades, and that reviewing trades over a longer period may detect issues with the overall account activity even if each trade is suitable for the client — exactly how commission-driven excessive trading is surfaced. Options B and C confine supervision to the single-trade lens the guidance warns against; D outsources detection to clients, abandoning the dealer's review obligation.
questions.source_quote: "Patterns of activity may not be apparent by reviewing single trades. If a Dealer reviews trades over a longer period, they may detect issues with the overall account activity even if each trade is suitable for the client." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 190

questions.stem: Drumlin Bay Securities runs a two-tier review system. For its second-tier monthly review, which selection criteria does CIRO's Account Supervision Guidance give as an example that would satisfy Rule 3900?

options.A: All accounts that traded any exchange-traded fund during the month
options.B: Accounts of clients charged more than $3,000 in commission during the month, and accounts of all clients and non-clients charged more than $1,500 in commissions during the month not subject to a first-tier level review, including producing first level Supervisors' client accounts
options.C: Only accounts domiciled outside Canada
options.D: A fixed list of the dealer's twenty largest accounts

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 s.2.2.2 gives these example criteria for second-tier monthly selection: accounts charged more than $3,000 in commission during the month, and accounts of clients and non-clients charged more than $1,500 not subject to a first-tier review, including producing first level Supervisors' client accounts — capturing both high-commission activity and accounts that escaped first-tier coverage. Option A keys selection to a product rather than commission-driven risk; C and D are arbitrary slices not designed around the supervision gaps the second tier exists to close.
questions.source_quote: "accounts of clients charged more than $3,000 in commission during the month, and accounts of all clients and non-clients charged more than $1,500 in commissions during the month not subject to a first-tier level review," — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 301

questions.stem: A managed account client of Ferncliff Capital negotiates a flat annual fee of $9,000 for all services to the account — the fee does not vary with trading activity, asset value changes, or performance. Under IDPC section 3281, does the dealer need a special written fee agreement before charging on this basis?

options.A: Yes — every managed account fee of any kind requires the section 3281 written agreement
options.B: No fee of any kind may be charged on a managed account
options.C: No — the written-agreement condition in 3281(1) applies to charges based upon the volume or value of transactions or contingent upon profit or performance, which a negotiated flat fee is not
options.D: Yes, and the agreement must also be filed with the client's bank

questions.rationale: Subsection 3281(1) prohibits charging a managed account client directly on a basis that is based upon the volume or value of transactions or contingent upon profit or performance unless the client has provided a written agreement setting out how such fees may be charged — a negotiated flat fee tied to neither trigger falls outside the prohibition, though its supervisory risk (service received versus fee paid) remains a 3.10 concern. Option A overextends the written-agreement condition to all fees; B converts a conditional prohibition into a total ban; D invents a bank filing.
questions.source_quote: "(i) based upon the volume or value of transactions in the account initiated for the account, or (ii) contingent upon profit or performance of the client's account," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5474

questions.stem: A study group preparing Supervisors at Millbarrow Securities is mapping outcome 3.10 of the Supervisor Exam syllabus. At what cognitive level, and for which fee arrangements, does the syllabus frame this outcome?

options.A: Apply the supervisory requirements for trailer fees and switch fees
options.B: Remember the maximum permitted referral fee percentage set by CIRO
options.C: Analyze the conflicts of commission grids and payout ratios
options.D: Understand the supervisory risks associated with negotiated flat fees and referral fees

questions.rationale: The 3.10 parameter reads: understand the supervisory risks associated with negotiated flat fees and referral fees — an Understand-level outcome about those two arrangements. Option C describes analysis of internal compensation conflicts closer to 3.9's commission/fee-based framing; A names fee types the parameter does not enumerate at an Apply level the outcome does not carry; B presumes a CIRO referral-fee cap that the syllabus parameter does not state.
questions.source_quote: "3.10 Understand the supervisory risks associated with negotiated flat fees and referral fees." — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 770

questions.stem: Kestrelwood Capital's derivatives desk handles several discretionary and managed accounts that trade options. The designated Supervisor reviews these accounts monthly only, alongside ordinary retail derivatives accounts. What review frequency does IDPC section 3962 require for derivatives accounts designated as discretionary or managed?

options.A: Quarterly review at an aggregate level only
options.B: Weekly review by the Chief Compliance Officer
options.C: Annual review at the managed account committee meeting
options.D: On a daily and monthly basis, the designated Supervisor must review all derivatives accounts that are designated as discretionary accounts and managed accounts

questions.rationale: Subsection 3962(3) requires the designated Supervisor, on a daily and monthly basis, to review all derivatives accounts designated as discretionary accounts and managed accounts — discretion plus derivatives compounds supervisory risk, so the review cadence is daily and monthly, not monthly alone. Option A imports the general managed-account quarterly review under 3973, the closest distractor — that quarterly objective-pursuit review does not displace the derivatives-specific daily and monthly review; B reassigns the duty to the CCO; C confuses the committee's annual policy review with account reviews.
questions.source_quote: "On a daily and monthly basis, the designated Supervisor must review all derivatives accounts that are designated as discretionary accounts and managed accounts." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8314

questions.scenario: Northgale Digital Wealth Inc. is a CIRO Investment Dealer launching an online advice platform for retail clients. Clients complete a web questionnaire; portfolios of exchange-traded funds are then managed on a continuing basis by the firm's Portfolio Managers under managed account agreements, with rebalancing decisions made centrally and applied across all platform accounts. The platform charges a single asset-based fee and no trade commissions; under written client consents, Northgale does not send individual trade confirmations and instead sends compliant monthly statements. To launch quickly, the product team also wants Northgale's separate self-directed division to give platform clients occasional ETF picks. Chief Compliance Officer Idris Banerjee is reviewing the launch plan, including how the new accounts are classified, what must happen before each account opens, and how the centrally managed portfolios will be reviewed.
questions.stem: Banerjee first confirms the account classification. Given that investment decisions for platform clients are made on a continuing basis by Northgale's Portfolio Managers, and the accounts are subject to a suitability determination, how should these online advice accounts be classified under IDPC Rule 1200?

options.A: As order execution only accounts, because clients interact through a website
options.B: As managed accounts, because investment decisions are made on a continuing basis by a Portfolio Manager and the dealer and Portfolio Manager are responsible for all investment decisions made
options.C: As direct electronic access accounts under NI 23-103
options.D: As discretionary accounts limited to a 12-month term

questions.rationale: The Rule 1200 managed account definition fits: an account subject to a suitability determination where investment decisions are made on a continuing basis by a Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager or a third party hired by the dealer, with the dealer (or third party) and the PM/APM responsible for all investment decisions — the web interface is the delivery channel, not the classification driver. Option A fails because OEO accounts involve no recommendations and the client is solely responsible for decisions; C describes marketplace access without recommendations; D is the temporary unsolicited-accommodation regime, not continuing professional management.
questions.source_quote: "(ii) the Dealer Member, or a third party hired by the Dealer Member, and the Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager are responsible for all investment decisions made." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 532

questions.scenario: Northgale Digital Wealth Inc. is a CIRO Investment Dealer launching an online advice platform for retail clients. Clients complete a web questionnaire; portfolios of exchange-traded funds are then managed on a continuing basis by the firm's Portfolio Managers under managed account agreements, with rebalancing decisions made centrally and applied across all platform accounts. The platform charges a single asset-based fee and no trade commissions; under written client consents, Northgale does not send individual trade confirmations and instead sends compliant monthly statements. To launch quickly, the product team also wants Northgale's separate self-directed division to give platform clients occasional ETF picks. Chief Compliance Officer Idris Banerjee is reviewing the launch plan, including how the new accounts are classified, what must happen before each account opens, and how the centrally managed portfolios will be reviewed.
questions.stem: The launch checklist for each platform account ends with the managed account agreement and a welcome email. Banerjee flags two missing steps from IDPC section 3277. Which pair completes the account-opening requirements?

options.A: CIRO pre-approval of each account, and a client interview at a physical branch
options.B: An annual fee rebate election, and a paper copy of the IDPC Rules
options.C: Written approval of each managed account by the designated Supervisor, and providing the client with a copy of the dealer's policy ensuring fair allocation of investment opportunities
options.D: A margin agreement, and a leverage risk disclosure statement

questions.rationale: Subsection 3277(1) requires, among other things, that the designated Supervisor approve each managed account in writing (with the approval record retained) and that the dealer provide the client with a copy of its policy ensuring fair allocation of investment opportunities — both fit naturally before a centrally managed platform account begins trading. Option A inserts regulator pre-approval and an in-person interview that the rule does not require; D lists documents tied to margin lending and borrowed-money investing, not managed account opening as such; B invents documents with no basis in 3277.
questions.source_quote: "(iv) the designated Supervisor must approve each managed account in writing, (v) the Dealer Member must retain a record of the designated Supervisor's approval, and (vi) the Dealer Member must provide the client with a copy of its policy ensuring fair allocation of investment opportunities." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5416

questions.scenario: Northgale Digital Wealth Inc. is a CIRO Investment Dealer launching an online advice platform for retail clients. Clients complete a web questionnaire; portfolios of exchange-traded funds are then managed on a continuing basis by the firm's Portfolio Managers under managed account agreements, with rebalancing decisions made centrally and applied across all platform accounts. The platform charges a single asset-based fee and no trade commissions; under written client consents, Northgale does not send individual trade confirmations and instead sends compliant monthly statements. To launch quickly, the product team also wants Northgale's separate self-directed division to give platform clients occasional ETF picks. Chief Compliance Officer Idris Banerjee is reviewing the launch plan, including how the new accounts are classified, what must happen before each account opens, and how the centrally managed portfolios will be reviewed.
questions.stem: A junior compliance analyst asks Banerjee why the platform may lawfully skip individual trade confirmations on the ETF rebalancing trades. Which combination of conditions in IDPC section 3816 supports Northgale's practice for these dealer-managed accounts?

options.A: Prior written client consent to waive the confirmation requirement, no commissions or fees based on the volume or value of transactions, and a monthly statement containing the prescribed confirmation information
options.B: Verbal consent from the client obtained during onboarding calls
options.C: An exemption that applies automatically to all ETF trades
options.D: A quarterly compliant trade percentage of at least 85% for two of the last four quarters

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 3816(2)(x)(a) permits omitting confirmations for managed account trades where, before the trade, the client consented in writing to waive the requirement (terminable on written notice), confirmation is not required under applicable securities laws or an exemption exists, and — where the dealer manages the account — the account is charged no volume- or value-based commissions or fees and the client receives a compliant monthly statement with the confirmation content. Option D states the DAP/RAP trade-matching condition from 3816(2)(x)(b), the closest distractor — a different waiver stream; B fails the written-consent form; C invents a product-class exemption.
questions.source_quote: "(I) prior to the trade, the client has consented in writing to waive the trade confirmation requirement," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7669

questions.scenario: Northgale Digital Wealth Inc. is a CIRO Investment Dealer launching an online advice platform for retail clients. Clients complete a web questionnaire; portfolios of exchange-traded funds are then managed on a continuing basis by the firm's Portfolio Managers under managed account agreements, with rebalancing decisions made centrally and applied across all platform accounts. The platform charges a single asset-based fee and no trade commissions; under written client consents, Northgale does not send individual trade confirmations and instead sends compliant monthly statements. To launch quickly, the product team also wants Northgale's separate self-directed division to give platform clients occasional ETF picks. Chief Compliance Officer Idris Banerjee is reviewing the launch plan, including how the new accounts are classified, what must happen before each account opens, and how the centrally managed portfolios will be reviewed.
questions.stem: Northgale's separate self-directed division opens order execution only accounts online. Banerjee asks what the division must obtain from each new OEO client — beyond delivering the prescribed written disclosures — before the account opens, and in what form the record may be kept. Which answer is correct?

options.A: A positive acknowledgement from the client, and each beneficial owner of the account, confirming they received and understood the disclosures, recorded as a signature or initials on a form, an electronic acknowledgement attached to the disclosure text, or a tape recording of a verbal acknowledgement
options.B: A notarized statutory declaration from the client only, renewed annually
options.C: Nothing further — delivering the disclosures completes the OEO opening requirements
options.D: A suitability questionnaire completed by each beneficial owner

questions.rationale: Clause 3241(3)(ii) requires, prior to opening an OEO account, a positive acknowledgement from the client and each beneficial owner confirming receipt and understanding of the clause 3241(3)(i) disclosures, and subsection 3241(4) prescribes the acceptable record forms: the client's signature or initials on a form specifically related to the disclosure and acknowledgement, an electronic acknowledgement attached to the text, or a tape recording of a verbal acknowledgement. Option C stops at delivery and misses the acknowledgement — the closest distractor; D contradicts the OEO model, where no suitability determination is made; B invents notarization and renewal formalities.
questions.source_quote: "obtain a positive acknowledgement from the client, and each beneficial owner of the account, confirming that the client, and each beneficial owner, has received and understood the disclosures described in clause 3241(3)(i)." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5014

questions.scenario: Briarholm Institutional Markets Inc. is a CIRO Investment Dealer whose clients are predominantly pension funds, asset managers and other large non-individual clients. Most clients settle through custodians on a delivery against payment / receipt against payment basis, and several have signed written waivers of trade confirmations under the institutional trade-matching regime; Briarholm's quarterly compliant trade percentages have ranged between 88% and 93% for the past year. One prospective client, Selkirk Pension Trust, has a master account with several sub-accounts. Another prospect, Vontara Holdings Inc., manages $8 million in securities and asks to be treated as an institutional client. Head of supervision Mireille Castonguay is reviewing classification, documentation and supervisory screens for the institutional book.
questions.stem: Castonguay reviews Vontara Holdings Inc., a non-individual with $8 million of securities under management and no other qualifying status. Can Briarholm classify Vontara as an institutional client under the IDPC Rule 1200 definition?

options.A: Yes — any corporation is automatically an institutional client
options.B: Yes, if Vontara signs a request-and-consent form, regardless of asset level
options.C: No — as a non-individual it must have total securities and precious metals bullion under administration or management exceeding $10 million (absent another qualifying branch), and $8 million does not meet that threshold
options.D: No — only individuals can be institutional clients

questions.rationale: Branch (v) of the institutional client definition requires a non-individual to have total securities and precious metals bullion under administration or management exceeding $10 million; Vontara's $8 million does not qualify, and the facts give no acceptable-counterparty, acceptable-institution, regulated-entity, registrant or hedger basis. Option B is the closest distractor: request-and-consent features in branches (vi) (individuals over $10 million) and (vii) (hedgers), but consent does not substitute for the non-individual asset threshold; A and D contradict the multi-branch structure of the definition.
questions.source_quote: "(v) a non-individual with total securities and precious metals bullion under administration or management exceeding $10 million," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 478

questions.scenario: Briarholm Institutional Markets Inc. is a CIRO Investment Dealer whose clients are predominantly pension funds, asset managers and other large non-individual clients. Most clients settle through custodians on a delivery against payment / receipt against payment basis, and several have signed written waivers of trade confirmations under the institutional trade-matching regime; Briarholm's quarterly compliant trade percentages have ranged between 88% and 93% for the past year. One prospective client, Selkirk Pension Trust, has a master account with several sub-accounts. Another prospect, Vontara Holdings Inc., manages $8 million in securities and asks to be treated as an institutional client. Head of supervision Mireille Castonguay is reviewing classification, documentation and supervisory screens for the institutional book.
questions.stem: Selkirk Pension Trust, an institutional client, will open several sub-accounts under its master file, including an order execution only trade account. Beyond complying with the applicable parts of Rule 3200, what does IDPC section 3240 require for the institutional client's sub-account files?

options.A: Each sub-account file must contain duplicate originals of every master-file document
options.B: The sub-account files of an institutional client must refer to the documentation contained in the master file to which it is related
options.C: Sub-accounts of institutional clients are exempt from all documentation
options.D: Each sub-account requires a separate CIRO approval number

questions.rationale: Clause 3240(2)(ii) requires the dealer opening an order execution only account for an institutional client to ensure the sub-account files refer to the documentation contained in the related master file — a cross-reference structure rather than duplication, fitting how institutional master/sub-account documentation works. Option A demands the duplication the cross-reference mechanism avoids; C overstates the institutional accommodations (sections 3216 through 3219 are excepted, not all documentation); D invents per-sub-account regulator approvals.
questions.source_quote: "ensure the sub-account files of an institutional client refer to the documentation contained in the master file to which it is related." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4979

questions.scenario: Briarholm Institutional Markets Inc. is a CIRO Investment Dealer whose clients are predominantly pension funds, asset managers and other large non-individual clients. Most clients settle through custodians on a delivery against payment / receipt against payment basis, and several have signed written waivers of trade confirmations under the institutional trade-matching regime; Briarholm's quarterly compliant trade percentages have ranged between 88% and 93% for the past year. One prospective client, Selkirk Pension Trust, has a master account with several sub-accounts. Another prospect, Vontara Holdings Inc., manages $8 million in securities and asks to be treated as an institutional client. Head of supervision Mireille Castonguay is reviewing classification, documentation and supervisory screens for the institutional book.
questions.stem: A pension-fund client that signed a DAP/RAP confirmation waiver two years ago writes to Briarholm stating it now wants trade confirmations again. How does IDPC section 3816 treat this request?

options.A: The waiver is irrevocable for the life of the account
options.B: The client may terminate the waiver only at calendar year-end
options.C: The client may terminate the trade confirmation waiver by providing a written notice, and the termination takes effect upon the dealer's receipt of the notice
options.D: Termination requires CIRO's prior written approval

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 3816(2)(x)(b) closes with: a client may terminate their trade confirmation waiver by providing a written notice confirming this fact to the dealer, and the termination notice takes effect upon the dealer's receipt of the notice — the waiver is a client accommodation the client can withdraw. Option A makes the waiver permanent, contrary to the express termination mechanism; B adds a timing window that does not exist; D inserts the regulator into a bilateral client-dealer arrangement.
questions.source_quote: "A client may terminate their trade confirmation waiver, referred to in sub-clause 3816(2)(x)(b), by providing a written notice confirming this fact to the Dealer Member. The termination notice takes effect upon the Dealer Member's receipt of the notice." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7742

questions.scenario: Briarholm Institutional Markets Inc. is a CIRO Investment Dealer whose clients are predominantly pension funds, asset managers and other large non-individual clients. Most clients settle through custodians on a delivery against payment / receipt against payment basis, and several have signed written waivers of trade confirmations under the institutional trade-matching regime; Briarholm's quarterly compliant trade percentages have ranged between 88% and 93% for the past year. One prospective client, Selkirk Pension Trust, has a master account with several sub-accounts. Another prospect, Vontara Holdings Inc., manages $8 million in securities and asks to be treated as an institutional client. Head of supervision Mireille Castonguay is reviewing classification, documentation and supervisory screens for the institutional book.
questions.stem: Briarholm executes exchange-traded futures for an institutional client under a give-up agreement; Calverley Clearing Corp., another Dealer Member, clears and settles. Castonguay asks whether Briarholm, as executing broker, must send the client a confirmation for each transaction. Under IDPC section 3816, when is Briarholm relieved of that obligation?

options.A: When the client, executing dealer and clearing dealer are parties to the give-up agreement, the clearing dealer is responsible under the agreement for issuing the transaction confirmation to the client, and the executing dealer provides limited execution service without maintaining client account documentation or holding the client's money, securities, margin or collateral
options.B: Whenever the client is institutional, with no other conditions
options.C: Never — both the executing and clearing dealers must each send a confirmation
options.D: Only if the transaction is under $50,000 in notional value

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 3816(2)(x)(d) relieves the executing dealer for a derivatives transaction executed for an institutional client under a give-up agreement where the client, executing dealer and clearing dealer are all parties, the clearing dealer is responsible under the agreement for issuing the confirmation, and the executing dealer executes per the client's give-up instructions, provides limited execution service without maintaining account documentation or receiving the client's money, securities, margin or collateral, and invoices the clearing dealer monthly for reconciliation. Option B drops every condition except client status; C requires double confirmations the provision is designed to avoid; D invents a notional cap.
questions.source_quote: "(II) the clearing Dealer Member is responsible, under the give-up agreement, for issuing the transaction confirmation to the client, and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7760

questions.scenario: Silvermarsh Securities Ltd. is a full-service CIRO Investment Dealer. Its retail advisory division compensates Registered Representatives by commission; a growing wrap program charges asset-based fees with no per-trade commissions; and a small order execution only channel lets self-directed clients trade the same shelf without recommendations. The product shelf includes leveraged ETFs, principal-at-risk notes and exchange-traded options. During a supervision overhaul, the firm discovers that: (1) its product committee approved a leveraged ETF two years ago but the prospectus has since been amended to double the fund's management fee and shift its reset mechanics, with no re-examination by the committee; (2) a commission-paid RR, Dario Velez, shows monthly commissions just below the firm's $1,500 first-tier monthly review selection level across many small accounts; and (3) several wrap-program accounts have had no trades, no rebalancing and no documented client contact in 18 months while fees accrue. Supervision head Lavinia Okafor must decide how each finding maps to CIRO requirements.
questions.stem: Okafor notes that self-directed clients buy the leveraged ETF through Silvermarsh's order execution only channel with no Approved Person involved. A branch Supervisor asks whether the know-your-product obligation in IDPC section 3302 applies to those OEO purchases. What does section 3303 provide?

options.A: Section 3302 applies with extra force in OEO accounts because no advisor is present
options.B: Section 3302 does not apply in respect to an order execution only account or a direct electronic access account
options.C: Section 3302 applies only if the ETF's leverage exceeds two times
options.D: Section 3302 is replaced in OEO accounts by a CIRO-administered client quiz

questions.rationale: Subsection 3303(2) exempts order execution only accounts and direct electronic access accounts (and carrying-broker / execution-clearing-settlement-custody-only accounts) from the section 3302 know-your-product obligation — KYP attaches to an Approved Person's purchases, sales and recommendations, and in these channels no recommendation is given; this is precisely why complex products such as leveraged ETFs reaching self-directed clients carry a distinct supervisory risk profile. Option A inverts the exemption; note the dealer-level product due diligence in 3301 still applies to what the dealer makes available — the exemption is from AP-level KYP; C invents a leverage threshold; D invents a quiz regime.
questions.source_quote: "Section 3302 does not apply in respect to: (i) an order execution only account, (ii) a direct electronic access account, or" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5514

questions.scenario: Silvermarsh Securities Ltd. is a full-service CIRO Investment Dealer. Its retail advisory division compensates Registered Representatives by commission; a growing wrap program charges asset-based fees with no per-trade commissions; and a small order execution only channel lets self-directed clients trade the same shelf without recommendations. The product shelf includes leveraged ETFs, principal-at-risk notes and exchange-traded options. During a supervision overhaul, the firm discovers that: (1) its product committee approved a leveraged ETF two years ago but the prospectus has since been amended to double the fund's management fee and shift its reset mechanics, with no re-examination by the committee; (2) a commission-paid RR, Dario Velez, shows monthly commissions just below the firm's $1,500 first-tier monthly review selection level across many small accounts; and (3) several wrap-program accounts have had no trades, no rebalancing and no documented client contact in 18 months while fees accrue. Supervision head Lavinia Okafor must decide how each finding maps to CIRO requirements.
questions.stem: On finding (3), a colleague argues the dormant wrap accounts need no review attention because they generate no commissions and trigger no commission-based screens. Why is this position inconsistent with IDPC section 3945?

options.A: Wrap accounts must be closed automatically after 12 months without trades
options.B: Because dormant accounts can only be reviewed by CIRO examiners
options.C: Commission screens are the only selection method Rule 3900 recognizes
options.D: The dealer must have policies and procedures that specifically address supervising retail client accounts where a commission is not charged for trades, and those policies must use criteria other than commission levels

questions.rationale: Subsection 3945(3) requires policies and procedures specifically addressing the supervision of retail accounts where no commission is charged for trades, such as fee based accounts, which must address account activity review requirements and use criteria other than commission levels — fee-accruing accounts with no activity or service are exactly what non-commission criteria are meant to surface. Option C is the inverse of the rule; A invents an automatic-closure rule; B abdicates the dealer's own supervision obligation to the regulator.
questions.source_quote: "These policies and procedures must: (i) address account activity review Corporation requirements, and (ii) use criteria other than commission levels." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8177

questions.scenario: Silvermarsh Securities Ltd. is a full-service CIRO Investment Dealer. Its retail advisory division compensates Registered Representatives by commission; a growing wrap program charges asset-based fees with no per-trade commissions; and a small order execution only channel lets self-directed clients trade the same shelf without recommendations. The product shelf includes leveraged ETFs, principal-at-risk notes and exchange-traded options. During a supervision overhaul, the firm discovers that: (1) its product committee approved a leveraged ETF two years ago but the prospectus has since been amended to double the fund's management fee and shift its reset mechanics, with no re-examination by the committee; (2) a commission-paid RR, Dario Velez, shows monthly commissions just below the firm's $1,500 first-tier monthly review selection level across many small accounts; and (3) several wrap-program accounts have had no trades, no rebalancing and no documented client contact in 18 months while fees accrue. Supervision head Lavinia Okafor must decide how each finding maps to CIRO requirements.
questions.stem: On finding (2), Velez's commission totals sit just under the $1,500 first-tier monthly selection level, spread across many small accounts. Drawing on CIRO's Account Supervision Guidance, what should Okafor conclude about Silvermarsh's review design?

options.A: No change is needed — activity below a dollar threshold is conclusively proper
options.B: The dealer should exercise reasonable judgment in selecting items for further investigation and is not limited to threshold screens; reviews over a longer period can reveal patterns not apparent from single trades or single-month screens
options.C: The first-tier threshold must be lowered to zero so every account is fully reviewed monthly
options.D: Velez should be asked to self-certify that his trading is suitable

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001's executive summary states the dealer is not required to investigate every trade meeting the criteria but should exercise reasonable judgment in selecting items for further investigation, and s.1.2 adds that patterns may not be apparent from single trades — commission totals hovering just below a fixed screen across many accounts are a pattern reasonable judgment should catch. Option A treats the screen as a safe harbour, the very gaming the guidance's judgment standard addresses; C overcorrects to a full-population review the guidance does not demand (it acknowledges dealers may not be able to review every statement); D substitutes self-certification for supervision.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer is not required to enquire into or investigate every trade that meets the criteria in Rule 3900 or this Guidance, but should exercise reasonable judgment in selecting items for further investigation." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 39

questions.scenario: Silvermarsh Securities Ltd. is a full-service CIRO Investment Dealer. Its retail advisory division compensates Registered Representatives by commission; a growing wrap program charges asset-based fees with no per-trade commissions; and a small order execution only channel lets self-directed clients trade the same shelf without recommendations. The product shelf includes leveraged ETFs, principal-at-risk notes and exchange-traded options. During a supervision overhaul, the firm discovers that: (1) its product committee approved a leveraged ETF two years ago but the prospectus has since been amended to double the fund's management fee and shift its reset mechanics, with no re-examination by the committee; (2) a commission-paid RR, Dario Velez, shows monthly commissions just below the firm's $1,500 first-tier monthly review selection level across many small accounts; and (3) several wrap-program accounts have had no trades, no rebalancing and no documented client contact in 18 months while fees accrue. Supervision head Lavinia Okafor must decide how each finding maps to CIRO requirements.
questions.stem: As part of the overhaul, Silvermarsh proposes paying its wrap-program Portfolio Managers a quarterly bonus equal to a percentage of each managed account's investment gains, arguing this aligns the PMs with clients. Under IDPC section 3281, how does this proposal differ from the negotiated client fee arrangements the rule permits?

options.A: It is permitted because section 3281 regulates only client charges, never internal compensation
options.B: It is permitted if each client signs the written fee agreement contemplated by 3281(1)
options.C: It is prohibited — 3281(2) bars compensating persons authorized to deal with managed accounts on the basis of the value or volume of transactions, and the 3281(1) written-agreement relief applies only to fees charged directly to clients, not to internal compensation
options.D: It is prohibited only if the bonus exceeds 25% of base salary

questions.rationale: Subsection 3281(1)'s written-agreement mechanism governs what the dealer may charge the client directly — including performance-contingent fees with a written agreement — while 3281(2) separately prohibits compensating a person referred to in section 3279 on the basis of the value or volume of transactions in the account, with no written-agreement cure; a transaction-driven internal bonus cannot be papered over with client consent. Option B applies the client-fee relief to the wrong relationship — the closest distractor; A reads 3281(2) out of the rule; D invents a percentage threshold.
questions.source_quote: "unless the client has provided the Dealer Member with a written agreement which sets out the manner in which the fees may be charged based on volume or value of transactions or contingent upon profit or performance." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5476

questions.stem: A retail client of Arbour Securities tells her advisor to buy 200 shares 'right away at whatever the market is asking.' Under the UMIR 1.1 definition, this instruction creates a:

options.A: Market order — an order to buy a security to be executed upon entry to a marketplace at the best ask price
options.B: Limit order at the best ask price
options.C: Call Market Order
options.D: Opening Order

questions.rationale: UMIR 1.1 defines a market order as an order to buy a security or a derivative to be executed upon entry to a marketplace at the best ask price (or sell at the best bid price). A limit order (B) requires a client-specified maximum or minimum price, which this client did not give. A Call Market Order (C) trades at particular times established by the marketplace, not on entry. An Opening Order (D) is entered before the opening to execute at the opening price.
questions.source_quote: "“market order” means an order to: (a)     buy a security or a derivative to be executed upon entry to a marketplace at the best ask price" — umir.txt ~line 1381

questions.stem: A trader at Cardingley Capital wants to enter a single order that combines a retail client's buy order with a buy order for the firm's own inventory account. Under UMIR 1.1 definitions, the combined order is a:

options.A: Multiple client order
options.B: Bundled order — an order that includes a client order as well as a non-client order or principal order, or both
options.C: Jitney order
options.D: Program Trade

questions.rationale: UMIR 1.1 defines a bundled order as an order that includes a client order as well as a non-client order or principal order, or both — the inventory side makes this a principal order combined with a client order. A multiple client order (A) is the closest distractor but combines orders from more than one client and expressly excludes principal and non-client orders. A jitney order (C) is entered by one Participant for another Participant. A Program Trade (D) involves market orders in index-component securities tied to an index derivative trade.
questions.source_quote: "“bundled order” means an order that includes a client order as well as a non-client order or principal order, or both." — umir.txt ~line 370

questions.stem: The arbitrage desk at Farrowdale Capital sells an index futures contract and simultaneously enters a series of market orders to buy the particular securities underlying that index, which has been designated by the Market Regulator. Under UMIR 1.1, the resulting trade in the basket of securities is a:

options.A: Special Terms Order
options.B: Program Trade — a trade resulting from a series of market orders for securities underlying a designated index, undertaken in conjunction with a trade in a derivative on that index
options.C: Basis Order
options.D: Contingent Derivative Order

questions.rationale: UMIR 1.1 defines a Program Trade as a trade resulting from a series of market orders for the purchase or sale of particular securities underlying an index designated by a Market Regulator where the trade is undertaken in conjunction with a trade in a derivative the underlying interest of which is the index — the futures-plus-basket structure here. A Basis Order (C) is the closest distractor but is a single order priced off executions in the related derivative, not a series of market orders. A Special Terms Order (A) is defined by size, conditions or settlement. A Contingent Derivative Order (D) is a separate defined term, not this index-basket structure.
questions.source_quote: "“Program Trade” means a trade resulting from a series of market orders for the purchase or sale of particular securities underlying an index that has been designated by a Market Regulator" — umir.txt ~line 1757

questions.stem: A retail client of Glenbrook Securities repeatedly enters and quickly cancels large visible buy orders in a thinly traded security with no intention that they execute, drawing other buyers in before he sells. Which paragraph of Policy 2.2 Part 2 most directly captures the order entry conduct?

options.A: Entering orders to effect a high closing price
options.B: Making a fictitious trade
options.C: Effecting a trade between accounts under the direction of the same person
options.D: Entering an order or a series of orders for a security that are not intended to be executed

questions.rationale: Policy 2.2 Part 2(f) lists entering an order or a series of orders for a security or a derivative that are not intended to be executed — the classic spoofing/layering pattern here — as a violation of Rule 2.2(2). High-closing (A) is Part 2(e)(ii) and turns on end-of-day price setting, not cancelled orders. A fictitious trade (B) is a Part 1 act requiring an actual trade. Same-person account trades (C) are Part 2(i) wash-trade conduct, also requiring executions, not cancellations.
questions.source_quote: "entering an order or a series of orders for a security or a derivative that are not intended to be executed" — umir.txt ~line 3469

questions.stem: A trader at Gairloch Securities hears a vague market rumour that a large institutional buyer is 'circling' a particular stock and buys for his own account. No client order exists at the firm. Under Policy 4.1 Part 2, his trading:

options.A: Violates Rule 4.1 because rumours are equivalent to knowledge
options.B: Violates Rule 2.1(2)(b) automatically
options.C: Does not constitute frontrunning, because trading based on non-specific pieces of market information, including rumours, requires no specific knowledge of a client order
options.D: Is permitted only if he first reports the rumour to the Market Regulator

questions.rationale: Policy 4.1 Part 2 requires that, to constitute frontrunning, the person must have specific knowledge concerning the client order that on entry could reasonably be expected to affect the market price, and states that trading based on non-specific pieces of market information, including rumours, does not constitute frontrunning. Equating rumours with knowledge (A) contradicts the Policy. Rule 2.1(2)(b) (B) requires knowledge of a client's expression of interest at the Participant, which is absent. There is no rumour-reporting precondition (D) in Rule 4.1.
questions.source_quote: "Trading based on non-specific pieces of market information, including rumours, does not constitute frontrunning." — umir.txt ~line 4680

questions.stem: Under UMIR 5.3(2), which of the following principal or non-client orders is NOT required to give priority to a previously received client order on the same side at the same price?

options.A: An inventory order entered manually by the trader who took the client order
options.B: A principal order entered after the head of trading verbally approves it
options.C: A principal order for a managed account where the client order is for a managed account under the direction of the same person, with executions allocated between the managed accounts on an equitable basis
options.D: Any order under 50 standard trading units

questions.rationale: UMIR 5.3(2)(b)(iii) exempts a principal or non-client order for a managed account where the client order is for a managed account under the direction of the same person and executions are allocated between the various managed accounts on an equitable basis in accordance with the Participant's established practices. A manually entered competing inventory order (A) is the core conduct Rule 5.3(1) prohibits. Head-of-trading approval (B) is not an exemption — only the routes in 5.3(2) are. Order size (D) plays no role in the client priority exemptions.
questions.source_quote: "for a managed account and the client order is for a managed account under the direction of the same person and in respect of which executions are allocated between the various managed accounts on an equitable basis in accordance with the established practices of the Participant" — umir.txt ~line 5064

questions.stem: A client of Hollins Securities instructs that his large order be worked in parts at various times during the trading day. Under UMIR 5.3(3), with respect to the firm entering principal orders in the same security that day, the client:

options.A: Must sign a separate written priority waiver before the order is accepted
options.B: Retains absolute priority over every principal order until his order is fully filled
options.C: Is protected only if the firm's order is larger than his
options.D: Is deemed to have consented to the Participant entering principal and non-client orders, because his order is, on his specific instructions, to be executed in part at various times during the trading day

questions.rationale: UMIR 5.3(3) deems a client to have consented to the Participant entering principal orders and non-client orders for the same security at the same price on the same side where the client order, in accordance with the client's specific instructions, is to be executed in part at various times during the trading day or at various prices. A separate written waiver (A) is not what the rule requires — the deemed consent flows from the over-the-day instruction itself. Absolute priority (B) is displaced by the deeming provision. Relative order size (C) is irrelevant to 5.3(3).
questions.source_quote: "if the client order, in accordance with the specific instructions of the client, is to be executed in part at various times during the trading day or at various prices during the trading day." — umir.txt ~line 5109

questions.stem: A client of Larchmont Securities instructs the firm to withhold her order from the market. Under Policy 5.3 Part 3, for how long must details of the instruction be retained, and where must they be kept initially?

options.A: Five years, in the compliance department's central file
options.B: Seven years from the date of the instruction, and for the first two years in a readily accessible location
options.C: Two years, with the branch manager
options.D: Ten years, in offsite archival storage

questions.rationale: Policy 5.3 Part 3 requires that if a client has instructed a Participant to withhold an order or granted discretion over entry, details of the instruction or grant of discretion must be retained for seven years from the date of the instruction and, for the first two years, the consent must be kept in a readily accessible location. Five years (A) is the Policy 7.1 Part 2 retention period for compliance review results — the closest distractor. Two years (C) is only the readily-accessible window, not the full retention period. Ten years (D) appears nowhere in the provision.
questions.source_quote: "details of the instruction or grant of discretion must be retained for a period of seven years from the date of the instruction or grant of discretion and, for the first two years, the consent must be kept in a readily accessible location." — umir.txt ~line 5181

questions.stem: Under IDPC Rule 5113, the loan value of a long position in a client margin account is generally calculated as:

options.A: [100% + applicable margin rate %] x positive market value of the security
options.B: Applicable margin rate % x market value of the security
options.C: [100% - applicable margin rate %] x positive market value of the security
options.D: 50% of market value in all cases

questions.rationale: IDPC 5113(2) sets the loan value of a long position at [100% - applicable margin rate %] x positive market value of the security. Adding the margin rate (A) is the formula for the loan value of a short position under 5113(3), which uses [100% + applicable margin rate %] times the negative market value — the closest distractor because it inverts the sign. Margin rate times market value (B) is the Dealer Member inventory margin formula under 5112, not client loan value. A flat 50% (D) ignores that margin rates are prescribed by the Corporation and vary by security.
questions.source_quote: "[100% - applicable margin rate %] x positive market value of the security" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 12067

questions.stem: A client of Quenby Securities owes the firm a margin debt. Under IDPC Rule 5114, which securities held by the firm for that client are collateral for payment of the debt?

options.A: Only securities the client has expressly pledged in writing
options.B: All securities the Dealer Member holds for the client, up to an amount that reasonably covers the margin debt
options.C: Only securities purchased on margin in that account
options.D: All securities in the account without any limit tied to the debt

questions.rationale: IDPC 5114(1) provides that if a client is in debt to a Dealer Member, all securities the Dealer Member holds for the client, up to an amount that reasonably covers the margin debt, are collateral for payment of the debt. An express written pledge (A) is not the trigger — the rule operates on the debt itself, subject to Form 1 Schedule 4 and any agreement. Limiting collateral to margin-purchased securities (C) is narrower than the rule. Unlimited collateralization (D) ignores the 'reasonably covers' cap.
questions.source_quote: "If a client is in debt to a Dealer Member, all securities the Dealer Member holds for the client, up to an amount that reasonably covers the margin debt, are collateral for payment of the debt." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 12084

questions.stem: A client of Oakhurst Securities acquires a position in an unusual security for which no margin rate or requirement is specified within the Corporation requirements. Under IDPC Rule 5120, the Dealer Member must:

options.A: Apply a default margin rate of 100%
options.B: Apply the margin rate of the most similar listed security
options.C: Obtain a margin rule interpretation from Corporation staff specifying the margin rate or requirement to be used
options.D: Exclude the position from margin calculations until a rate is published

questions.rationale: IDPC 5120(1) requires that where a security position is held in inventory or a client account for which a margin rate or requirement is not specified within the Corporation requirements, the Dealer Member must obtain a margin rule interpretation from Corporation staff specifying the rate or requirement to be used. A default 100% rate (A) is a plausible conservative practice but is not what the rule prescribes. Analogizing to a similar security (B) substitutes the dealer's judgment for the required interpretation. Excluding the position (D) would understate margin entirely.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must obtain a margin rule interpretation from Corporation staff specifying the margin rate or requirement to be used." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 12108

questions.stem: Which disclosure does IDPC Rule 3816(2) require on a written trade confirmation for a trade in securities?

options.A: The client's risk profile and investment objectives
options.B: The Registered Representative's compensation grid
options.C: The names of all other clients who traded the security that day
options.D: Whether or not the person or company that executed the trade acted as principal or agent

questions.rationale: IDPC 3816(2)(i)(c) requires the confirmation for trades in securities to state whether or not the person or company that executed the trade acted as principal or agent, alongside quantity and description, consideration, marketplace, regulatory fees, salesperson name and settlement date. Risk profile and objectives (A) are KYC records, not confirmation content. The RR's grid (B) is internal compensation information. Other clients' trading (C) would breach confidentiality and is not a confirmation element.
questions.source_quote: "whether or not the person or company that executed the trade acted as principal or agent" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7526

questions.stem: The desk at Brindley Securities receives instructions to cross a block between two clients at $9.00 when the best bid is $10.00 — a price about 10% below the best bid and more than 10 trading increments away. Under UMIR 2.1(3), before entering the order intended to execute as this intentional cross, the desk must:

options.A: Obtain the prior approval of a Market Regulator
options.B: Simply mark the order as a Special Terms Order
options.C: Wait until the last 15 minutes of trading
options.D: Obtain the written consent of the issuer

questions.rationale: UMIR 2.1(3) prohibits entering an order intended to execute as a pre-arranged trade or intentional cross without the prior approval of a Market Regulator if the price would be less than the lesser of 95% of the best bid price and the best bid price less 10 trading increments — a $9.00 cross against a $10.00 bid breaches both limbs. A Special Terms marker (B) does not substitute for regulator approval. Timing the entry late in the day (C) has no exempting effect. Issuer consent (D) plays no role in UMIR pre-arranged trade approval.
questions.source_quote: "A Participant or Access Person shall not enter an order on a marketplace that is intended to execute as a pre-arranged trade or an intentional cross without the prior approval of a Market Regulator if the pre-arranged trade or intentional cross would be undertaken at a price that will be:" — umir.txt ~line 2888

questions.stem: A trader at Castell Securities tries to enter a buy order at $12.503 for a security trading around $12.50. Under UMIR 6.1(1), the order entry desk should reject it because:

options.A: Orders cannot be priced within one cent of the last sale price
options.B: No order may be entered on a marketplace at a price that includes a fraction of a cent, other than a half-cent increment for an order priced below $0.50
options.C: Three-decimal pricing is allowed only for Dark Orders
options.D: Buy orders must always be entered at whole-dollar prices

questions.rationale: UMIR 6.1(1) provides that no order shall be entered to trade on a marketplace at a price that includes a fraction or a part of a cent other than an increment of one-half of one cent in respect of an order with a price of less than $0.50 — at $12.503 the sub-penny increment is impermissible. Proximity to the last sale (A) is not a pricing restriction. Dark Orders (C) are about display, not sub-penny entry; only an intentional cross under 6.1(3) may execute at a fractional increment when both sides get a better price. Whole-dollar pricing (D) is invented.
questions.source_quote: "No order to purchase or sell a security shall be entered to trade on a marketplace at a price that includes a fraction or a part of a cent other than an increment of one-half of one cent in respect of an order with a price of less than $0.50." — umir.txt ~line 5390

questions.stem: An order entry clerk at Thornbury Securities is preparing a bundled order for entry and asks whether the order must also carry the client identifier required by UMIR 6.2(1)(a)(iv). Under UMIR 6.2(1)(c):

options.A: The client identifier is always required in addition to the bundled order designation
options.B: Bundled orders are prohibited from entry on a marketplace
options.C: The client identifier may be replaced by the head of trading's identifier
options.D: Where the bundled order or multiple client order designation is required, the Participant does not need to include a client identifier on the order

questions.rationale: UMIR 6.2(1)(c) provides that where a designation is required under 6.2(1)(b)(xx) (bundled order) or (xxi) (multiple client order), the Participant does not need to include a client identifier on the order under 6.2(1)(a)(iv) — the combined-order designations substitute because a single client identifier cannot describe several interests. Requiring both (A) contradicts the express relief. Bundled orders are a recognized designation, not prohibited (B). Substituting a supervisor's identifier (C) has no basis in Rule 6.2.
questions.source_quote: "Where a designation is required under 6.2(1)(b)(xx) or (xxi), the Participant does not need to include a client identifier on the order under 6.2(1)(a)(iv)." — umir.txt ~line 5584

questions.stem: A retail client of Umberley Securities places a single order for 40 standard trading units of a liquid security worth $130,000. The desk asks whether the order exposure rule compels immediate entry for display. Under UMIR 6.3(1)(f), the order:

options.A: Must be displayed immediately because it is 50 standard trading units or less
options.B: Is excepted from the immediate-display requirement because the order has a value of more than $100,000
options.C: Must be split into smaller pieces and each displayed immediately
options.D: May be withheld only with the Market Regulator's consent

questions.rationale: UMIR 6.3(1) requires immediate entry for display of client orders of 50 standard trading units or less unless an exception applies, and clause (f) excepts an order with a value of more than $100,000 — this $130,000 order qualifies even though it is under 50 units. Relying on unit count alone (A) ignores the value exception. Splitting and displaying pieces (C) is not required; the exception removes the display obligation. Market Regulator consent (D) is not how the 6.3(1) exceptions operate.
questions.source_quote: "the order has a value of more than $100,000" — umir.txt ~line 5915

questions.stem: Vantage Securities discovers that 300 shares of a client's executed buy order were posted to the wrong client's account. To fix the error, the firm proposes to move the shares between the accounts by journal entry rather than entering offsetting orders on a marketplace. Under UMIR 6.4(2)(c), this is:

options.A: Prohibited because all transfers must occur on a marketplace
options.B: Permitted only with the consent of both clients' Registered Representatives
options.C: Permitted — Rule 6.4(1) does not apply to a trade to adjust by a journal entry an error in connection with a client order
options.D: Permitted only if the shares are first sold to the firm's inventory account

questions.rationale: UMIR 6.4(2)(c) exempts from the trades-on-a-marketplace requirement a trade to adjust by a journal entry an error in connection with a client order — the precise mechanism proposed. An absolute marketplace requirement (A) ignores the enumerated exemptions in 6.4(2). RR consent (B) is not a UMIR condition for the error-adjustment exemption. Routing through inventory (D) is unnecessary and, if used to re-print the trade, could raise double-printing concerns under Rule 2.2 guidance.
questions.source_quote: "to adjust by a journal entry an error in connection with a client order" — umir.txt ~line 6060

questions.stem: After marketplace hours, Marwood Securities agrees as principal to trade a listed security with a Canadian institutional account, conditional on executing the trade on a marketplace at the next opening. At the opening the cross cannot be completed at the agreed price. Under Policy 6.4 Part 1, Wexford must:

options.A: Book the trade through its error account to preserve the transaction
options.B: Cancel the agreement to trade — and the use of an error account to preserve the transaction is prohibited
options.C: Execute the trade over-the-counter at the agreed price
options.D: Hold the agreement open until conditions allow execution later in the week

questions.rationale: Policy 6.4 Part 1 provides that an after-hours agreement to trade with a Canadian account must be conditional on execution on a marketplace or foreign organized regulated market at or immediately following the opening; if the Participant determines that the condition cannot be met, the agreement to trade shall be cancelled, and use of an error account to preserve the transaction is prohibited — making (A) the directly prohibited act. OTC execution (C) would breach Rule 6.4(1) absent an exemption. Holding the agreement open for days (D) defeats the at-or-immediately-following-the-opening condition.
questions.source_quote: "If the Participant determines that the condition of recording the agreement to trade on a marketplace or foreign organized regulated market cannot be met, the agreement to trade shall be cancelled. Use of an error account to preserve the transaction is prohibited." — umir.txt ~line 6140

questions.stem: A Registered Representative at Aldercroft Securities wants to personally take the other side of his retail client's sell order. Under Policy 8.1 Part 2, before the trade the RR must ensure that the client:

options.A: Has signed a general consent covering all future trades with the Registered Representative
options.B: Holds the security in a margin account
options.C: Is informed and consents to the trade in advance, with the consent specific to that trade — it cannot be a general consent to any future trades with the Registered Representative
options.D: Receives a commission rebate of at least 50%

questions.rationale: Policy 8.1 Part 2 states that if the Registered Representative wishes to take the other side of the trade with their client, the client must be informed and consent to the trade in advance, and such consent must be specific to that trade and cannot be in a general consent to any future trades with the Registered Representative — making the blanket consent in (A) exactly what the Policy forbids. Account type (B) is irrelevant to the consent requirement. A commission rebate (D) is not a Policy 8.1 condition; in fact a higher-than-agency commission makes the principal trade harder to justify.
questions.source_quote: "Such consent must be specific to that trade and cannot be in a general consent to any future trades with the Registered Representative." — umir.txt ~line 9110

questions.stem: Denholm Securities hires a new assistant who has not completed any trading proficiency requirement. The desk Supervisor is asked whether the assistant can begin entering marketplace orders right away. Under UMIR 7.2(1), no order may be entered by the Participant unless the person entering or responsible for the order has:

options.A: Worked on a trading desk for at least two years
options.B: Passed the firm's internal ethics quiz
options.C: Obtained personal approval from the firm's board of directors
options.D: Completed the Trader Training Course of the Canadian Securities Institute, an acceptable equivalent demonstration of proficiency in UMIR, or approval of an Exchange or QTRS for order entry

questions.rationale: UMIR 7.2(1) prohibits the entry of an order unless the Participant or the director, officer, partner or employee entering or responsible for the order has completed the Trader Training Course of the Canadian Securities Institute or such other acceptable demonstration of proficiency in UMIR and Policies, or has received approval of an Exchange or QTRS for the entry of orders. Tenure (A), an internal quiz (B) and board sign-off (C) are not recognized proficiency routes — the rule fixes the course/acceptable-equivalent/exchange-approval standard.
questions.source_quote: "completed the Trader Training Course of the Canadian Securities Institute" — umir.txt ~line 8004

questions.stem: Under UMIR 7.3(2), a Participant is responsible for bids and offers that originate from a terminal or computer system allowing access to trading on a marketplace that is operated by or under the control of the Participant:

options.A: Only if a registered employee personally keyed the order
options.B: Whether or not the Participant has authorized the entry of the order
options.C: Only during regular trading hours
options.D: Only after the trade has settled

questions.rationale: UMIR 7.3(2) makes a Participant responsible for all bids and offers entered into, or arising by operation of the trading system of a marketplace, that originate from any terminal or computer system allowing access to trading that is operated by or under the control of the Participant, whether or not the Participant has authorized the entry of the order. Limiting responsibility to keyed orders (A) ignores system-originated orders. Trading-hours (C) and post-settlement (D) limits are invented — responsibility attaches at entry, irrespective of authorization.
questions.source_quote: "A Participant shall be responsible for all bids and offers that are entered into, or arise by operation of the trading system of a marketplace and that originate from any terminal or computer system allowing access to trading on the marketplace" — umir.txt ~line 8045

questions.stem: Elphin Securities has retained an independent third party under UMIR 7.1(7) to provide its electronic trading risk management controls. Under UMIR 7.1(10)(b), how often must Eastvale review and confirm the adequacy and consistent application of the third party's controls and its compliance with the written agreement?
