questions.rationale: Subsection 3620(2) provides that a Dealer Member must not pay any bonus, salary or other compensation to an analyst that is directly based upon a specific investment banking transaction — a bonus calculated as a percentage of one underwriting's fees is the direct, transaction-specific link the prohibition targets, and it is a ban, not a disclosure item. Option B is the closest distractor because it confuses this with subsection 3620(1), under which compensation based upon the dealer's overall investment banking revenues is handled through disclosure; transaction-specific pay sits on the prohibited side of that line. Options C and D invent a size threshold and a payment-form exception that the subsection does not recognize.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must not pay any bonus, salary or other compensation to an analyst that is" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6056

questions.stem: Fennimore Securities makes a market in the plain, non-convertible senior unsecured bonds of Orvalle Transport — fixed-coupon debt with no conversion feature and no performance link to Orvalle's shares. The firm holds no equity positions and makes no market in any Orvalle share or derivative. Does IDPC 3608(2)(vi) require the upcoming Orvalle research report to carry a market-making disclosure?

options.A: Yes — market making in any security of a covered issuer is disclosable
options.B: Yes, because all bonds are equity related securities
options.C: No, but only because bond market making is disclosed to CIRO instead
options.D: No — clause (vi) requires disclosure only of market making in an equity security or equity related security of the subject issuer, and plain non-convertible debt with no performance link to an underlying equity falls outside the equity related security definition

questions.rationale: Clause 3608(2)(vi) requires disclosure if the dealer is making a market in any equity security or equity related security of the subject issuer, and the 3606 definition of 'equity related security' is a security whose performance is based on the performance of an underlying equity security or a basket of income producing assets, including derivatives, convertible securities and income trust units — a plain fixed-coupon, non-convertible bond has no such performance link, so the clause is not triggered. Option A is the closest distractor: it broadens the clause from equity and equity related securities to all securities, dropping the words that limit it. Option B misapplies the definition that pulls in convertibles, not plain debt, and option C invents a CIRO-facing substitute disclosure that does not exist.
questions.source_quote: "if it is making a market in any equity security or equity related security of the subject issuer." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5950

questions.stem: An analyst at Welbeck Sound Securities identifies a conflict that does not match any of the six items enumerated in IDPC 3608(2): the issuer's CEO is the largest investor in a private fund managed by the dealer's asset-management arm. Because the situation is not on the list, the analyst proposes omitting it. What does IDPC 3608(1) say?

options.A: Omission is correct — 3608(2) is an exhaustive code of disclosable conflicts
options.B: The conflict need only be reported internally to the CCO
options.C: Disclosure can be replaced by recusing the analyst
options.D: The report must disclose any matter which might reasonably indicate an existing or potential conflict of interest for the dealer or the analyst — the 3608(2) items are included but the obligation is not limited to them

questions.rationale: Subsection 3608(1) requires a research report prepared by the Dealer Member to disclose any matter which might reasonably indicate an existing or potential conflict of interest for the Dealer Member or the analyst, which includes, but is not limited to, the matters set out in subsection 3608(2) — the enumerated list is a floor, not a ceiling, so a conflict outside the list that reasonably indicates a conflict must still be disclosed. Option A is the closest distractor because it treats the list as exhaustive, contradicting the 'not limited to' words. Option B substitutes internal escalation for the report-level disclosure the subsection requires, and option C swaps a management technique for the disclosure obligation, which 3608(1) does not permit.
questions.source_quote: "analyst, which includes, but is not limited to, the matters set out in subsection 3608(2)." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5927

questions.stem: An internal review at Ostermill Securities finds that for two years, the research department has emailed the investment banking group a 'heads-up' list every Friday showing ratings the analysts expect to change the following week, 'so banking can manage client relationships.' Which minimum policy requirement of IDPC 3621(2) does this practice violate?

options.A: The requirement to disclose analyst compensation in research reports
options.B: The requirement that policies prohibit and prevent the investment banking department from receiving advance notice of new ratings or rating changes on covered issuers
options.C: The requirement to publish quarterly rating-distribution percentages
options.D: The requirement to obtain issuer consent before changing a rating

questions.rationale: Clause 3621(2)(iii) requires the dealer's policies and procedures to specifically address, at a minimum, prohibiting and preventing the investment banking department from receiving advance notice of new ratings or rating changes on covered issuers — a standing weekly preview of expected rating changes is exactly the advance notice the clause requires the firm to prohibit and prevent. Option A is the closest distractor because it names a real research control, but the 3620(1) compensation disclosure has nothing to do with leaking pending ratings to banking. Option C cites the 3609(2) quarterly distribution disclosure, also unrelated to the leak, and option D invents an issuer-consent requirement that would itself violate the rule's independence purpose.
questions.source_quote: "prohibiting and preventing the investment banking department from receiving advance" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6066

questions.stem: Aldwych Park Securities acted as co-manager of a secondary offering of Renato Textiles common shares that closed on October 2. The research department, citing the 10-day rule it applies to new issues, blocks any Renato report until October 13. The analyst objects that the block is one week too long. Who is right under IDPC 3622(1)?

options.A: The research department — every offering attracts a 10-day quiet period
options.B: Neither — quiet periods last 30 days for all offerings
options.C: The analyst — for a secondary offering of equity securities the quiet period is three days after the date of the offering; the 10-day period applies to an initial public offering
options.D: The research department — secondary offerings attract a 40-day quiet period

questions.rationale: Subsection 3622(1) sets two distinct periods for a dealer that acted as manager or co-manager: clause (i) prohibits a research report for 10 days after the date of the offering of an initial public offering of equity securities, and clause (ii) for three days after the date of the offering of a secondary offering of equity securities. Renato's transaction is a secondary offering, so the prohibition runs three days from October 2, far short of October 13. Option A is the closest distractor — it takes the real IPO period and applies it to every offering, ignoring the rule's two-track structure. Options B and D invent 30-day and 40-day periods that appear nowhere in the subsection.
questions.source_quote: "for three days after the date of the offering of a secondary offering of equity securities of" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6076

questions.stem: Three days after Pellington Securities co-managed Corvid Aerospace's initial public offering, Corvid's only factory burns down and the stock falls 30%. Clients flood the desk with questions. The research Supervisor believes the firm is locked out of publishing anything on Corvid until the 10-day quiet period ends. Applying IDPC 3622(2), what may the firm do?

options.A: Issue a research report on the effects of the fire — the quiet period does not prevent a report on the effects of significant news about or a significant event affecting the issuer within the applicable period
options.B: Nothing — the quiet period admits no exceptions of any kind
options.C: Publish only if every other syndicate member publishes simultaneously
options.D: Publish only a report that reiterates the IPO pricing rationale

questions.rationale: Subsection 3622(2) provides that the 3622(1) quiet period does not prevent a Dealer Member from issuing a research report on the effects of significant news about or a significant event affecting the issuer within the applicable 10 day or three day period — a factory fire that erases 30% of the stock's value is a significant event, so a report addressing its effects may be issued inside the window. Option B is the closest distractor because it states the general prohibition while missing the express exception built for exactly this situation. Option C invents a syndicate-coordination condition, and option D would publish promotional reiteration rather than the event-effects analysis the exception covers.
questions.source_quote: "effects of significant news about or a significant event affecting the issuer within the applicable" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6079

questions.stem: Vannier Crossing Securities took a small allocation in the selling group for Helsdon Mining's initial public offering, with no managerial role of any kind — another dealer managed and two others co-managed. Five days after the offering date, Vannier Crossing's analyst wants to initiate coverage. Does the IDPC 3622(1) quiet period block the report?

options.A: Yes — every syndicate participant is bound for 10 days
options.B: Yes — selling-group members are bound for three days, so two more days remain
options.C: No, but only because initiation reports are always exempt from quiet periods
options.D: No — the prohibition applies to a Dealer Member that has acted as manager or co-manager, and Vannier Crossing acted in neither capacity, so 3622(1) does not restrict its report

questions.rationale: Subsection 3622(1) prohibits issuing a research report on equity securities of a subject issuer 'for which the Dealer Member has acted as manager or co-manager' — the trigger is the dealer's own role, and a selling-group participant with no managerial role has not acted as manager or co-manager, so the quiet period does not attach to it. Option A is the closest distractor because syndicate-wide restriction feels prudent, but the rule's words confine the prohibition to managers and co-managers. Option B misappropriates the three-day period, which belongs to secondary offerings by managers and co-managers, and option C invents a general initiation-report exemption — the actual reason the report may proceed is the dealer's non-managerial role.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must not issue a research report on equity securities of a subject issuer for" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6072

questions.stem: Editor Leona Nandakumar was directly involved in preparing Ridgeline Stoke Securities' research report on Talvera Biotech, which was issued on March 3. On March 10 — seven days later — she asks to buy Talvera shares in her personal account, consistent with the report's positive recommendation. Under IDPC 3619(2), does she need a designated Executive's prior written approval for this trade?

options.A: Yes — anyone involved in a report can never trade the subject issuer again
options.B: No — the trading restriction runs for a period beginning 30 days prior to and ending five days after the issuance of the research report, and March 10 is outside that window, so the 3619(2) prohibition no longer applies
options.C: Yes — the restriction ends only 30 days after issuance
options.D: No, because editors are never covered by 3619(2)

questions.rationale: Subsection 3619(2) prohibits an individual directly involved in the preparation or approval of a research report from trading in equity securities or equity related securities of the subject issuer for a period beginning 30 days prior to and ending five days after the issuance of the research report — the window closed on March 8, so a March 10 trade falls outside the prohibition and the 3619(3) Executive-approval mechanism, which exists to permit trades inside the window, is not needed. Option C is the closest distractor: it mirrors the real 30-day figure but attaches it to the wrong side of issuance. Option D wrongly excludes editors — the subsection turns on direct involvement in preparation, which she had — and option A invents a perpetual ban. The firm's own policies and the 3619(1) detection framework still apply generally.
questions.source_quote: "prior to and ending five days after the issuance of the research report." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6040

questions.stem: Analyst Eli Vandermeer of Glenoak Pier Securities faces a court-ordered family settlement that compels him to liquidate all personal holdings within two weeks, including shares of an issuer he rates 'Buy' and on which a report is due out in ten days. He seeks permission to sell inside the trading window. Under IDPC 3619(3) and (4), what is the correct supervisory analysis?

options.A: The sale is absolutely prohibited because it is contrary to his current recommendation
options.B: The sale may proceed on the research Supervisor's verbal say-so
options.C: A designated Executive may grant prior written approval — although approval may not be granted for trades contrary to the analyst's current recommendation, that bar lifts where special circumstances exist, and a court-ordered liquidation is the kind of circumstance the exception contemplates
options.D: The sale is automatically permitted because court orders override CIRO rules

questions.rationale: Subsection 3619(3) permits an individual otherwise caught by the 3619(2) window to trade with the prior written approval of a designated Executive, and subsection 3619(4) provides that approval may not be granted for trades that are contrary to the analyst's current recommendation, unless special circumstances exist — a court-compelled liquidation is a non-discretionary, externally forced sale, the natural content of the special-circumstances exception, so the Executive may consider granting written approval. Option A is the closest distractor: it states the 3619(4) bar but reads out its 'unless special circumstances exist' tail. Option B substitutes the wrong approver and the wrong form — the rule requires prior written approval of a designated Executive. Option D removes the approval step entirely; the order compels the sale but the rule still channels it through Executive approval.
questions.source_quote: "analyst’s current recommendation, unless special circumstances exist." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6044

questions.stem: During a pitch for Maddox Rail's upcoming bond mandate, a managing director of Penleigh Securities tells Maddox's CFO: 'Hire us and our research desk will put out a glowing initiation on your equity within the month.' Which clause of IDPC 3617(1) does this pitch breach?

options.A: The clause on threatening to change a rating
options.B: The clause on delaying the issue date of a report
options.C: The clause on quiet periods after an offering
options.D: The clause prohibiting an offer to issue favourable research report on the issuer as consideration or inducement for the receipt of business or compensation from the issuer

questions.rationale: Clause 3617(1)(i) prohibits a Dealer Member, as consideration or inducement for the receipt of business or compensation from an issuer, directly or indirectly, from offering to issue favourable research report on the issuer — promising a 'glowing initiation' to win the bond mandate is that offer in its plainest form. Option B is the closest distractor among the 3617 clauses because it also involves trading research treatment for business, but clause (iii) concerns delaying changes to ratings, targets or issue dates, not promising favourable coverage. Option A describes the clause (iv) threat scenario, the coercive mirror image of what happened here, and option C cites 3622, a timing rule unrelated to inducements.
questions.source_quote: "offer to issue favourable research report on the issuer," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6018

questions.stem: After Aldenham Quay Securities loses an advisory mandate to a rival, its head of equity capital markets phones the issuer's CEO and says: 'Reconsider, or our analyst will be revisiting that price target of yours — and not upward.' The analyst knew nothing of the call. Analyze the call under IDPC 3617(1).

options.A: It breaches the prohibition on threatening to change a rating or a price target on the issuer's securities, made as consideration or inducement for the receipt of business — and the clause reaches conduct done directly or indirectly, so the analyst's ignorance does not cure the dealer's breach
options.B: It is acceptable hard bargaining, since no report has actually changed
options.C: It breaches only the disclosure rules, fixable by a footnote in the next report
options.D: It is acceptable because the threat concerned a price target rather than a rating

questions.rationale: Clause 3617(1)(iv) prohibits a Dealer Member, as consideration or inducement for the receipt of business or compensation from an issuer, directly or indirectly, from threatening to change a rating or a price target on one or more of the issuer's securities or any other element of a research report — the call is a threat to move the price target to coerce the mandate back, and the lead-in's 'directly or indirectly' captures the dealer's conduct through its banking head regardless of the analyst's knowledge. Option B is the closest distractor: the clause prohibits the threat itself, so no report needs to change for the breach to be complete. Option D fails because the clause covers ratings and price targets alike, and option C miscasts a conduct prohibition as a curable disclosure defect.
questions.source_quote: "threaten to change a rating or a price target on one or more of the issuer’s securities or any" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6023

questions.stem: Corwin Hale, titled 'Senior Investment Strategist' at Faulkner Bay Securities, is never called an analyst publicly, but his job description includes preparing written sector reports — distributed to clients — that recommend specific securities. He insists the analyst rules of Part B cannot apply to him because of his title. Under the IDPC 3606 definition of 'analyst,' is he covered?

options.A: No — only employees with 'analyst' in their title are covered
options.B: No, because strategists report to sales rather than research
options.C: Yes — an analyst includes an employee or Approved Person whose responsibilities to the dealer include the preparation, for distribution to clients or prospective clients, of any written report which includes a recommendation with respect to a security
options.D: Yes, but only if he holds the CFA designation

questions.rationale: The 3606 definition captures a Dealer Member's employee or Approved Person who is held out to the public as an analyst or whose responsibilities to the Dealer Member include the preparation, for distribution to clients or prospective clients, of any written report which includes a recommendation with respect to a security — the second branch is functional, so Corwin's client-distributed, security-recommending reports bring him within the definition whatever his title. Option A is the closest distractor because it relies only on the first, holding-out branch and ignores the responsibilities branch. Option B substitutes reporting lines for the definition's functional test, and option D adds a credential requirement that appears nowhere in the definition.
questions.source_quote: "preparation, for distribution to clients or prospective clients, of any written" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5899

questions.stem: The investor-relations head of Solverra Hydrogen, an issuer covered by Tellsworth Securities, calls Tellsworth's analyst weekly to 'review the model line by line' and demands sign-off rights over any change to the recommendation before publication, threatening to cut management access otherwise. Tellsworth's banking group is uninvolved. Which IDPC 3621 requirement is engaged, and why does banking's non-involvement not end the analysis?

options.A: None — 3621 governs only the investment banking department
options.B: Only the quarterly distribution disclosure in 3609(2)
options.C: The site-visit disclosure in 3613
options.D: Subsection 3621(1) — the dealer's policies and procedures must specifically address preventing recommendations in research reports from being influenced by the investment banking department or the issuer, so issuer pressure is itself within the provision's scope

questions.rationale: Subsection 3621(1) requires the dealer's policies and procedures to specifically address preventing recommendations in research reports from being influenced by the investment banking department or the issuer — the provision names two influence sources, and an issuer demanding sign-off rights over recommendation changes is squarely the second, so the absence of banking involvement does not take the conduct outside 3621. Option A is the closest distractor: it reads the section's title and minimum-content list as exhausting its scope, missing the words 'or the issuer' in 3621(1). Option B points to the rating-distribution disclosure and option C to site-visit disclosure, neither of which addresses an issuer trying to control recommendations.
questions.source_quote: "recommendations in research reports from being influenced by the investment banking" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6060

questions.scenario: Karwood Securities is a Toronto Investment Dealer with a twelve-person research department. Analyst Ingrid Solberg covers Drayford Composites, an industrial materials issuer, and her new report is in final review with designated research Supervisor Mateo Cruz. The file shows: (1) Karwood is currently engaged, for a fee, as financial adviser to Drayford on its proposed acquisition of a U.S. competitor, an engagement that began four months ago; (2) at the end of last month — the report will be issued on the 25th of this month — Karwood and its affiliates held 1.8% of Drayford's common shares; (3) the report appears in a six-page single-issuer format with a 'Buy' recommendation. Meanwhile, an institutional sales trader on Karwood's desk learns through office chatter that Ingrid is about to initiate coverage on a second issuer, Bessamer Tools, with a favourable first-time recommendation, and quietly buys Bessamer shares for his personal account two days before that initiation is issued. Separately, Mateo Cruz himself has authored a report on a bank he personally covers, and the head of research asks who can approve it.
questions.stem: Regarding fact (1) — the ongoing acquisition-advisory engagement — what must Ingrid's Drayford report disclose, and why?

options.A: Nothing, because the engagement has not yet closed
options.B: The investment banking services provided to Drayford for remuneration within the preceding 12 months — acting as a financial adviser in a merger or acquisition is within the IDPC 3606 definition of investment banking
options.C: Only the fee amount, rounded to the nearest thousand dollars
options.D: Nothing, because advisory work is covered by the normal-course trade execution carve-out

questions.rationale: The 3606 definition of investment banking includes acting as a financial adviser in a merger or acquisition, and clause 3608(2)(iv) requires a research report to disclose any investment banking services provided by the Dealer Member to the issuer for remuneration during the 12 months immediately preceding the report — a four-month-old, ongoing, fee-paying M&A advisory engagement is squarely disclosable. Option D is the closest distractor: it borrows the carve-out from clause 3608(2)(iii), which excludes normal course investment advisory or trade execution services from the prior-services disclosure, but M&A advisory is investment banking, not normal-course trade execution. Option A invents a closing condition the clause does not contain, and option C substitutes a fee-quantification duty for the services disclosure the rule actually requires.
questions.source_quote: "(ii) acting as a financial adviser in a merger or acquisition, or" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5906

questions.scenario: Karwood Securities is a Toronto Investment Dealer with a twelve-person research department. Analyst Ingrid Solberg covers Drayford Composites, an industrial materials issuer, and her new report is in final review with designated research Supervisor Mateo Cruz. The file shows: (1) Karwood is currently engaged, for a fee, as financial adviser to Drayford on its proposed acquisition of a U.S. competitor, an engagement that began four months ago; (2) at the end of last month — the report will be issued on the 25th of this month — Karwood and its affiliates held 1.8% of Drayford's common shares; (3) the report appears in a six-page single-issuer format with a 'Buy' recommendation. Meanwhile, an institutional sales trader on Karwood's desk learns through office chatter that Ingrid is about to initiate coverage on a second issuer, Bessamer Tools, with a favourable first-time recommendation, and quietly buys Bessamer shares for his personal account two days before that initiation is issued. Separately, Mateo Cruz himself has authored a report on a bank he personally covers, and the head of research asks who can approve it.
questions.stem: Regarding fact (2): the Drayford report will be issued on the 25th of the month. Which month-end governs the 1% beneficial-ownership measurement under IDPC 3608(2)(i), and is disclosure required for the 1.8% position?

options.A: The end of the month prior to the issuance date — because the 25th is not less than 10 days after that month-end, the standard branch applies, and the 1.8% position at that date exceeds one percent, so disclosure is required
options.B: The end of the second most recent month, because that branch always applies to mid-month issuance
options.C: The issuance date itself, with positions averaged over the trading day
options.D: No measurement matters, because affiliates' holdings are excluded from the test

questions.rationale: Clause 3608(2)(i)(a) measures beneficial ownership as of the end of the month prior to the issuance date of the research report, with clause (b) substituting the end of the second most recent month only if the report issuance date is less than 10 days after the end of the prior month — issuing on the 25th is well past 10 days, so the standard prior-month-end branch governs, and the 1.8% position at that date exceeds the one percent threshold, requiring disclosure. Option B is the closest distractor: it deploys the real alternative branch but in the wrong circumstances, since that branch exists for early-month issuance when prior-month data may not be compiled. Option C uses a measurement date the clause does not recognize, and option D contradicts the clause's express inclusion of the Dealer Member 'or its affiliates.'
questions.source_quote: "(a)     as of the end of the month prior to the issuance date of the research report, or" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5931

questions.scenario: Karwood Securities is a Toronto Investment Dealer with a twelve-person research department. Analyst Ingrid Solberg covers Drayford Composites, an industrial materials issuer, and her new report is in final review with designated research Supervisor Mateo Cruz. The file shows: (1) Karwood is currently engaged, for a fee, as financial adviser to Drayford on its proposed acquisition of a U.S. competitor, an engagement that began four months ago; (2) at the end of last month — the report will be issued on the 25th of this month — Karwood and its affiliates held 1.8% of Drayford's common shares; (3) the report appears in a six-page single-issuer format with a 'Buy' recommendation. Meanwhile, an institutional sales trader on Karwood's desk learns through office chatter that Ingrid is about to initiate coverage on a second issuer, Bessamer Tools, with a favourable first-time recommendation, and quietly buys Bessamer shares for his personal account two days before that initiation is issued. Separately, Mateo Cruz himself has authored a report on a bank he personally covers, and the head of research asks who can approve it.
questions.stem: The sales trader argues his Bessamer purchase is untouchable by Karwood's research trading policies because the upcoming publication is a first-time initiation, 'not a change to anything.' How should the firm's IDPC 3619(1) policies treat his trade?

options.A: As permitted — 3619(1) covers only changes to existing recommendations
options.B: As permitted, because sales traders are exempt from research trading policies
options.C: As trading the policies must detect and restrict — 3619(1) covers trading done with knowledge of or in anticipation of the issuance of a research report, a new recommendation, or a change in a recommendation that could reasonably be expected to affect the price
options.D: As permitted, provided he discloses the trade in next quarter's attestation

questions.rationale: Subsection 3619(1) requires policies and procedures that specifically address detecting and restricting any trading in equity securities or equity related securities of a subject issuer done with knowledge of or in anticipation of (i) the issuance of a research report, (ii) a new recommendation, or (iii) a change in a recommendation, where it could reasonably be expected to affect the price — a first-time favourable initiation is both the issuance of a research report and 'a new recommendation,' so buying ahead of it on office chatter is precisely what the policies must detect and restrict. Option A is the closest distractor: it isolates clause (iii) and ignores clauses (i) and (ii), which exist to close exactly this initiation gap. Option B has no basis — the subsection addresses any such trading, not particular job categories — and option D substitutes after-the-fact attestation for detection and restriction.
questions.source_quote: "(ii) a new recommendation, or" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6034

questions.scenario: Karwood Securities is a Toronto Investment Dealer with a twelve-person research department. Analyst Ingrid Solberg covers Drayford Composites, an industrial materials issuer, and her new report is in final review with designated research Supervisor Mateo Cruz. The file shows: (1) Karwood is currently engaged, for a fee, as financial adviser to Drayford on its proposed acquisition of a U.S. competitor, an engagement that began four months ago; (2) at the end of last month — the report will be issued on the 25th of this month — Karwood and its affiliates held 1.8% of Drayford's common shares; (3) the report appears in a six-page single-issuer format with a 'Buy' recommendation. Meanwhile, an institutional sales trader on Karwood's desk learns through office chatter that Ingrid is about to initiate coverage on a second issuer, Bessamer Tools, with a favourable first-time recommendation, and quietly buys Bessamer shares for his personal account two days before that initiation is issued. Separately, Mateo Cruz himself has authored a report on a bank he personally covers, and the head of research asks who can approve it.
questions.stem: Mateo Cruz, the designated research Supervisor, has himself authored a report on a bank he covers. He proposes signing his own approval. Using IDPC 3607(2) and the syllabus 8.4 parameters, what should the head of research require instead?

options.A: Self-approval, since Mateo is the most qualified reviewer
options.B: Review and approval by another designated Supervisor — the rule permits designating one or more Supervisors to be responsible for reviewing and approving research reports, and the syllabus identifies cross-supervision as the answer when the designated Supervisor's own work needs approval
options.C: Approval by the head of investment banking as a neutral senior party
options.D: Publication without approval, since Supervisors are exempt from review

questions.rationale: Subsection 3607(2) requires the dealer to designate one or more Supervisors to be responsible for reviewing and approving research reports, and the syllabus 8.4 parameter pairs that designation requirement with 'Cross-supervision' — the arrangement under which a second designated Supervisor reviews and approves material produced by the first, preserving an independent check when the designated Supervisor is the author. Option A is the closest distractor: expertise does not solve the self-review problem the approval requirement exists to prevent. Option C is independently prohibited — clause 3621(2)(i) requires policies prohibiting the approval of research reports by the investment banking department — and option D abandons the 3602(3)(i) requirement that research reports be approved before use or publication.
questions.source_quote:
"Designating one or more Supervisors to be responsible for reviewing
and approving research reports
- Cross-supervision" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 1544-1546

questions.scenario: Ossington Bluffs Securities runs a spring marketing campaign out of its Winnipeg head office. Designated advertising Supervisor Sylvie Kowalczyk audits the campaign and finds: (i) Registered Representative Joaquin Stannard has added the designation 'Family Stewardship Counsellor' to his client emails — the firm formally approved this designation last year, but only for his colleague Greta Iverson, the person who completed the issuing program; (ii) an email blast was sent to 4,000 purchased addresses of people with no relationship to the firm, with no consent records, though its investment content satisfied every CIRO content standard; (iii) a live 'Spring Portfolio Strategies' webinar deck pitching the firm's new managed program is scheduled for next Tuesday, and the deck has not yet been approved by anyone; and (iv) the campaign's centrepiece print advertisement was created by an outside advertising agency, whose creative director 'signed off on compliance' before it ran in two newspapers, with no internal review of any kind before publication.
questions.stem: Item (i): the firm approved the 'Family Stewardship Counsellor' designation for Greta Iverson. May Joaquin Stannard use it in his client emails under IDPC 3640(2)?

options.A: Yes — once a dealer approves a designation, every Approved Person at the firm may use it
options.B: Yes, provided Joaquin enrols in the issuing program within a year
options.C: No — clause 3640(2)(iii) prohibits use where the dealer has not approved the use by that Approved Person of the title or designation, and Ossington Bluffs approved its use by Greta Iverson, not by Joaquin
options.D: No, because designations may never appear in client emails

questions.rationale: Clause 3640(2)(iii) prohibits a client-facing Approved Person from using a title or designation 'if the Approved Person's Dealer Member has not approved the use by that Approved Person' of it — the approval is person-specific, so the firm's approval of Greta Iverson's use does not authorize Joaquin's, and his emails breach the clause until the firm approves his own use. Option A is the closest distractor: it converts a per-person approval into a firm-wide licence, contradicting the clause's 'by that Approved Person' wording. Option B invents a grace-period cure, and option D overshoots — approved designations may be used; the rule controls who has approval, not the email medium.
questions.source_quote: "if the Approved Person’s Dealer Member has not approved the use by that Approved Person" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6113

questions.scenario: Ossington Bluffs Securities runs a spring marketing campaign out of its Winnipeg head office. Designated advertising Supervisor Sylvie Kowalczyk audits the campaign and finds: (i) Registered Representative Joaquin Stannard has added the designation 'Family Stewardship Counsellor' to his client emails — the firm formally approved this designation last year, but only for his colleague Greta Iverson, the person who completed the issuing program; (ii) an email blast was sent to 4,000 purchased addresses of people with no relationship to the firm, with no consent records, though its investment content satisfied every CIRO content standard; (iii) a live 'Spring Portfolio Strategies' webinar deck pitching the firm's new managed program is scheduled for next Tuesday, and the deck has not yet been approved by anyone; and (iv) the campaign's centrepiece print advertisement was created by an outside advertising agency, whose creative director 'signed off on compliance' before it ran in two newspapers, with no internal review of any kind before publication.
questions.stem: Item (ii): the email blast met every CIRO content standard but went to purchased addresses with no consent records. Why can Sylvie still treat the blast as a breach of IDPC 3602(1)?

options.A: Because purchased mailing lists are banned by name in Rule 3600
options.B: Because all marketing emails require CIRO pre-clearance
options.C: Because emails may never be sent to more than 1,000 recipients
options.D: Because clause 3602(1)(vii) prohibits communications that fail to comply with Corporation requirements or any applicable laws — and the syllabus identifies Canadian Anti-spam Legislation among the legislative requirements relevant to supervising client communications, so satisfying CIRO content standards alone does not make the blast compliant

questions.rationale: Clause 3602(1)(vii) prohibits any advertisement, sales literature or correspondence that fails to comply with Corporation requirements or any applicable laws — the clause incorporates external law into the communications standard, and the syllabus 8.1 parameter expressly lists 'Legislative requirements, including Canadian Anti-spam Legislation,' the regime governing consent for commercial electronic messages. A blast to purchased addresses without consent records can therefore breach clause (vii) even though every CIRO content rule was met. Option A is the closest distractor: Rule 3600 names no mailing-list ban; the defect arrives through the applicable-laws clause. Options B and C invent a CIRO pre-clearance regime and a recipient cap that exist nowhere in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "fails to comply with Corporation requirements or any applicable laws." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5863

questions.scenario: Ossington Bluffs Securities runs a spring marketing campaign out of its Winnipeg head office. Designated advertising Supervisor Sylvie Kowalczyk audits the campaign and finds: (i) Registered Representative Joaquin Stannard has added the designation 'Family Stewardship Counsellor' to his client emails — the firm formally approved this designation last year, but only for his colleague Greta Iverson, the person who completed the issuing program; (ii) an email blast was sent to 4,000 purchased addresses of people with no relationship to the firm, with no consent records, though its investment content satisfied every CIRO content standard; (iii) a live 'Spring Portfolio Strategies' webinar deck pitching the firm's new managed program is scheduled for next Tuesday, and the deck has not yet been approved by anyone; and (iv) the campaign's centrepiece print advertisement was created by an outside advertising agency, whose creative director 'signed off on compliance' before it ran in two newspapers, with no internal review of any kind before publication.
questions.stem: Item (iii): the 'Spring Portfolio Strategies' webinar pitching the firm's new managed program is set for Tuesday, and its deck is unapproved. The presenter argues a live webinar 'isn't a seminar' under the rule. What must Sylvie require?

options.A: The deck must be approved by a designated Supervisor before the webinar — it is a promotional seminar text within 3602(3)(iv), and presenting it in a virtual format does not move it off the pre-approval list
options.B: Nothing — webinars are spoken events outside Rule 3600 entirely
options.C: Recording the webinar and reviewing the recording within a week
options.D: Approval is needed only if attendees pay an admission fee

questions.rationale: Clause 3602(3)(iv) places promotional seminar texts (excluding educational seminar texts) on the list of items the dealer must ensure are approved by a designated Supervisor before use or publication — a deck built to pitch the firm's new managed program is promotional seminar text, and nothing in the clause confines 'seminar' to in-person formats, so the Tuesday session cannot proceed on an unapproved deck. Option C is the closest distractor: record-and-review is a 3602(4) post-use method, available only for material not listed in 3602(3). Option B wrongly exempts a prepared, presented text because the event is live, and option D invents an admission-fee trigger; what matters is the promotional character of the text.
questions.source_quote: "(iv)     promotional seminar texts (excluding educational seminar texts)," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5871

questions.scenario: Ossington Bluffs Securities runs a spring marketing campaign out of its Winnipeg head office. Designated advertising Supervisor Sylvie Kowalczyk audits the campaign and finds: (i) Registered Representative Joaquin Stannard has added the designation 'Family Stewardship Counsellor' to his client emails — the firm formally approved this designation last year, but only for his colleague Greta Iverson, the person who completed the issuing program; (ii) an email blast was sent to 4,000 purchased addresses of people with no relationship to the firm, with no consent records, though its investment content satisfied every CIRO content standard; (iii) a live 'Spring Portfolio Strategies' webinar deck pitching the firm's new managed program is scheduled for next Tuesday, and the deck has not yet been approved by anyone; and (iv) the campaign's centrepiece print advertisement was created by an outside advertising agency, whose creative director 'signed off on compliance' before it ran in two newspapers, with no internal review of any kind before publication.
questions.stem: Item (iv): the centrepiece print advertisement ran after the outside agency's creative director 'signed off on compliance,' with no internal review before publication. Applying IDPC 3602(3), why does the agency sign-off fail?

options.A: Because outside agencies may design but never write advertising copy
options.B: Because newspaper advertising requires CIRO approval rather than agency approval
options.C: Because agency sign-offs are valid only when countersigned by the firm's lawyers
options.D: Because the rule places the obligation on the Dealer Member to ensure that original advertisements are approved by a designated Supervisor before use or publication — outsourcing production does not transfer the approval function to the vendor

questions.rationale: Subsection 3602(3) opens with 'A Dealer Member must ensure that the following items are approved by a designated Supervisor before use or publication,' and original advertisements are on the list — the approval must come from a designated Supervisor of the dealer, and the dealer's duty to ensure it cannot be discharged by an external agency's creative director, however confident the sign-off. Option C is the closest distractor: it at least recognizes the agency sign-off is insufficient, but it substitutes legal countersignature for the designated-Supervisor approval the rule names. Option A invents a design-versus-copy restriction, and option B invents CIRO approval of dealer advertising, which the rule does not require.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must ensure that the following items are approved by a designated Supervisor" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5866

questions.scenario: Vanstone Capital Markets is an Investment Dealer with an active equity research group. Within one week, four matters land on the desk of designated research Supervisor Adaeze Nwabueze: (1) Vanstone co-managed an offering of Trembleau Energy's equity securities, and the syndicate desk advises that the subject securities are exempted from restrictions under the market-stabilization provisions in Corporation requirements and securities laws, while the research team asks whether the usual post-offering publication blackout applies; (2) a compliance audit reports that conversations between Vanstone analysts and investment banking staff about issuers under current research coverage occur freely by phone and hallway, with no system anywhere that controls or records that information flow; (3) analyst Bruno Akhtar discloses that he has just accepted an unpaid seat on the volunteer board of a community housing charity and argues no firm sign-off is needed since no securities and no pay are involved; and (4) analyst Mei-Lin Cho, with the firm's written pre-approval, has for six months provided paid strategic advice to Quarstadt Minerals — and now proposes to initiate research coverage on Quarstadt herself.
questions.stem: Matter (1): the subject securities of the Trembleau Energy offering are exempted under the market-stabilization provisions. Does the IDPC 3622(1) quiet period bind Vanstone's research on Trembleau?

options.A: Yes — stabilization status is irrelevant to research timing
options.B: Yes, but the period shrinks from 10 days to five
options.C: No — subsection 3622(3) provides that 3622(1) does not apply where the subject securities are exempted from restrictions under provisions relating to market stabilization set out in Corporation requirements and securities laws
options.D: No, because co-managers were never subject to quiet periods

questions.rationale: Subsection 3622(3) states that subsection 3622(1) does not apply where the subject securities are exempted from restrictions under provisions relating to market stabilization set out in Corporation requirements and securities laws — on the syndicate desk's advice that the exemption applies, the quiet period in 3622(1) does not bind the research. Option A is the closest distractor: it asserts the general co-manager prohibition while missing the express carve-out in 3622(3). Option B invents a shortened period found nowhere in the rule, and option D is wrong on the law — 3622(1) expressly applies to a dealer that has acted as manager or co-manager, so absent the exemption the quiet period would bind Vanstone.
questions.source_quote: "under provisions relating to market stabilization set out in Corporation requirements and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6082

questions.scenario: Vanstone Capital Markets is an Investment Dealer with an active equity research group. Within one week, four matters land on the desk of designated research Supervisor Adaeze Nwabueze: (1) Vanstone co-managed an offering of Trembleau Energy's equity securities, and the syndicate desk advises that the subject securities are exempted from restrictions under the market-stabilization provisions in Corporation requirements and securities laws, while the research team asks whether the usual post-offering publication blackout applies; (2) a compliance audit reports that conversations between Vanstone analysts and investment banking staff about issuers under current research coverage occur freely by phone and hallway, with no system anywhere that controls or records that information flow; (3) analyst Bruno Akhtar discloses that he has just accepted an unpaid seat on the volunteer board of a community housing charity and argues no firm sign-off is needed since no securities and no pay are involved; and (4) analyst Mei-Lin Cho, with the firm's written pre-approval, has for six months provided paid strategic advice to Quarstadt Minerals — and now proposes to initiate research coverage on Quarstadt herself.
questions.stem: Matter (2): the audit found no system anywhere that controls or records the flow of information between analysts and investment banking staff about covered issuers. Which minimum requirement of IDPC 3621(2) is Vanstone missing?

options.A: The requirement that policies and procedures address establishing systems to control and record the flow of information between analysts and investment banking department staff regarding issuers that are the subject of current or prospective research reports
options.B: The requirement that analysts and bankers work on separate floors of the building
options.C: The requirement that all analyst-banker conversations be chaperoned by external counsel
options.D: The requirement to file conversation logs with CIRO monthly

questions.rationale: Clause 3621(2)(iv) requires the dealer's policies and procedures to specifically address, at a minimum, establishing systems to control and record the flow of information between analysts and investment banking department staff, regarding issuers that are the subject of current or prospective research reports — uncontrolled, unrecorded phone and hallway exchanges about covered issuers mean the required systems simply do not exist. Option B is the closest distractor: physical separation is one way firms implement information barriers, but the clause mandates control-and-record systems, not a floor plan. Options C and D invent chaperoning by external counsel and CIRO log filings, neither of which appears in the clause.
questions.source_quote: "establishing systems to control and record the flow of information between analysts and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6068

questions.scenario: Vanstone Capital Markets is an Investment Dealer with an active equity research group. Within one week, four matters land on the desk of designated research Supervisor Adaeze Nwabueze: (1) Vanstone co-managed an offering of Trembleau Energy's equity securities, and the syndicate desk advises that the subject securities are exempted from restrictions under the market-stabilization provisions in Corporation requirements and securities laws, while the research team asks whether the usual post-offering publication blackout applies; (2) a compliance audit reports that conversations between Vanstone analysts and investment banking staff about issuers under current research coverage occur freely by phone and hallway, with no system anywhere that controls or records that information flow; (3) analyst Bruno Akhtar discloses that he has just accepted an unpaid seat on the volunteer board of a community housing charity and argues no firm sign-off is needed since no securities and no pay are involved; and (4) analyst Mei-Lin Cho, with the firm's written pre-approval, has for six months provided paid strategic advice to Quarstadt Minerals — and now proposes to initiate research coverage on Quarstadt herself.
questions.stem: Matter (3): Bruno Akhtar contends his unpaid charity board seat needs no firm sign-off because it involves no securities and no compensation. How does IDPC 3623(1) apply?

options.A: He is right — only paid outside activities require approval
options.B: He is wrong — the rule states that a Dealer Member must pre-approve an analyst's outside activities, without qualification as to payment or subject matter, so the board seat requires the firm's pre-approval
options.C: He is right — charitable roles are expressly exempted by the rule
options.D: He is wrong, but only because housing charities can issue bonds

questions.rationale: Subsection 3623(1) states in unqualified terms that a Dealer Member must pre-approve an analyst's outside activities — the text draws no line between paid and unpaid roles or between securities-related and other activities, so the charity board seat requires pre-approval before he takes it up; vetting even benign-seeming roles is how the firm detects relationships that could later trigger the 3614 issuing prohibition or 3608 disclosures, and the syllabus pairs analyst conduct with outside activities. Option A is the closest distractor: it implies a remuneration threshold the one-sentence rule does not contain. Option C invents a charitable exemption, and option D reaches the right conclusion through an irrelevant speculation about the charity's financing rather than the rule's unqualified wording.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must pre-approve an analyst’s outside activities." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6085

questions.scenario: Vanstone Capital Markets is an Investment Dealer with an active equity research group. Within one week, four matters land on the desk of designated research Supervisor Adaeze Nwabueze: (1) Vanstone co-managed an offering of Trembleau Energy's equity securities, and the syndicate desk advises that the subject securities are exempted from restrictions under the market-stabilization provisions in Corporation requirements and securities laws, while the research team asks whether the usual post-offering publication blackout applies; (2) a compliance audit reports that conversations between Vanstone analysts and investment banking staff about issuers under current research coverage occur freely by phone and hallway, with no system anywhere that controls or records that information flow; (3) analyst Bruno Akhtar discloses that he has just accepted an unpaid seat on the volunteer board of a community housing charity and argues no firm sign-off is needed since no securities and no pay are involved; and (4) analyst Mei-Lin Cho, with the firm's written pre-approval, has for six months provided paid strategic advice to Quarstadt Minerals — and now proposes to initiate research coverage on Quarstadt herself.
questions.stem: Matter (4): Mei-Lin Cho's paid strategic-advice role at Quarstadt Minerals carries the firm's written pre-approval, and she now wants to publish research initiating coverage on Quarstadt. May Vanstone issue her report?

options.A: Yes — the 3623 pre-approval immunizes any consequence of the outside activity
options.B: Yes, provided the advisory role is disclosed prominently in the report
options.C: Yes, if a second analyst co-signs the report
options.D: No — IDPC 3614 prohibits the dealer from issuing a research report prepared by an analyst on any issuer for which the analyst serves in any advisory capacity, and the 3623 pre-approval of the outside activity does not convert that issuing prohibition into a disclosure matter

questions.rationale: Section 3614 prohibits a Dealer Member from issuing a research report prepared by an analyst on any issuer for which the analyst, an associate of the analyst or the designated Supervisor serves as an officer, director or employee of the issuer, or serves in any advisory capacity to the issuer — Mei-Lin's ongoing paid strategic-advice role is service in an advisory capacity, so her Quarstadt report cannot be issued; pre-approval under 3623 governs whether she may hold the outside role, not whether the dealer may publish her research on that issuer. Option B is the closest distractor: it treats 3614 as if it were a 3608 disclosure item, but 3614 is a prohibition on issuing, which disclosure cannot satisfy. Option A overreads the effect of outside-activity approval, and option C's co-signature does not change that the report was prepared by an analyst with the prohibited relationship.
questions.source_quote: "(ii)    serves in any advisory capacity to the issuer." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5999

questions.stem: Compliance at Ullswater Securities is drafting its business location supervision program and must first record which locations, activities and registrant populations the program will cover before any visit is scheduled. Within the syllabus 9.1 guideline components, this task corresponds to:

options.A: Audit report and follow up
options.B: Scope
options.C: Risk identification
options.D: Training

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.1 lists scope first among the business location supervision guidelines: defining which locations, activities and people the program covers is the scope decision that precedes everything else. Risk identification (option C) is the closest distractor, but it ranks the covered locations by risk after coverage has been defined — it does not decide what is covered. Option A documents results at the end of the cycle, and option D equips the audit staff rather than defining coverage.
questions.source_quote: "• Scope" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1613

questions.stem: Otterell Partners proposes to route all order tickets for one remote branch through an unrelated numbered company so that the branch's activity never appears in the dealer's supervision systems. Which foundational IDPC organizational requirement does this arrangement breach?

options.A: A dealer's business must be organized to enable adequate supervision of all of its activities and cannot be organized to avoid Corporation requirements
options.B: A dealer must pay an annual fee for each branch office member
options.C: A dealer must hold its annual meeting at head office
options.D: A dealer must obtain CIRO approval of every branch lease

questions.rationale: Subsection 2201(1) requires that a Dealer Member's business be organized to enable adequate supervision of all of its activities and provides that it cannot be organized to avoid Corporation requirements — routing one branch's activity outside the supervision system is organization-to-avoid. Option B is false: section 2268 says no annual or entrance fee is payable for branch office members, so it is the closest trap for candidates who recall Part D. Options C and D invent requirements that appear nowhere in Rule 2200.
questions.source_quote: "of its activities and cannot be organized to avoid Corporation requirements." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1282

questions.stem: When deciding how many Supervisors to appoint across its business locations, what does section 3905 of the IDPC Rules require an Investment Dealer to take into account?

options.A: The average age of its Registered Representatives
options.B: The number of CIRO Districts in which it operates
options.C: The scope and complexity of the Dealer Member's business
options.D: The supervisor-to-registrant ratios published annually by CIRO

questions.rationale: Subsection 3905(2) requires a dealer to appoint as many Supervisors as necessary to properly supervise its employees and Approved Persons taking into account the scope and complexity of the Dealer Member's business. Option D is the closest distractor because candidates expect a prescribed ratio, but the rule sets a principle, not a published ratio. Option B confuses geographic district count with the business-based test, and option A is irrelevant to supervisory capacity.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must appoint as many Supervisors as necessary to properly supervise its" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7923

questions.stem: Absent unusual circumstances, within what time does CIRO's account supervision guidance expect a dealer to complete the first-tier daily review of a business location's trading?

options.A: Before the market opens on the day of the trading
options.B: Within 21 days of the trading activity
options.C: Within six months, aligned with the branch audit cycle
options.D: One business day after the trading activity occurs

questions.rationale: The guidance directs dealers to design their first-tier daily review of the previous day's trading to detect the section 3945 issues and to complete this review one business day after the trading activity occurs, unless precluded by unusual circumstances. Option B is the closest distractor — 21 days is the completion window the guidance sets for the monthly review, not the daily one. Option A describes a pre-trade model rather than the daily post-trade review timing, and option C confuses the trade-review cycle with location audit scheduling.
questions.source_quote: "issues in section 3945. They should complete this review one business day after the trading" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 239

questions.stem: In CIRO's account supervision guidance, the first-tier monthly review of a business location's retail activity should be completed within what period, unless precluded by unusual circumstances?

options.A: 21 days of the review period
options.B: One business day after month-end
options.C: 90 days of the review period
options.D: Seven years of the review period

questions.rationale: The guidance states that dealers should complete the first-tier monthly review within 21 days of the review period, unless precluded by unusual circumstances. Option B is the closest distractor because one business day is the guidance's completion expectation for daily reviews, not monthly ones. Option C has no basis in the guidance, and option D confuses the record-retention period in section 3803 with a review-completion deadline.
questions.source_quote: "Dealers should complete this review within 21 days of the review period, unless precluded" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 254

questions.stem: Petrel Cove Capital's head office is writing the criteria for its second-tier daily review of branch trading. Which criterion appears in CIRO's guidance as an example that would satisfy Rule 3900?

options.A: All trades executed before 10:00 a.m. Eastern time
options.B: Trades in accounts whose holders were born in even-numbered years
options.C: All client accounts not reviewed by a branch manager
options.D: Only trades the client has complained about in writing

questions.rationale: Among the example second-tier daily review criteria the guidance says would satisfy Rule 3900 is all client accounts not reviewed by a branch manager — second-tier review closes the gap where first-tier coverage is absent, alongside criteria such as producing branch managers' client accounts, trade cancellations and outstanding margin calls. Option D is the closest distractor because complaints do trigger separate handling under 3946(1), but a written-complaint filter is not the listed second-tier daily criterion. Options A and B are arbitrary filters unconnected to risk and appear nowhere in the guidance.
questions.source_quote: "all client accounts not reviewed by a branch manager," — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 284

questions.stem: Anstruther Capital's Yellowknife branch is the only one in the firm permitted to distribute a structured note with embedded leverage; every other branch sells conventional securities. In the syllabus 9.2 framework, the elevated risk this single product creates for that branch is classified as:

options.A: Key person reliance, an internal risk factor
options.B: Number of complaints, a reputational risk factor
options.C: Exposure inherent to providing a particular product or service at a business location — a business activity risk
options.D: Risk assessment and control practices, a size risk consideration

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2 lists, within the business location's business activity risk, the exposure inherent to providing a particular product or service at a business location — a leveraged structured note sold only at one branch concentrates that inherent product exposure there. Option D is the closest distractor because controls must respond to the exposure, but risk assessment and control practices is a size-risk consideration describing the location's control maturity, not the product exposure itself. Option A concerns dependence on an individual and option B concerns complaint volume, neither of which the facts raise.
questions.source_quote: "- Exposure inherent to providing a particular product or service at a" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1631

questions.stem: Rosemere Financial's Victoria branch runs retail advisory, options, new-issue distribution and a fee-based managed program side by side, and head office wants to assess how those businesses combine. Which syllabus 9.2 business activity consideration speaks directly to this combined assessment?

options.A: Any internal disciplinary action
options.B: Adequacy of resources
options.C: The business location's lines of business and the aggregate risk present in its overall portfolio
options.D: Community-specific marketing strategy and sales practices

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2 lists, under business activity risk, the business location's lines of business and the aggregate risk present in its overall portfolio — exactly the combined, portfolio-level view of multiple businesses run at one location. Option B is the closest distractor because more lines of business strain resources, but adequacy of resources is an internal factor about capacity, not about how the businesses aggregate. Options A and D are reputational factors about conduct signals, not about the mix of businesses.
questions.source_quote: "- Business location’s lines of business and the aggregate risk present" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1633

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT one of the internal risk factors listed for business locations in syllabus 9.2?

options.A: Adequacy of resources
options.B: Key person reliance
options.C: Principal-agent
options.D: Number of complaints

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2's internal risk factors are adequacy of resources, key person reliance and principal-agent. Number of complaints is a real 9.2 item, but it sits in the reputational risk list, making option D the item that does not belong. Options A, B and C each name a listed internal factor, so none can be the exception.
questions.source_quote: "- Adequacy of resources" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1622

questions.stem: According to the opening text of syllabus 9.2, a Supervisor considers additional risk factors of using different business locations because of their potential impact on whom?

options.A: CIRO's enforcement department and hearing panels
options.B: The Investment Dealers and its clients
options.C: The location's landlord and insurers
options.D: Competing dealers in the same Region

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2 directs the Supervisor to understand additional risk factors and the potential impact on the Investment Dealers and its clients of using different business locations — the framework exists to protect both the firm and the people it serves. Option A is the closest distractor because location failures can end in enforcement, but enforcement is a consequence, not the stated impact population. Options C and D name commercial third parties the syllabus does not mention.
questions.source_quote: "Investment Dealers and its clients of using different business locations." — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1619

questions.stem: Head office at Lindenfield Capital is rolling out cross-training so that at least two people at each branch can run supervision, production support and back-office workflows. Which syllabus 9.2 internal risk factor is this program designed to mitigate?

options.A: Number of outside activities
options.B: Client base risk
options.C: Key person reliance
options.D: Exposure inherent to a particular product

questions.rationale: Key person reliance is the syllabus 9.2 internal risk factor describing a location's dependence on one individual; cross-training a second person in each critical workflow directly reduces that dependence. Option B is the closest distractor in form because it is also a standalone risk concept, but client base risk concerns clients using the location for inappropriate actions, which staffing redundancy does not address. Option A is a reputational indicator about a registrant's external involvements, and option D is a product-exposure measure — neither is touched by cross-training.
questions.source_quote: "- Key person reliance" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1623

questions.stem: Which pairing correctly matches a syllabus 9.2 risk item to its category?

options.A: Number of trading errors — reputational risk
options.B: Risk-weighting — business location size risk
options.C: Adequacy of resources — client base risk
options.D: Number of complaints — internal risk factor

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2 lists risk-weighting under business location size risk, together with risk assessment and control practices. Option A misfiles trading errors, which belong to business activity risk — the closest trap because errors do embarrass a branch, but the syllabus categorizes the count as an activity metric. Option C misfiles adequacy of resources, an internal factor, and option D misfiles number of complaints, a reputational factor.
questions.source_quote: "Business location size risk, including: - Risk-weighting" — syllabus-raw.txt ~lines 1663-1664

questions.stem: To manage the client base risk that syllabus 9.2 assigns to every business location, subsection 3926(2) of the IDPC Rules requires a dealer's policies and procedures to specifically address its obligations to do which of the following?

options.A: Identify clients that present a high risk to the dealer, identify clients that present a high risk of conducting improper activities in the securities markets, and comply with all anti-money laundering and terrorist financing requirements under applicable laws
options.B: Refuse accounts for any client residing more than 100 kilometres from a branch
options.C: Report every cash deposit at a branch to the local police service
options.D: Obtain CIRO pre-approval before accepting politically exposed clients

questions.rationale: Subsection 3926(2) requires policies and procedures that specifically address the dealer's obligations to identify clients that present a high risk to the Dealer Member, identify clients that present a high risk of conducting improper activities in the securities markets, and comply with all anti-money laundering and terrorist financing requirements under applicable laws — the control framework for the 9.2 risk that clients may use a location for inappropriate actions. Option C is the closest distractor because suspicious activity does get reported, but the statutory channel is AML reporting under applicable laws, not blanket police reports of every deposit. Option B invents a residency bar and option D invents a CIRO pre-approval step, neither of which exists.
questions.source_quote: "identify clients that present a high risk to the Dealer Member," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8099

questions.stem: Mardale Capital's Brandon location shares premises with a mortgage brokerage. To control the location risk to client information that this arrangement creates, what does section 2216 of the IDPC Rules require for the physical layout and operation of the shared premises?

options.A: A separate street entrance for each entity's clients
options.B: That all client meetings occur off-site at neutral venues
options.C: That the premises be laid out and operated in a manner that ensures the control and confidentiality of client information and client records, with client records and account process areas effectively controlled and physically secure
options.D: That the mortgage brokerage staff sign CIRO membership applications

questions.rationale: Subsection 2216(6) requires a dealer's shared office premises to be laid out and operated in a manner that ensures the control and confidentiality of client information and client records by ensuring that client records and account process areas are effectively controlled and physically secure. Option A is the closest distractor because separation matters, but the rule requires prominent display of legal names and differentiating signage, not separate entrances. Option B has no basis in the rule, and option D confuses sharing premises with becoming a Dealer Member.
questions.source_quote: "client records and account process areas are effectively controlled and physically secure." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1398

questions.stem: At a shared business location, how must an Investment Dealer keep its electronic client records relative to the co-located financial services entity under subsection 2216(11)?

options.A: On a shared drive with read-only access for the other entity
options.B: With separate passwords or another similar control to ensure the financial services entity has no access to the dealer's electronic client records
options.C: Printed and stored off-site, with electronic copies deleted weekly
options.D: Merged with the other entity's records if both firms have common ownership

questions.rationale: Subsection 2216(11) requires client records to be kept separate from those of the other financial services entity: the entity must not have access to the client's hard copy records, and electronic records must have separate passwords or another similar control to ensure the financial services entity has no access to the dealer's electronic client records. Option A is the closest distractor — even read-only access is access, which the rule forbids. Option C invents a deletion regime that would breach retention requirements, and option D is wrong because the separation duty applies whether or not the entities are related or affiliated.
questions.source_quote: "electronic records must have separate passwords or another similar control to ensure the" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1417

questions.stem: Kelsale Securities scores each location's size risk before setting oversight intensity. Two branches sell identical products, but Branch P has 26 registrants and a layered control function while Branch Q has 4 registrants and informal controls. How should the syllabus 9.2 size-risk considerations shape the comparison?

options.A: Size risk is assessed through risk-weighting and the location's risk assessment and control practices, so Branch P's larger registrant base raises its weighting while its stronger control practices partially offset, and Branch Q's weak practices count against its small size
options.B: Only revenue determines size risk, so the comparison cannot be made
options.C: Branch Q automatically scores higher risk because small branches always rank above large ones
options.D: Size risk ignores controls entirely; the registrant count alone decides the score

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2's business location size risk comprises risk-weighting and risk assessment and control practices, and the number of registrants is a listed sizing consideration — so a proper comparison weights Branch P's scale upward while crediting its control maturity, and penalizes Branch Q's informal practices despite its size. Option D is the closest distractor because registrant count matters, but it wrongly excludes the listed control-practices consideration. Option C inverts the weighting logic into an absolute rule the framework does not state, and option B substitutes a revenue-only test that appears nowhere in the list.
questions.source_quote: "- Risk assessment and control practices" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1665

questions.stem: According to its opening text, syllabus 9.3 requires a Supervisor to apply supervisory requirements to retail distribution risks including which two service categories?

options.A: Proprietary trading and institutional sales
options.B: Carrying-broker services and clearing
options.C: Investment banking and research distribution
options.D: Advisory services and fully discretionary managed products

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.3 directs the Supervisor to apply supervisory requirements to retail distribution risks, including advisory services and fully discretionary managed products, to specific situations relevant to a Supervisor. Option A is the closest distractor because trading supervision exists elsewhere in the rules, but 9.3's stated coverage is retail distribution, not proprietary or institutional activity. Options B and C name back-office and capital-markets functions outside the retail distribution frame.
questions.source_quote: "advisory services and fully discretionary managed products to specific" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1641

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT among the retail distribution risks listed in syllabus 9.3?

options.A: Inadequate control over books and records, with particular attention to poor control of client address changes
options.B: New or complex products that are not understood by retail investors
options.C: Key person reliance
options.D: Improper reassignment of terminated registrants' accounts

questions.rationale: Key person reliance is a real Element 9 concept, but it belongs to the syllabus 9.2 internal risk factors of business locations, not the 9.3 retail distribution risk list. Options A, B and D each quote risks that appear on the 9.3 list, so none can be the exception. The trap is mis-categorization between the two adjacent parameter lists rather than invented text.
questions.source_quote: "New or complex products that are not understood by retail investors" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1655

questions.stem: A retired teacher tells the Supervisor at Bourassa & Finch Securities that her RR forged her initials on a switch form, misrepresented a fund's redemption fee, and ignored her written instruction to stop trading. Applying syllabus 9.3, this pattern is best characterized as which listed retail distribution risk?

options.A: Business location size risk
options.B: Inadequate follow-up procedures on compliance audit findings
options.C: Failure to meet requirements for managing the margin activities of clients
options.D: An Approved Person's failure to meet regulatory, contractual and legal obligations and to apply high levels of integrity

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.3 lists the Approved Person's failure to meet regulatory, contractual and legal obligations and to apply high levels of integrity as a retail distribution risk — forging initials, misrepresenting fees and defying written client instructions is a direct integrity and obligations failure by the registrant. Option B is the closest structural distractor but requires a prior audit finding left untracked, which the facts do not mention. Option C requires margin activity, absent here, and option A imports a 9.2 location-size concept into a 9.3 conduct question.
questions.source_quote: "obligations and to apply high levels of integrity" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1657

questions.stem: An RR at Trelawney Securities returns from a regulatory suspension for discretionary trading without authorization. Under subsection 3927(3) of the IDPC Rules, what must the dealer have in place for this registrant?

options.A: A permanent ban on the RR holding any retail accounts
options.B: Established procedures for the implementation of additional supervisory measures applicable to Approved Persons with a history of regulatory infractions or questionable conduct
options.C: An exemption from daily trade reviews, since the suspension already punished the conduct
options.D: A requirement that the RR work only from head office

questions.rationale: Subsection 3927(3) requires a dealer to establish and follow procedures for the implementation of additional supervisory measures applicable to Approved Persons with a history of regulatory infractions or questionable conduct — heightened, not relaxed, oversight follows a disciplinary history. Option D is the closest distractor because relocation can be one such measure, but the rule mandates procedures for additional measures, not a fixed head-office posting. Option A overstates the rule into a ban it does not impose, and option C inverts it entirely.
