questions.source_quote: "supervisory measures applicable to Approved Persons with a history of regulatory infractions or" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8132

questions.stem: A snowbird client of Silverthorn Securities asks her branch to stop mailing statements while she winters abroad. Under subsection 3219(1) of the IDPC Rules, the dealer's hold-mail procedures for retail clients must, at a minimum, include which provision?

options.A: A requirement that the dealer obtain written authorization from the client to hold mail
options.B: A requirement that the RR personally store the held mail at his residence
options.C: A rule permitting hold-mail orders to run indefinitely if the client is travelling
options.D: A requirement that held mail be destroyed after 90 days

questions.rationale: Subsection 3219(1) sets the minimums for retail hold-mail procedures: a requirement that the dealer obtain written authorization from the client to hold mail, a limit so a hold-mail order remains in force no longer than six months in any 12-month period, and a rule requiring control and regular review of hold-mail accounts by a Supervisor. Option C is the closest distractor because extensions are possible under 3219(2), but only with policies and procedures and Supervisor pre-approval — never indefinitely by default. Option B routes client mail to the very person hold-mail controls guard against, and option D invents a destruction rule that would eliminate records.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member’s hold‐mail account procedures for retail clients must, at a minimum, include" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4879

questions.stem: Without an approved extension, what is the maximum time a retail "hold mail" order may remain in force under clause 3219(1)(ii) of the IDPC Rules?

options.A: No fixed limit, provided the account is dormant
options.B: No longer than 30 days, in any calendar year
options.C: No longer than 12 months, renewable verbally
options.D: No longer than six months, in any 12 month period

questions.rationale: Clause 3219(1)(ii) requires a provision that limits the length of time a hold-mail order may remain in force for no longer than six months, in any 12 month period. Option C is the closest distractor because a longer period is possible — but only under 3219(2), which requires the dealer's policies and procedures to permit it, policies specifically addressing close supervision of such accounts, and pre-approval by the appropriate Supervisor, none of which is verbal renewal. Option B understates the limit and option A removes it entirely, defeating the anti-concealment purpose of the control.
questions.source_quote: "for no longer than six months, in any 12 month period, and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4884

questions.stem: Ullswater Securities plans to begin offering retail clients a newly engineered triple-leveraged volatility note. Under subsection 2246(3) of the IDPC Rules, what must the dealer do before offering it?

options.A: Nothing beyond internal product due diligence, since notification rules cover only name changes
options.B: Notify CIRO in writing and receive written approval from the Corporation before offering retail clients any highly-leveraged securities or derivatives
options.C: Post the product's term sheet on its public website for 30 days
options.D: Obtain a personal indemnity from each selling RR

questions.rationale: Subsection 2246(3) requires a dealer to notify in writing and receive written approval from the Corporation before offering retail clients any highly-leveraged securities or derivatives, or previously approved highly-leveraged products based on a new underlying interest — a regulatory gate that sits ahead of the 9.3 risk of new or complex products that are not understood by retail investors. Option A is the closest distractor because internal due diligence is also required, but for this product class the rule adds prior written CIRO approval. Options C and D invent disclosure and indemnity steps found nowhere in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "(i)      offering retail clients any highly-leveraged securities or derivatives, or" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 1598

questions.stem: Designing a branch review program limited strictly to executed trades, the Supervisor at Jorvik Securities asks what else CIRO's guidance expects reviews to cover. According to the guidance, dealers should also review non-trade issues such as:

options.A: Branch utility costs, parking allocations and equipment leases
options.B: Late payment, margin problems, trade cancellations or suspicious transfers and flows of funds or securities
options.C: Employee vacation schedules and overtime claims
options.D: The marketing budget of each Registered Representative

questions.rationale: The guidance states that dealers should review non-trade issues such as late payment, margin problems, trade cancellations or suspicious transfers and flows of funds or securities — the non-trade signals through which margin-management failures and improper money movement surface at a location. Option D is the closest distractor because marketing does require supervisory review, but that is the separate advertising-review regime under Rule 3600, not the non-trade account-activity items the guidance lists here. Options A and C are administrative matters with no account-supervision content.
questions.source_quote: "Dealers should review non-trade issues such as late payment, margin problems, trade" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 194

questions.stem: Penhale Securities' branch review program samples only customer accounts, never the accounts of its RRs or of the agents operating one of its locations. What does CIRO's account supervision guidance say about this design?

options.A: It is acceptable, because staff accounts are private
options.B: It is acceptable if each RR signs a quarterly self-review attestation
options.C: It is deficient — dealers should include employee and agent account activity in their review
options.D: It is deficient only for branches with more than ten registrants

questions.rationale: The guidance states that dealers should include employee and agent account activity in their review — staff and agent accounts are a recognized channel for improper trading, and section 3945(2) separately requires detection of improper or excessive employee trading. Option B is the closest distractor because attestations exist in compliance programs, but self-attestation by the account holder is not the independent review the guidance expects. Option A asserts a privacy carve-out that does not exist for supervisory review, and option D invents a headcount threshold found nowhere in the guidance.
questions.source_quote: "Dealers should include employee and agent account activity in their review." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 204

questions.stem: Head office at Rosemere Financial is drafting example criteria for its second-tier monthly review of branch retail activity. Which criterion does CIRO's guidance give as one that would satisfy Rule 3900?

options.A: Accounts of clients charged more than $50 in commission during the month
options.B: Accounts holding any exchange-traded fund
options.C: Accounts whose statements were mailed late
options.D: Accounts of clients charged more than $3,000 in commission during the month

questions.rationale: The guidance's example second-tier monthly review criteria include accounts of clients charged more than $3,000 in commission during the month, and accounts of all clients and non-clients charged more than $1,500 in commissions during the month not subject to a first-tier level review, including producing first level Supervisors' client accounts. Option A is the closest distractor because it copies the structure with an implausibly low threshold that appears nowhere in the guidance. Option B targets a product class rather than activity intensity, and option C describes an operations problem, not a stated review-selection criterion.
questions.source_quote: "accounts of clients charged more than $3,000 in commission during the month, and" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 301

questions.scenario: Wystan Securities, an Investment Dealer based in Halifax, is absorbing a five-office retail network in northern Ontario after an acquisition. None of the five offices currently has a resident Supervisor. Chief Compliance Officer Ines Beaudry is redesigning location supervision before the conversion date. Her working paper proposes: (a) a decision matrix for whether each office needs a resident Supervisor; (b) first-tier trade reviews performed centrally at the Halifax head office rather than at each office; (c) a calendar committing first-tier daily reviews to completion one business day after trading and first-tier monthly reviews within 21 days of the review period, absent unusual circumstances; and (d) second-tier daily criteria that include producing branch managers' client accounts and all client accounts not reviewed by a branch manager. One office, in Timmins, has two recently licensed RRs, no experienced staff, a speculative-resource client base, and the nearest Supervisor four hours away.
questions.stem: Applying the guidance's resident-Supervisor factors to the Timmins office described in the scenario, which conclusion is best supported?

options.A: The factors weigh toward stronger on-site supervision: the registrants are inexperienced, the business is speculative, and no Supervisor is available nearby, so remote arrangements alone would need substantial mitigating systems and controls
options.B: No resident Supervisor can be needed because the office has only two registrants
options.C: The four-hour distance is irrelevant since reviews are electronic
options.D: The speculative client base reduces supervision needs because such clients accept risk

questions.rationale: The guidance's factors — number of Registered Individuals, their experience in the location, the business conducted, Supervisor availability in nearby locations, and other systems and controls mitigating remote supervision risk — cut toward closer supervision here: inexperience, speculative business and no nearby Supervisor all aggravate, and only strong mitigating systems could justify a remote model. Option B is the closest distractor because low headcount alone can support remote supervision, but the factors are weighed together, not singly. Option C ignores the listed Supervisor-availability factor, and option D confuses client risk tolerance with the dealer's supervision obligation, which the speculative business heightens rather than reduces.
questions.source_quote: "the number of Registered Individuals in the location," — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 163

questions.scenario: Wystan Securities, an Investment Dealer based in Halifax, is absorbing a five-office retail network in northern Ontario after an acquisition. None of the five offices currently has a resident Supervisor. Chief Compliance Officer Ines Beaudry is redesigning location supervision before the conversion date. Her working paper proposes: (a) a decision matrix for whether each office needs a resident Supervisor; (b) first-tier trade reviews performed centrally at the Halifax head office rather than at each office; (c) a calendar committing first-tier daily reviews to completion one business day after trading and first-tier monthly reviews within 21 days of the review period, absent unusual circumstances; and (d) second-tier daily criteria that include producing branch managers' client accounts and all client accounts not reviewed by a branch manager. One office, in Timmins, has two recently licensed RRs, no experienced staff, a speculative-resource client base, and the nearest Supervisor four hours away.
questions.stem: A director challenges item (b), insisting that first-tier reviews must always be performed by a resident Supervisor at each office. Under CIRO's account supervision guidance, is the director correct?

options.A: Yes — first-tier review may never leave the business location
options.B: Yes — central review is permitted only for institutional accounts
options.C: No — although resident Supervisors will normally conduct the first-tier review at their business location, dealers can conduct these reviews in a central location such as head office or a regional office, subject to the guidance's resource and procedure conditions
options.D: No — because first-tier reviews are entirely optional for acquired offices in their first year

questions.rationale: The guidance states that resident Supervisors will normally conduct the first-tier review at their respective business location, but dealers can conduct these reviews in a central location (head office or regional office) provided there are resources available to conduct the review at that location and the dealer has adequate procedures for dealing with any issues identified. Option A converts the word normally into a prohibition the guidance does not impose. Option B invents an institutional-only carve-out, and option D invents a first-year holiday from supervision that exists nowhere — acquired locations are supervised from day one.
questions.source_quote: "Resident Supervisors will normally conduct the ﬁrst-tier review at their respective business" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 228

questions.scenario: Wystan Securities, an Investment Dealer based in Halifax, is absorbing a five-office retail network in northern Ontario after an acquisition. None of the five offices currently has a resident Supervisor. Chief Compliance Officer Ines Beaudry is redesigning location supervision before the conversion date. Her working paper proposes: (a) a decision matrix for whether each office needs a resident Supervisor; (b) first-tier trade reviews performed centrally at the Halifax head office rather than at each office; (c) a calendar committing first-tier daily reviews to completion one business day after trading and first-tier monthly reviews within 21 days of the review period, absent unusual circumstances; and (d) second-tier daily criteria that include producing branch managers' client accounts and all client accounts not reviewed by a branch manager. One office, in Timmins, has two recently licensed RRs, no experienced staff, a speculative-resource client base, and the nearest Supervisor four hours away.
questions.stem: Beaudry includes "producing branch managers' client accounts" among the second-tier daily criteria in item (d). Why does this criterion align with CIRO's guidance?

options.A: Producing branch managers are barred from holding client accounts, so the criterion is a detection rule for that prohibition
options.B: Second-tier daily reviews exist only to recalculate commissions, and managers earn the most
options.C: Branch managers' trades settle on a different cycle requiring separate review
options.D: The guidance lists producing branch managers' client accounts among example second-tier daily review criteria that would satisfy Rule 3900, ensuring activity a first-tier reviewer cannot independently review is still examined

questions.rationale: The guidance's example criteria for second-tier daily reviews that would satisfy Rule 3900 include producing branch managers' client accounts, alongside items such as non-client trading, trade cancellations and outstanding margin calls — a producing manager's own client activity escapes meaningful first-tier review at their location, so the second tier picks it up. Option A is the closest distractor because it senses the conflict, but no rule bars producing managers from having clients; the control is independent review, not prohibition. Option B trivializes second-tier review into commission accounting, and option C invents a settlement-cycle difference that does not exist.
questions.source_quote: "producing branch managers’ client accounts," — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 283

questions.scenario: Quarrystone Capital, an Investment Dealer with 14 business locations, holds its annual location risk-rating meeting. The committee reviews four files. File 1: the Cranbrook branch is the only location authorized to distribute a thinly traded private-placement royalty product, and it also runs options, new-issue and fee-based lines whose combined exposures have never been assessed together. File 2: the Rimouski branch lost its operations manager, leaving branch Supervisor Jules Pelletier as the only person able to run trade corrections, banking reconciliation and supervision; the branch also operates through agents who own their own premises. File 3: the Brampton branch recorded a spike in complaint volume and two internal disciplinary actions after a billboard campaign aimed at new immigrants promised "guaranteed family wealth." File 4: the Estevan branch's client roster includes several accounts flagged for rapid in-and-out third-party wire activity inconsistent with stated occupations.
questions.stem: File 1 raises which two syllabus 9.2 business activity risk considerations for the Cranbrook branch?

options.A: Key person reliance, and any internal disciplinary action
options.B: Number of complaints, and community-specific marketing strategy
options.C: Exposure inherent to providing a particular product or service at a business location, and the location's lines of business and the aggregate risk present in its overall portfolio
options.D: Risk-weighting, and client base risk

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2's business activity risk list includes the exposure inherent to providing a particular product or service at a business location — the branch-exclusive thinly traded royalty product — and the business location's lines of business and the aggregate risk present in its overall portfolio, which the never-assessed combination of options, new-issue and fee-based lines engages. Option D is the closest distractor because a riskier branch will ultimately attract a higher risk-weighting, but risk-weighting is a size-risk consideration and client base risk requires client misuse, which File 1 does not describe. Option A names an internal and a reputational factor, and option B names two reputational factors, none describing product or line-of-business exposure.
questions.source_quote: "in its overall portfolio" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1634

questions.scenario: Quarrystone Capital, an Investment Dealer with 14 business locations, holds its annual location risk-rating meeting. The committee reviews four files. File 1: the Cranbrook branch is the only location authorized to distribute a thinly traded private-placement royalty product, and it also runs options, new-issue and fee-based lines whose combined exposures have never been assessed together. File 2: the Rimouski branch lost its operations manager, leaving branch Supervisor Jules Pelletier as the only person able to run trade corrections, banking reconciliation and supervision; the branch also operates through agents who own their own premises. File 3: the Brampton branch recorded a spike in complaint volume and two internal disciplinary actions after a billboard campaign aimed at new immigrants promised "guaranteed family wealth." File 4: the Estevan branch's client roster includes several accounts flagged for rapid in-and-out third-party wire activity inconsistent with stated occupations.
questions.stem: File 2 concentrates which category of syllabus 9.2 risk factors at the Rimouski branch?

options.A: Internal risk factors — both key person reliance on Pelletier and the principal-agent structure of the location
options.B: Reputational risk — outside activities and complaints
options.C: Business activity risk — trading errors and product exposure
options.D: Business location size risk — risk-weighting alone

questions.rationale: Syllabus 9.2's internal risk factors are adequacy of resources, key person reliance and principal-agent; File 2 stacks two of them — total dependence on Pelletier for corrections, reconciliation and supervision, plus an agent-owned-premises structure — and the lost operations manager also strains adequacy of resources. Option C is the closest distractor because trade corrections sound like trading errors, but File 2 describes who can process corrections, not an error count, and no product exposure is mentioned. Option B requires conduct signals such as complaints or outside activities, which File 2 lacks, and option D reduces the analysis to a sizing input the facts do not raise.
questions.source_quote: "• Internal risk factors, including:" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1621

questions.scenario: Quarrystone Capital, an Investment Dealer with 14 business locations, holds its annual location risk-rating meeting. The committee reviews four files. File 1: the Cranbrook branch is the only location authorized to distribute a thinly traded private-placement royalty product, and it also runs options, new-issue and fee-based lines whose combined exposures have never been assessed together. File 2: the Rimouski branch lost its operations manager, leaving branch Supervisor Jules Pelletier as the only person able to run trade corrections, banking reconciliation and supervision; the branch also operates through agents who own their own premises. File 3: the Brampton branch recorded a spike in complaint volume and two internal disciplinary actions after a billboard campaign aimed at new immigrants promised "guaranteed family wealth." File 4: the Estevan branch's client roster includes several accounts flagged for rapid in-and-out third-party wire activity inconsistent with stated occupations.
questions.stem: File 3 combines which three syllabus 9.2 reputational risk indicators at the Brampton branch?

options.A: Adequacy of resources, principal-agent, and risk-weighting
options.B: Number of complaints, any internal disciplinary action, and community-specific marketing strategy and sales practices
options.C: Number of trading errors, lines of business, and number of registrants
options.D: Client base risk, key person reliance, and risk assessment practices

questions.rationale: All three File 3 facts are listed reputational risk indicators in syllabus 9.2: the complaint spike is number of complaints, the two sanctions are any internal disciplinary action, and the billboard campaign targeting a specific community with a guarantee-style promise is community-specific marketing strategy and sales practices. Option C is the closest categorical trap because it lists three real 9.2 items, but they are business activity and sizing measures absent from File 3. Option A mixes internal and size factors, and option D mixes client base, internal and size concepts — none of which the File 3 facts describe.
questions.source_quote: "- Number of complaints" — syllabus-raw.txt ~line 1629

questions.scenario: Quarrystone Capital, an Investment Dealer with 14 business locations, holds its annual location risk-rating meeting. The committee reviews four files. File 1: the Cranbrook branch is the only location authorized to distribute a thinly traded private-placement royalty product, and it also runs options, new-issue and fee-based lines whose combined exposures have never been assessed together. File 2: the Rimouski branch lost its operations manager, leaving branch Supervisor Jules Pelletier as the only person able to run trade corrections, banking reconciliation and supervision; the branch also operates through agents who own their own premises. File 3: the Brampton branch recorded a spike in complaint volume and two internal disciplinary actions after a billboard campaign aimed at new immigrants promised "guaranteed family wealth." File 4: the Estevan branch's client roster includes several accounts flagged for rapid in-and-out third-party wire activity inconsistent with stated occupations.
questions.stem: For File 4, which syllabus 9.2 risk has the committee identified at the Estevan branch, and which IDPC control framework must the dealer's policies and procedures bring to bear?

options.A: Reputational risk, addressed through advertising pre-approval under Rule 3600
options.B: Business location size risk, addressed by adding registrants
options.C: Internal risk, addressed by converting agents to employees
options.D: Client base risk — clients may be using the location for inappropriate actions — addressed through the 3926(2) obligations to identify high-risk clients, identify clients presenting a high risk of improper securities-market activities, and comply with anti-money laundering and terrorist financing requirements

questions.rationale: Rapid in-and-out third-party wires inconsistent with stated occupations is the syllabus 9.2 client base risk — the business location assumes the risk that clients may use it for inappropriate actions — and subsection 3926(2) requires policies and procedures addressing the dealer's obligations to identify clients that present a high risk to it, identify clients presenting a high risk of conducting improper activities in the securities markets, and comply with all anti-money laundering and terrorist financing requirements under applicable laws. Option A is the closest wrong frame because the branch's reputation will suffer if misuse surfaces, but the operative risk and control set are client-conduct and AML, not advertising. Option B treats an AML problem as a headcount problem, and option C addresses a structure File 4 never mentions.
questions.source_quote: "comply with all anti-money laundering and terrorist financing requirements under" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8102

questions.stem: A managed account client at Verecrest Partners updates his investment objectives. His managed account application was approved 10 months ago. Under subsection 3215(4), the firm may record the change on a copy of his current managed account application provided which condition is met?

options.A: The client signs a fresh managed account agreement before the change is recorded
options.B: The Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager and their Supervisor initial any changes, since the application was approved within the past 12 months
options.C: The application was approved within the past 36 months and the designated Supervisor re-approves the account in writing
options.D: The change is reported to the Corporation within one business day

questions.rationale: Subsection 3215(4) provides that if the client's managed account or discretionary account application was approved within the past 12 months, the Dealer Member may use a copy of the current application to record changes, but must have the Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager and their Supervisor initial any changes. Option C, the closest distractor, imports the 36-month window from subsection 3215(3), which applies to ordinary account applications, not managed or discretionary applications. Options A and D invent requirements found nowhere in section 3215.
questions.source_quote: "If the client's managed account or discretionary account application was approved within the past 12 months, the Dealer Member may use a copy of a client's current managed account or discretionary account application to record any changes" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4677

questions.stem: Beyond having policies for receiving documents within a reasonable time, what specific system does clause 3213(2)(ii) require a Dealer Member such as Strathmere Investments to operate for accounts opened with outstanding paperwork?

options.A: An automated block on all trading until every document is on file
options.B: A system for recording pending account documentation and following up where it is not received within a reasonable time
options.C: A quarterly report to the Corporation listing every account with missing documents
options.D: A requirement that clients deposit a minimum balance before documents become due

questions.rationale: Clause 3213(2)(ii) requires a Dealer Member to have a system for recording pending account documentation and following up where it is not received within a reasonable time. Option A is the closest distractor because the rules do impose account restrictions in one circumstance, but only under subsection 3214(5) — after a designated Supervisor declines to approve the account following the initial trade; missing documents alone do not trigger an automatic trading block under section 3213. Options C and D describe obligations that do not exist in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "have a system for recording pending account documentation and following up where it is not received within a reasonable time" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4629

questions.stem: When Brockfield Securities must determine whether an institutional client is capable of independently evaluating investment risk and is exercising independent judgment, which of the following is among the relevant considerations listed in subsection 3403(2)?

options.A: The presence or absence of a pattern of acceptance of the Dealer Member's recommendations
options.B: Whether the client maintains total securities under administration exceeding $25 million
options.C: Whether the client has held an account with the Dealer Member for at least five years
options.D: The number of complaints the client has previously filed with the Corporation

questions.rationale: Subsection 3403(2) lists relevant considerations for the sophistication determination, including the presence or absence of a pattern of acceptance of the Dealer Member's recommendations. Option B is the closest distractor because a dollar threshold ($10 million, not $25 million) appears in the Rule 1201 definition of institutional client — but that threshold governs classification, not the separate 3403(2) capability assessment. Options C and D appear nowhere among the listed considerations.
questions.source_quote: "the presence or absence of a pattern of acceptance of the Dealer Member's recommendations" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5607

questions.stem: Montrave Securities maintains a long-standing account for an institutional client. Independent of any particular order or recommendation, what account-level determination does subsection 3403(4) require Montrave to make, on a reasonable basis and putting the institutional client's interest first?

options.A: That the client remains a permitted client under National Instrument 31-103
options.B: That the client's annual trading volume justifies the account's fee structure
options.C: That it is suitable for the institutional client to continue having an account with the Dealer Member, and that the scope of products, services and account relationships the client has access to within the account are suitable for the client
options.D: That the client has executed a new letter of undertaking each calendar year

questions.rationale: Subsection 3403(4) requires the Dealer Member to determine, on a reasonable basis and putting the institutional client's interest first, that (i) it is suitable for the institutional client to continue having an account with the Dealer Member, and (ii) the scope of products, services and account relationships the client has access to within the account are suitable for the institutional client. Option A is the closest distractor because permitted-client status matters under clause 3404(3)(ii) for the written waiver exemption — but that is a different mechanism, not the 3403(4) determination. Options B and D invent volume and annual-renewal tests found nowhere in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "it is suitable for the institutional client to continue having an account with the Dealer Member" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5638

questions.stem: Esterbrook Securities is opening an account that will be held by a bank. With respect to the account appropriateness determination, what does subsection 3211(3) provide?

options.A: Subsection 3211(1) does not apply, because the account is held by a bank
options.B: Only clause 3211(1)(ii) is waived; the dealer must still determine that opening the account is appropriate
options.C: The full appropriateness determination applies, but the bank may complete it on the dealer's behalf
options.D: The determination applies unless the bank waives it in writing

questions.rationale: Subsection 3211(3) provides that subsection 3211(1) — the entire account appropriateness determination — does not apply in respect to an account held by a Dealer Member, regulated entity, exempt market dealer, portfolio manager, bank, trust company or insurance company. Option B is the closest distractor because subsection 3211(2) waives only clause 3211(1)(ii), but that narrower relief applies to order execution only and direct electronic access accounts, not to accounts held by banks. Options C and D invent delegation and waiver mechanisms the rule does not contain.
questions.source_quote: "an account held by a Dealer Member, regulated entity, exempt market dealer, portfolio manager, bank, trust company or insurance company" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4589

questions.stem: Clause 3211(1)(i) requires a Dealer Member, before opening an account for a person, to determine what about the act of opening the account itself?

options.A: That the person can afford the account's minimum fees
options.B: That the person has at least one year of investment experience
options.C: That this action is appropriate for the person
options.D: That the person has been referred by an existing client

questions.rationale: Clause 3211(1)(i) requires the Dealer Member to determine that 'this action' — opening the account — 'is appropriate for the person'. This is distinct from clause 3211(1)(ii), which addresses the scope of products, services and account relationships accessible within the account. Option A is the closest distractor because fee impacts feature in relationship disclosure under section 3216, but affordability of fees is not the 3211(1)(i) test. Options B and D state qualification criteria that appear nowhere in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "(i) this action is appropriate for the person, and (ii) the scope of products, services and account relationships which the person would have access to within the account are appropriate for the person." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4577

questions.stem: Under the section 3210 definition, the term "client account records" is broadly defined to capture which category of materials, beyond the four specifically listed items?

options.A: Any information, disclosure statement or agreement the Dealer Member is required to provide to or obtain from the client in accordance with Corporation requirements or applicable laws
options.B: Only documents the client has physically signed in ink
options.C: All internal compliance memoranda discussing the client
options.D: Marketing materials sent to the client during the previous year

questions.rationale: The section 3210 definition opens with a broad chapeau: client account records means any information, disclosure statement or agreement the Dealer Member is required to provide to or obtain from the client in accordance with Corporation requirements or applicable laws, 'including, but not limited to' the four listed items. Option B is the closest distractor because signed agreements are within scope, but the definition is not limited to signed documents — it includes information and disclosure statements. Options C and D describe internal and promotional materials, which are not items required to be provided to or obtained from the client.
questions.source_quote: "Any information, disclosure statement or agreement the Dealer Member is required to provide to or obtain from the client in accordance with Corporation requirements or applicable laws" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4560

questions.stem: Delphine Okonjo, an accountant, holds trading authorizations over the accounts of eleven different clients at Birkdale Securities. Beyond keeping each signed authorization, what does subsection 3220(4) require of Birkdale's records concerning persons like Delphine?

options.A: An annual report to each affected client listing every other client Delphine acts for
options.B: A requirement that no person hold authorizations over more than ten client accounts
options.C: A record of persons with trading authorization sufficient to allow the firm to identify any persons with trading authorization for multiple clients or client accounts
options.D: A certified copy of Delphine's professional licence on each account file

questions.rationale: Subsection 3220(4) requires the Dealer Member to maintain a record of persons with trading authorization over one or more client accounts and to ensure the record is sufficient to allow the Dealer Member to identify any persons with trading authorization for multiple clients or client accounts. Option B is the closest distractor because it implies a numerical control, but the rule imposes a record-keeping and identification requirement, not a cap on the number of authorizations. Options A and D describe disclosure and documentation duties found nowhere in section 3220.
questions.source_quote: "must ensure that such record is sufficient to allow the Dealer Member to identify any persons with trading authorization for multiple clients or client accounts" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4913

questions.stem: Gravenhurst Wealth drafts its retail account-opening package and labels the disclosure document "Your Account Guide". Under clause 3216(5)(i), what is wrong with this approach?

options.A: Nothing — the title of the document is not prescribed
options.B: The relationship disclosure information must be entitled "Relationship Disclosure"
options.C: The document must be entitled "Client Relationship Model"
options.D: Disclosure documents may only use titles pre-approved by the Corporation each year

questions.rationale: Clause 3216(5)(i) requires that the relationship disclosure information must be entitled 'Relationship Disclosure'. Option A is the closest distractor because subsection 3216(4) states the FORMAT of the information is not prescribed — but the title is expressly prescribed by clause 3216(5)(i), so a different title does not comply. Option C invents a title, and option D describes an annual Corporation pre-approval process that does not exist; approval of the document is an internal obligation under subsection 3216(6).
questions.source_quote: "The relationship disclosure information must be entitled "Relationship Disclosure"." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4727

questions.stem: A retail client of Hollybrook Securities holds an advisory account, a margin account and a registered savings account at the firm. May Hollybrook give her a single combined relationship disclosure document covering all three accounts?

options.A: No — a separate relationship disclosure document is mandatory for each account
options.B: Yes, automatically, because all the accounts belong to the same client
options.C: Only if the client signs a waiver releasing the firm from per-account disclosure
options.D: Yes, as long as the Dealer Member determines that the combined disclosure is appropriate for the client in light of the relevant circumstances, including the nature of the various accounts

questions.rationale: Clause 3216(3)(iii) permits combined relationship disclosure information where a client has more than one account, as long as the Dealer Member determines that the combined disclosure is appropriate for the client in light of the relevant circumstances, including the nature of the various accounts. Option B is the closest distractor because combination is indeed allowed, but it is conditional on the appropriateness determination, not automatic. Option A wrongly mandates separate documents, and option C invents a waiver requirement.
questions.source_quote: "Where a client has more than one account, combined relationship disclosure information may be provided to the client as long as the Dealer Member determines that the combined disclosure is appropriate for the client" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4711

questions.stem: Kelmscott Investments distributes only in-house mutual funds and notes to its retail clients. Which relationship disclosure content item in subsection 3216(5) speaks most directly to this business model?

options.A: A statement of when trade confirmations and account statements will be sent
options.B: A general explanation of how investment performance benchmarks might be used
options.C: A description of the circumstances for disclosing information to a trusted contact person
options.D: A general description of any limits on products and services, including whether the firm will primarily or exclusively provide proprietary products to the client

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 3216(5)(ii)(b) requires a general description of any limits on the products and services the Dealer Member will offer, including whether the firm will primarily or exclusively provide proprietary products to the client. A proprietary-only shelf is precisely this disclosure. Option B is the closest distractor because benchmark disclosure under sub-clause 3216(5)(ii)(m) is also required content, but it does not address product-shelf limits. Options A and C are likewise required content items but are unrelated to a proprietary product model.
questions.source_quote: "whether the firm will primarily or exclusively provide proprietary products to the client" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4739

questions.stem: Alongside describing its complaint handling procedures in the relationship disclosure, what related statement does sub-clause 3216(5)(ii)(l) require Norwell Securities to include for retail clients?

options.A: A statement that the client will be provided with a copy of a Corporation approved complaint handling process brochure at time of account opening
options.B: A statement that all complaints must be submitted within 90 days of the disputed trade
options.C: A statement that complaints may only be made through the Ombudsman for Banking Services and Investments
options.D: A statement that the firm guarantees a written response to any complaint within 24 hours

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 3216(5)(ii)(l) requires a description of the Dealer Member's complaint handling procedures and a statement that the client will be provided with a copy of a Corporation approved complaint handling process brochure at time of account opening. Option C is the closest distractor because external recourse bodies exist, but the rule does not restrict complaints to any single channel and the required statement concerns the brochure, not OBSI exclusivity. Options B and D invent deadlines and service guarantees not found in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "a statement that the client will be provided with a copy of a Corporation approved complaint handling process brochure at time of account opening" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4818

questions.stem: The prescribed leverage risk disclosure statement in subsection 3217(3) opens by comparing borrowing to another way of investing. Which comparison does the prescribed wording make?

options.A: Using borrowed money involves greater risk than investing through a managed account
options.B: Using borrowed money involves greater risk than trading on margin
options.C: Using borrowed money to finance the purchase of securities involves greater risk than using cash resources only
options.D: Using borrowed money involves greater risk than purchasing guaranteed investment certificates

questions.rationale: Subsection 3217(3) prescribes wording substantially as follows: 'Using borrowed money to finance the purchase of securities involves greater risk than using cash resources only.' The comparison is between borrowed money and cash resources, not between account types or product categories. Option B is the closest distractor because margin is itself a form of borrowing, but the prescribed statement contrasts leverage with cash, not one form of borrowing with another. Options A and D substitute comparisons that do not appear in the prescribed text.
questions.source_quote: "Using borrowed money to finance the purchase of securities involves greater risk than using cash resources only." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4858

questions.stem: A retail margin client of Ivernes Brokers argues that because his margin account agreement gives the firm broad rights over his account, the firm itself is free to execute his trades however it wishes. Which clause of subsection 3247(2) contradicts him?

options.A: The clause obliging the client to pay interest on debit balances
options.B: The clause describing the dealer's right to sell assets to cover short sales
options.C: The clause describing the dealer's right to use assets as collateral for the client's debt
options.D: The clause setting out the Dealer Member's obligation to carry out all transactions in accordance with Corporation requirements and, where applicable, the requirements of the marketplace on which the transaction has been executed

questions.rationale: Clause 3247(2)(ix) requires the margin account agreement to contain the Dealer Member's obligation to carry out all transactions in accordance with Corporation requirements and, where applicable, the requirements of the marketplace on which the transaction has been executed — the dealer's rights do not free it from regulatory execution standards. Option B is the closest distractor because it is also mandatory agreement content under clause 3247(2)(v), but it is a dealer RIGHT and does not answer the client's claim about how transactions must be carried out. Options A and C are likewise required content but concern client obligations and collateral rights, not execution standards.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member's obligation to carry out all transactions in accordance with Corporation requirements and, where applicable, the requirements of the marketplace on which the transaction has been executed" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5152

questions.stem: Juneau Securities holds securities in a client's margin account against her outstanding debit. Which clause of the margin account agreement, required by subsection 3247(2), covers the firm's ability to do this?

options.A: The clause describing the extent of the Dealer Member's right to use assets in the client's account and to hold them as collateral for the client's debt
options.B: The clause describing the client's obligation to maintain adequate margin
options.C: The clause describing the dealer's right to exercise discretion in accepting orders
options.D: The clause describing the time periods during which the dealer accepts orders for execution

questions.rationale: Clause 3247(2)(viii) requires the margin account agreement to describe the extent of the Dealer Member's right to use assets in the client's account and to hold them as collateral for the client's debt — exactly the practice described. Option B is the closest distractor because the client's margin-maintenance obligation under clause 3247(2)(i) is connected to the same debit, but it is the client's obligation, not the dealer's collateral right. Options C and D are content items of the derivatives trading agreement under section 3252, not the margin account agreement.
questions.source_quote: "the extent of the Dealer Member's right to use assets in the client's account and to hold them as collateral for the client's debt" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5144

questions.stem: Which of the following items in a margin account agreement under subsection 3247(2) is a CLIENT obligation rather than a Dealer Member right or obligation?

options.A: Raising money on and pledging assets held in the account
options.B: Paying interest on debit balances in the account
options.C: Selling assets in the account and making purchases to cover short sales
options.D: Carrying out all transactions in accordance with Corporation requirements

questions.rationale: Clause 3247(2)(ii) requires the agreement to describe the client's obligation to pay interest on debit balances in their account — a client obligation. Option D is the closest distractor because it is an obligation, but under clause 3247(2)(ix) it is the Dealer Member's obligation, not the client's. Options A and C are Dealer Member rights under clauses 3247(2)(iii) and 3247(2)(v) respectively.
questions.source_quote: "the client's obligation to pay interest on debit balances in their account" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5132

questions.stem: Ostwell Derivatives mistakenly books a client's futures order to the wrong account and must unwind the position. Which Dealer Member obligation that clause 3252(1)(v) requires in the derivatives trading agreement is engaged by events of this kind?

options.A: The obligation to address situations when errors and omissions occur
options.B: The obligation to obtain client consent before taking the other side of a transaction
options.C: The obligation to provide regulators with position limit information
options.D: The obligation to distribute assignment notices by a stated method

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 3252(1)(v)(c) requires the derivatives trading agreement to include the Dealer Member's obligation to address situations when errors and omissions occur, which directly covers a misbooked order. Option B is the closest distractor because it is also a 3252(1)(v) obligation, but consent to take the other side under sub-clause (v)(b) concerns principal trading against the client, not operational mistakes. Option C is sub-clause (v)(a) and concerns regulatory reporting; option D concerns options assignment under clause 3252(1)(xi)(b).
questions.source_quote: "address situations when errors and omissions occur" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5213

questions.stem: Sigrid Bessette's derivatives account at Zellner Capital carries open futures positions and a debit. The firm holds the securities in her account as security for both. Which extent-of-right item under clause 3252(1)(iii) must her derivatives trading agreement describe to support this?

options.A: The right to exercise discretion in accepting orders
options.B: The right to apply cash-only terms before expiry
options.C: The right to set maximum limits on short positions
options.D: The right to use client account assets as collateral for the clients' debit and position obligations

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 3252(1)(iii)(b) requires the agreement to set out the extent of the Dealer Member's right to use client account assets as collateral for the clients' debit and position obligations — covering both the debit and the open positions described. Option A is the closest distractor in form, but exercising discretion in accepting orders under sub-clause 3252(1)(ii)(a) concerns order handling, not collateral. Options B and C are option-specific disclosures under clause 3252(1)(xi)(c), not collateral rights.
questions.source_quote: "use client account assets as collateral for the clients' debit and position obligations" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5202

questions.stem: Clauses 3252(1)(viii)(c) and (d) require a derivatives trading agreement to include two further client payment obligations. How does the rule qualify each of these obligations?

options.A: Both are unconditional: every client must pay commission and interest in all cases
options.B: Commission is payable only on losing trades; interest only on credit balances
options.C: Both apply only to institutional clients
options.D: Each is qualified by "if any": to pay commission or other compensation, if any, and to pay interest, if any, on account debit balances

questions.rationale: Sub-clauses 3252(1)(viii)(c) and (d) require the agreement to set out the client's obligation to 'pay commission or other compensation, if any' and to 'pay interest, if any, on account debit balances' — both expressly qualified by 'if any', reflecting that fee structures vary. Option A is the closest distractor because the obligations are mandatory agreement content, but the underlying payments are conditional, not universal. Option B misstates both triggers, and option C invents an institutional limitation.
questions.source_quote: "(c) pay commission or other compensation, if any, (d) pay interest, if any, on account debit balances" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5227

questions.stem: A client of Fontayne Securities who has written covered calls asks how she will learn if her short options are assigned. For options and similar derivatives, what does clause 3252(1)(xi)(b) require the derivatives trading agreement to state?

options.A: That assignment can never occur before expiry
options.B: That assignment notices are distributed solely by the clearing house directly to clients
options.C: The method the Dealer Member will use to distribute assignment notices
options.D: That the client must check the exchange website daily for assignments

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 3252(1)(xi)(b) requires the derivatives trading agreement to include the method the Dealer Member will use to distribute assignment notices. Option B is the closest distractor because assignment originates at the clearing level, but the disclosure obligation concerns the Dealer Member's own distribution method to its clients. Option A is factually wrong for American-style options and is not a prescribed disclosure; option D shifts the burden to the client in a way the rule does not.
questions.source_quote: "the method the Dealer Member will use to distribute assignment notices" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5242

questions.stem: For options and similar derivatives, clause 3252(1)(xi)(c) requires three disclosures in the derivatives trading agreement. In addition to the disclosure that the dealer may apply cash-only terms during the last 10 days before expiry, which pair completes the list?

options.A: That the Dealer Member may set maximum limits on short positions, and that the Corporation may impose other rules affecting existing or subsequent transactions
options.B: That the dealer will guarantee execution prices, and that exercise is automatic
options.C: That the client may exceed position limits with consent, and that margin is waived near expiry
options.D: That short positions are prohibited for retail clients, and that the Corporation pre-approves each trade

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 3252(1)(xi)(c) requires disclosures indicating that (I) the Dealer Member may set maximum limits on short positions, (II) the Dealer Member may apply cash-only terms during the last 10 days before expiry, and (III) the Corporation may impose other rules affecting existing or subsequent transactions. Option A supplies disclosures (I) and (III). Option D is the closest distractor because it echoes short positions and Corporation involvement, but short positions are limited, not prohibited, and the Corporation imposes rules rather than pre-approving each trade. Options B and C invert the rule's substance.
questions.source_quote: "(I) the Dealer Member may set maximum limits on short positions, (II) the Dealer Member may apply cash-only terms during the last 10 days before expiry" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5244

questions.stem: Blackwood Institutional Markets accepts a letter of undertaking from a pension plan whose derivatives account is charged interest on a debit balance. The plan's master custody agreement already sets out, with the plan's acknowledgement, the conditions for transfers between its accounts. Under clause 3253(2)(ii), must the letter of undertaking repeat those transfer conditions?

options.A: Yes — the letter must always state the transfer conditions, with no exceptions
options.B: No — the conditions need not appear in the letter because they are acknowledged by the client in another document
options.C: Yes — but only if the debit balance exceeds the firm's house margin requirement
options.D: No — transfer conditions are never required content for a letter of undertaking

questions.rationale: Clause 3253(2)(ii) requires the letter of undertaking to state, where the client has an account charged interest on a debit balance, the conditions under which transfers of the client's funds, securities or other property may be made between accounts, 'unless these conditions are acknowledged by the client in another document.' The custody agreement acknowledgement satisfies the exception. Option A is the closest distractor because it states the default duty but ignores the express exception. Option D ignores the conditional requirement entirely, and option C invents a margin-based trigger.
questions.source_quote: "unless these conditions are acknowledged by the client in another document" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5282

questions.stem: Coverack Asset Management wants to begin accepting managed accounts. Under clause 3277(1)(i), what is the first structural requirement concerning supervisory personnel?

options.A: The Dealer Member must designate a Supervisor to be responsible for managed accounts
options.B: The Chief Financial Officer must be appointed to oversee all managed accounts
options.C: Each Portfolio Manager must supervise their own managed accounts
options.D: The Corporation must appoint a Supervisor for the firm's managed account program

questions.rationale: Clause 3277(1)(i) requires that, to accept managed accounts, the Dealer Member must designate a Supervisor to be responsible for managed accounts. Option C is the closest distractor because Portfolio Managers make the investment decisions, but self-supervision is not what the rule prescribes — a designated Supervisor must be responsible for the accounts. Option B names the wrong officer, and option D wrongly assigns the designation to the Corporation rather than the Dealer Member.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must designate a Supervisor to be responsible for managed accounts" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5406

questions.stem: A new compliance officer at Drummore Private Wealth proposes relying on the firm's general retail supervision manual to govern its managed account program. Why does clause 3277(1)(ii) make this insufficient?

options.A: Managed accounts may only be supervised under policies written by the Corporation
options.B: Managed account supervision requires no written policies, only Supervisor judgment
options.C: General policies suffice if the firm has fewer than 100 managed accounts
options.D: The Dealer Member's policies and procedures must specifically address the supervision and operation of managed accounts in accordance with Corporation requirements

questions.rationale: Clause 3277(1)(ii) requires that the Dealer Member's policies and procedures must specifically address the supervision and operation of managed accounts in accordance with Corporation requirements — a general retail manual that does not specifically address managed accounts falls short. Option C is the closest distractor because it suggests a proportionality threshold, but the rule contains no account-count exemption. Option A misattributes authorship of the policies to the Corporation, and option B contradicts the express requirement for policies and procedures.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member's policies and procedures must specifically address the supervision and operation of managed accounts in accordance with Corporation requirements" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5407

questions.stem: While reviewing a draft managed account agreement at Orpington Counsel, a Supervisor notices it is silent on how the relationship may end. Which clause of subsection 3278(1) does the draft fail to satisfy?

options.A: The requirement to describe investment restrictions imposed by the client
options.B: The requirement to describe or refer to the client's risk profile
options.C: The requirement to set out the terms of termination in accordance with subsection 3278(2)
options.D: The requirement to state a maximum agreement term of 12 months

questions.rationale: Clause 3278(1)(iii) requires the managed account agreement to set out the terms of termination in accordance with subsection 3278(2), under which termination is by written notice — by the client effective on receipt, or by the dealer effective not less than 30 days from delivery. Option D is the closest distractor because a 12-month, non-renewable maximum term exists, but under clauses 3274(1)(iii) and (iv) it applies to DISCRETIONARY account agreements, not managed account agreements. Options A and B are other required contents under clauses 3278(1)(ii) and (i), but the identified gap concerns termination.
questions.source_quote: "set out the terms of termination in accordance with subsection 3278(2)" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5426

questions.stem: A managed account client of Larrimore Counsel telephones her Portfolio Manager and states she is terminating her managed account agreement effective immediately; she sends nothing in writing. Under subsection 3278(2), what is the status of the agreement?

options.A: Terminated immediately, because a client may terminate by any means at any time
options.B: Still in force, because the managed account agreement may only be terminated by written notice
options.C: Terminated, but only effective 30 days after the telephone call
options.D: Suspended pending a hearing before the firm's managed account committee

questions.rationale: Subsection 3278(2) provides that the managed account agreement may only be terminated by written notice — by the client, effective on receipt by the Dealer Member, or by the Dealer Member, effective not less than 30 days from delivery. An oral instruction is not written notice, so the agreement remains in force until written notice is received. Option C is the closest distractor because it borrows the 30-day period, but that period applies to termination by the DEALER, and in any case no written notice exists here. Option A ignores the written-notice exclusivity, and option D invents a committee process that subsection 3278(2) does not contain.
questions.source_quote: "The managed account agreement may only be terminated by written notice" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5428

questions.stem: Ridgevale Securities hires a foreign sub-advisor under a written sub-advisor agreement to manage certain client mandates. The sub-advisor's home regime has weak conflict-of-interest legislation. Under subsection 3279(2), what must be true before the sub-advisor may act?

options.A: The sub-advisor must relocate its principal place of business to Canada
options.B: The sub-advisor must have entered into an agreement with the Dealer Member that it will comply with section 3280, since its home regime's conflict provisions are not at least equivalent
options.C: Nothing further — sub-advisors are exempt from managed-account conflict rules
options.D: The Corporation must individually license the sub-advisor

questions.rationale: Clause 3279(2)(ii) requires the sub-advisor to be subject to legislation or regulations containing conflict of interest provisions at least equivalent to those set out in section 3280, OR to have entered into an agreement with the Dealer Member that it will comply with section 3280. Where the home regime is not equivalent, the contractual route is the remaining path. Option C is the closest distractor because the Rule 3270 definition of responsible person excludes a sub-adviser under section 3279 — but section 3279 itself then imposes the equivalence-or-agreement condition, so the conflict rules are not simply avoided. Options A and D invent relocation and licensing requirements not in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "subject to legislation or regulations containing conflict of interest provisions at least equivalent to those set out in section 3280 or has entered into an agreement with the Dealer Member that it will comply with section 3280" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5443

questions.stem: A managed account client of Westerly Counsel proposes, in a written agreement she initiates, a fee for services rendered to her managed account calculated on the volume of transactions initiated for the account. Under subsection 3281(1), may Westerly charge this fee?

options.A: No — volume-based charges to managed account clients are prohibited in all circumstances
options.B: Yes, but only if the fee is also contingent on the account being profitable
options.C: No — only flat annual fees may be charged on managed accounts
options.D: Yes — a charge based on the volume or value of transactions is permitted where the client has provided the Dealer Member with a written agreement setting out the manner in which the fees may be charged

questions.rationale: Subsection 3281(1) prohibits charging a client directly for managed account services based upon the volume or value of transactions, or contingent upon profit or performance, 'unless the client has provided the Dealer Member with a written agreement which sets out the manner in which the fees may be charged.' The written agreement here engages the exception. Option A is the closest distractor because an unconditional prohibition does exist in subsection 3281(2) — but it governs compensating persons authorized to deal with managed accounts, not direct client charges. Option B adds a contingency requirement that does not exist, and option C invents a flat-fee-only rule.
questions.source_quote: "unless the client has provided the Dealer Member with a written agreement which sets out the manner in which the fees may be charged based on volume or value of transactions or contingent upon profit or performance" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5476

questions.stem: Subsection 3281(1) restricts two types of direct client charges for services rendered to a managed account unless a written agreement is in place. Which pair correctly states them?

options.A: Charges payable in foreign currency, and charges exceeding 2% of assets
options.B: Charges deducted monthly, and charges deducted quarterly
options.C: Charges for custody services, and charges for tax reporting
options.D: Charges based upon the volume or value of transactions initiated for the account, and charges contingent upon profit or performance of the client's account

questions.rationale: Subsection 3281(1) identifies the restricted direct charges as those '(i) based upon the volume or value of transactions in the account initiated for the account, or (ii) contingent upon profit or performance of the client's account' — chargeable only under a written agreement setting out the manner of charging. Option A is the closest distractor because it invokes plausible-sounding quantitative limits, but neither a currency restriction nor a 2% cap appears in the rule. Options B and C describe deduction frequencies and service categories the rule does not regulate.
questions.source_quote: "(i) based upon the volume or value of transactions in the account initiated for the account, or (ii) contingent upon profit or performance of the client's account" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5474

questions.stem: Section 3907 lets a Supervisor delegate supervisory tasks generally, yet subsection 3970(3) forbids the designated Supervisor for discretionary accounts from delegating certain reviews. How do these provisions fit together?

options.A: Section 3907 overrides subsection 3970(3), so the discretionary reviews may be delegated to a qualified delegate
options.B: Both provisions permit delegation provided the delegate reports weekly
options.C: Under section 3907 a Supervisor may delegate supervisory tasks but not the responsibility for their performance, and subsection 3970(3) goes further by prohibiting even the performance of the discretionary account reviews from being delegated
options.D: Subsection 3970(3) applies only when the delegate is outside the compliance department

questions.rationale: Subsection 3907(1) provides that a Supervisor 'may delegate supervisory tasks and procedures, but not the responsibility for their performance' — the general regime. Subsection 3970(3) carves out the discretionary account reviews in subsections 3970(1) and 3970(2), whose performance must not be delegated to any other person at all. Option A is the closest distractor because it assumes the general delegation rule prevails, but subsection 3907(2) requires that any delegation not be contrary to Corporation requirements, and 3970(3) is exactly such a requirement. Options B and D invent reporting and departmental conditions found in neither section.
questions.source_quote: "A Supervisor may delegate supervisory tasks and procedures, but not the responsibility for their performance." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 7950

questions.stem: Clause 3273(1)(ii) requires a Dealer Member accepting discretionary accounts to have policies and procedures that specifically address the supervision and operation of discretionary accounts in accordance with which body of requirements?

options.A: Rule 3900
options.B: Rule 2600
options.C: Rule 3400
options.D: National Instrument 31-103

questions.rationale: Clause 3273(1)(ii) requires the Dealer Member's policies and procedures to specifically address the supervision and operation of discretionary accounts in accordance with Rule 3900. Option B is the closest distractor because Rule 2600 is also cited in section 3273 — but in clause 3273(1)(i), as the proficiency standard for the designated Supervisors, not as the supervision framework for the policies and procedures. Rule 3400 (option C) governs suitability determinations, and NI 31-103 (option D) is not the reference in this clause.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member's policies and procedures must specifically address the supervision and operation of discretionary accounts in accordance with Rule 3900" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5356

questions.stem: Yardley Securities accepts a discretionary account for a retail client. Under subsection 3271(1), with which Parts of Rule 3200 must the firm comply for the purposes of that account?

options.A: Parts D and E only
options.B: The requirements in Parts A, B and G of Rule 3200, and if applicable, Parts C, E and F of Rule 3200
options.C: Part G only, since discretionary accounts are governed exclusively by Part G
options.D: Whichever Parts the firm's policies and procedures select

questions.rationale: Subsection 3271(1) provides that, for the purposes of Rule 3200, a Dealer Member that accepts a discretionary account or a managed account for a retail client must comply with the requirements in Parts A, B and G of Rule 3200, and if applicable, Parts C, E and F of Rule 3200. Option C is the closest distractor because Part G contains the discretionary and managed account provisions, but Parts A and B — the general account provisions — also apply, with Parts C, E and F applying as applicable. Option A names the order execution only and margin parts in isolation, and option D wrongly makes applicability elective.
questions.source_quote: "managed account for a retail client must comply with the requirements in Parts A, B and G of Rule 3200, and if applicable, Parts C, E and F of Rule 3200" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5337

questions.stem: Bessette Capital is underwriting a secondary offering. A Registered Representative exercising authority over a client's discretionary account wants the account to participate in the offering. Under subsection 3276(3), what is required before the discretionary account may invest?

options.A: Nothing — discretionary authority already covers new issues
options.B: Disclosure to the client after the purchase settles
options.C: The prior written consent of the client
options.D: Approval from the Corporation's market surveillance group

questions.rationale: Clause 3276(3)(ii) prohibits a responsible person or Dealer Member, without the prior written consent of the client, from knowingly allowing a discretionary account to invest in new issues or secondary offerings underwritten by the Dealer Member. Option B is the closest distractor because disclosure has a role elsewhere in subsection 3276(3) — clause 3276(3)(i) refers to disclosing an officer or director position with an issuer — but for dealer-underwritten offerings the consent must be prior and written, not after-the-fact disclosure. Options A and D respectively ignore and misplace the consent requirement.
questions.source_quote: "A responsible person or a Dealer Member must not, without the prior written consent of the client, knowingly allow a discretionary account to" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5394

questions.stem: Reviewing a draft discretionary account agreement at Winslow & Frost, a Supervisor confirms the document states the 12-month term and the termination terms, but says nothing about what the firm may actually do with the account. Which two contents required by subsection 3274(1) are missing?

options.A: The client's investment objectives, and the firm's fee schedule
options.B: A definition of the extent of the discretionary authority given to the Dealer Member by the client, and any restrictions on the discretionary authority
options.C: The fair-allocation policy, and the client's risk profile
options.D: The names of every Registered Representative at the firm, and the firm's proficiency records

questions.rationale: Clauses 3274(1)(i) and (ii) require the discretionary account agreement to define the extent of the discretionary authority given to the Dealer Member by the client and to include any restrictions on the discretionary authority — the 'what the firm may do' content the draft omits. Option C is the closest distractor because a client's risk profile and the fair-allocation policy are required in the MANAGED account context (clause 3278(1)(i) and clause 3277(1)(vi)), not in the discretionary account agreement. Options A and D mix in fee and personnel items not required by subsection 3274(1).
questions.source_quote: "(i) define the extent of the discretionary authority given to the Dealer Member by the client, (ii) include any restrictions on the discretionary authority" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5371

questions.stem: A retail client of Mirabel Ridge Commodities received the Corporation-approved derivatives risk disclosure statement when her futures account was approved last year. The Corporation has since approved an amended version of the statement. Applying section 3254 to her ongoing derivatives account, what must the firm now do?

options.A: Nothing — the disclosure obligation was discharged when the account was opened
options.B: Close the account until the client re-applies under the amended statement
options.C: Provide her with the amendments to the statement, since the firm must provide each derivatives retail client with any amendments to the statement or document originally provided
options.D: Obtain a new signed derivatives trading agreement reflecting the amendments

questions.rationale: Clause 3254(1)(iii) requires a Dealer Member to provide each derivatives retail client with any amendments to the statement or document provided under clause 3254(1)(i). Delivery at account opening does not exhaust the obligation when the statement is later amended. Option A is the closest distractor because the initial-delivery duty in clause 3254(1)(i) was indeed satisfied, but the amendments duty is a separate, continuing obligation. Option D confuses the risk disclosure statement with the derivatives trading agreement under section 3252, and option B invents a closure remedy the rule does not impose.
questions.source_quote: "provide each derivatives retail client with any amendments to the statement or document provided under clause 3254(1)(i)" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5291

questions.stem: Evander Direct operates its order execution only account services as a separate business unit of a full-service Investment Dealer. A client holds both an OEO account at the unit and an advisory account at the parent firm, and asks for one combined monthly statement covering both. Applying subsection 3241(18), how must Evander Direct respond?

options.A: Decline — the client monthly statements of its order execution only account services must not be consolidated with any other client account statements, including those of any other business unit of the Dealer Member
options.B: Combine the statements, provided the OEO positions appear on a separate page
options.C: Combine the statements, provided the client signs a consolidation waiver
options.D: Decline only if the advisory account holds derivatives

questions.rationale: Clause 3241(18)(ii) requires a Dealer Member approved to provide order execution only account services within a separate legal entity or separate business unit to ensure that the client monthly statements of its order execution only account services are not consolidated with any other client account statements, including those of any other business unit of the Dealer Member or of the Dealer Member itself. Option B is the closest distractor because clause 3241(18)(i) does require labeling all OEO account documentation, but labeling within a combined statement does not cure the prohibition on consolidation. Options C and D invent a waiver mechanism and a derivatives-only trigger the rule does not contain.
questions.source_quote: "the client monthly statements of its order execution only account services are not consolidated with any other client account statements, including those of any other business unit of the Dealer Member" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5098

questions.scenario: Marchbank & Blackwood Securities, an Investment Dealer, is working through several client-file matters at its midtown branch. First, Anneliese Gruber, a retail advisory client whose account application was approved 30 months ago, reports a large inheritance that significantly changes her financial circumstances. Second, the branch's records team is reviewing how Registered Representatives keep copies of their clients' account applications. Third, Casimir Odell, a Registered Representative, is preparing to recommend for the first time that his retail client Beata Lindgren borrow against her home equity line to purchase securities in her cash account; Beata opened that account two years ago and has never received a leverage risk disclosure statement.
questions.stem: The Registered Representative wants to record Anneliese's changed financial circumstances on a copy of her current account application rather than complete a new one. Under subsection 3215(3), is this acceptable?

options.A: Yes, because the application was approved within the past 36 months, provided the Registered Representative and their Supervisor initial any changes
options.B: No, a brand-new account application must be completed whenever a client's financial circumstances change
options.C: Yes, but only if the application was approved within the past 12 months
options.D: Yes, and no initials are required because the original application was already approved

questions.rationale: Subsection 3215(3) permits a Dealer Member to use a copy of a client's current account application to record changes if the application was approved within the past 36 months, but the Registered Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager and their Supervisor must initial any changes. Option C is the closest distractor: the 12-month window in subsection 3215(4) applies only to managed account or discretionary account applications, not to a regular advisory account. Option B overstates the rule — a new application is not mandatory inside the 36-month window — and option D omits the mandatory initials.
questions.source_quote: "if the client's account application was approved within the past 36 months, the Dealer Member may use a copy of a client's current account application to record any changes to a client's information" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4673

questions.scenario: Marchbank & Blackwood Securities, an Investment Dealer, is working through several client-file matters at its midtown branch.
