The policies must specifically address:

options.A: Identifying when a Portfolio Manager or sub-adviser, as described in section 3279, has contravened the managed account conflict of interest related requirements set out in section 3280
options.B: Obtaining the sub-adviser's annual audited financial statements
options.C: Capping the sub-adviser's fees at the rate charged for in-house management
options.D: Prohibiting the use of sub-advisers for any retail managed account

questions.rationale: Clause 3971(2)(i) requires the policies and procedures dealing with the supervision of managed accounts to specifically address identifying when a Portfolio Manager or sub-adviser, as described in section 3279, has contravened the managed account conflict of interest related requirements set out in section 3280 — the detection obligation extends to sub-advisers, not just in-house Portfolio Managers. Option D is the closest distractor because it treats the sub-adviser arrangement itself as the conflict, but the rules permit sub-advisers and instead require contravention detection; B and C describe due-diligence and fee terms the clause does not mention.
questions.source_quote: "identifying when a Portfolio Manager or sub-adviser, as described in section 3279, has contravened managed account conflict of interest related requirements set out in section 3280, and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8413

questions.stem: A Supervisor at Quaverly Securities asks what foundational obligation section 3927 places on the firm with respect to account activity, against which all of its review procedures are measured. Under subsection 3927(1), the Dealer Member must:

options.A: Delegate all account activity reviews to an external audit firm
options.B: Review account activity as required by Corporation requirements and take reasonable steps to provide reasonable assurance that account activity complies with Corporation requirements, securities laws and other applicable laws and the Dealer Member's policies and procedures
options.C: Forward all account activity records to the Corporation each quarter
options.D: Review account activity only when a client complaint has been received

questions.rationale: Subsection 3927(1) requires the Dealer Member to review account activity as required by Corporation requirements and to take reasonable steps to provide reasonable assurance that account activity complies with Corporation requirements, securities laws and other applicable laws and the Dealer Member's policies and procedures. Option D is the closest distractor because complaints do trigger inquiry, but the review obligation is standing, not complaint-driven; A outsources what must remain the dealer's obligation, and C invents a quarterly filing requirement.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must review account activity as required by Corporation requirements and must take reasonable steps to provide reasonable assurance that account activity complies with" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8117

questions.stem: An operations analyst at Huxleigh Capital is configuring which clients receive monthly account statements. Under subsection 3808(2), in addition to clients who request monthly statements, a monthly statement must be sent to each client who, at the end of the month, has in their account which of the following?

options.A: Any security position whatsoever, even with no activity
options.B: A balance exceeding $100,000
options.C: Had a transaction during the month, experienced a cash or account position modification other than dividend or interest payments, an unexpired and unexercised option contract or similar derivative position, or an open futures contract, forward contract, contract for difference or similar derivative position
options.D: A registered plan of any type

questions.rationale: Subsection 3808(2) requires a monthly statement for each client who requests one, or who at the end of the month has in their account: a transaction during the month, a cash or account position modification other than dividend or interest payments, an unexpired and unexercised option contract, futures contract option or similar derivative position, or an open futures contract, forward contract, contract for difference or similar derivative position. Option A is the closest distractor because holding any position does trigger the quarterly statement under subsection 3808(3), but the monthly trigger requires activity, modification or an open derivative position; B and D use balance and account-type triggers the rule does not contain.
questions.source_quote: "(a) had a transaction during the month, (b) has experienced a cash or account position modification, other than dividend or interest payments," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6890

questions.scenario: Marrowfield Capital is a mid-sized Investment Dealer with eleven retail branches. A newly hired head-office compliance director, Elspeth Varga, conducts a baseline assessment of the firm's retail account supervision program and records four observations: (1) branch Supervisors cannot see deposits, withdrawals or journal entries in the review system — only executed trades; (2) the first-tier monthly review selects retail accounts solely by listing every account that traded during the month, with no commission-based or risk-based criteria, and several branches finish the review seven or eight weeks after month-end; (3) one Supervisor who detected a pattern of questionable trades says she dropped the matter because she had no power to restrict the account and no procedure told her where to send it; (4) the firm's procedures designate non-client accounts, discretionary accounts and managed accounts for supervision purposes but are silent on registered accounts and restricted accounts.
questions.stem: Consider observation (1): Supervisors can see only executed trades. Under CIRO's account supervision guidance, why is this an information-access defect?

options.A: Because to review client accounts, Supervisors should have readily accessible client and account activity information including receipts, deposits, withdrawals and journal entries
options.B: Because Supervisors must personally approve every deposit before it is credited
options.C: Because executed trades may only be reviewed by the Chief Financial Officer
options.D: Because the Guidance prohibits review systems that display trade data without client consent

questions.rationale: The Guidance states Supervisors should have access to the information necessary to properly conduct their supervision, and that reviewing client accounts includes readily accessible client and account activity information including receipts, deposits, withdrawals and journal entries — a trades-only view omits the money- and position-movement records the Guidance calls for. Option B is the closest distractor because it involves deposits, but it converts an information-access expectation into a pre-approval duty the Guidance does not impose; C and D misassign review responsibility and invent a consent restriction.
questions.source_quote: "Supervisors should have access to the information necessary to properly conduct their supervision." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 114

questions.scenario: Marrowfield Capital is a mid-sized Investment Dealer with eleven retail branches. A newly hired head-office compliance director, Elspeth Varga, conducts a baseline assessment of the firm's retail account supervision program and records four observations: (1) branch Supervisors cannot see deposits, withdrawals or journal entries in the review system — only executed trades; (2) the first-tier monthly review selects retail accounts solely by listing every account that traded during the month, with no commission-based or risk-based criteria, and several branches finish the review seven or eight weeks after month-end; (3) one Supervisor who detected a pattern of questionable trades says she dropped the matter because she had no power to restrict the account and no procedure told her where to send it; (4) the firm's procedures designate non-client accounts, discretionary accounts and managed accounts for supervision purposes but are silent on registered accounts and restricted accounts.
questions.stem: Observation (2) shows the first-tier monthly review selecting every retail account that traded, with no commission-based or risk-based criteria. Under CIRO's account supervision guidance on first-tier monthly reviews, what is the design principle this selection method ignores?

options.A: Monthly selections must be drawn exclusively from accounts flagged by clients themselves
options.B: Every retail account statement produced must be reviewed in full each month, so no selection is permitted at all
options.C: A first-tier monthly review starts with the selection of retail client accounts, and the Dealer should design this selection to detect improper account activity
options.D: Selection criteria may only be changed with the Corporation's prior written approval

questions.rationale: The Guidance states that Dealers may not be able to review every retail account statement produced, that a first-tier monthly review starts with the selection of retail client accounts, and that Dealers should design this selection to detect improper account activity — an undesigned list of every account that traded is selection without detection-oriented design, and the branches' seven-to-eight-week completion is a separate timing deficiency. Option B is the closest distractor because reviewing everything sounds safest, but the Guidance expressly recognizes Dealers may be unable to review every statement and instead requires designed selection; A and D invent client-driven selection and Corporation pre-approval requirements the Guidance does not contain.
questions.source_quote: "A ﬁrst-tier monthly review starts with the selection of retail client accounts. Dealers should design this selection to detect improper account activity." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 246

questions.scenario: Marrowfield Capital is a mid-sized Investment Dealer with eleven retail branches. A newly hired head-office compliance director, Elspeth Varga, conducts a baseline assessment of the firm's retail account supervision program and records four observations: (1) branch Supervisors cannot see deposits, withdrawals or journal entries in the review system — only executed trades; (2) the first-tier monthly review selects retail accounts solely by listing every account that traded during the month, with no commission-based or risk-based criteria, and several branches finish the review seven or eight weeks after month-end; (3) one Supervisor who detected a pattern of questionable trades says she dropped the matter because she had no power to restrict the account and no procedure told her where to send it; (4) the firm's procedures designate non-client accounts, discretionary accounts and managed accounts for supervision purposes but are silent on registered accounts and restricted accounts.
questions.stem: Observation (3) describes a Supervisor who detected questionable activity but took no action for lack of authority and direction. According to CIRO's account supervision guidance on supervisory structure, what should the firm's design have provided?

options.A: An automatic six-month trading suspension for any account a Supervisor questions
options.B: Supervisors should have the authority to take effective and timely remedial action when they detect activity that falls or appears to fall outside the bounds of proper conduct, just and equitable principles of trade or good business practice — and escalating to a more senior Supervisor or Executive is an acceptable action
options.C: A rule that questionable activity may be acted on only after the affected client consents
options.D: A requirement that detected issues be reported directly to the Corporation before any internal step is taken

questions.rationale: The Guidance states Supervisors should have the authority to take effective and timely remedial action when they detect activity that falls or appears to fall outside the bounds of proper conduct, just and equitable principles of trade or good business practice, and that escalating to a more senior Supervisor or Executive is considered an acceptable action — the firm should have armed the Supervisor with authority or an escalation path. Option D is the closest distractor because escalation is the right concept, but the Guidance's acceptable action is internal escalation, not a mandatory first report to the Corporation; A imposes an automatic sanction the Guidance does not, and C subordinates supervision to client consent.
questions.source_quote: "Supervisors should have the authority to take ecective and timely remedial action when they detect activity that falls or appears to fall outside the bounds of proper conduct" — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 122

questions.scenario: Marrowfield Capital is a mid-sized Investment Dealer with eleven retail branches. A newly hired head-office compliance director, Elspeth Varga, conducts a baseline assessment of the firm's retail account supervision program and records four observations: (1) branch Supervisors cannot see deposits, withdrawals or journal entries in the review system — only executed trades; (2) the first-tier monthly review selects retail accounts solely by listing every account that traded during the month, with no commission-based or risk-based criteria, and several branches finish the review seven or eight weeks after month-end; (3) one Supervisor who detected a pattern of questionable trades says she dropped the matter because she had no power to restrict the account and no procedure told her where to send it; (4) the firm's procedures designate non-client accounts, discretionary accounts and managed accounts for supervision purposes but are silent on registered accounts and restricted accounts.
questions.stem: Observation (4): the designation list omits two account categories. Under subsection 3945(4), which two retail client account categories must Marrowfield add to the accounts it specifically designates for supervision purposes?

options.A: Cash accounts and DAP accounts
options.B: Joint accounts and corporate accounts
options.C: Fee-based accounts and leveraged accounts
options.D: Registered accounts and restricted accounts

questions.rationale: Subsection 3945(4) requires the Dealer Member to specifically designate non-client accounts, discretionary accounts, managed accounts, registered accounts and restricted accounts for supervision purposes — the firm's list omits the last two. Option C is the closest distractor because fee-based accounts do attract their own supervision design under subsection 3945(3), but they are not on the 3945(4) designation list; A and B name account types the subsection does not enumerate.
questions.source_quote: "(iii) managed accounts, (iv) registered accounts, and (v) restricted accounts." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8184

questions.scenario: Calverhall Hollings Securities, a Participant, provides direct electronic access to Brockfenner Fund Management, an institutional asset manager. During a periodic file review, the DEA oversight team documents four facts: (1) Brockfenner has begun entering orders for the accounts of several pension funds it manages under its adviser registration, routing those orders straight from the pension funds' own terminals to the marketplace without passing through Brockfenner's systems; (2) Calverhall Hollings' monitoring has flagged order flow consistent with Brockfenner quietly executing trades on behalf of an unrelated family office that is not its advisory client; (3) Brockfenner's chief operating officer mentions that a regulatory investigation into certain marketplace trades is under way and asks what cooperation the DEA agreement requires; (4) the oversight team has concluded that Brockfenner no longer meets one of the standards Calverhall Hollings established for granting direct electronic access, yet the desk proposes to keep transmitting Brockfenner's orders while the deficiency is remediated over the coming quarter.
questions.stem: Consider fact (1): pension-fund orders bypass Brockfenner's systems entirely. Under sub-clause 7.13(3)(b)(iii) of UMIR, when a DEA client trades for the account of another person as a registered adviser, what must the client ensure?

options.A: That each pension fund signs its own direct electronic access agreement with the Market Regulator
options.B: That the orders for the other person are transmitted through the systems of the client before being entered on a marketplace, and are subject to reasonable risk management and supervisory controls, policies and procedures established and maintained by the client
options.C: That the orders execute only during the opening auction
options.D: That the pension funds compensate the Participant directly for each order

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 7.13(3)(b)(iii) requires that, if the client trades for the account of any other person in accordance with sub-clause (ii), the client must ensure the orders for the other person are transmitted through the systems of the client before being entered on a marketplace and are subject to reasonable risk management and supervisory controls, policies and procedures established and maintained by the client — direct terminal-to-marketplace routing breaches both limbs. Option A is the closest distractor because it correctly senses third parties need a compliance pathway, but DEA agreements run between the Participant and its client, not the Market Regulator; C and D impose execution and payment conditions UMIR does not.
questions.source_quote: "ensure that the orders for the other person are transmitted through the systems of the client before being entered on a marketplace, and" — umir.txt ~line 8884

questions.scenario: Calverhall Hollings Securities, a Participant, provides direct electronic access to Brockfenner Fund Management, an institutional asset manager. During a periodic file review, the DEA oversight team documents four facts: (1) Brockfenner has begun entering orders for the accounts of several pension funds it manages under its adviser registration, routing those orders straight from the pension funds' own terminals to the marketplace without passing through Brockfenner's systems; (2) Calverhall Hollings' monitoring has flagged order flow consistent with Brockfenner quietly executing trades on behalf of an unrelated family office that is not its advisory client; (3) Brockfenner's chief operating officer mentions that a regulatory investigation into certain marketplace trades is under way and asks what cooperation the DEA agreement requires; (4) the oversight team has concluded that Brockfenner no longer meets one of the standards Calverhall Hollings established for granting direct electronic access, yet the desk proposes to keep transmitting Brockfenner's orders while the deficiency is remediated over the coming quarter.
questions.stem: Fact (2) suggests trading for a person who is not Brockfenner's advisory client. Under UMIR Policy 7.1 Part 9, monitoring of a DEA client's orders must be designed to identify, among other things, whether the client may have:

options.A: Engaged in unauthorized trading on behalf of the account of another person
options.B: Achieved a market share exceeding 5% in any single security
options.C: Paid commissions below the Participant's standard institutional schedule
options.D: Used more than one order type in a single trading session

questions.rationale: Policy 7.1 Part 9 requires a Participant that has granted direct electronic access to monitor orders entered by the client to identify whether the client may have, among other things, engaged in unauthorized trading on behalf of the account of another person — trading for a non-client family office is exactly that pattern. Option B is the closest distractor because concentration metrics are legitimate surveillance inputs, but the Part 9 monitoring list targets access misuse and agreement breaches, not market share; C and D concern commercial terms and order-type variety, which the Policy does not list.
questions.source_quote: "engaged in unauthorized trading on behalf of the account of another person; or" — umir.txt ~line 7063

questions.scenario: Calverhall Hollings Securities, a Participant, provides direct electronic access to Brockfenner Fund Management, an institutional asset manager. During a periodic file review, the DEA oversight team documents four facts: (1) Brockfenner has begun entering orders for the accounts of several pension funds it manages under its adviser registration, routing those orders straight from the pension funds' own terminals to the marketplace without passing through Brockfenner's systems; (2) Calverhall Hollings' monitoring has flagged order flow consistent with Brockfenner quietly executing trades on behalf of an unrelated family office that is not its advisory client; (3) Brockfenner's chief operating officer mentions that a regulatory investigation into certain marketplace trades is under way and asks what cooperation the DEA agreement requires; (4) the oversight team has concluded that Brockfenner no longer meets one of the standards Calverhall Hollings established for granting direct electronic access, yet the desk proposes to keep transmitting Brockfenner's orders while the deficiency is remediated over the coming quarter.
questions.stem: On fact (3): under sub-clause 7.13(3)(a)(iii) of UMIR, what must the written DEA agreement provide regarding an investigation or proceeding by a marketplace or the Market Regulator into trading conducted through the access?

options.A: The client may decline to participate in any investigation that concerns its proprietary strategies
options.B: The Participant must conduct the investigation itself before the Market Regulator may become involved
options.C: The client will fully co-operate with the Participant in connection with any such investigation or proceeding, including, upon request by the Participant, providing access to information to the marketplace or Market Regulator that is necessary for the purposes of the investigation or proceeding
options.D: Cooperation is required only after a formal finding of violation has been made

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 7.13(3)(a)(iii) requires the written agreement to provide that the client will fully co-operate with the Participant in connection with any investigation or proceeding by any marketplace or the Market Regulator with respect to trading conducted pursuant to direct electronic access, including, upon request by the Participant, providing access to information to the marketplace or Market Regulator that is necessary for the purposes of the investigation or proceeding. Option D is the closest distractor because it concedes a duty but delays it until a finding is made, whereas the obligation applies to the investigation itself; A carves out proprietary strategies, and B inverts the regulatory sequence.
questions.source_quote: "operate with the Participant in connection with any investigation or proceeding by any marketplace or the Market Regulator with respect to trading conducted pursuant to direct electronic access" — umir.txt ~line 8851

questions.scenario: Calverhall Hollings Securities, a Participant, provides direct electronic access to Brockfenner Fund Management, an institutional asset manager. During a periodic file review, the DEA oversight team documents four facts: (1) Brockfenner has begun entering orders for the accounts of several pension funds it manages under its adviser registration, routing those orders straight from the pension funds' own terminals to the marketplace without passing through Brockfenner's systems; (2) Calverhall Hollings' monitoring has flagged order flow consistent with Brockfenner quietly executing trades on behalf of an unrelated family office that is not its advisory client; (3) Brockfenner's chief operating officer mentions that a regulatory investigation into certain marketplace trades is under way and asks what cooperation the DEA agreement requires; (4) the oversight team has concluded that Brockfenner no longer meets one of the standards Calverhall Hollings established for granting direct electronic access, yet the desk proposes to keep transmitting Brockfenner's orders while the deficiency is remediated over the coming quarter.
questions.stem: Fact (4): the desk wants to keep transmitting Brockfenner's orders despite the firm's conclusion that Brockfenner no longer meets a DEA standard. Under subsection 7.13(4) of UMIR, why must this proposal be rejected?

options.A: Because a standards deficiency may be tolerated for up to one quarter if a remediation plan exists
options.B: Because a Participant must not allow any order to be transmitted using direct electronic access unless the Participant is maintaining and applying its established standards and is satisfied the client meets those standards and is in compliance with the written agreement
options.C: Because only the Market Regulator may decide whether transmission continues after a deficiency is found
options.D: Because the client must first post additional collateral equal to its average daily order value

questions.rationale: Clause 7.13(4)(a) prohibits a Participant from allowing any order to be transmitted using direct electronic access unless the Participant is maintaining and applying the standards established under subsection 7.13(1), is satisfied the client meets those standards, and is satisfied the client is in compliance with the written agreement — once Calverhall Hollings has concluded Brockfenner fails a standard, it cannot be 'satisfied' and must not allow transmission. Option A is the closest distractor because remediation plans are commercially sensible, but UMIR contains no tolerance period for known standards failures; C misallocates the decision to the Market Regulator, and D invents a collateral cure.
questions.source_quote: "maintaining and applying the standards established by the Participant under subsection (1), (ii) satisfied the client, investment dealer or foreign dealer equivalent meets the standards established by the Participant under subsection (1)" — umir.txt ~line 8918

questions.scenario: Pellbridge Futures Inc. carries retail derivatives accounts. Theodora Lindgaard, the designated Supervisor for futures contracts, forwards, CFDs and similar derivatives, conducts the month-end review and documents four findings: (1) a client whose account is governed by a cumulative loss limit insists the limit was permanently fixed when the account opened four years ago and refuses to discuss it, while the file shows no validation or update since opening; (2) a second client's index option position has grown beyond the position limit prescribed by the exchange on which the options trade, and the desk argues the limit is the exchange's problem, not Pellbridge's; (3) a third client with open CFD positions at the end of each day complains he receives only a quarterly account summary; (4) the daily and monthly statements the firm does produce for open futures positions show the description of each open contract but omit the price at which each open contract was entered into.
questions.stem: On finding (1): under subsection 3252(2), on what basis must a client's cumulative loss limit be determined?

options.A: On a lifetime basis and validated with the client on an annual basis, or on an annual basis and updated annually
options.B: On a fixed basis set once at account opening with no further validation required
options.C: On a monthly basis reset automatically by the surveillance system
options.D: On whatever basis the client unilaterally selects, including declining any limit

questions.rationale: Clause 3252(2)(iii) requires that the client's cumulative loss limit be determined, notwithstanding obligations under Rule 3400, on a lifetime basis and validated with the client on an annual basis, or on an annual basis and updated annually — a four-year-old limit with no validation or update satisfies neither branch. Option B is the closest distractor because a lifetime limit is indeed set once, but it must still be validated with the client annually; C substitutes a monthly system reset the rule does not contemplate, and D lets the client opt out of the loss-limit regime entirely.
questions.source_quote: "(a) a lifetime basis and validated with the client on an annual basis, or (b) an annual basis and updated annually." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5265

questions.scenario: Pellbridge Futures Inc. carries retail derivatives accounts. Theodora Lindgaard, the designated Supervisor for futures contracts, forwards, CFDs and similar derivatives, conducts the month-end review and documents four findings: (1) a client whose account is governed by a cumulative loss limit insists the limit was permanently fixed when the account opened four years ago and refuses to discuss it, while the file shows no validation or update since opening; (2) a second client's index option position has grown beyond the position limit prescribed by the exchange on which the options trade, and the desk argues the limit is the exchange's problem, not Pellbridge's; (3) a third client with open CFD positions at the end of each day complains he receives only a quarterly account summary; (4) the daily and monthly statements the firm does produce for open futures positions show the description of each open contract but omit the price at which each open contract was entered into.
questions.stem: On finding (2): the desk claims an exchange position limit is not Pellbridge's concern. Under section 3255, what is the Dealer Member's own obligation?

options.A: The Dealer Member has no obligation, because position limits bind only the client
options.B: The Dealer Member need only act if the exchange issues a formal demand
options.C: The Dealer Member's only obligation is to disclose the limit in the relationship disclosure document
options.D: The Dealer Member must comply with the requirements of any entity through which the derivative is traded, cleared, or issued, including, without limitation, reporting, position limit and exercise limit requirements

questions.rationale: Section 3255 places the obligation on the Dealer Member itself: it must comply with the requirements of any entity through which the derivative is traded, cleared, or issued, including, without limitation, reporting, position limit and exercise limit requirements — the exchange limit is therefore Pellbridge's compliance problem, not merely the client's. Option B is the closest distractor because exchange enforcement may eventually involve demands, but the dealer's compliance duty is standing and does not await a demand; A wrongly confines the limit to the client, and C reduces a compliance obligation to a disclosure item.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must comply with the requirements of any entity through which the derivative is traded, cleared, or issued, including, without limitation, reporting, position limit and exercise limit requirements." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5301

questions.scenario: Pellbridge Futures Inc. carries retail derivatives accounts. Theodora Lindgaard, the designated Supervisor for futures contracts, forwards, CFDs and similar derivatives, conducts the month-end review and documents four findings: (1) a client whose account is governed by a cumulative loss limit insists the limit was permanently fixed when the account opened four years ago and refuses to discuss it, while the file shows no validation or update since opening; (2) a second client's index option position has grown beyond the position limit prescribed by the exchange on which the options trade, and the desk argues the limit is the exchange's problem, not Pellbridge's; (3) a third client with open CFD positions at the end of each day complains he receives only a quarterly account summary; (4) the daily and monthly statements the firm does produce for open futures positions show the description of each open contract but omit the price at which each open contract was entered into.
questions.stem: Finding (4): statements for open futures positions omit the entry price of each open contract. Under subsection 3808(6), what minimum content must the daily and monthly statements contain for clients with open futures contracts, forward contracts, CFDs or similar derivatives?

options.A: The description and quantity of each open contract, and the price at which each open contract was entered into
options.B: Only the aggregate market value of all derivative positions combined
options.C: The description of each contract and the firm's internal risk rating for it
options.D: The quantity of each contract and the commission charged on the next anticipated trade

questions.rationale: Subsection 3808(6) requires that, for clients with any open futures contracts, forward contracts, contracts for difference or similar derivatives, the daily and monthly statements contain, at a minimum, the description and quantity of each open contract and the price at which each open contract was entered into — Pellbridge's statements omit the second element. Option B is the closest distractor because market value is required statement content under subsection 3808(4), but it cannot replace the contract-by-contract description, quantity and entry price 3808(6) demands; C and D introduce risk ratings and prospective commissions the rule does not require.
questions.source_quote: "(i) the description and quantity of each open contract, and (ii) the price at which each open contract was entered into." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 6950

questions.stem: Thornquist Securities is registered as both an investment dealer and a mutual fund dealer. It hires Maja Holmberg, who is registered as a Mutual Fund Dealer - Dealing Representative, to act as a Registered Representative dealing in mutual funds only. The Supervisor asks whether a separate CIRO approval step must be completed before Maja begins. What is correct?

options.A: Maja must write the Supervisor Exam before she may deal in mutual funds at the firm
options.B: No approval of any kind applies to individuals who deal only in mutual funds
options.C: Maja may begin immediately and seek Corporation approval within 90 days of her start date
options.D: Approval is automatic upon her registration as a Mutual Fund Dealer - Dealing Representative, because she is an employee of a firm registered as both an investment dealer and a mutual fund dealer

questions.rationale: Clause 2551(1)(iii) requires Corporation approval before an individual begins working in an Approved Person role, but states that for a Registered Representative dealing in mutual funds only who is an employee of a firm registered as both an investment dealer and a mutual fund dealer, approval is automatic upon registration as a Mutual Fund Dealer - Dealing Representative. Option C is the closest distractor — there is no 90-day grace period for approval; the 90-day timeline in 2604(1)(i) concerns post-approval training, not approval itself. A invents an exam requirement for this category; B wrongly removes mutual-fund-only individuals from the approval regime altogether.
questions.source_quote: "such approval will be automatic upon the individual's registration as a Mutual Fund Dealer - Dealing Representative." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 2917

questions.stem: Registered Representative Tomas Eklund of Belmare Wealth properly informed the firm of a paid outside activity coaching a hockey team, and the firm gave its prior approval. Its policies and procedures address continuous service to clients and potential conflicts of interest. The Supervisor closes the file without any external filing. Which required step under section 2554 remains outstanding?

options.A: Obtaining the client's written consent to the outside activity
options.B: Notifying the Corporation of the outside activity within the time period and manner required by National Instrument 33-109
options.C: Having the Approved Person resign from the outside activity within 30 days
options.D: Filing a monthly report on the outside activity under Rule 8200

questions.rationale: Clause 2554(2)(iv) requires that the Dealer Member notify the Corporation of the outside activity within the time period and manner required by National Instrument 33-109 — a firm-level filing obligation that survives even when the inform/approve/policies conditions are all met. Option A is the closest distractor: section 2554 imposes no client-consent condition. C contradicts the rule, which permits the Approved Person to have and continue in a compliant outside activity; D confuses this with the 2552(4) report on conditions imposed under Rule 8200 or 9200.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member notifies the Corporation of the outside activity within the time period and manner required by National Instrument 33-109." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3048

questions.stem: A Supervisor at Caldwynne Capital reviews the Rule 1201 definitions to confirm what activity Portfolio Manager approval itself authorizes Priya Chowdhury to perform. According to the definition, a Portfolio Manager is an individual designated by the Dealer Member and approved by the Corporation to do what?

options.A: Provide discretionary portfolio management for managed accounts
options.B: Trade in, but not advise on, securities or derivatives on the Dealer Member's behalf
options.C: Supervise the activities of all Approved Persons at the Dealer Member
options.D: Provide discretionary portfolio management only under the supervision of another Portfolio Manager

questions.rationale: Rule 1201 defines a Portfolio Manager as an individual designated by the Dealer Member and approved by the Corporation to provide discretionary portfolio management for managed accounts. Option D is the closest distractor — it states the Associate Portfolio Manager definition, which adds 'under the supervision of a Portfolio Manager'; a Portfolio Manager has no such condition. B is the Investment Representative definition; C describes a Supervisor, not a Portfolio Manager.
questions.source_quote: "An individual designated by the Dealer Member and approved by the Corporation to provide discretionary portfolio management for managed accounts." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 699

questions.stem: Lars Bergstrom is approved as a Registered Representative dealing in mutual funds only under subsection 2605(3) and acts as an agent of Drisdale Securities in compliance with Rule 2300. He asks that his commissions be paid to his personal holding corporation, which is not registered under securities laws. Under subsection 2551(8), how should the Supervisor respond?

options.A: It is always prohibited for a Dealer Member to pay remuneration to an unregistered corporation
options.B: It is permitted without conditions, because the corporation belongs to the Approved Person
options.C: It can be permitted: remuneration in respect of his business may be paid by the Dealer Member to a corporation that is not registered under securities laws, provided the prescribed conditions are met, including a written agreement in the form prescribed by the Corporation
options.D: It is permitted only if the corporation registers with the Corporation within 90 days

questions.rationale: Subsection 2551(8) provides that, for an individual approved as a Registered Representative dealing in mutual funds only under subsection 2605(3) who acts as an agent of a Dealer Member under Rule 2300, remuneration in respect of business conducted on the dealer's behalf may be paid to a corporation that is not registered under securities laws, provided conditions are met — including that the arrangement is not prohibited by securities laws, the corporation is incorporated in Canada, and the individual, dealer and corporation enter a written agreement in a form prescribed by the Corporation. Option A is the closest distractor — it states the general 2551(7) prohibition while missing this express exception; B drops the conditions; D substitutes corporate registration for the prescribed-agreement mechanism.
questions.source_quote: "any remuneration, gratuity, benefit or other consideration in respect of business conducted by the individual on behalf of the Dealer Member may be paid by the Dealer Member to a corporation that is not registered under securities laws" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 2943

questions.scenario: Roxana Dragomir is the newly appointed branch Supervisor at Nakamura-Pryce Investments, an Investment Dealer. During her first compliance sweep she opens four files: (1) the firm has been allowing Olaf Sundqvist, an independent consultant who is not a director, partner, officer or employee of the firm, to act as an Approved Person on its behalf; (2) the firm has not yet told the Corporation whether a newly hired Registered Representative will deal in only mutual funds and government-guaranteed instruments, in derivatives, or in general securities business; (3) the Corporation imposed conditions on one Approved Person under Rule 8200 two months ago, and no report on those conditions has been filed; and (4) Ines Quintana, a Registered Representative whom the Corporation approved to deal with retail clients, has been taking orders from and giving advice to a municipal pension board that qualifies as an institutional client, and a colleague insists she is offside her approval.
questions.stem: File (1): the consultant arrangement with Olaf Sundqvist. Which provision does this arrangement breach?

options.A: Subsection 2551(2) — only a Dealer Member's director, partner, officer or employee can be an Approved Person
options.B: Subsection 2551(5) — Approved Persons must use accurate titles when dealing with the public
options.C: Subsection 2551(6) — a former Approved Person must immediately cease activity requiring approval
options.D: Subsection 2551(7) — an Approved Person must not accept remuneration from persons other than the Dealer Member

questions.rationale: Subsection 2551(2) states that only a Dealer Member's director, partner, officer or employee can be an Approved Person; an outside consultant with none of those relationships cannot act in the role. Option D is the closest distractor because consultants are paid externally, but the defect here is the missing employment/office relationship, not the source of remuneration. B concerns titles and C concerns ceased approvals — neither fits an active outsider.
questions.source_quote: "Only a Dealer Member's director, partner, officer or employee can be an Approved Person." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 2919

questions.scenario: Roxana Dragomir is the newly appointed branch Supervisor at Nakamura-Pryce Investments, an Investment Dealer. During her first compliance sweep she opens four files: (1) the firm has been allowing Olaf Sundqvist, an independent consultant who is not a director, partner, officer or employee of the firm, to act as an Approved Person on its behalf; (2) the firm has not yet told the Corporation whether a newly hired Registered Representative will deal in only mutual funds and government-guaranteed instruments, in derivatives, or in general securities business; (3) the Corporation imposed conditions on one Approved Person under Rule 8200 two months ago, and no report on those conditions has been filed; and (4) Ines Quintana, a Registered Representative whom the Corporation approved to deal with retail clients, has been taking orders from and giving advice to a municipal pension board that qualifies as an institutional client, and a colleague insists she is offside her approval.
questions.stem: File (2): the missing notification about the new Registered Representative's type of business. Under clause 2553(3)(iv), into which categories must the Dealer Member's notification to the Corporation classify the individual's dealing or advising?

options.A: Retail clients, institutional clients, or both
options.B: Exchange-traded products, over-the-counter products, or both
options.C: Equities only, fixed income only, or balanced business
options.D: Only mutual funds and specified government-guaranteed and deposit instruments; derivatives subject to the subsection 2625(3) limitations; or general securities business

questions.rationale: Clause 2553(3)(iv) requires the Dealer Member to notify the Corporation which approved individuals will deal in or advise in: (a) only mutual funds, government or government-guaranteed debt instruments and specified deposit instruments, (b) derivatives subject to the limitations set out in subsection 2625(3), and (c) general securities business. Option A is the closest distractor — the retail/institutional client-type approval is the separate requirement in clause 2553(3)(i), not the type-of-business notification in (iv). B and C invent classifications not in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "derivatives subject to the limitations set out in subsection 2625(3)," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3017

questions.scenario: Roxana Dragomir is the newly appointed branch Supervisor at Nakamura-Pryce Investments, an Investment Dealer. During her first compliance sweep she opens four files: (1) the firm has been allowing Olaf Sundqvist, an independent consultant who is not a director, partner, officer or employee of the firm, to act as an Approved Person on its behalf; (2) the firm has not yet told the Corporation whether a newly hired Registered Representative will deal in only mutual funds and government-guaranteed instruments, in derivatives, or in general securities business; (3) the Corporation imposed conditions on one Approved Person under Rule 8200 two months ago, and no report on those conditions has been filed; and (4) Ines Quintana, a Registered Representative whom the Corporation approved to deal with retail clients, has been taking orders from and giving advice to a municipal pension board that qualifies as an institutional client, and a colleague insists she is offside her approval.
questions.stem: File (3): the unfiled report on the Rule 8200 conditions. What does subsection 2552(4) require, and what follows under subsection 2552(5) from filing late?

options.A: An annual report on all conditions; late filing triggers automatic suspension of the Approved Person
options.B: A report within 30 days of the conditions being imposed; late filing requires Board notification
options.C: No report is required unless the Corporation specifically requests one in writing
options.D: A report specified by the Corporation within 10 business days of the end of each month; if the report is not filed or is filed late, the firm must pay the applicable late filing fee

questions.rationale: Subsection 2552(4) requires a Dealer Member to file a report specified by the Corporation on conditions imposed on an Approved Person under Rule 8200 or Rule 9200 within 10 business days of the end of each month, and subsection 2552(5) imposes the applicable late filing fee if the report is not filed or is filed late. Option A is the closest distractor — automatic suspension under 2552(2) attaches to incomplete post-approval proficiency, not to late condition reports. B substitutes an invented 30-day deadline; C contradicts the standing monthly obligation.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must file a report specified by the Corporation on the conditions imposed on an Approved Person under Rule 8200 or Rule 9200 within 10 business days of the end of each month." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 2984

questions.scenario: Roxana Dragomir is the newly appointed branch Supervisor at Nakamura-Pryce Investments, an Investment Dealer. During her first compliance sweep she opens four files: (1) the firm has been allowing Olaf Sundqvist, an independent consultant who is not a director, partner, officer or employee of the firm, to act as an Approved Person on its behalf; (2) the firm has not yet told the Corporation whether a newly hired Registered Representative will deal in only mutual funds and government-guaranteed instruments, in derivatives, or in general securities business; (3) the Corporation imposed conditions on one Approved Person under Rule 8200 two months ago, and no report on those conditions has been filed; and (4) Ines Quintana, a Registered Representative whom the Corporation approved to deal with retail clients, has been taking orders from and giving advice to a municipal pension board that qualifies as an institutional client, and a colleague insists she is offside her approval.
questions.stem: File (4): Ines Quintana — approved to deal with retail clients — serving the institutional pension board. Is the colleague right that she is acting outside her approval?

options.A: Yes — a Registered Representative dealing with retail clients may serve retail clients only
options.B: Yes, unless the pension board signs a waiver of suitability protections
options.C: No — under clause 2553(3)(ii), a Registered Representative dealing with retail clients may take orders from, or give advice to, all types of clients
options.D: No, but only because pension boards are deemed retail clients under Rule 1201

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 2553(3)(ii)(a) provides that a Registered Representative dealing with retail clients may take orders from, or give advice to, all types of clients; it is the institutional-side approval in sub-clause (b) that is limited to institutional clients only. Option A is the closest distractor — it imports the institutional-side restriction in mirror image onto the retail-side approval; B confuses client-type approval with the separate 3404(3) suitability-waiver mechanism; D is wrong because the pension board's institutional status under Rule 1201 is irrelevant to her permission, which already extends to all client types.
questions.source_quote: "retail clients, may take orders from, or give advice to, all types of clients, or" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3005

questions.stem: Roussel Capital receives an account application from Veldora Interbank Ltd., a bank incorporated offshore that has no physical presence in any country and is not affiliated with any depository institution. The Supervisor reviewing the new-account file must conclude that:

options.A: The account may be opened if enhanced identity verification is completed within 30 days
options.B: The account may be opened, but must be designated as a non-client account
options.C: The account must not be opened or maintained, because the applicant is a shell bank
options.D: The account may be opened if the bank's home regulator confirms its registration in writing

questions.rationale: Section 3205 prohibits a Dealer Member from opening or maintaining an account for a shell bank, defined as a bank that does not have a physical presence in any country. The subsection 3205(2) relief applies only to an affiliate of a bank, loan or trust company, credit union, or other depository institution with a physical presence — which this applicant lacks. Option A is the closest distractor, borrowing the 30-day identity timeline in 3206(3), which cannot cure an outright prohibition; B misapplies the non-client designation for other dealers' employees; D invents a regulator-letter cure.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must not open or maintain an account for a shell bank, which is defined as a bank that does not have a physical presence in any country." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4478

questions.stem: Everline Wealth is told by a prospective corporate client that listing who beneficially owns 25% or more of its voting securities 'will take a couple of months.' The account has been open for 32 days and the identities still have not been established under section 3206. What does subsection 3206(4) require the Supervisor to ensure now?

options.A: Close the account and return all assets to the client immediately
options.B: Restrict the account solely to liquidating trades, transfers, paying out funds or delivering securities until the identities are established
options.C: Suspend trading for 10 business days, after which normal trading may resume
options.D: Escalate to the Chief Compliance Officer, who may grant a 60-day extension

questions.rationale: Under subsection 3206(4), if the identity of an individual referred to in subsection 3206(1) cannot be established within 30 days of opening an account, the Dealer Member must restrict the account solely to liquidating trades, transfers, paying out funds or delivering securities, and the restrictions must remain until identity is established. Option A is the closest distractor — the rule mandates restriction, not closure. C invents a time-limited suspension that lapses automatically; D invents a CCO extension power that the rule does not contain.
questions.source_quote: "the Dealer Member must restrict the account solely to liquidating trades, transfers, paying out funds or delivering securities." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4497

questions.stem: Foxhollow Capital is opening an initial account for a federally regulated pension fund. The new-accounts clerk starts assembling the trust-style identification package required by sections 3203 and 3206 — trustee names and addresses, identification of controlling individuals. The Supervisor should advise that:

options.A: The full package is required, since every trust account must satisfy section 3203
options.B: Only the trustee names are required; controlling-individual identification can be skipped
options.C: Sections 3203, 3204 and 3206 do not apply, because a pension fund regulated by or under an Act of Parliament or the legislature of a province is an identification exception under section 3207
options.D: The package may be deferred for 90 days for regulated pension funds

questions.rationale: Subsection 3207(1) lists the entities to which sections 3203, 3204 and 3206 do not apply, including, in clause (vi), a pension fund that is regulated by or under an Act of Parliament or the legislature of a province. Option A is the closest distractor — it states the general trust rule while missing the exception; B invents a partial exemption the rule does not draw; D substitutes deferral for exemption.
questions.source_quote: "a pension fund that is regulated by or under an Act of Parliament or the legislature of a province," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4510

questions.stem: Greymont Securities' order execution only division receives an account application from Calder Trading House, a non-individual that is registered as a dealer under securities laws and trades on a Marketplace for which the Corporation is the regulation services provider. Under subsections 3241(1) and 3241(2), what should the Supervisor conclude?

options.A: The account may be opened, since OEO services are available to any applicant who acknowledges the OEO disclosures
options.B: The account must be refused: an OEO provider must not provide order execution only account services to such a person — unless the person is exempted from registration as a dealer under section 8.4 of National Instrument 31-103
options.C: The account may be opened only if Calder posts additional margin
options.D: The account must be refused because no non-individual may ever hold an OEO account

questions.rationale: Clause 3241(1)(iii) prohibits providing order execution only account services to any person that is not an individual and is acting as and registered or exempted from registration as a dealer in accordance with securities laws, and trades on a Marketplace for which the Corporation is the regulation services provider. Subsection 3241(2) then permits the service despite that clause where the person is exempted from registration as a dealer under section 8.4 of National Instrument 31-103. Option D is the closest distractor — the prohibition targets dealer-status non-individuals trading on CIRO-regulated marketplaces, not all non-individuals; A ignores the prohibition; C substitutes margin for an eligibility rule.
questions.source_quote: "a Dealer Member may provide an order execution only account service to a person that is exempted from registration as a dealer under section 8.4 of National Instrument 31-103." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4995

questions.stem: Hollowell Futures is opening an initial account for Brightwater Holdings LP, a limited partnership. The new-accounts clerk has the partnership agreement signature page but has done nothing to confirm what the partnership actually is or does. Beyond identifying controlling individuals, what does clause 3203(1)(ii) require the dealer to do when opening the initial account?

options.A: Establish the existence of the partnership or trust and the nature of its business
options.B: Obtain audited financial statements for the partnership's two most recent fiscal years
options.C: Obtain the names and addresses of all limited partners regardless of their stake
options.D: Register the partnership with the Corporation as an institutional client

questions.rationale: Clause 3203(1)(ii) requires a Dealer Member, when opening an initial account for a partnership or trust, to establish the existence of the partnership or trust and the nature of its business; clauses (i), (iii) and (iv) add the trust-specific naming, controlling-individual identification and insider-determination steps. Option C is the closest distractor — the names-and-addresses requirement in clause 3203(1)(i) applies to trustees, known beneficiaries and settlors of a trust, not to every limited partner of a partnership; B invents an audited-statements condition; D confuses identification with institutional classification.
questions.source_quote: "establish the existence of the partnership or trust and the nature of its business," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4459

questions.stem: During onboarding at Ivydale Investments, identity verification for a prospective retail client succeeds, but the Registered Representative learns the applicant was recently named in press reports alleging investment fraud at another firm. He proposes proceeding since identity is established. Under clause 3202(1)(i), what does the Supervisor remind him the dealer must do?

options.A: Refuse the account automatically — adverse press always bars account opening
options.B: Proceed; only government watchlists are relevant once identity is established
options.C: Report the applicant to the Corporation before any account decision
options.D: Make reasonable inquiries as to the reputation of the client, because the dealer has cause for concern

questions.rationale: Clause 3202(1)(i) requires a Dealer Member to establish the identity of a client and, if the Dealer Member has any cause for concern, make reasonable inquiries as to the reputation of the client — credible fraud allegations are exactly such cause for concern. Option A is the closest distractor — the rule mandates inquiry, not automatic refusal; B wrongly limits the duty to watchlists and treats identity verification as exhaustive; C invents a pre-opening report to the Corporation.
questions.source_quote: "establish the identity of a client and, if the Dealer Member has any cause for concern, make reasonable inquiries as to the reputation of the client," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4420

questions.scenario: Henrik Wachowski is the designated Supervisor for new accounts at Larkmoor Capital, an Investment Dealer that recently acquired an affiliated Mutual Fund Dealer Member. Four account-opening matters await his decision: (a) operations wants to open Larkmoor accounts for clients transferring from the affiliate by re-using the affiliate's existing client documentation rather than collecting new records; (b) the new-accounts checklist demands the name of a trusted contact person, and written consent to contact that person, for every applicant — and a clerk has now halted the account opening of Pinegate Logistics Inc., a corporation, because it cannot supply one; (c) the institutional desk asks whether a new discretionary account for an institutional client requires the firm's Relationship Disclosure package; and (d) a new corporate client has supplied its director list but, 31 days after opening, the identity of the individual who controls 40% of its voting rights still has not been established.
questions.stem: Matter (a): re-using the affiliate firm's documentation. Under subsection 3212(4), Larkmoor may use the documentation maintained by the affiliate firm to meet its client account record requirement only if certain conditions are met. Which of the following is one of them?

options.A: The affiliate firm account agreement has an acceptable assignment clause that in substance protects the client's interests as if the client had signed a new account agreement with the Dealer Member
options.B: The client signs a blanket waiver of the requirement to maintain client account records
options.C: The Corporation pre-approves each transferred account individually
options.D: The affiliate firm retains sole responsibility for supervising the transferred accounts

questions.rationale: Subsection 3212(4) permits a Dealer Member receiving accounts from an affiliated Dealer Member or affiliated Mutual Fund Dealer Member to use the affiliate's documentation provided four conditions are met, including clause (iv): the affiliate firm account agreement has an acceptable assignment clause that in substance protects the client's interests in the same manner as if the client had signed a new account agreement with the Dealer Member. Option D is the closest distractor — supervision of the accounts passes to the receiving dealer; nothing in 3212(4) leaves it with the affiliate. B and C invent waiver and pre-approval mechanisms not in the rule.
questions.source_quote: "the affiliate firm account agreement has an acceptable assignment clause that in substance protects the client's interests in the same manner as if the client had signed a new account agreement with the Dealer Member." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4613

questions.scenario: Henrik Wachowski is the designated Supervisor for new accounts at Larkmoor Capital, an Investment Dealer that recently acquired an affiliated Mutual Fund Dealer Member. Four account-opening matters await his decision: (a) operations wants to open Larkmoor accounts for clients transferring from the affiliate by re-using the affiliate's existing client documentation rather than collecting new records; (b) the new-accounts checklist demands the name of a trusted contact person, and written consent to contact that person, for every applicant — and a clerk has now halted the account opening of Pinegate Logistics Inc., a corporation, because it cannot supply one; (c) the institutional desk asks whether a new discretionary account for an institutional client requires the firm's Relationship Disclosure package; and (d) a new corporate client has supplied its director list but, 31 days after opening, the identity of the individual who controls 40% of its voting rights still has not been established.
questions.stem: Matter (b): the halted account opening for Pinegate Logistics Inc. over the missing trusted contact person. How should Henrik resolve it under section 3202?

options.A: The clerk is right — every account requires a trusted contact person before opening
options.B: The account may proceed only if a director of the corporation personally names a trusted contact
options.C: The corporation must instead designate two authorized signatories as substitute trusted contacts
options.D: Release the hold — subsection 3202(4) does not apply to a Dealer Member in respect of a client that is not an individual

questions.rationale: Subsection 3202(5) provides that the trusted-contact-person obligation in subsection 3202(4) does not apply to a Dealer Member in respect of a client that is not an individual — a corporation cannot be financially exploited or lack mental capacity in the sense the provision protects against, so the checklist requirement is over-broad. Option B is the closest distractor — it tries to satisfy 3202(4) through a director, but the subsection simply does not apply to non-individual clients; A states the individual-client rule without the carve-out; C invents a substitute mechanism.
questions.source_quote: "Subsection 3202(4) does not apply to a Dealer Member in respect of a client that is not an individual." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4453

questions.scenario: Henrik Wachowski is the designated Supervisor for new accounts at Larkmoor Capital, an Investment Dealer that recently acquired an affiliated Mutual Fund Dealer Member. Four account-opening matters await his decision: (a) operations wants to open Larkmoor accounts for clients transferring from the affiliate by re-using the affiliate's existing client documentation rather than collecting new records; (b) the new-accounts checklist demands the name of a trusted contact person, and written consent to contact that person, for every applicant — and a clerk has now halted the account opening of Pinegate Logistics Inc., a corporation, because it cannot supply one; (c) the institutional desk asks whether a new discretionary account for an institutional client requires the firm's Relationship Disclosure package; and (d) a new corporate client has supplied its director list but, 31 days after opening, the identity of the individual who controls 40% of its voting rights still has not been established.
questions.stem: Matter (c): the Relationship Disclosure question for the institutional discretionary account. Under subsection 3271(2), when Larkmoor opens a discretionary account or managed account for an institutional client, it must comply with the applicable Parts of Rule 3200:

options.A: Including sections 3216 through 3219 in full
options.B: Only if the institutional client requests compliance in writing
options.C: With the exception of section 3216 only — sections 3217 through 3219 still apply
options.D: With the exception of sections 3216 through 3219

questions.rationale: Clause 3271(2)(i) provides that a Dealer Member opening a discretionary account or managed account for an institutional client must comply with the requirements in Parts A, B and G of Rule 3200, and if applicable Parts C, E and F, with the exception of sections 3216 through 3219 — so the Relationship Disclosure (3216), leverage risk disclosure (3217), pre-trade charge disclosure (3218) and client mail (3219) requirements do not apply. Option C is the closest distractor, carving out only 3216; the exception spans through 3219. A ignores the exception entirely; B makes compliance elective, which the rule does not.
questions.source_quote: "with the exception of sections 3216 through 3219," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5342

questions.scenario: Henrik Wachowski is the designated Supervisor for new accounts at Larkmoor Capital, an Investment Dealer that recently acquired an affiliated Mutual Fund Dealer Member. Four account-opening matters await his decision: (a) operations wants to open Larkmoor accounts for clients transferring from the affiliate by re-using the affiliate's existing client documentation rather than collecting new records; (b) the new-accounts checklist demands the name of a trusted contact person, and written consent to contact that person, for every applicant — and a clerk has now halted the account opening of Pinegate Logistics Inc., a corporation, because it cannot supply one; (c) the institutional desk asks whether a new discretionary account for an institutional client requires the firm's Relationship Disclosure package; and (d) a new corporate client has supplied its director list but, 31 days after opening, the identity of the individual who controls 40% of its voting rights still has not been established.
questions.stem: Matter (d): the corporate account that is 31 days old with the 40% controlling individual still unidentified. What must Henrik require, and until when?

options.A: Liquidate the account's positions and remit the proceeds within five business days
options.B: Report the corporation to the Corporation as a suspicious client
options.C: Restrict the account solely to liquidating trades, transfers, paying out funds or delivering securities, with the restrictions remaining in place until the Dealer Member establishes the individual's identity
options.D: Freeze the account entirely, including any liquidating trades, until identity is established

questions.rationale: Identity of individuals described in 3204(1)(iii) must be established as soon as practicable but not more than 30 days after opening the account (3206(3)); when that fails, subsection 3206(4) requires restricting the account solely to liquidating trades, transfers, paying out funds or delivering securities, and the restrictions must remain in place until the Dealer Member establishes the individual's identity. Option D is the closest distractor — the rule expressly preserves liquidating trades and pay-outs, so a total freeze overstates it. A converts a restriction into forced liquidation; B invents a reporting obligation in place of the prescribed restriction.
questions.source_quote: "These account restrictions must remain in place until the Dealer Member establishes the individual's identity." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4499

questions.stem: Retail advisory client accounts at Mistvale Securities are being redistributed after a Registered Representative retires; Amaia Castellanos is designated as responsible for forty of the accounts. No deposits, transfers or client-information changes have occurred. The Supervisor asks whether anything is owed under subsection 3402(2). What applies?

options.A: Nothing — subsection 3402(2) is triggered only by deposits or transfers of securities
options.B: The firm must review each affected client's account and the securities in it against the suitability criteria, within a reasonable time, because a Registered Representative being designated as responsible for the account is a triggering event
options.C: Only the accounts holding derivatives must be reviewed
options.D: A review is owed only at the next scheduled 36-month KYC refresh

questions.rationale: Clause 3402(2)(ii) lists, among the events after which a Dealer Member must review the retail client's account and its securities against the subsection 3402(1) criteria within a reasonable time, that a Registered Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager is designated as responsible for the account. Option A is the closest distractor — deposits and transfers-in are clause (i), but the trigger list also covers representative changes, client-information changes, security changes, and the 3209(4) review. C invents a derivatives carve-down; D defers the obligation to a cycle the rule does not tie it to.
questions.source_quote: "a Registered Representative, Portfolio Manager or Associate Portfolio Manager is designated as responsible for the account," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5559

questions.stem: A retail client of Pellindale Wealth insists on an order the firm has determined does not satisfy the subsection 3402(1) suitability criteria. The Registered Representative informs her of the basis for that determination and advises against proceeding, then obtains her recorded confirmation to proceed. The Supervisor reviewing the file should flag that one required step under subsection 3402(5) was skipped. Which one?

options.A: Obtaining the Chief Compliance Officer's written sign-off on the trade
options.B: Filing a suitability-override report with the Corporation
options.C: Converting the account to order execution only before accepting the order
options.D: Recommending to the retail client an alternative action that satisfies subsection 3402(1)

questions.rationale: Subsection 3402(5) permits carrying out the client's instruction only if the dealer has (i) informed the client of the basis for the determination and advised against proceeding, (ii) recommended to the retail client an alternative action that satisfies subsection 3402(1), and (iii) received recorded confirmation of the client's instruction to proceed. The file shows (i) and (iii) but not the mandatory alternative recommendation in (ii). Option A is the closest distractor — the rule requires no CCO sign-off; B invents a regulatory filing; C invents an account-conversion precondition.
questions.source_quote: "recommended to the retail client an alternative action that satisfies subsection3402(1), and" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5590

questions.stem: Quarrybrook Securities must gauge the level of suitability owed to an institutional client that wants to begin trading currency-linked structured notes — an instrument it has never held. Under clause 3403(2)(vi), which consideration speaks directly to this situation?

options.A: Whether the client has appointed a designated complaints officer
options.B: The number of years the institutional client has held accounts at the dealer
options.C: Whether the client's total assets under management exceed $10 million
options.D: The specific experience of the client with the type of instrument under consideration, including its ability to independently evaluate how market developments would affect the instrument and ancillary risks such as currency rate risk

questions.rationale: Clause 3403(2)(vi) lists, among the considerations relevant to whether an institutional client can independently evaluate investment risk, the specific experience of the client with the type of instrument under consideration, including the client's ability to independently evaluate how market developments would affect the security, derivative or precious metals bullion and ancillary risks such as currency rate risk — squarely engaged by a first-time structured-note trade with currency exposure. Option C is the closest distractor — the $10 million threshold goes to institutional classification under Rule 1201, not to the sophistication factors; B substitutes relationship length for instrument-specific experience; A imports a complaint-handling concept.
questions.source_quote: "the specific experience of the client with the type of instrument under consideration, including the client's ability to independently evaluate how market developments would" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5615

questions.stem: Ravensmere Capital's order execution only division asks the Supervisor exactly how far the suitability exemption for its accounts extends. Under subsection 3404(1), which statement is accurate?

options.A: Sections 3402 and 3403 are entirely inapplicable to order execution only and direct electronic access accounts, with no residual obligations
options.B: Other than clauses 3402(3)(i) and 3403(4)(i), sections 3402 and 3403 do not apply in respect to an order execution only account or a direct electronic access account
options.C: The exemption covers order execution only accounts but not direct electronic access accounts
options.D: The exemption applies only where the client has waived suitability protections in writing

questions.rationale: Subsection 3404(1) exempts order execution only accounts and direct electronic access accounts from sections 3402 and 3403, but expressly carves out clauses 3402(3)(i) and 3403(4)(i) — the determinations that it is suitable for the client to continue having an account with the Dealer Member. Option A is the closest distractor because it states the exemption without the carve-out; C wrongly excludes DEA accounts, which clause 3404(1)(ii) covers; D describes the separate written-waiver mechanism for certain non-individual permitted clients in subsection 3404(3), not the OEO/DEA exemption.
questions.source_quote: "Other than clauses 3402(3)(i) and 3403(4)(i), sections 3402 or 3403 do not apply in respect to: (i) an order execution only account, or (ii) a direct electronic access account." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5643

questions.stem: Skylark Direct's institutional desk serves pension plans and corporate treasuries. The head of the desk argues that because institutional suitability under section 3403 is owed by the assigned Registered Representatives, the Dealer Member itself has no separate supervisory duty over it. How should the Supervisor respond, per section 3951?

options.A: He is correct — Rule 3900 imposes suitability supervision only for retail clients
options.B: He is correct, provided each institutional client is a regulated entity
options.C: He is wrong — the Dealer Member must supervise each Registered Representative, Investment Representative, Portfolio Manager and Associate Portfolio Manager to confirm their compliance with their responsibilities relating to the suitability determination to institutional clients under section 3403
options.D: He is wrong, but the duty applies only to Portfolio Managers handling institutional managed accounts

questions.rationale: Subsection 3951(1) requires the Dealer Member to supervise each Registered Representative, Investment Representative, Portfolio Manager and Associate Portfolio Manager to confirm their compliance with their responsibilities relating to the suitability determination to institutional clients under section 3403 — a firm-level supervisory duty parallel to the retail one in section 3948. Option A is the closest distractor because section 3948 does cover retail, but 3951 separately covers institutional; B's regulated-entity point goes to the 3404(3) exemption from suitability itself, not to supervision; D narrows the duty to one category when the rule names four.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member must supervise each Registered Representative, Investment Representative, Portfolio Manager and Associate Portfolio Manager to confirm their compliance with their responsibilities relating to the suitability determination to institutional clients under section 3403." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8240

questions.stem: Trentwood Securities' designated Supervisors for retail derivatives accounts maintain the loss-limit approvals required by clause 3962(1)(i). The Supervisor asks what else subsection 3962(1) makes the designated Supervisors responsible for. Which answer is correct?

options.A: Ensuring that all recommendations made for an account are and continue to be suitable for the client, and putting the client's interest first
options.B: Personally executing every derivatives order over a prescribed size
options.C: Setting the firm's margin interest rates on derivatives accounts
options.D: Approving the firm's derivatives advertising before publication

questions.rationale: Subsection 3962(1) makes the designated Supervisors for retail derivatives accounts responsible for (i) reviewing and approving client loss limits when set annually or updated, (ii) ensuring that all recommendations made for an account are and continue to be suitable for the client, and (iii) put the client's interest first. Option D is the closest distractor — advertising pre-approval belongs to the designated Supervisor under Rule 3600, a different function; B and C invent execution and pricing duties the rule does not assign.
questions.source_quote: "ensuring that all recommendations made for an account are and continue to be suitable for the client; and (iii) put the client's interest first." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8300

questions.stem: Wexcombe Wealth delivers all the training Rule 2600 prescribes but keeps no documentation of who attended or what was covered. During an examination, the Corporation asks for evidence. Under clause 2604(1)(iv), what was the dealer required to do?

options.A: Nothing — training delivery itself is sufficient and no records are required
options.B: File training summaries with the Corporation every quarter without being asked
