options.C: Keep a record of all training provided, as prescribed in Rule 2600, and provide the record to the Corporation on request to demonstrate compliance with the proficiency principle
options.D: Retain only the records for Supervisors, since other Approved Persons self-certify

questions.rationale: Clause 2604(1)(iv) requires the Dealer Member to keep a record of all training provided, as prescribed in Rule 2600, and to provide the record to the Corporation on request to demonstrate compliance with the proficiency principle. Option B is the closest distractor — the record is provided on request, not filed on a standing quarterly schedule; A treats delivery as discharging the documentation duty; D invents a Supervisor-only carve-down and a self-certification regime.
questions.source_quote: "The Dealer Member must keep a record of all training provided, as prescribed in Rule 2600, and provide the record to the Corporation on request to demonstrate compliance with the" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3208

questions.stem: Yarrowood Capital sponsors an Approved Person who will serve retail clients trading complex structured products. The firm gives him only its generic onboarding module, with nothing about the firm's client base or product shelf. Against clause 2604(1)(ii), what is deficient?

options.A: Nothing — any training module discharges the sponsorship obligation
options.B: A Dealer Member who sponsors an Approved Person must ensure the individual has received the appropriate training relevant for its business type, including its client and product type, to ensure compliance with the proficiency principle in section 2602
options.C: The firm needed Corporation pre-approval of the module's contents
options.D: Sponsored Approved Persons must instead complete an additional Corporation exam

questions.rationale: Clause 2604(1)(ii) requires a Dealer Member who sponsors an Approved Person to ensure the individual has received the appropriate training relevant for its business type, including its client and product type, to ensure compliance with the proficiency principle in section 2602 — a generic module untethered to the firm's retail clients and complex products fails that standard. Option A is the closest distractor — adequacy is judged against business, client and product type, not mere delivery; C invents a pre-approval requirement for training content; D substitutes an exam for the training obligation.
questions.source_quote: "A Dealer Member who sponsors an Approved Person must ensure that the individual has received the appropriate training relevant for its business type including its client and product type to ensure compliance with the proficiency principle in section 2602." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3196

questions.stem: Zedmont Futures receives trailing commissions from fund companies whose products its retail clients hold. A client asks her Registered Representative whether the firm 'gets anything from anyone else' on her investments. The Supervisor confirms the Relationship Disclosure addresses this. Under paragraph 3216(5)(ii)(g), the document must contain:

options.A: A dollar-by-dollar accounting of every third-party payment received in the prior year
options.B: A statement that the firm receives no compensation from anyone other than its clients
options.C: The names of the fund companies' sales staff assigned to the firm
options.D: A general description of any benefits received, or expected to be received, by the Dealer Member or the Approved Person from a person or company other than the client, in connection with the client's purchase or ownership of a security through the Dealer Member

questions.rationale: Paragraph 3216(5)(ii)(g) requires the relationship disclosure information to include a general description of any benefits received, or expected to be received, by the Dealer Member or the Approved Person, from a person or company other than the Dealer Member's client, in connection with the client's purchase or ownership of a security through the Dealer Member. Option A is the closest distractor — the requirement is a general description, not an itemized dollar accounting; B would be false where trailing commissions exist; C is not required content.
questions.source_quote: "a general description of any benefits received, or expected to be received, by the Dealer Member or the Approved Person, from a person or company other than the Dealer Member's client, in connection with the client's purchase or ownership of a security through the Dealer Member," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4804

questions.stem: Birchhaven Securities' institutional trading Supervisor takes a two-month medical leave. A back-up Supervisor is appointed, but is told nothing about an unresolved pattern of restricted-list trading enquiries the departing Supervisor had been pursuing. Under the Account Supervision Guidance, what is deficient in the handover?

options.A: Nothing — appointing any back-up Supervisor fully satisfies the guidance
options.B: For a prolonged Supervisor absence, the back-up Supervisor should be aware of any specific ongoing issues they need to monitor
options.C: Back-up Supervisors may not supervise institutional accounts at all
options.D: The departing Supervisor was required to delay the leave until the enquiries closed

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 states that Dealers must provide back-up during Supervisors' absences, and that for any prolonged Supervisor absences, the back-up Supervisor(s) should be aware of any specific ongoing issues they need to monitor — here the open restricted-list enquiries. Option A is the closest distractor because back-up was provided, but appointment alone does not satisfy the awareness expectation for prolonged absences. C invents an institutional prohibition; D imposes a leave restriction found nowhere in the guidance.
questions.source_quote: "For any prolonged Supervisor absences, the back-up Supervisor(s) should be aware of any specific ongoing issues they need to monitor." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 144

questions.scenario: Coldstream Capital runs retail advisory and discretionary books plus a centrally managed account program that uses an offshore sub-advisor. Noor Farouk, the firm's compliance director, is finalizing the annual review of how account activity is supervised, and four matters are on her list: (i) the firm's institutional desk has identified recurring problem trades in institutional accounts, but the supervision policies say nothing about what happens after issues are found; (ii) nobody has ever verified that the offshore sub-advisor holds any registration or licence in the jurisdiction of its head office; (iii) the retail trade-supervision policies and procedures list sixteen of the matters whose detection section 3945 prescribes, but are silent on short positions that clients never disclosed; and (iv) all supervisory account reviews are batched and performed in a single sweep at each quarter-end, regardless of what the activity is.
questions.stem: Matter (i): the silent institutional supervision policies. Under subsection 3950(1), what must the policies and procedures for institutional client accounts also do, beyond addressing supervision and review of trading activity?

options.A: Outline the actions to deal with problems or issues identified from supervisory reviews
options.B: Require Corporation notification of every issue identified
options.C: Mandate quarterly suitability reassessments for each institutional account
options.D: Require that all institutional trading be pre-approved by the Chief Compliance Officer

questions.rationale: Subsection 3950(1) requires that the policies and procedures addressing supervision and review of trading activity in institutional clients' accounts must outline the actions to deal with problems or issues identified from supervisory reviews — exactly the gap Noor found. Option B is the closest distractor — issues must be remediated internally per the outlined actions, not all reported to the Corporation; C imports a retail-style suitability cycle that 3950 does not impose; D invents CCO pre-approval of institutional trading.
questions.source_quote: "These policies and procedures must outline the actions to deal with problems or issues identified from supervisory reviews." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8225

questions.scenario: Coldstream Capital runs retail advisory and discretionary books plus a centrally managed account program that uses an offshore sub-advisor. Noor Farouk, the firm's compliance director, is finalizing the annual review of how account activity is supervised, and four matters are on her list: (i) the firm's institutional desk has identified recurring problem trades in institutional accounts, but the supervision policies say nothing about what happens after issues are found; (ii) nobody has ever verified that the offshore sub-advisor holds any registration or licence in the jurisdiction of its head office; (iii) the retail trade-supervision policies and procedures list sixteen of the matters whose detection section 3945 prescribes, but are silent on short positions that clients never disclosed; and (iv) all supervisory account reviews are batched and performed in a single sweep at each quarter-end, regardless of what the activity is.
questions.stem: Matter (ii): the unverified offshore sub-advisor. Under subsection 3279(2), what condition attaching to a sub-advisor designated to deal with managed accounts has Coldstream failed to confirm?

options.A: That the sub-advisor employs at least one CIRO-approved Portfolio Manager
options.B: That the sub-advisor maintains an office in Canada
options.C: That the sub-advisor is registered or licensed, or operating under an exemption, under the securities laws of the jurisdiction where its head office or principal place of business is located, permitting it to carry on managed account activities
options.D: That the sub-advisor's fees are below the Corporation's prescribed ceiling

questions.rationale: Clause 3279(2)(i) requires that a sub-advisor designated under clause 3279(1)(iii) be registered or licensed, or operating under an exemption from registration or licensing, under securities laws of the jurisdiction in which its head office or principal place of business is located, that permits it to carry on managed account activities, or its equivalent, in such jurisdiction; clause (ii) separately requires equivalent conflict-of-interest coverage. Option A is the closest distractor — the sub-advisor route in 3279(1)(iii) exists precisely as an alternative to using the dealer's own Portfolio Managers; B substitutes a Canadian-office requirement for home-jurisdiction licensing; D invents a fee ceiling.
questions.source_quote: "registered or licensed, or operating under an exemption from registration or licensing, under securities laws of the jurisdiction in which its head office or principal place of business is located, that permits it to carry on managed account activities," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 5440

questions.scenario: Coldstream Capital runs retail advisory and discretionary books plus a centrally managed account program that uses an offshore sub-advisor. Noor Farouk, the firm's compliance director, is finalizing the annual review of how account activity is supervised, and four matters are on her list: (i) the firm's institutional desk has identified recurring problem trades in institutional accounts, but the supervision policies say nothing about what happens after issues are found; (ii) nobody has ever verified that the offshore sub-advisor holds any registration or licence in the jurisdiction of its head office; (iii) the retail trade-supervision policies and procedures list sixteen of the matters whose detection section 3945 prescribes, but are silent on short positions that clients never disclosed; and (iv) all supervisory account reviews are batched and performed in a single sweep at each quarter-end, regardless of what the activity is.
questions.stem: Matter (iii): the gap in the retail trade-supervision policies. Which detection item prescribed by subsection 3945(2) is missing?

options.A: Undisclosed short sales
options.B: Failure to pay annual account fees
options.C: Client use of multiple Investment Dealers
options.D: Trading in securities denominated in foreign currencies

questions.rationale: Clause 3945(2)(xv) lists undisclosed short sales among the matters the policies and procedures for the supervision of retail client accounts must specifically address detecting — undisclosed client short positions are exactly the silent gap. Option B is the closest distractor — late payment is a listed item under clause 3945(2)(xiii), but unpaid account fees as such are not; C is a 3403(2) institutional sophistication factor, not a 3945(2) detection item; D — foreign-currency denomination — appears nowhere in the list.
questions.source_quote: "undisclosed short sales," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 8164

questions.scenario: Coldstream Capital runs retail advisory and discretionary books plus a centrally managed account program that uses an offshore sub-advisor. Noor Farouk, the firm's compliance director, is finalizing the annual review of how account activity is supervised, and four matters are on her list: (i) the firm's institutional desk has identified recurring problem trades in institutional accounts, but the supervision policies say nothing about what happens after issues are found; (ii) nobody has ever verified that the offshore sub-advisor holds any registration or licence in the jurisdiction of its head office; (iii) the retail trade-supervision policies and procedures list sixteen of the matters whose detection section 3945 prescribes, but are silent on short positions that clients never disclosed; and (iv) all supervisory account reviews are batched and performed in a single sweep at each quarter-end, regardless of what the activity is.
questions.stem: Matter (iv): batching every supervisory account review into a single quarter-end sweep. How does the Account Supervision Guidance assess this timing?

options.A: Acceptable — the guidance leaves review timing entirely to the dealer with no design expectation
options.B: Deficient, because the guidance mandates real-time review of every order before execution
options.C: Acceptable, provided the quarterly sweep is performed by the Chief Compliance Officer personally
options.D: Deficient — dealers should review accounts on a timely basis, with timing designed to identify matters requiring attention as quickly as possible

questions.rationale: GN-3900-20-001 states that Dealers should review accounts on a timely basis and that their timing should be designed to identify matters requiring attention as quickly as possible — a uniform quarter-end batch lets daily-detectable problems sit for up to three months and also cannot satisfy the daily and monthly supervision requirements of section 3945. Option B is the closest distractor — the guidance permits pre-trade or post-trade review approaches; it does not mandate real-time pre-execution review of every order; A denies the timeliness expectation; C substitutes reviewer seniority for timing design.
questions.source_quote: "Dealers should review accounts on a timely basis. Their timing should be designed to identify matters requiring attention as quickly as possible." — gn-3900-20-001.txt ~line 206

questions.stem: Eastbrook Wealth opens an advisory account for a large insurance company that qualifies as an institutional client. The Registered Representative begins collecting the full retail-style KYC package — needs and objectives, risk profile, time horizon — and schedules the 36-month refresh cycle. Under section 3208, what should the Supervisor clarify?

options.A: The full retail KYC package and refresh cycle apply equally to institutional clients
options.B: Clause 3202(1)(iii) and subsection 3209(4) do not apply in respect to an account held by an institutional client
options.C: Only the 36-month refresh is excused; the initial collection is still required in full
options.D: Institutional accounts are exempt from all of Rule 3200's Part A, including identity establishment

questions.rationale: Subsection 3208(1) provides that clause 3202(1)(iii) — collection of sufficient information on the client's circumstances, needs and objectives, knowledge, risk profile and time horizon — and subsection 3209(4) — the periodic KYC review — do not apply in respect to, among others, an account held by an institutional client. Option C is the closest distractor — the exemption covers both the collection clause and the review subsection, not just the refresh; A ignores the exemption; D overshoots, since identity establishment under 3202(1)(i) and the other Part A duties are not within the 3208(1) exemption.
questions.source_quote: "Clause 3202(1)(iii) and subsection 3209(4) do not apply in respect to:" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4536

questions.stem: The compliance team at Thornquist Securities maps which know-your-client information-collection duties apply to the firm's order execution only division. With respect to clause 3202(1)(iii) and subsection 3209(4), section 3208 treats an order execution only account:

options.A: The same as an advisory account — both duties apply in full
options.B: As exempt from the periodic review only, with initial collection still required
options.C: As one of the exempt account types, alongside direct electronic access accounts, carrying-broker-serviced accounts and institutional client accounts
options.D: As exempt only where the client signs an annual waiver

questions.rationale: Subsection 3208(1) exempts four account types from clause 3202(1)(iii) and subsection 3209(4): an order execution only account, a direct electronic access account, an account maintained at a Dealer Member who is a carrying broker for that account or who only provides trade execution, clearing, settlement or custody services, and an account held by an institutional client. Option B is the closest distractor — both the collection clause and the review subsection are exempted, not just the review. A ignores the exemption; D invents a waiver condition that section 3208 does not contain.
questions.source_quote: "an account held by an institutional client." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4543

questions.stem: A long-standing retail client of Belmare Wealth emails her Registered Representative that she has retired and her income has fallen sharply. The Representative replies politely but leaves her KYC record untouched, planning to deal with it at the account's next scheduled review. Under subsection 3209(3), what is the firm's obligation?

options.A: Take reasonable steps to keep the information current, including updating it within a reasonable time after becoming aware of a significant change in the client's information
options.B: No obligation arises until the next 36-month review date
options.C: Update the record only if the client submits the change in a prescribed form
options.D: Transfer the obligation to the designated Supervisor, who must update all client records personally

questions.rationale: Subsection 3209(3) requires the Dealer Member to take reasonable steps to keep current the information required under Part A of Rule 3200, including updating the information within a reasonable time after the Dealer Member becomes aware of a significant change in the client's information required under section 3202 — retirement and a sharp income drop are exactly such a change. Option B is the closest distractor — the 36-month cycle in 3209(4) is a floor for periodic review, not a shield against known significant changes; C invents a form requirement; D misallocates the duty, since KYC compliance is primarily the assigned Representative's responsibility under 3209(1).
questions.source_quote: "including updating the information within a reasonable time after the Dealer Member becomes aware of a significant change in the client's information required under section 3202." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4551

questions.stem: A new retail client at Caldwynne Capital asks her Registered Representative how often she will hear from the firm about her account. The Supervisor verifies that the Relationship Disclosure's description of client account reporting meets sub-paragraph 3216(5)(ii)(e)(I). That provision requires:

options.A: A guarantee of weekly portfolio updates by telephone
options.B: A statement indicating when trade confirmations and account statements will be sent to the client
options.C: A promise that statements will only be issued when the client requests them
options.D: A list of the personnel who prepare the firm's account statements

questions.rationale: Sub-paragraph 3216(5)(ii)(e)(I) requires the description of client account reporting in the relationship disclosure information to include a statement indicating when trade confirmations and account statements will be sent to the client; (II) and (III) add performance-information and percentage-return-option statements. Option C is the closest distractor — it converts a when-will-be-sent statement into an on-request regime the rule does not describe; A invents a weekly-update guarantee; D is not required content.
questions.source_quote: "a statement indicating when trade confirmations and account statements will be sent to the client," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4785

questions.stem: Drisdale Securities offers retail clients access to exempt-market securities that carry resale restrictions. The Supervisor checks the firm's Relationship Disclosure against sub-paragraph 3216(5)(ii)(a)(I). Within the general description of products and services, the document must include:

options.A: A description of the restrictions on the client's ability to liquidate or resell a security
options.B: A commitment that the firm will repurchase restricted securities on demand
options.C: The full text of the resale provisions of securities legislation
options.D: A waiver releasing the firm from liability for illiquid products

questions.rationale: Sub-paragraph 3216(5)(ii)(a)(I) requires the general description of the types of products and services in the relationship disclosure information to include a description of the restrictions on the client's ability to liquidate or resell a security; (II) adds a statement of investment fund management expense fees or other ongoing fees. Option C is the closest distractor — a description is required, not the reproduction of legislative text; B invents a repurchase commitment; D substitutes a liability waiver for a disclosure.
questions.source_quote: "a description of the restrictions on the client's ability to liquidate or resell a security," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4732

questions.stem: Before accepting a retail client's instruction to buy a mutual fund in her advisory account at Everline Wealth, the Registered Representative must make the pre-trade disclosure required by subsection 3218(1). Which of the following correctly states part of that disclosure?

options.A: The dealer's projected profit on the transaction
options.B: The charges the client will be required to pay, directly or indirectly, in respect of the purchase, or a reasonable estimate if the actual amount is not known to the firm at the time of disclosure
options.C: The fund's historical rate of return for the last ten years
options.D: The identity of the counterparty to the trade

questions.rationale: Clause 3218(1)(i) requires disclosing, before accepting a retail client's instruction to purchase or sell a security or transact in derivatives in an account other than a managed account, the charges the client will be required to pay, directly or indirectly, in respect of the purchase, sale or transaction, or a reasonable estimate if the actual amount of the charges is not known to the firm at the time of disclosure; clauses (ii) to (iv) add deferred sales charge, trailing commission and ongoing-fee disclosures. Option A is the closest distractor — the rule targets client-borne charges, not dealer profit; C and D are not pre-trade disclosure content under 3218.
questions.source_quote: "the charges the client will be required to pay, directly or indirectly, in respect of the purchase, sale or transaction, or a reasonable estimate if the actual amount of the charges is not known to the firm at the time of disclosure," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4866

questions.stem: Foxhollow Capital is opening an initial account for a family trust. The trustees are identified, the trust's existence is established, and controlling individuals are verified. Before the account can be opened, clause 3203(1)(iv) imposes one further determination tied to the trust's beneficiaries. Which one?

options.A: Whether each beneficiary has investment knowledge appropriate to the trust's strategy
options.B: Whether any beneficiary resides outside Canada
options.C: Whether the controlling individuals and, in the case of a trust, any of the known beneficiaries of more than 10% of the trust are insiders of a reporting issuer or any other issuer whose securities are publicly traded
options.D: Whether the beneficiaries have consented in writing to the account opening

questions.rationale: Clause 3203(1)(iv) prohibits opening a partnership or trust account unless the dealer first obtains the controlling-individual information and determines whether those individuals and, in the case of a trust, any of the known beneficiaries of more than 10% of the trust are insiders of a reporting issuer or any other issuer whose securities are publicly traded. Option D is the closest distractor — beneficiary consent is not a 3203 condition; A imports a KYC concept into an identification rule; B substitutes residency for insider status.
questions.source_quote: "in the case of a trust, any of the known beneficiaries of more than 10% of the trust are insiders of a reporting issuer or any other issuer whose securities are publicly traded." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4463

questions.stem: Greymont Securities obtained CIRO approval for new Registered Representative Sofia Grigoryan five weeks ago. The Supervisor checks the post-approval file: no firm-provided training has been scheduled. Under clause 2604(1)(i), what must the Dealer Member do?

options.A: Provide training, as prescribed by the Corporation, within 90 days after approval, considering the type of client and product she will be dealing with, and notify the Corporation of completion within 90 days after approval
options.B: Provide training within 12 months of approval, with no notification required
options.C: Provide training only if the Corporation requests it after an examination
options.D: Provide training before her first trade, then file an annual attestation

questions.rationale: Clause 2604(1)(i) requires a Dealer Member to provide training to its Registered Representatives and Investment Representatives within 90 days after approval, considering the type of client and product the Approved Person will be dealing with, and sub-clause (a) requires notifying the Corporation of completion of the training within 90 days after approval. Option D is the closest distractor — the deadline is 90 days after approval, not the first trade, and the notification is tied to completion, not an annual cycle; B stretches the timeline and drops the notice; C makes a standing duty conditional.
questions.source_quote: "provide training to its Registered Representatives and Investment Representatives within 90 days after approval considering the type of client and product the Approved Person will be dealing with," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3191

questions.stem: Hollowell Futures asks its Supervisor about the conduct training obligation for a newly approved Approved Person. Under clause 2604(2)(i), the individual must complete the conduct training prescribed by the Corporation within what period?

options.A: Within 18 months after approval
options.B: Before approval is granted
options.C: Within 90 days after approval, with no notification requirement
options.D: Within 30 days after approval, and the Dealer Member must notify the Corporation of completion within 30 days after approval

questions.rationale: Clause 2604(2)(i) requires each Approved Person to complete the conduct training prescribed by the Corporation within 30 days after approval, and sub-clause (a) requires the Dealer Member to notify the Corporation of completion within 30 days after approval. Option C is the closest distractor — 90 days is the Dealer Member training timeline in clause 2604(1)(i), not the conduct training deadline; A states the mutual-fund-only upgrade window from section 2605; B confuses post-approval conduct training with pre-approval exam proficiency.
questions.source_quote: "Each Approved Person must complete the conduct training prescribed by the Corporation within 30 days after approval," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3215

questions.stem: Ivydale Investments sponsors Karim Noorani for approval. He passed the prescribed exam four years ago and has never been approved in any category, but he worked continuously in a relevant securities industry role for the past two years, which the Corporation finds acceptable. Under subsection 2628(1), is his exam still valid?

options.A: No — an exam expires absolutely three years after it is written
options.B: No — only prior approval in the same category can preserve exam validity
options.C: Yes, automatically, because exam results never expire for sponsored applicants
options.D: Yes — an individual is deemed to have successfully completed an exam if they gained one year of relevant securities industry experience, acceptable to the Corporation, within three years prior to the date of application for approval

questions.rationale: Clause 2628(1)(iii) deems an exam successfully completed where the individual gained one year of relevant securities industry experience, acceptable to the Corporation, within three years prior to the date of application for approval — Karim's recent two years of acceptable experience satisfies this branch even though the exam itself is more than three years old. Option A is the closest distractor — it states only the clause (i) three-year completion branch and ignores the (ii) prior-approval and (iii) experience branches; B ignores the experience branch; C overstates validity, which is conditional.
questions.source_quote: "the individual who successfully completed the prescribed exam gained one year of relevant securities industry experience, acceptable to the Corporation, within three years prior to the date of application for approval." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3371

questions.stem: Larkmoor Capital sponsors a Portfolio Manager applicant who earned her CFA Charter nine years ago and has used the designation in good standing ever since. The new-registrations clerk worries the Charter has 'gone stale' under the exam validity rules. Under subsection 2628(3), how should the Supervisor respond?

options.A: The Charter must be re-earned every three years like a prescribed exam
options.B: The validity periods do not apply to the CFA Charter, the Canadian Investment Manager Designation or the CIM Designation, provided the holder continues to have the right to use the designation and it has not been revoked or otherwise restricted
options.C: The Charter is valid only if accompanied by one year of recent experience
options.D: The Charter is valid only for Associate Portfolio Manager applications, not Portfolio Manager applications

questions.rationale: Subsection 2628(3) provides that the validity periods do not apply to the Canadian Investment Manager Designation, the Chartered Investment Manager Designation (CIM) and the CFA Charter, provided the holders continue to have the right to use the designation and the designation has not been revoked or otherwise restricted. Option C is the closest distractor — the one-year-experience branch in 2628(1)(iii) revives expired exams, but designations are exempt from the validity periods altogether; A applies the exam regime to designations; D contradicts sub-clause 2603(1)(i)(h), which accepts the CFA Charter for Portfolio Manager approval.
questions.source_quote: "The validity periods do not apply to the Canadian Investment Manager Designation, the Chartered Investment Manager Designation (CIM®) and the CFA Charter" — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3379

questions.stem: Mistvale Securities is sponsoring a derivatives Registered Representative applicant who, within the past two years, was registered and dealing in options and futures with FINRA and the National Futures Association, and who completed the Series 3 and Series 7 exams offered by FINRA. Under subsection 2627(2), what exam relief applies?

options.A: He is exempt from writing the Derivatives Exam
options.B: He is exempt from writing every CIRO-prescribed exam, including the Supervisor Exam
options.C: He is exempt from the Canadian Investment Regulatory Exam only
options.D: No relief — foreign registrations never affect CIRO exam requirements

questions.rationale: Subsection 2627(2) exempts an applicant from writing the Derivatives Exam where the applicant was previously registered and dealing in options and futures with FINRA and the NFA within three years prior to the date of application, and completed the Series 3 and Series 7 exams offered by FINRA. Option C is the closest distractor — the Canadian Investment Regulatory Exam exemption is the separate subsection 2627(1) test, keyed to a similar FINRA registration but not to the Series 3/7 exams; B overstates the relief; D denies the exemption regime in section 2627.
questions.source_quote: "completed the Series 3 and Series 7 exams offered by FINRA." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3359

questions.stem: Nakamura-Pryce Investments is hiring an Associate Portfolio Manager whose managed accounts will include derivatives positions. She has completed Level 1 of the CFA program and has the required investment management experience. Under sub-clause 2603(1)(i)(g), what additional exam requirement attaches because of the derivatives?

options.A: The Institutional Securities Exam
options.B: The Trader Exam
options.C: If managing accounts with derivatives, the Derivatives Exam
options.D: The Chief Compliance Officer Exam

questions.rationale: Sub-clause 2603(1)(i)(g) sets the Associate Portfolio Manager pre-approval exam requirement — Level 1 of the CFA program, or the Canadian Investment Manager Designation or CIM — and adds, in paragraph (I), that if managing accounts with derivatives, the Derivatives Exam is also required. Option A is the closest distractor — the Institutional Securities Exam attaches to institutional Registered Representative categories in sub-clauses 2603(1)(i)(e) and (f), not to the APM category; B applies to Traders; D applies to Chief Compliance Officers.
questions.source_quote: "If managing accounts with derivatives, the Derivatives Exam," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 3137

questions.stem: Quarrybrook Securities executes trades for a retail client strictly as directed by the client's registered adviser, a portfolio management firm acting for her. The trading desk asks whether it must make the section 3218 pre-trade charge disclosure to the client before each instruction. Under subsection 3218(2):

options.A: Yes — pre-trade charge disclosure applies to every retail instruction without exception
options.B: No — subsection 3218(1) does not apply to an instruction involving a client for whom the dealer purchases, sells or transacts only as directed by a registered adviser acting for the client
options.C: Yes, but the disclosure may be delivered annually in arrears
options.D: No, provided the trade value is under a Corporation-prescribed threshold

questions.rationale: Subsection 3218(2) provides that the pre-trade disclosure obligation in subsection 3218(1) does not apply to a Dealer Member in respect of an instruction involving a client for whom the Dealer Member purchases, sells or transacts only as directed by a registered adviser acting for the client. Option A is the closest distractor — 3218(1) itself already excludes managed accounts and 3218(2) adds the adviser-directed exemption, so the duty is not exceptionless; C invents an annual-arrears alternative; D invents a value threshold.
questions.source_quote: "a client for whom the Dealer Member purchases, sells or transacts only as directed by a registered adviser acting for the client." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4876

questions.stem: A retail client of Ravensmere Capital complains she 'never knows what paperwork to expect.' The Supervisor points to the firm's Relationship Disclosure, which must satisfy paragraph 3216(5)(ii)(k). That paragraph requires the document to contain:

options.A: A schedule of the firm's internal records-retention periods
options.B: A copy of every agreement the client will eventually sign
options.C: A listing of the account documents required to be provided to the client with respect to the account
options.D: An index of the Corporation's rules applicable to the account

questions.rationale: Paragraph 3216(5)(ii)(k) requires the relationship disclosure information to contain a listing of the account documents required to be provided to the client with respect to the account — a forward index of the client-facing paperwork. Option B is the closest distractor — a listing is required, not copies of the documents themselves; A concerns internal retention, which is governed by section 3803, not client disclosure; D substitutes a rule index for the document listing.
questions.source_quote: "a listing of the account documents required to be provided to the client with respect to the account," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4815

questions.stem: Skylark Direct's Relationship Disclosure describes its account reporting but says nothing about whether clients can get percentage-return figures. Against sub-paragraph 3216(5)(ii)(e)(III), what must the Supervisor require be added?

options.A: A statement indicating whether or not the provision of account percentage return information will be an option available to the client as part of the account service offering
options.B: A guarantee that percentage returns will beat the client's chosen benchmark
options.C: A monthly percentage-return calculation mailed to every client automatically
options.D: Nothing — percentage-return availability is never a disclosure topic

questions.rationale: Sub-paragraph 3216(5)(ii)(e)(III) requires the relationship disclosure information to include a statement indicating whether or not the provision of account percentage return information will be an option available to the client as part of the account service offering. Option C is the closest distractor — the rule requires a statement about whether the option is available, not the automatic delivery of the calculation itself; B converts disclosure into a performance guarantee; D denies a content requirement that is expressly listed.
questions.source_quote: "a statement indicating whether or not the provision of account percentage return information will be an option available to the client as part of the account service offering," — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4791

questions.stem: Trentwood Securities already delivers a standalone fee schedule to every new retail client. Rather than repeating the full fee detail inside the Relationship Disclosure document, compliance proposes a short general description plus a pointer to the fee schedule. Under clause 3216(4)(i), is this format acceptable?

options.A: No — every item of required content must be reproduced in full within the Relationship Disclosure document itself
options.B: No — fee information may never be delivered in a separate document
options.C: Yes, but only for institutional clients
options.D: Yes — where specific information has otherwise been provided to the client, the document may include a general description and a reference to the other disclosure materials containing the required information

questions.rationale: Sub-paragraph 3216(4)(i)(c) requires the relationship disclosure information to include all the required content set out in subsection 3216(5), or, where specific information has otherwise been provided to the client by the Dealer Member, a general description and a reference to the other disclosure materials containing the required information — exactly the proposed structure. Option A is the closest distractor — it states the default while missing the express reference alternative; C is wrong because relationship disclosure is not required for institutional clients at all under 3216(1); B contradicts the reference mechanism.
questions.source_quote: "where specific information has otherwise been provided to the client by the Dealer Member, a general description and a reference to the other disclosure materials containing the required information." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4716

questions.stem: Wexcombe Wealth wants to stop producing a stand-alone Relationship Disclosure booklet and instead weave the required relationship disclosure information into its account opening package. Under clause 3216(4)(ii), is this delivery approach permitted?

options.A: No — relationship disclosure information must always be a stand-alone document
options.B: Yes — Dealer Members may choose to provide the relationship disclosure information as a separate document or to integrate it with other account opening materials
options.C: Only for institutional clients
options.D: Only if each client consents in writing to the integrated format

questions.rationale: Clause 3216(4)(ii) provides that Dealer Members may choose to provide the relationship disclosure information as a separate document or to integrate it with other account opening materials. Option D is the closest distractor — the choice belongs to the dealer; no client consent condition attaches to the format decision; A states the opposite of the rule; C is wrong because relationship disclosure is not required for institutional clients at all under subsection 3216(1).
questions.source_quote: "Dealer Members may choose to provide the relationship disclosure information as a separate document or to integrate it with other account opening materials." — idpc-rules.txt ~line 4724

questions.stem: An operations clerk at Wetherby Securities, an Investment Dealer, processes a new account and files the account application without noting the newly assigned account number anywhere on it. Under subsection 3212(3), what has the firm failed to do?

options.A: Record the account number on the account application
options.B: Assign the account number before the client's full name was known
options.C: Obtain the designated Supervisor's approval of the account number
options.D: File the application with the Corporation within one business day

questions.rationale: Subsection 3212(3) requires that the Dealer Member record the account number on the account application. Option B describes the separate condition in subsection 3214(1) about when a number may be assigned, which is not what was missed here. Option C invents a Supervisor approval specific to account numbers, and option D invents a Corporation filing requirement; neither appears in section 3212.
questions.source_quote: "The Dealer Member must record the account number on the account application." — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 4598

questions.stem: Bowmore Clearing Inc., a Dealer Member, acts solely as carrying broker for accounts introduced by another Dealer Member. The Supervisor asks whether any suitability determination under Rule 3400 is owed on those carried accounts. Under subsection 3404(2), what is the position?

options.A: Section 3402 is excluded but section 3403 still applies to institutional carried accounts
options.B: Only the continuing-account determinations in subsections 3402(3) and 3403(4) apply
options.C: Both sections apply unless each introduced client waives them in writing
options.D: Sections 3402 and 3403 do not apply in respect to an account maintained at a Dealer Member who is a carrying broker for that account

questions.rationale: Subsection 3404(2) provides that sections 3402 and 3403 do not apply in respect to an account maintained at a Dealer Member who is a carrying broker for that account or who only provides trade execution, clearing, settlement or custody services. Option B is the closest distractor because the separate exemption in subsection 3404(1) for OEO and direct electronic access accounts preserves clauses 3402(3)(i) and 3403(4)(i); subsection 3404(2) contains no such carve-out. Option A invents a partial exclusion and option C imports the written waiver mechanism from subsection 3404(3)(ii), which is not needed under 3404(2).
questions.source_quote: "Sections 3402 and 3403 do not apply in respect to an account maintained at a Dealer Member who is a carrying broker for that account" — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 5646

questions.stem: Cathcart Trust Company, a trust company, holds an institutional account at Loxley Securities. A new Supervisor asks whether Loxley must periodically determine that it remains suitable for Cathcart to continue having the account and that the account's scope of products and services remains suitable. Under subsection 3404(4), is that determination required?

options.A: Yes — subsection 3403(4) applies to every institutional client without exception
options.B: Yes, but only if Cathcart has not waived its protections in writing
options.C: No — subsection 3403(4) does not apply to an account held by an institutional client who is a trust company
options.D: No — trust companies are retail clients under the IDPC Rules

questions.rationale: Subsection 3404(4) provides that subsection 3403(4) — the continuing-account and scope-of-services determination — does not apply to an account held by an institutional client who is a Dealer Member, regulated entity, exempt market dealer, portfolio manager, bank, trust company or insurance company. Option B is the closest distractor: the written waiver in clause 3404(3)(ii)(c) relieves subsections 3403(1) and 3403(2) for certain permitted clients, but listed entities such as trust companies are excluded from subsection 3403(4) by subsection 3404(4) without any waiver. Options A and D contradict the rule text.
questions.source_quote: "Subsection 3403(4) does not apply to an account held by an institutional client who is a Dealer Member, regulated entity, exempt market dealer, portfolio manager, bank, trust company or insurance company." — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 5658

questions.stem: Strathmore Importing Ltd., a non-individual, earns revenue in euros but pays suppliers in U.S. dollars. It enters derivatives transactions that swap its euro exposure for U.S. dollar exposure, and the firm confirms the aggregate amount of currency risk Strathmore faces is not increased by the substitution. Under the Rule 1201 definition of "hedger", does this intended effect satisfy the definition?

options.A: No — only transactions that eliminate or reduce market-value risk can ever qualify
options.B: Yes — the definition includes derivatives intended to substitute the risk of one currency for the risk of another, provided the aggregate currency risk is not increased
options.C: No — currency transactions are excluded from the hedger definition entirely
options.D: Yes, but only if Strathmore is also classified as an institutional client first

questions.rationale: The hedger definition in Rule 1201 covers derivatives whose intended effect is to substitute the risk associated with one currency for the risk associated with another currency, provided the aggregate amount of currency risk to which the hedger is exposed is not increased by the substitution. Option A is the closest distractor because eliminating or reducing market-value risk is only the first branch of the intended-effect test; currency substitution is the express second branch. Option C contradicts that express branch, and option D reverses the sequence — hedger status is assessed before any institutional classification, not after.
questions.source_quote: "substitute the risk associated with one currency for the risk associated with another currency, provided the aggregate amount of currency risk to which the hedger is exposed is not increased by the substitution" — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 441

questions.stem: Penhaligon Bank, a bank and an institutional client of Rookwood Securities, is asked by a junior compliance officer to sign a written waiver of the protections in subsections 3403(1) and 3403(2) so the firm can stop making transaction-level institutional suitability determinations. Under section 3404, is the waiver necessary?

options.A: No — an account held by a bank is already excluded from section 3403 under clause 3404(3)(i); the written waiver route applies to non-individual institutional clients not described in that clause
options.B: Yes — every institutional client must waive in writing before any suitability relief is available
options.C: No — banks are exempt only if they are also permitted clients under National Instrument 31-103
options.D: Yes — but the waiver must be renewed annually

questions.rationale: Clause 3404(3)(i) excludes from section 3403 (other than subsection 3403(4)) an account held by a Dealer Member, regulated entity, exempt market dealer, portfolio manager, bank, trust company or insurance company. The written waiver path in clause 3404(3)(ii) applies to a non-individual institutional client that is a permitted client and is not a client described in clause 3404(3)(i). Option C is the closest distractor: the permitted-client condition belongs to the waiver path, not to the listed-entity exclusion, so a bank needs no permitted-client status. Options B and D invent universal and annual waiver requirements found nowhere in section 3404.
questions.source_quote: "is not a client described in clause 3404(3)(i), and (c) has waived, in writing, the protections offered to them under subsections 3403(1) and 3403(2)" — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 5654

questions.stem: A new client of Elsinore Securities wants an account in which she makes every investment decision herself but can rely on advice from her Registered Representative, with the firm and the Representative answerable for all advice given. Under the Rule 1201 definitions, which account relationship matches this description?

options.A: A managed account
options.B: A discretionary account
options.C: An order execution only account
options.D: An advisory account

questions.rationale: Rule 1201 defines an advisory account as an account subject to a suitability determination where the client is responsible for all investment decisions but is able to rely on advice given by a Registered Representative, and the Dealer Member and the Registered Representative are responsible for all advice given. Option C is the closest distractor — in an order execution only account the client is also solely responsible for decisions, but the Dealer Member provides no recommendations at all, so reliance on advice is impossible. Options A and B describe accounts where the firm exercises decision-making or discretionary authority, the opposite of this client's wish.
questions.source_quote: "the client is responsible for all investment decisions but is able to rely on advice given by a Registered Representative" — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 172

questions.stem: Two years after opening a retail advisory account, a Supervisor at Verlaine Securities asks what ongoing account-level determination the firm owes the client under subsection 3402(3), separate from any transaction-level suitability determination. What must the firm determine, on a reasonable basis and putting the retail client's interest first?

options.A: That the account has been profitable for the client over the period
options.B: That the client has accepted every recommendation made by the firm
options.C: That it is suitable for the retail client to continue having an account with the firm, and that the scope of products, services and account relationships the client has access to within the account are suitable for the client
options.D: That the client's account application has been re-signed within the past 12 months

questions.rationale: Subsection 3402(3) requires the Dealer Member to determine, on a reasonable basis and putting the retail client's interest first, that it is suitable for the retail client to continue having an account with the Dealer Member, and that the scope of products, services and account relationships the client has access to within the account are suitable. Option A is the closest distractor because account performance is not the standard — suitability of the continuing relationship and its scope is. Option B describes a consideration relevant to institutional sophistication under subsection 3403(2), and option D invents a re-signing requirement.
questions.source_quote: "it is suitable for the retail client to continue having an account with the Dealer Member, and (ii) the scope of products, services and account relationships which the retail client has access to within the account are suitable for the retail client" — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 5578

questions.stem: Tillsonburg Securities receives client accounts from its affiliated Mutual Fund Dealer Member and wants to rely on the affiliate's documentation rather than re-papering each client. One condition in subsection 3212(4) concerns the account offering itself. What must be true of the account offering and the investment products and services to be made available at Tillsonburg?

options.A: They must be materially the same as those at the affiliate firm
options.B: They must be broader than those offered at the affiliate firm
options.C: They must be limited to proprietary products of the affiliate
options.D: They must be identical in every respect, with no variation permitted

questions.rationale: Clause 3212(4)(i) permits reliance on the affiliate's documentation only if the account offering and investment products and services to be made available to the client at the Dealer Member are materially the same as those at the affiliate firm. Option D is the closest distractor — the standard is material sameness, not absolute identity. Option B has no basis in the rule, and option C confuses the condition with proprietary-product disclosure under the relationship disclosure rules.
questions.source_quote: "the account offering and investment products and services to be made available to the client at the Dealer Member are materially the same as those at the affiliate firm" — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 4602

questions.stem: Continuing its review of accounts received from an affiliated Mutual Fund Dealer Member, Tarringford Capital checks the know-your-client condition in subsection 3212(4). Beyond fees and the assignment clause, what must be materially the same as at the affiliate firm?

options.A: The compensation grid applied to Registered Representatives at each firm
options.B: The know-your-client information collected by the Dealer Member and the approach used by the Dealer Member to assess that information
options.C: The trading platforms through which client orders are entered
options.D: The names of the Supervisors responsible for the transferred accounts

questions.rationale: Clause 3212(4)(iii) conditions reliance on the affiliate's documentation on the know-your-client information collected by the Dealer Member and the approach used by the Dealer Member to assess the know-your-client information collected being materially the same as at the affiliate firm. Option A is the closest distractor because compensation arrangements, while supervised elsewhere, are not among the four 3212(4) conditions. Options C and D concern operational systems and personnel, neither of which appears in subsection 3212(4).
questions.source_quote: "the know‐your‐client information collected by the Dealer Member and the approach used by the Dealer Member to assess the know‐your‐client information collected are materially the same as at the affiliate firm" — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 4610

questions.stem: A designated Supervisor at Ravensbourne Investments reviews a new account file. It contains identity-verification support, the know-your-client record and the signed account application, but nothing evidencing why the account itself was judged appropriate. Which item listed in the section 3210 definition of "client account records" is missing?

options.A: The client's trusted contact person form
options.B: A record of the client's first three trades
options.C: Documentation supporting the account appropriateness assessment
options.D: The Corporation's written approval of the account opening

questions.rationale: The definition of client account records in subsection 3210(1) lists, among other items, documentation supporting the account appropriateness assessment — the file here lacks exactly that. Option A is the closest distractor because trusted contact information relates to know-your-client collection, not the enumerated appropriateness-documentation item. Option B confuses account records with trading records, and option D invents Corporation-level approval of individual account openings.
questions.source_quote: "(ii) documentation supporting the account appropriateness assessment," — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 4566

questions.stem: In the suitability-process portion of its relationship disclosure, Umbermere Securities describes its approach to assessing client circumstances. Under paragraph 3216(5)(ii)(d)(II), what related statement about the client's own information must also appear?

options.A: A statement that the firm's suitability methodology is proprietary and confidential
options.B: A statement that the client will be provided with a copy of the know-your-client information obtained from the client and documented at the time of account opening and when there are significant changes to the information
options.C: A statement that know-your-client information will be shared with the Corporation annually
options.D: A statement that the client must re-attest to their information every six months

questions.rationale: Paragraph 3216(5)(ii)(d)(II) requires the relationship disclosure to include a statement that the client will be provided with a copy of the know-your-client information that is obtained from the client and documented at time of account opening and when there are significant changes to the information. Option D is the closest distractor — updating obligations exist, but no six-month client re-attestation appears in the rule, and the required statement concerns giving the client a copy. Options A and C invent confidentiality and Corporation-filing statements not found in the content list.
questions.source_quote: "information that is obtained from the client and documented at time of account opening and when there are significant changes to the information" — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 4762

questions.stem: Glenfiddich Insurance Corp., an insurance company classified as an institutional client, opens a margin account at Whitlock Securities and intends to borrow against it. A junior officer asks whether the firm must deliver the leverage risk disclosure statement under section 3217. What is the correct answer?

options.A: Yes — every margin client must receive the statement before borrowing
options.B: Yes, but only an abbreviated institutional version of the statement
options.C: No — for an institutional client's margin account the firm complies with the exception of sections 3216 through 3219, and section 3217 falls within that excepted range
options.D: No — insurance companies receive the statement from their own regulator instead

questions.rationale: Subsection 3245(2) provides that a Dealer Member opening a margin account for an institutional client complies with the applicable Parts of Rule 3200 with the exception of sections 3216 through 3219; section 3217, the leverage risk disclosure statement, sits inside that excepted range, and subsection 3217(1) itself is framed around retail clients. Option A is the closest distractor because the statement obligation in subsection 3217(1) attaches to retail clients, not to every margin client. Option B invents an institutional version, and option D invents delivery by another regulator.
questions.source_quote: "comply with the requirements in Parts A, B and E of Rule 3200, and if applicable, Parts C, D, F and G of Rule 3200, with the exception of sections 3216 through 3219" — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 5117

questions.stem: Pellworth Securities wants to reformat the leverage risk disclosure statement to match its branded client documents, adjusting layout and minor phrasing while keeping the message about borrowing risk and the unchanged repayment obligation intact. Under subsection 3217(3), is this permissible?

options.A: Yes — the statement must be in substantially the prescribed words, so the substance must be preserved but exact replication of the wording is not demanded
options.B: No — the statement must reproduce the prescribed words exactly, with no deviation
options.C: Yes — the firm may write any leverage warning it considers adequate
options.D: No — only the Corporation may produce the document, and firms must use the Corporation's printed version

questions.rationale: Subsection 3217(3) requires that a leverage risk disclosure statement be in substantially the prescribed words — the standard is substantial conformity with the prescribed text, which preserves the substance while not demanding verbatim replication. Option B is the closest distractor: it overstates the standard by reading "substantially" out of the rule. Option C understates it — the firm is not free to draft any warning it likes — and option D invents a Corporation-printed document requirement.
questions.source_quote: "A leverage risk disclosure statement must be in substantially the following words:" — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 4857

questions.stem: A compliance trainer at Kirkbride Wealth lists situations said to trigger delivery of the leverage risk disclosure statement under subsection 3217(1). Which listed situation is NOT one of the triggers in that subsection?

options.A: When opening a new account for a retail client
options.B: Prior to making an initial recommendation to a retail client to purchase securities using borrowed money
options.C: When first becoming aware of a retail client's intention to purchase securities using borrowed money
options.D: When a retail client's existing margin debit first exceeds fifty per cent of the account's value

questions.rationale: Subsection 3217(1) sets out three triggers: when opening a new account for a retail client, prior to making an initial recommendation to a retail client to purchase securities using borrowed money, or when first becoming aware of a retail client's intention to purchase securities using borrowed money. A margin debit crossing a percentage threshold appears nowhere in the subsection — margin maintenance is governed by the margin account rules, not the leverage statement triggers. Options A, B and C each restate one of the three actual triggers, with option C the closest to plausible variation since awareness of intention is the least-known trigger.
questions.source_quote: "When opening a new account for a retail client, prior to making an initial recommendation to a retail client to purchase securities using borrowed money, or when first becoming aware of a retail client’s intention to purchase securities using borrowed money" — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 4848

questions.stem: Caldermuir Brokers sometimes needs securities held in a client's own margin account to make delivery against a short sale in that same client's account. Which clause of the margin account agreement content in subsection 3247(2) addresses this practice?

options.A: The clause describing the client's obligation to pay interest on debit balances
options.B: The clause describing the firm's right to sell assets in the client's account and make purchases to cover short sales
options.C: The clause describing the firm's obligation to carry out transactions in accordance with Corporation requirements
options.D: The clause describing the extent of the Dealer Member's right, if any, to use a security in the client's account for delivery against a short sale

questions.rationale: Clause 3247(2)(vi) requires the margin account agreement to describe the extent of the Dealer Member's right, if any, to use a security in the client's account for delivery against a short sale. Option B is the closest distractor: clause 3247(2)(v) concerns selling account assets and covering short sales when remedying deficiencies, not using a held security to make delivery. Note that the separate clause 3247(2)(vii) covers delivery against short sales in accounts owned or controlled by the Dealer Member, a partner or Director — a different situation from this client's own account. Options A and C describe other required clauses that do not touch delivery against short sales.
questions.source_quote: "the extent of the Dealer Member’s right, if any, to use a security in the client’s account for delivery against a short sale," — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 5139

questions.stem: A trainee at Vexford Derivatives reviews the firm's derivatives trading agreement and labels four of its required items as "Dealer Member rights". Which of the four items is actually a Dealer Member OBLIGATION under section 3252, not a right?

options.A: Exercising discretion in accepting orders
options.B: If required under any applicable laws or requested to do so, providing information to regulators regarding position limit, exercise limit requirements and reporting derivative positions or derivative transactions related data
options.C: Imposing trading or position limits or closing out positions under specified conditions
options.D: Raising money on and pledging assets held in the client's account

questions.rationale: Clause 3252(1)(v)(a) places the provision of information to regulators regarding position limit, exercise limit requirements and reporting derivative positions or derivative transactions related data among the Dealer Member's obligations, conditioned on legal requirement or request. Options A and C restate the rights in clause 3252(1)(ii), and option D restates the right in clause 3252(1)(iii)(c). Option D is the closest distractor for a trainee because pledging client assets sounds onerous, but the rule frames it as an extent-of-right disclosure, not an obligation.
questions.source_quote: "if required under any applicable laws or requested to do so, provide information to regulators regarding position limit, exercise limit requirements and reporting derivative positions or derivative transactions related data" — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 5208

questions.stem: The clearing house through which Norwich Futures Inc. clears imposes a documentation condition on client agreements that is not specifically enumerated in clauses 3252(1)(i) through (ix). Must Norwich's derivatives trading agreement nonetheless cover it?

options.A: Yes — clause 3252(1)(x) requires the agreement to include any other matter required by a derivatives trading, clearing or issuing entity
options.B: No — the agreement's content is exhaustively fixed by the enumerated clauses
options.C: Only if the Corporation separately adopts the clearing house condition into its own rules
options.D: Only for institutional client agreements

questions.rationale: Clause 3252(1)(x) requires the derivatives trading agreement to include any other matter required by a derivatives trading, clearing or issuing entity, sweeping clearing house documentation conditions into the agreement even when not separately enumerated. Option C is the closest distractor — Corporation adoption is unnecessary because the clause defers directly to the entity's requirements. Option B ignores the catch-all, and option D invents a retail and institutional distinction the clause does not draw.
questions.source_quote: "any other matter required by a derivatives trading, clearing or issuing entity," — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 5239

questions.stem: Ostergaard Commodities opens futures accounts subject to a futures segregation and portability customer protection regime. Beyond the porting disclosure document and the client's acknowledgement, what third step does subsection 3256(1) require concerning the clearing corporation?

options.A: Obtaining the clearing corporation's written approval of each client account
options.B: Filing each client's porting election with the Corporation
options.C: Guaranteeing that the client's positions will be ported within one business day of a default
options.D: Notifying the client of the Dealer Member's obligation to provide the clearing corporation with information and reports related to the client's positions

questions.rationale: Clause 3256(1)(iii) requires the Dealer Member to notify the client of the obligation of the Dealer Member to provide the clearing corporation with information and reports related to the client's positions. Option C is the closest distractor — the disclosure document covers the benefits, risks, requirements and conditions for porting, but no one-business-day porting guarantee exists in the rule. Options A and B invent clearing corporation account approvals and Corporation filings not found in section 3256.
questions.source_quote: "notify the client of the obligation of the Dealer Member to provide the clearing corporation with information and reports related to the client’s positions." — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 5312

questions.stem: Hawthorne Capital opens a retail account that will trade only plain futures contracts — no options of any kind. A new Supervisor asks whether the derivatives trading agreement must state the firm's deadlines for submitting an exercise notice and its method of distributing assignment notices. What is the correct answer?

options.A: Yes — every derivatives trading agreement must contain those items regardless of product
options.B: Yes — futures contracts settle by exercise, so the items are essential
options.C: No — those items are required by clause 3252(1)(xi) for options, futures contract options and similar derivatives; for futures contracts the agreement must instead include the minimum-margin disclosure in clause 3252(1)(xii)
options.D: No — exercise and assignment items are required only for institutional clients

questions.rationale: The chapeau of clause 3252(1)(xi) confines the exercise-notice deadline, assignment-notice method and related disclosures to options, futures contract options and similar derivatives, while clause 3252(1)(xii) prescribes, for futures contracts, forward contracts, contracts for difference and similar derivatives, the disclosure that the client must maintain minimum margin equal to the greatest of the marketplace or clearing house amount, the Corporation's requirements, or the Dealer Member's requirements. Option A is the closest distractor because most agreement content in subsection 3252(1) is indeed universal — but clause (xi) is expressly product-scoped. Options B and D misstate futures mechanics and invent a client-type distinction.
questions.source_quote: "for options, futures contract options and similar derivatives:" — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 5240

questions.stem: A retail client opens an order execution only account at Wrexley Direct that will transact speculatively in futures contract options. The OEO firm's compliance staff argue that cumulative loss limits are only for advised accounts. Under subsection 3252(2), is a client cumulative loss limit required for this account?

options.A: Yes — the requirement covers transactions in futures contract options and applies to an account other than a hedging account whether it is an advisory, discretionary, managed or OEO account
options.B: No — loss limits never apply to order execution only accounts
options.C: No — futures contract options are outside the products listed in subsection 3252(2)
options.D: Yes, but only if the client's account equity exceeds a prescribed threshold

questions.rationale: Clause 3252(2)(i) applies the cumulative loss limit where transactions involve futures contracts, forward contracts, contracts for difference, futures contract options or similar derivatives, or highly-leveraged securities or derivatives, and clause 3252(2)(ii) applies it to an account, other than a hedging account, whether the account is an advisory account, a discretionary account, a managed account or an OEO account. Option B is the closest distractor — the compliance staff's view fails because OEO accounts are expressly named. Option C wrongly excludes futures contract options, which are expressly listed, and option D invents an equity threshold.
questions.source_quote: "applies to an account, other than a hedging account, whether the account is an advisory account, a discretionary account, a managed account or an order execution only account" — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 5261

questions.stem: Yardarm Pension Services, an institutional client, provides Iverness Futures with a letter of undertaking. None of Yardarm's accounts is charged interest on a debit balance. The drafting lawyer asks whether the letter must state the conditions under which transfers of the client's funds, securities or other property may be made between accounts. What is the correct answer?

options.A: Yes — every letter of undertaking must state those transfer conditions
options.B: No — that content is triggered under clause 3253(2)(ii) only if the client has an account that is charged interest on a debit balance
options.C: Yes, unless the Corporation grants an exemption
options.D: No — transfer conditions may never appear in a letter of undertaking

questions.rationale: Clause 3253(2)(ii) requires the letter of undertaking to state the conditions under which transfers of the client's funds, securities or other property may be made between accounts if the client has an account that is charged interest on a debit balance — with no such account, the trigger is absent. Option A is the closest distractor because the compliance commitment in clause 3253(2)(i) is unconditional, inviting the assumption that the second item is too. Option C invents a Corporation exemption process, and option D wrongly forbids content the rule merely conditions.
questions.source_quote: "if the client has an account that is charged interest on a debit balance, the conditions under which transfers of the client’s funds, securities or other property may be made between accounts" — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 5281

questions.stem: Jocelyn Mercer, a Supervisor at Severn Institutional Markets, prepares to open a derivatives account for an institutional client using a letter of undertaking. Which of the four steps in subsection 3251(1) does the letter of undertaking replace?

options.A: Obtaining a completed derivatives account application from the client
options.B: Providing the client with the most recent derivatives disclosure statement or similar disclosure document
options.C: Recording the relevant designated Supervisors' approval in writing
options.D: Obtaining a completed and signed derivatives trading agreement from the client

questions.rationale: Subsection 3253(1) provides that, instead of a derivatives trading agreement, a Dealer Member may obtain a letter of undertaking for accounts where the client is classified as an institutional client — the substitution runs to the trading agreement in clause 3251(1)(ii) alone. Option B is the closest distractor: clients sometimes assume institutional status waives the disclosure document, but clause 3251(1)(iii) is not displaced by section 3253. The application in option A and the written approval record in option C likewise remain required under clauses 3251(1)(i) and (iv).
questions.source_quote: "Instead of a derivatives trading agreement, a Dealer Member may obtain a letter of undertaking for accounts where the client is classified as an institutional client." — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 5268

questions.stem: Reviewing a draft managed account agreement at Lindenmoor Counsel, a Supervisor notes that instead of restating the client's financial circumstances, investment knowledge, time horizon, needs and objectives and risk profile, the agreement cross-references the client's documented know-your-client record. Under clause 3278(1)(i), is this drafting acceptable?

options.A: No — the agreement must restate each attribute in full within its own text
options.B: No — only the risk profile may be incorporated by reference
options.C: Yes — the clause requires the agreement to describe or refer to those client attributes, so a reference satisfies it
options.D: Yes — because managed account agreements have no required content at all

questions.rationale: Clause 3278(1)(i) requires the managed account agreement to describe or refer to the client's personal and financial circumstances, investment knowledge, investment time horizon, investment needs and objectives and risk profile applicable to the managed account — the disjunctive "describe or refer to" permits incorporation by reference. Option A is the closest distractor because it reads the clause as if it said only "describe". Option B invents a single-attribute carve-out, and option D ignores the prescribed content in subsection 3278(1) entirely.
questions.source_quote: "describe or refer to the client’s personal and financial circumstances, investment knowledge, investment time horizon, investment needs and objectives and risk profile that are applicable to the managed account or accounts" — research/sources/rules/idpc-rules.txt ~line 5422

questions.stem: Farnsworth Securities wants to authorize Cormac Bell, a Registered Representative with eight years of advisory experience, to make ongoing investment decisions for clients in the firm's managed account program. Under subsection 3279(1), may the firm designate him?

options.A: Yes — any Approved Person with five or more years of experience may be designated
options.B: No — the individual authorized to deal with managed accounts must be a Portfolio Manager, an Associate Portfolio Manager, or a sub-advisor with whom the firm has a written sub-advisor agreement; a Registered Representative is not on that list
options.C: Yes — provided the designated Supervisor approves each of his trades in advance
options.D: No — only external sub-advisors may handle managed accounts

questions.rationale: Subsection 3279(1) requires the Dealer Member to designate an individual authorized to deal with managed accounts who is a Portfolio Manager, an Associate Portfolio Manager, or a sub-advisor with whom the Dealer Member has entered into a written sub-advisor agreement — the list is closed, and a Registered Representative, however experienced, is not on it. Option A is the closest distractor because a two-year experience standard does exist, but under subsection 3275(1) for discretionary accounts, not managed accounts. Option C cannot cure the proficiency category, and option D excludes the in-house Portfolio Manager and Associate Portfolio Manager limbs.
